“Yeah,” Mara said. Her voice came out thin.

“You closed so hard,” Lila said. “You were, like, flying.”

“Not hard enough,” Mara said. She tried to make it a joke; it landed flat between them.

Lila’s smile faltered. “That Jackson girl’s a senior. This was her last shot. You’ve got more meets.”

Mara nodded. She knew the math. Knew exactly how many meets, how many chances, how many tenths of a second separated a scholarship from four more years of the diner’s grease-smell and Rosa’s tired eyes. Fifty-five point three was nothing if fifty-five point one belonged to someone else.

“Come on,” Lila said softly. “Coach wants to talk splits.”

“I’ll catch up,” Mara lied.

She walked it off alone. The metal bleachers shadows striped the track. In the stands, scouts shook hands with Jackson’s coach. Someone pointed. Nobody pointed at her.

She found Rosa near the exit, Nico bouncing beside her.

“You were so fast,” Nico said, grabbing Mara’s arm. “Like, zoooom.” He made a swooping gesture with his whole body.

“New PR,” Rosa said. Her English flattened the letters. “That’s good.”

“Not good enough,” Mara said before she could stop herself.

Rosa’s mouth thinned. “Don’t start. We go?” Her eyes flicked toward the scouts, the other girl laughing with her offer already half-made. Something in Rosa’s shoulders sank.

“Yeah,” Mara said.

They rode the Q bus home. Mara watched her reflection in the smeared window: ponytail frayed, medal-less, just another girl with a backpack.

At their stop, Rosa went straight to her evening shift. Nico trailed a few steps behind, humming a game theme. Mara told him she’d be home later, that she needed a cooldown run, that Coach said so. She lied again. It was getting easier.

She cut down side streets she knew by their cracks. Past the laundromat where Lila’s parents had already propped their neon-open sign. Past a half-shuttered botanica with a saint’s eyes peeled halfway off the glass. Her regular sneakers slapped the concrete, soles worn nearly bald.

She didn’t mean to find the shop. It just appeared at the corner of 41st and a nothing side street that usually only held an empty lot and a hydrant.

Today, there was a storefront.

LACE & LEGEND, the flickering sign said, one tube of neon failing so it read LAC & LE END in pulses.

Mara slowed. The windows were crowded with sneakers, all kinds, all eras. Old leather high-tops, glossy new runners, vintage things she’d only seen on sneakerhead accounts. The inside glowed a tired fluorescent.

She should’ve kept running.

Instead, she pushed the door.

A bell chimed, the sound oddly crisp.

It smelled like rubber, dust, and—weirdly—rain on hot pavement, that first hit of relief in August. Rows of shoes climbed the walls. Some were tagged with logos she knew; others had symbols she didn’t. Little wing shapes, eyes, waves.

“Welcome,” a voice said.

The guy behind the counter looked like he’d been in every city at once. Late thirties or early forties, wiry, tan, black hair buzzed close with a streak of silver. Thin gold chain at his throat. Hoodie, track pants, spotless white sneakers despite the grime.

“You’re closed?” Mara asked. It felt half-true. The place had the emptiness of an after-hours train.

“Just opened,” he said. “For you, anyway. Hell of a 400.”

She froze. “You weren’t there.”

“I get around.”

He said it like a joke, but his eyes were too sharp. Amber, maybe. Or gray. They didn’t settle.

“You need new shoes,” he went on. “Those things on your feet are an OSHA violation.”

She almost laughed. “Don’t have money for anything in here.”

“See, that’s the tragedy,” he said. “They make you think you gotta pay for every inch of track. Sometimes, something’s just owed.”

He bent, disappeared behind the counter, came up with a box. Unmarked brown cardboard. He slid it toward her like a magician offering a card.

Mara hovered. “I’m not—”

“Open it,” he said.

She did, because of course she did.

They were beautiful. White and gold, not shiny, just… clean. Light. A stitched wing unfurled along each side, subtle. The laces were thin and pale as breath. She picked one up and it weighed almost nothing, but her fingers tingled like she’d grabbed static.

“I can’t afford these,” she said.

“You can,” he said. “Paid in full, far as I’m concerned.”

“For what?” she asked.

He smiled, crooked. “For all the times you were a step short and didn’t quit.” He shrugged. “Call it professional admiration.”

“Nah,” she said automatically. “Nothing’s free.”

“Smart girl.” He drummed his fingers on the counter. “Consider it a loan. Try them. See if they fit. If they don’t, toss them. No contracts, no small print.”

His gaze slid away as he said it.

She narrowed her eyes. “What’s the catch?”

“If I tell you, you won’t believe me.” His grin widened. “If I don’t, you’ll find out. Either way, I get a story. Everybody wins.”

“That’s not how winning works,” she muttered.

He cocked his head, like he heard more than the words. “You’ve got a meet Saturday, yeah?”

She stiffened. “You stalking me?”

“I’m in the business of routes,” he said. “You’re interesting. Take the shoes.”

She should’ve left them. She should’ve told him off. Instead, she imagined fifty-five point one. Fifty-four point nine. Rosa’s face when a real offer came. Nico’s inhaler not being a math problem. Her chest hurt.

She closed the box. “How much?” she asked, one last test.

“On the house, Ortiz.”

Her name in his mouth. She flinched.

“I didn’t tell you—”

He waved a hand. “Bring me a win,” he said lightly. “That’s all.”

The bell chimed again as she left. The street outside was suddenly louder, buses, horns, kids yelling. She turned back once. The sign buzzed, half-out. The shop’s windows reflected only sky.

By the time she reached the alley near their building, the box felt heavier, like it held a choice instead of shoes.

She told herself she’d just try them on.

She sat on the cool concrete, pulled off her beat-up trainers, slid a new shoe onto her right foot.

It hugged her like a hand that knew every bone.

The city dimmed, like somebody had dropped a blanket over most of the sound. The remaining noises were sharp: pigeon wingbeats, distant drumming heels, her own heartbeat.

She laced the left. Stood.

“One step,” she whispered. Just to see. Just to feel something go right.

She stepped.

The world lurched. The alley blurred; walls streaked past; trash bags became a single smear. She stopped twenty yards away, breathless, not from exertion but from the wrongness of it.

Her pulse thundered. Her legs felt… fine.

Mara looked down at the white and gold and felt the ground hum faintly beneath them.

“Shit,” she said, softly.

For the first time all day, the word tasted like possibility.
step_outputs.7:
Mara woke up before her alarm, heart already beating like a starter pistol.

She lay there a second, staring at the hairline crack in the ceiling above her bed. The box with the shoes sat under the rickety IKEA dresser, pushing against the dust bunnies. As if it might walk itself out.

You dreamed that, she told herself. The alley. The—whatever that was. You’re tired.

In the bunk above her, Nico snored softly, one arm dangling. Rosa had left an hour ago for the diner; the apartment had that hollowed-out quiet of early shift mornings.

Mara slid out of bed and onto the cold floor. Before she could think too hard, she pulled the box out.

The shoes were exactly as she remembered. Clean. Trim. Those stitched wings like they owned the idea.

She thought of Lila’s grin. Fifty-five point three. On the edge. A half-step short. Again.

Ten minutes, she told herself. Just to see if it was a fluke.

She dressed in her usual shorts and worn tank, grabbed the shoes and slipped out, careful not to bang the door. The hallway smelled like bleach and someone’s burnt toast. On the street, the sky was just starting to gray up from the East River.

At the corner, she sat on the stoop of the closed deli. Untied her regular sneakers. Her fingers moved fast, like if she was quick enough it wouldn’t count as a choice.

The new shoes went on smooth. The laces slid under her fingers like they were helping.

The world pulled in.

It wasn’t dramatic, not this time. Just… sharper. The distant hiss of the BQE, a dog collar jingle three blocks away. Every crack in the sidewalk suddenly a thing she knew.

She stood. Flexed. No roll in the sole. No give.

“Okay,” she muttered. “Fine. Magic demon shoes. Sure.”

She jogged, babying it at first. Past the bodega where Mr. Singh dragged up the gate, nodding at her. Past the laundromat, lights off, Lila’s parents maybe still asleep upstairs. Her feet landed and the contact felt… exact. Like the shoes negotiated with gravity on her behalf.

At the next block, the walk sign started to flash red. A car revved to gun the yellow. Mara should’ve slowed.

She didn’t.

Her stride didn’t break as she hit the crosswalk. The air thickened, then parted. The car’s horn stretched long and low like someone holding a note, but the moment bent around her. She reached the other side with time to spare.

“That’s not real,” she whispered.

She cut toward the park. There was a locked gate she usually looped around. Today, when she reached it, there was a sliver of space between bars she’d never noticed.

The shoes tugged. She aimed for the gap and somehow—somehow—she was through it, body angled without conscious thought.

Her breath came easy. She let go.

The next hundred meters dissolved under her. Trees flicked past. The dirt path brightened. Her steps made hardly any sound. She passed an old man walking a dog; his shape blurred for a fraction and in that smear she saw horns, then just a knit cap again.

She stumbled, almost wiping out.

“Hey,” the man called after her. “Slow down, hija.”

She didn’t.

At the far edge of the park, she popped out onto a street she didn’t know, even though she knew this neighborhood. A mural of a woman with an owl on her shoulder watched her; as Mara’s shadow crossed her painted feet, the owl’s eyes seemed to flick, just for her.

“You’re freaking yourself out,” Mara told herself. She checked her watch. She’d run her usual loop in half the time and wasn’t winded.

She stopped near the promenade by the river. The water was flat pewter. Out there, the city looked normal. Bridges. Buildings. Smoke.

On this side, her skin buzzed.

If this is real, the thought came, fast and uninvited, then what do you owe for it?

Her foot tapped restlessly. She thought of the man in the shop. The way he’d said paid in full. The way he’d known her time. There were words for that, from church when she was younger. There were words her mother would spit and make the sign of the cross for.

Mara shook her head. She turned back home.

At school, it all pretended to be ordinary. Hallway jam. Locker slam. The sour smell of old books in homeroom. Only now, each sound came with an extra layer: the click of cheap sneakers, the soft squeak of Lila’s training shoes on the linoleum, the sloppy drag of a kid who didn’t lift his feet.

“Hey, speed demon,” Lila said, sliding into her seat. “You ghosted Coach yesterday.”

“Sorry.” Mara kept her eyes on her notebook. Her hand had doodled a little wing in the corner.

“You see the times?” Lila pushed. “He said scouts wrote your name down.”

“They wrote hers down,” Mara said. “Jackson’s.”

“Mara—”

“Drop it, Lila.”

The words were sharper than she meant. Lila’s jaw clicked shut. She fiddled with her pen, tapping a rhythm that sounded like impatient spikes on track.

“Whatever,” Lila said lightly. “We’ve got another meet Saturday.”

“Yeah,” Mara said. “We do.”

At practice, the track had puddles from an earlier drizzle. Coach lined them up for 200 repeats.

“Controlled,” he barked. “Don’t blow your legs.”

Mara looked at the shoes in her bag. She should leave them. Prove she could do it on her own.

Then she thought about Jackson’s neon spikes. About Zeno’s windswept scouts. About Rosa counting tips.

She pulled the white and gold out. Lila whistled low.

“New kicks?” Lila asked. “Damn, Ortiz, who’d you rob?”

“Got ‘em cheap,” Mara said. It wasn’t even a good lie.

She laced up. The world nudged.

When Coach yelled, she went.

Even at “controlled,” the others fell behind like they were pulling sleds. The track stretched and compressed under her, lanes curving in her favor. The puddles parted. On the third rep, she heard Coach shout something about pacing, but his voice came from far away.

She eased up, forcing herself to slow. Her lungs still felt like they had extra rooms.

After, Lila eyed her while pretending not to.

In the locker room, someone joked, “Mara made a deal with the devil.” They all laughed. Mara forced a smirk, fingers tight on her laces. It wasn’t a devil she’d dealt with. She didn’t know what he was.

On her way out, duffel thumping her hip, she nearly collided with a man in a sleek black jacket by the gate. Early thirties maybe, good jawline, Apex logo on his chest. His shoes caught her eye: black trainers with a thin gold line tracing the sole.

“Sorry,” she said, stepping back.

“No worries.” His gaze flicked to her bag. “You’re Ortiz, right?”

Her spine prickled. “Yeah.”

“Kade,” he said, offering a hand. His grip was warm, too confident. “I work with Apex. We sponsor the meet this weekend.”

“I know who you are,” she said. She’d seen his type in athlete videos. Smiling, promising.

“I saw your splits,” he said. “Impressive. Mind if I come watch Saturday?”

“It’s a free country,” she said. It came out more defensive than cool.

He smiled like she’d passed a test. “Careful where you run in those,” he added, nodding at her duffel. “People notice.”

Before she could ask what that meant, he was already striding up toward Coach.

Mara walked home with her heart in her throat. She tried to tell herself Kade had meant regular people. Coaches. Scouts.

At their building, she hesitated. The urge to see the shop again tugged at her like a hooked fish.

She detoured.

Same corner. Same alley. Today: no neon sign. Just metal shutters tagged with someone’s throw-up, the ghost of an older, painted-over ad.

She touched the gate. Rust flaked onto her fingers.

“You lost?” a voice rasped.

An older man on the stoop next door watched her, cigarette an ember in the dim.

“That store,” she said. “Lace & Legend?”

He snorted smoke. “That dump? Been closed, what, ten years? More.” He squinted. “You buy shoes from a ghost, niña?”

She pulled her hand back. “No,” she said quickly. “Just… thought it was open.”

He shrugged and went back to his smoke. The streetlight buzzed.

Mara walked away, pulse beating in her throat.

In their room, Nico was on the floor building a track out of pencil cases. “Run,” he ordered as she stepped over it.

“Later,” she said.

She tossed the shoes under her bed this time, not the dresser. Closer. She lay on her back and stared at the cracked ceiling again.

If they’re cursed, she thought, then I’m already using them. If they’re a blessing, someone’s gonna come along with a receipt.

Her phone buzzed. A text from Lila: u good?

Mara typed: yeah just tired. big saturday.

She added: we’ll smoke everyone, promise.

Then deleted that part. Sent only: yeah.

She rolled onto her side, watching the dark under her bed where the white and gold lay.

At least one thing was clear: whatever those shoes were, wherever they came from, they worked.

Saturday was coming.

She wasn’t sure if that thought scared her more than it thrilled her.
step_outputs.8:
The day of the meet broke too bright.

Mara woke to sunlight knifing through the cheap blinds, striping the bunk. Nico snored on, drooling into his Spider-Man pillowcase. Rosa was already gone; her note was on the counter next to a bruised banana and two slices of toast wrapped in foil.

“Buena suerte. Text me. Love, Mami.”

Mara chewed the toast without tasting it. The shoes sat in their box by the door, smug.

She’d told Coach she wanted to scratch this one, save her legs for a bigger meet. He’d laughed in her face. “You need race reps, Ortiz. You PR’d last week. You’re peaking.”

He didn’t know how much.

The bus ride to the track scraped her nerves raw. Lila sat beside her, tapping a rhythm on her thigh. Eight beats, pause, eight beats.

“You sure you’re okay?” Lila asked.

“I’m fine,” Mara said. “Just thinking.”

“Don’t overthink. Just run,” Lila said, like it was that easy. Her braid brushed Mara’s shoulder when the bus lurched.

The track complex loomed ahead: smaller than last time, but with real bleachers, proper timing system. Apex banners flapped on the fences—“CHASE LIMITLESS”—all black and gold.

Kade was there already, talking to some coach near the finish. He spotted Mara as she stepped off the bus and lifted two fingers in a lazy salute, like they shared a secret.

She looked away. Her hand clenched around the strap of her duffel.

In the locker room, girls milled and stretched, the air ripe with deodorant and nerves. Lila changed beside her, lacing her own modest spikes.

“You bust out those bougie shoes?” Lila asked, half-teasing.

“Maybe,” Mara said.

Lila glanced at her. “Okay, cryptic.”

Mara didn’t answer. If she said it out loud—magic shoes—it would become a joke, and she wasn’t ready to hear anyone else laugh.

When it was time, she sat on the bench and pulled the white and gold out.

They looked almost innocent in this fluorescent light. Plastic benches, chipped paint, and these… things. Like they’d been drawn on top of reality.

She slid them on. The world contracted, familiar now: sounds stacking neatly, track lines etching themselves sharper in her mind. Lila’s face ghosted at the edge of her vision, a crease between her brows.

“You sure about this?” Lila asked quietly.

“Yeah,” Mara said. “I need a win.”

Lila looked like she wanted to say something else. Didn’t. Just bumped Mara’s shoulder with hers. “Then go take it.”

Outside, the sun had climbed, bouncing off the track. Heat shimmered. The starter set up his pistol. The girls in her heat lined up: a couple in basic school-issued spikes, one in flashy Apex-branded shoes that glowed faintly in the glare.

Mara felt Kade’s gaze on her from the infield. Behind him stood Ny, all long lines and bright white sneakers, talking to someone with a camera crew.

Lane four. Again. She settled into the blocks.

Don’t look at them. Don’t look at anyone.

“On your marks.”

Her fingers kissed the track. The shoes hummed, not a sound but a pressure in her bones, like they knew their job.

“Set.”

The stadium held its breath.

The gun cracked.

Mara moved.

It wasn’t like last time, where effort and grind built into speed. This was… absence of friction. Her body did what it always did, but the world cooperated too much. The first step snapped, the second caught, and then she was in a tunnel of red rubber and air, everything else smeared.

Wind slid past her ears. The roar of the small crowd stretched thin, then whipped back. Every stride landed exactly where it should. Her arms pumped, her lungs burned just enough to remind her she was still inside her own chest.

She didn’t hear footsteps beside her.

At the 200 mark, she risked a flick of her eyes.

No one.

By the 300, she felt the pull—her usual fade. The shoes pushed back. Just a little. Just enough.

She hit the homestretch with no one in her peripherals. A kid in the stands stood up. Phones came up like flowers. Lila was a blur at the finish, screaming something.

She crossed.

Silence, for a fraction. Then the sound crashed down: shouts, whistles, someone yelling “What the hell?” The scoreboard flashed numbers, glitched, then settled.

“Fifty-three point eight,” the announcer said, voice almost cracking. “Unofficial fifty-three point eight for Ortiz—”

The rest of it drowned in the roar.

Mara’s legs didn’t quite believe they were done. She jogged through, turned, dizzy. Fifty-three eight. She’d skipped a whole tier of girls.

A strange sensation washed over her. Not just adrenaline. It was like every eye in the place was a hand pressed against her, warm, insistent. For a split second, she felt it funnel down through the laces into the soles, into the track.

The stadium lights flickered. Just once. A pop of static.

Then everything looked normal again.

“Ortiz!” Coach barreled toward her, face red, stopwatch in hand. “What did I tell you, huh?” He was half-laughing, half-demanding an explanation.

She swallowed. “I… felt good,” she managed.

“That’s more than good,” he said. “Scouts are gonna eat that up.”

Lila reached her next. She grabbed Mara in a hug that was both fierce and careful, like she was holding something hot.

“Holy shit,” Lila said into her ear. “You torched them. You freaking torched them.”

Mara wanted to sink into it. Instead, she stepped back, suddenly aware of Ny and Kade approaching, of kids still filming, of the way her shoes looked in direct sun.

Ny got there first. Up close, her presence was like being near a very tall flame: bright, precise.

“That was beautiful,” Ny said, extending a hand. “Nyla Kane. Apex.”

Mara took it. Ny’s grip was cool. Her eyes dipped to the shoes, lingered. Something like recognition flickered there, followed by something like disapproval.

“You’ve been working hard,” Ny said. “We like that.”

Kade drifted at her shoulder, grin easy. “Told you she was special,” he said to Ny, like Mara wasn’t there.

“We’d love to talk,” Ny said. “About opportunities. Sponsorship. Guidance.”

“Uh,” Mara said.

“Come by the tent after you cool down,” Kade added. His voice had that careful casualness. “No pressure.”

Ny smiled once more, all white teeth and promise. As they stepped away, Ny murmured something to Kade; he nodded, eyes flicking back to the shoes.

Lila nudged her. “Dude. That’s Apex.”

“I know,” Mara said.

“This could be it,” Lila whispered. “Like, actual it.”

Actual it. Money. Travel. Not doing algebra over a mop bucket.

Mara’s tongue felt thick. The shoes felt heavier.

She did a perfunctory cooldown jog. Every few steps, someone called to her, slapped her shoulder, threw out a “Damn, Ortiz!” She tried to catalog how her body felt. Was this still her? Or had she outsourced the final stretch?

Near the far turn, a figure joined her stride for stride.

“Nice run,” he said.

She didn’t need to look to know. The shopkeeper. Hermes, though she didn’t know the name yet.

He was in the same hoodie, same spotless white sneakers, like he’d stepped out of the memory.

She stumbled. “How are you—”

“Running,” he said. “It’s kind of my thing.”

“You did this,” she said. It came out accusation.

“I gave you shoes,” he said. “You did the running.”

The track blurred briefly at the edges of her vision. On the infield, she could see Ny and Kade under the Apex tent, conferring with a tall man in a suit who radiated authority even from here.

“What are you?” she asked.

He tilted his head. “Old,” he said. “Fast. Nosy.”

“Why me?”

He smiled, sadder this time. “You keep coming in second and still show up. You run like you’re mad at the ground. It’s interesting.”

“That’s not an answer.”

“It’s my answer,” he said. “Listen, kid. Those people in the tent? They want what’s on your feet. And what it does when people scream your name.”

“You want something too,” she said.

“Of course,” he said lightly. “Difference is, I’m telling you.”

They jogged past a cluster of kids playing tag under the bleachers. For a second, one of the kids’ sneakers flashed gold when he pivoted.

Mara shook her head. “What happens if I sign with them?”

“I don’t know,” he lied.

She glared.

He sighed. “Okay. They wrap you in contract and tech. They make sure every miracle you make has a logo on it. They take a messy, sharp thing”—he flicked her ponytail—“and smooth it into ad copy. You’ll eat better. Your mom will sleep. You’ll hate yourself sometimes.”

“You’re so sure.”

“I’ve watched a lot of deals,” he said. “Mortals sign away wild for safe all the time.”

“And you want me to what? Run rogue? For you?”

“I want you to run,” he said. “Where and for whom is the fun part.”

They neared the Apex tent. Ny looked up, eyes locking on Mara, a polished predator spotting movement. Kade lifted a hand again.

“Be careful,” the man beside her said softly. “They’ll say you’ve earned it. Ask what they earn from you.”

When she glanced back to snap, to ask his name, he was gone. No footsteps behind her. Just the track and the heat and her own pulse.

Mara slowed. Her heart slammed against her ribs, less from exertion than from too many choices piled up.

She wiped sweat from her forehead, straightened her shoulders, and walked toward the tent.

Ny greeted her with that camera-ready smile. “Mara. Sit. Let’s talk about your future.”

As Mara stepped into the shade, the white and gold on her feet seemed to glow against the black-and-gold of Apex.

For the first time, she had the distinct, unsettling sense that everyone there knew exactly what she’d just put on the table.
step_outputs.9:
The Apex lounge felt like it wanted to impress God.

White couches. Screens on every wall playing slow-motion clips of finish lines, dunks, impossible catches. Sneaker displays lit like relic cases. Air cool and citrus-scented, a relief after the raw sun outside.

Mara perched on the edge of a couch, conscious of her damp uniform and the grass clinging to her calves. Her shoes, perfectly clean despite the race, looked loud against the pale floor.

Ny sat across from her, perfectly composed, crossing one long leg over the other. Kade leaned against a glass display nearby, playing casual lookout. A man in a slate suit occupied the corner of the room, scrolling through something on a tablet. His presence pressed on the air; even the climate control seemed afraid to hum too loudly near him.

“This is Mr. Zeno,” Ny said, with the kind of lightness reserved for names everyone should know. “Our CEO. He loves a good race.”

Zeno looked up. His eyes were a dark, stormcloud grey. “Impressive work out there, Ms. Ortiz,” he said. His voice had that smooth corporate softness, backed by unspoken thunder. “Fifty-three eight. That took discipline.”

“Thanks,” Mara said. Her tongue felt too big for her mouth.

“You’ve been training on… what?” Ny asked. “Public school facilities? Street runs?”

“Yeah,” Mara said. “Um. Queens.”

“I grew up on cracked courts,” Ny said. “I remember what it feels like to race in borrowed shoes.” Her gaze flicked down. “Those are not borrowed, I assume.”

“Gift,” Mara said too fast.

“From who?” Zeno asked, mild.

Mara hesitated. The man from the shop. The not-shop. Hermes—she still didn’t have his name, just the feel of him.

“Just a guy,” she said.

Kade’s eyebrows twitched. Ny and Zeno exchanged the tiniest glance, almost nothing. Almost.

“Well,” Ny said. “They carried you beautifully. But we want to talk about the part that isn’t the shoes. The way you run. The way people reacted.”

She picked up a remote. One of the screens flipped to a replay of Mara’s race. Time slowed. Mara watched herself burst off the line, carve the curve, finish alone.

From this angle, it looked unreal. An effect.

“You feel that?” Ny asked.

“What?”

“The crowd,” Zeno said. He hit a button; the sound from the video swelled. Shouts, gasps. Mara’s name screamed by strangers. The noise seemed to vibrate in her sternum.

“It’s attention,” Zeno said. “Energy. We’ve learned to measure it.”

Another display lit up: a world map, tiny gold dots pulsing in clusters. “Engagement nodes,” the legend read.

“When someone sees something that moves them,” Ny said, “they give a little of themselves. Focus. Awe. Aspiration. Old religions called it worship. We call it data. Different language, same fuel.”

Mara stared. “Okay,” she said slowly. “And?”

“And right now,” Zeno said, “that fuel sprays everywhere. Leaks. Powers things—or people—who don’t have humanity’s best interests at heart.”

“Like whoever gave you those shoes,” Ny said, voice still velvet.

Mara swallowed. “He—he seemed…”

“Charming,” Ny supplied. “Fast. Vague. Left halfway through a sentence?”

Mara’s heart stuttered. “You know him.”

“We know the type,” Zeno said. He set the tablet down. “We’re trying to build something… safer. More deliberate. You’re exactly the kind of athlete we want to protect.”

“Protect,” Mara repeated.

“You’re good enough to do this without back-alley shortcuts,” Ny said. “We can help you train, get proper coaching, gear that doesn’t short out stadiums. Full scholarship. Money for your family. Health coverage. No shady relic strings attached.”

The words hit every pressure point she had. Rent. Rosa’s bad knee. Nico’s inhaler. The fear that when something finally came through, it would be too late.

“What’s the catch?” she asked.

Kade winced, like she’d jabbed him. Zeno smiled slightly.

“No catch,” he said. “A partnership. We ask that you wear our product, appear in some campaigns, align with our values. No more… unsanctioned anomalies on the track.”

Ny leaned forward. “Let us take those off your plate,” she said, and for a second her concern sounded almost real. “Whatever that man put on your feet, it’s raw. It can hurt people.”

Mara flashed on the flicker of the lights when she finished. The way sound had gone weird. Lila’s arms around her, too careful.

“You make… what?” Mara asked. “Your own versions?”

Ny gestured to the displays. “We’ve developed tech with the blessing of certain… stakeholders. Shoes that give a controlled lift. Tiny boosts. Nothing that will tear a hole in the sky. We don’t do miracles. We do enhancements.”

“Legal miracles,” Kade said, trying for a joke.

Mara didn’t laugh.

“And what happens to these?” She nudged a toe of her white-and-gold.

Ny’s gaze sharpened. “We would secure them,” she said. “Relics like that don’t belong in the wild. They attract the wrong kind of attention. From above and below.”

Mara caught a faint scent, like ozone, as Zeno shifted. “We’ve seen what happens when power like that is allowed to run loose,” he said. “Stadium collapses. Panics. People get hurt. It makes all of us look bad.”

All of us. Not all of you, she noticed.

“Look,” Ny said, and her tone softened again. “We’re not saying you cheated. We’re saying we can make sure nobody ever gets to say that. You deserve clean wins. We can give you that. And security. No more guessing if your life depends on some stranger’s idea of a good story.”

It was such a reasonable pitch. So much more reasonable than a ghost shop and a man who spoke in riddles.

Mara imagined bringing Rosa an Apex contract. Watching her shoulders drop half an inch. Imagined Nico bragging about her billboard. The knot in her chest loosened, wanting.

“You don’t have to decide today,” Kade said. “We’ll send something over. Talk to your mom.”

“Read the small letters,” Zeno said, almost pleasantly. “We’re very clear. We have to be, these days.”

Ny stood. That shimmer of something around her—wings in the reflection of the screen, only for a second—made Mara’s skin prickle. “In the meantime,” Ny added, “I’d strongly recommend you don’t wear those again in competition. Every clip from today has anomalies. People will talk.”

Anomalies. Mara pictured the feeling of eyes pouring through her, into the track. She pictured Zeno’s map, all those lights.

“They’ll talk either way,” she said.

Ny smiled, approving. “True. Let’s make sure they’re saying the right things.”

They walked her out. In the doorway, she glanced back: the victory footage looping, the map pulsing, the athletes on the walls frozen mid-flight, all wearing the same stylized logo.

Outside the tent, the sunlight felt wrong for a moment. Too bright. She blinked.

“Mara!” Coach waved her over, blustering. “You sign something? They offer something?”

“Just talking,” she said.

“We’ll talk later,” he said. Translation: Don’t screw this up.

Lila lingered near the fence. “So?” she asked when Mara approached.

“They’re… interested,” Mara said.

“That’s good, right?”

“I guess.”

Lila tilted her head. “You don’t look like you just got scouted by Olympus or whatever.”

“Just tired,” Mara said.

She didn’t tell Lila about relics, about maps of worship, about being “secured.”

On the walk to the bus, she felt a presence slip in beside her. The same wiry energy.

“Nice office,” Hermes said. He had a pretzel now, tearing off bite-sized pieces.

“Don’t,” she said.

“‘We don’t do miracles, we do enhancements,’” he mimicked in a prim voice. “That’s adorable.”

“They’re offering to help,” Mara hissed. “They’re not—” she almost said like you. Stopped.

“They’re offering to put a fence around you,” he said. “Soft fence. Gold-plated.”

“They’d help my family.”

He chewed, considering. “They probably would,” he said. “That’s the trick. They’re not Mustache-Twirlers United, Inc. They give you real things.”

“And you?” she shot back. “What do you give, besides cryptic warnings?”

He smiled, pretzel salt at the corner of his mouth. “Speed,” he said. “Routes nobody else gets. The chance to not be owned. It’s messier. Less dental coverage.”

“That’s not funny.”

“Didn’t say it was.”

She stopped walking. Kids and parents flowed around them toward the parking lot. No one seemed to notice she was talking to a grown man in a hoodie who had definitely not checked in at the front gate.

“Are you dangerous?” she asked.

“To you?” He considered. “Less than they are. More than nothing. Depends what you do.”

“Who are you?”

He glanced up at the Apex banners snapping in the breeze. “Once,” he said, “people left me coins at crossroads so their journeys would go well. Now they tap a map app and hope their data plan holds.”

“That’s not an answer either,” she said.

He grinned. “I’m Hermes,” he said finally, quietly. The name made the hairs on her arms stand up. “I like runners.”

“That’s a myth,” she said. “You’re a myth.”

“Everything’s a myth once you stop believing in it,” he said. “Doesn’t mean it’s gone.”

He popped the last piece of pretzel into his mouth. “Sign with them if you want,” he went on. “You’ll get things. But remember: every logo is a little altar. Every ad, a prayer. They’re very good at making people worship without using the word.”

“And you’re not?” she said.

“Please. I invented half their tricks.” He shrugged. “Difference is, I don’t pretend it’s a charity.”

He started to step away, then looked back. For a moment his face, still human, carried an older tiredness.

“They’ll say you’ve earned it,” he repeated. “Ask what they earn from you.”

Then he was gone, absorbed into the milling crowd like he’d never been there at all.

On the bus, Rosa texted a string of excited messages in Spanish, half about the time Coach had posted, half about “the company” that wanted to meet. Mara typed back: we’ll talk at home.

She stuck her earbuds in, even though she didn’t play anything. Lila sat across the aisle, watching her with a look Mara couldn’t read.

Through the window, an Apex billboard slid by: a girl not much older than them, mid-stride in glossy shoes, face lifted like she was catching light from heaven. Slogan: WEAR YOUR WINGS.

Mara’s gaze dropped to her own feet.

Different wings. Older. Wilder.

The bus rattled toward Queens. Every bump reminded her there was no version of this where she got to keep everything.
step_outputs.10:
The week before the invitational stretched thin and tight.

Rumors skittered through school: Mara’s time, Apex’s visit, scouts at the next meet. Coach handled her like something fragile and explosive—easing her workouts, snapping at anyone who joked about performance-enhancing anything.

At home Rosa oscillated between pride and fear. “Be careful what you sign,” she kept saying while pushing Apex’s brochure closer to Mara’s cereal bowl. “But don’t be stupid, mijita. You can help us.”

Rosa’s fingers traced the numbers on the mock scholarship package like they were beads on a rosary. Nico just asked if her face would be on a poster. “I’ll draw it if they don’t,” he promised.

Mara slept badly. Every time she closed her eyes, the track stretched longer, the crowd louder, the shoes heavier. Hermes’ warning and Ny’s polished concern braided in her head.

By Saturday, her nerves felt like stripped wires.

The invitational was bigger—stadium seating, TV cameras, corporate tents. The Apex logo was everywhere: on banners, on officials’ polos, on the starting blocks. Cloudless blue sky, hot enough that the track wavered in the distance.

“Big day,” Lila said as they stepped off the bus.

“Yeah,” Mara said.

“You been ghosty all week.”

“Just tired.”

Lila watched her for a moment. “You know if you sign with them, I’m still going to smoke you one day, right?”

The joke landed crooked.

“Try it,” Mara said, aiming for playful. It sounded like a dare.

Warm-ups bled into check-ins. The 400 was stacked: fast girls from all over the city, some already in Apex gear. The announcer hyped the field. “Keep an eye on lane four,” he said. “Mara Ortiz, coming off a huge PR…”

Coach pulled Mara aside. “You ready?”

“Yeah.”

“Remember, there’s eyes on you. Make it clean. No stupid theatrics. Just run your race.”

As if she was the one adding theatrics.

In the infield, an Apex rep opened a sleek case. Inside: black-and-gold shoes, humming faintly under the sun.

Kade intercepted Mara near the call tent. “Last chance demonstration,” he said in a low voice. “Run in ours. Let people see you’re with us. We’ve got your back if anyone talks.”

Ny stood a few yards away with Zeno, watching. She looked like a statue dropped into the wrong century.

Mara glanced at the prototype shoes. They looked heavier, thicker, like they’d sink into the track instead of kissing it.

“What happens if I don’t?” she asked.

Kade hesitated. “Then you’re out there in… those,” he said, eyes flicking to her bag. “And if something weird happens, it’s on you.”

Weird. Anomaly. Relic. Hermes.

“You offered to help,” she said.

“We are,” he said. “But we can’t attach our name to… uncontrolled variables. You get that, right?”

He looked genuinely pained. For a moment she saw the almost-runner in him, the kid who’d taken a deal.

Mara’s chest burned. “I have to think,” she said.

“You have five minutes,” he said, not unkindly.

She stepped away, heart slamming.

By the bleachers, Rosa stood with Nico, clutching her purse. When she saw Mara, a raw hope lit her face.

“They said on the TV,” Rosa said breathlessly, “if you do good again, they want to sign you for real. Is good, right?”

“Mami—”

“Don’t overthink, Mara,” Rosa said. “Good things don’t come twice. You take care of us, we take care of you. That’s how it works.”

“It’s not that simple.”

Rosa’s jaw tightened. “Nothing is simple. But you have something. Use it. Please.”

Nico tugged on Mara’s arm. “Run fast,” he said. “Make the slow girls cry.”

She snorted despite herself. “That’s rude.”

“Sorry. Make them… reconsider their choices.”

“Better,” she said.

From across the track, Hermes appeared like he’d just walked out of a concession line. Hoodie, shorts, sunglasses, as if the heat wasn’t touching him.

He didn’t come closer, but when she glanced his way he mimed tying laces, lifted his eyebrows.

Everyone wanted something.

Call time.

In the locker room tunnel, Lila adjusted her blocks. “You got this,” she said. “Just don’t forget me when you’re famous.”

“I won’t be famous,” Mara said.

“Sure,” Lila said. “I’ll remind you you said that.”

Any other day, that would’ve settled her. Today it just added weight.

Mara sat. Opened her duffel.

Two choices. Apex black-and-gold glinting from the loaner box Kade had pressed on her “just in case.” Hermes’ white-and-gold, quiet. Both smelled like new rubber and something more.

Safe is theirs, Hermes had said. My shoes, my rules. Ny had said relics burn people up.

Family, freedom, control, chaos. All of it boiling down to which pair she put on her feet.

“Two minutes!” an official shouted down the tunnel.

Mara exhaled. The air felt thin.

She grabbed Hermes’ sneakers.

When she laced them, the world snapped. The tunnel turned into a long throat of sound. Somewhere, she heard Kade swear under his breath.

At the start line, Ny’s eyes narrowed. Zeno’s glance sharpened. Hermes leaned on a railing, chewing gum, unreadable.

“On your marks.”

Lane four again. The track gleaming. The girls dropped into their blocks. To her right, one of the favorites flexed in Apex prototypes, soles faintly lit like coals.

“Set.”

The hush shivered. The shoes purred under her.

The gun burst.

Everything went sideways.

Mara launched. Speed came like a punch, like a door blown off its hinges. The track wasn’t something she ran on; it was something that rolled under her feet, eager. First curve: gone. Backstretch: she was a blade.

She heard the others—a scatter of footfalls, a grunt—but they were behind, already.

At 200, the Apex girl to her right surged. Her shoes flared, a synchronized glow along the line. Ny’s hand twitched at the rail. Zeno’s eyes flashed.

The air thickened. Mara felt a drag, like running through honey. Her laces buzzed hot against her skin.

“For you, then,” Hermes’ voice murmured—whether from the stands or inside her skull, she didn’t know.

The drag broke. The shoes bit. She slingshotted off the curve into the home straight, and for one impossible instant she wasn’t on a track at all.

She was in a split world.

One lane was red rubber, painted numbers, screaming faces. The other was a glowing causeway hung over nothing, lined with shadowy figures: men with antlers, women with wings, beings whose eyes were stadium lights. They leaned in as she passed, feeding on the roar.

Time schismed. The camera on the infield stuttered, frames dropping. To the crowd she was a smear.

The Apex-enhanced runners hit that rift a beat behind. Their shoes snagged on what hers cut through. Sparks flew. A body stumbled.

In the stands, phones overheated, glitching. Screens on the concourse showed her in duplicate, wings of static trailing. The announcer’s voice wobbled.

At the edge of her vision, Ny lifted her hand like a conductor. The Apex shoes on their athletes flared brighter, a desperate matching. Zeno’s mouth moved; he spoke into a mic clipped to his lapel, some command that wasn’t in human language.

Old power and new tech crashed together in the air.

The crack came half a second later.

Light exploded overhead as stadium bulbs blew out in a staccato chain. A boom like thunder rolled from one end of the track to the other. The ground shuddered. The glowing causeway snapped back into painted lanes.

Mara’s last strides were blind. She crossed the finish on instinct.

Behind her, someone screamed.

She stumbled to a stop and turned.

One of the Apex girls was on her knees in lane six, clutching her ankle, her shoes still smoking faintly. The timing display had reset itself to gibberish. A camera toppled.

Near the fence, a section of railing had buckled when the crowd jerked backward. Bodies pressed, panicked. In the crush, a figure went down hard.

Lila.

Her head hit concrete with a sound Mara felt more than heard.

“Stop! Hey!” Someone shouted. Whistles blew. Officials swarmed.

Mara’s heart telescoped. She ran, legs suddenly heavy, toward the stands. Hermes appeared for a flicker at the corner of her vision, then vanished. Ny and Zeno moved in different directions—Ny toward the fallen Apex athlete, Zeno toward the VIP box, already calling for calm on a mic.

Lila lay on the ground, eyes unfocused, one arm at a wrong angle. A smear of blood on her temple. An EMT was pushing through.

Mara dropped to her knees beside her. “Lila,” she said, voice shredded. “Lila, hey, look at me.”

Lila’s gaze found her and tried to focus. “You… did it,” she slurred. “You… won.”

The words were praise, accusation, awe—all at once.

Mara’s stomach hollowed out.

Around them, the noise was rising again: fear, speculation, the crackle of someone’s live stream. People were saying lightning, saying equipment malfunction, saying sabotage.

Zeno’s voice boomed from the PA, smooth as ever. “Everyone remain calm. There’s been a technical issue. Our team is responding. Please give the medics space.”

Beside Mara, an official grabbed at her arm. “Miss, you need to move.”

She yanked away, then realized her hands were shaking. She looked down.

The white-and-gold on her feet were unscathed. Clean. The track around them was charred in a faint pattern like feathered wings.

Kade’s face appeared over her shoulder, grim. “Mara,” he said quietly. “You need to come with us. Now.”

She stared at him. “I didn’t—”

“I know,” he said. “Doesn’t matter. Cameras saw… something. If you talk to anyone without us—”

“Is she okay?” Mara demanded, nodding at Lila.

“We’ll take care of her,” he said, and she could tell he meant the hospital bills, the spin.

Ny’s eyes met Mara’s from across the chaos. For the first time, her polished calm had a crack in it. Zeno’s gaze followed, calculating.

Mara’s victory was already dissolving under sirens and rumors. The sound of the crowd wasn’t admiration now. It was fear.

She had wanted to be undeniable.

She had wanted to run something that no one could take away.

Instead, she’d given everyone more ammunition.

And somewhere, above the sirens, she thought she heard Hermes laugh once—a short, rueful sound, like even he hadn’t expected the collision to be quite like that.
step_outputs.11:
The hospital smelled like lemon cleanser and stale coffee. The TV in the waiting room argued with itself, volume low, colors too bright.

Mara sat in a plastic chair that dug into her spine, her race bag crumpled at her feet. The white-and-gold shoes were inside, wrapped in her warm-up jacket like contraband.

Rosa sat next to her, arms crossed tight. “They say we wait,” she muttered. “So we wait.”

Across the room, Lila’s parents spoke in hushed, fast Korean. Lila’s mom was crying without making any sound, tears just sliding down. Her dad’s jaw flexed as he listened to a nurse, nodding too quickly.

On the TV, a news crawl mentioned “spectacular technical failure at Apex Invitational; several minor injuries reported.” Stock footage of a glittering Apex store ran. No mention of names yet.

Mara’s fingers had gone numb around her phone. Her notifications were a waterfall: are you okay?? what happened?? bro that vid is crazy. Clips and edits and slowed-down frames of her last strides, the lights blowing, Lila falling. People circling burnt patches on the track with red circles and captions like REAL or FAKE?

She shut the screen off. The afterimage of herself running burned on the inside of her eyelids.

Rosa glanced at her. “You put that away,” she said. “No reading garbage.”

“They’re calling me a hazard,” Mara said. It came out flat.

Rosa’s mouth tightened. “People always talk. Is what they do.”

“They’re saying I broke the track.”

“You did not break nothing. The machine broke. You run.”

“She’s in there because I ran,” Mara said. The guilt tasted like pennies.

Rosa didn’t answer. Her eyes slid to Lila’s parents, then back to her daughter.

A doctor finally came out, spoke to Lila’s parents. Concussion. Broken arm. No spine damage. Observations overnight.

Relief hit Mara so hard she went a little dizzy. Lila’s mom sagged into her husband’s arms.

“Can I see her?” Mara asked, half rising.

The doctor hesitated. “Family first,” he said. “Then maybe one friend.”

Lila’s mom looked at Mara for the first time. Her gaze was complicated: grateful, angry, afraid. “Later,” she said, not unkind but not yes.

Mara sank back into her chair.

Hours blurred. Nurses moved. The TV looped. Rosa paced, sat, got up again. Kade had tried to follow them from the stadium, but Rosa had cut him off at the curb with a look that could salt the earth.

“We will talk later,” she’d snapped at him. “Without my daughter bleeding in the street.”

Now, in the hospital light, that felt like a whole day ago.

Eventually a nurse with tired eyes came over. “Lila’s asking for you,” she said.

Rosa squeezed Mara’s hand hard. “Be gentle.”

“I—okay.”

The room was dim, machine light soft. Lila lay propped up, left arm in a temporary cast, bandage on her temple. She looked small in the bed, hair fanned messily, hospital bracelet too big.

“Hey,” Mara said, hovering at the doorway.

“Hey,” Lila said. Her voice was raspy. “Celebrity entrance.”

“That’s not funny,” Mara said.

“A little,” Lila said, then winced. “Ow. Laughing is over.”

Mara edged closer. “They said you’re gonna be okay.”

“Define okay,” Lila murmured. Her gaze sharpened. “How are you?”

“I’m—I’m fine,” Mara said. “I’m so sorry, Lila. I swear I didn’t—”

“You didn’t shove me,” Lila said. “Some idiot pushed when the lights went.”

“If I hadn’t—if that stuff hadn’t—”

“Everything was always about you running too fast,” Lila said, closing her eyes briefly. “Guess it finally caught up.”

The words stung more than any slap.

Lila opened her eyes again. “I watched the replay,” she said. “You looked… wrong.”

“Thanks,” Mara said helplessly.

“Like you were in the wrong movie,” Lila said. “Like something was chasing you. Or you were pulling it.”

Mara’s throat closed. “I’m so sorry.”

“You said that,” Lila said. “Look, I’m concussed, not stupid. Whatever’s going on with your shoes, or that company, or that creepy guy in the hoodie? It’s bigger than me getting knocked down. I get that.”

“I don’t,” Mara said. “I thought I did. I thought I could just… run.”

“We’re not in a Nike ad,” Lila said. “Everything costs.”

“I’d trade this for your arm,” Mara blurted.

“No, you wouldn’t,” Lila said, surprisingly gentle. “You’d like to think you would. But you’ve wanted this since we were babies.”

Mara flinched. “That’s not fair.”

“Neither is all this,” Lila said, leaning her head back. “I’m tired.”

Silence stretched.

“So what, you hate me now?” Mara forced herself to ask.

“I’m… mad,” Lila said. “And I’m scared for you. And I’m jealous, and I’m proud, and my head hurts. It’s all in there. You don’t get a neat answer.”

Mara nodded, tears burning. “Okay.”

“Tell me one thing,” Lila said, eyes closing again. “Those shoes. They’re not… normal, right?”

Mara opened her mouth. Closed it.

Lila huffed a tiny laugh. “Yeah,” she said. “Didn’t think so.”

A nurse knocked lightly. “Time’s up.”

Mara backed toward the door.

“Don’t let them own you,” Lila said suddenly, eyes open, looking too old. “Not the gods, not the brand. Whoever they are. You’re already killing yourself for free.”

Mara swallowed. “I’ll try.”

“Try better,” Lila muttered, and closed her eyes, done.

In the hallway, Rosa was waiting, arms folded.

“She is okay?”

“As okay as…” Mara trailed off. “She’s pissed.”

“Good,” Rosa said. “Means she fights.”

They left the hospital into early evening. The sky over Queens glowed a dirty orange. As they walked to the subway, Rosa said, “The man from Apex called. He say they want to help. Pay hospital bills. Fix… PR.”

Mara’s stomach lurched. “What did you say?”

“I said we talk later. When you are calm.” Rosa glanced at her. “Mara, this thing is messy. They are strong. You alone are—” she stopped, frowned, searching. “You alone are my daughter. Not superhero.”

“I’m the one who broke their meet,” Mara said. “Whatever they’re calling it. They’ll want a face to blame.”

“They want to use you,” Rosa said. “Same as everyone uses everything.”

“Even you?” It came out sharper than she intended.

Rosa’s jaw clenched. “I want to feed my children,” she said. “If that is using, then yes. I would sell my pride.”

Mara felt like she’d been slapped. “Mami—”

Rosa lifted a hand. “You think too big. Gods, corporations. Pfft.” She flicked her fingers. “I see rent. Medicine. You bring paper to make those go away, I sign with my own blood.”

“That’s what they’re counting on,” Mara said.

Rosa didn’t answer.

That night, back in their room, Mara sat on the floor with the shoes in front of her. The lights were off; the hall glow seeped under the door.

“You look like you’re about to break up with someone,” Nico said sleepily from the top bunk.

“Go to sleep,” she said.

He peered over. “Are they cursed? Like in movies?”

“Maybe,” she said.

“Cool,” he said, and flopped back down.

Mara stared at the shoes. The laces gleamed faintly.

“You did this,” she whispered. To them. To Hermes. To herself.

She grabbed a lace and pulled.

It resisted.

She yanked harder. There was a snap—not of cotton, but of something sharper. A crack like distant thunder, muffled through walls.

The lace broke in her hand, clean. The sneaker sagged.

Silence.

The air that had been taut around her for days… slackened. Her ears rang with the absence of that hum she hadn’t known she was hearing.

She ripped the other lace. Another soft thunderclap, somewhere far above the ceiling.

Nothing dramatic happened. No lightning bolt, no voice. Just a sudden, heavy tiredness dropping into her limbs.

She shoved the broken shoes back in the box, stuffed them under the bed.

Her phone buzzed immediately.

From Kade: We need to talk. Call me back.

From an unknown number: You know that doesn’t solve anything.

Mara turned the phone face-down.

Sleep came in jagged pieces. She dreamed of running barefoot on broken glass, of stadium lights turning into eyes.

In the morning, she felt slow. When she trotted down the stairs, her knees ached like a normal person’s. On the street, no extra edge sharpened the cracks.

At school, the weight of regular shoes was insulting.

Kids stared in the hallways. Someone whispered “storm girl” as she passed.

Coach pulled her into his office.

“They’re calling it a freak malfunction,” he said, rubbing his temples. “But some board members are asking what you were wearing.”

“Regular spikes,” Mara lied.

His gaze flicked to her, to the way she wouldn’t meet his eyes. “I’m trying to protect you,” he said. “But you need to give me something.”

“I don’t know what happened,” she said, which was technically true in the way a knife technically isn’t responsible for where it’s swung.

He sighed. “You might be suspended while they ‘investigate.’”

“Cool,” she said. “Add it to the list.”

After school she rode the F train alone, letting the dirty windows blur the city. At West 4th, the car emptied out all at once, a wave of people spilling into the station, leaving her in a sudden, unnatural quiet.

Hermes sat on the bench across from her, elbows on his knees, hoodie up. No one looked at him.

“You snapped them,” he said.

She glared. “You knew that would… what? Cut the connection?”

“Mostly,” he said. “You severed my direct route. Brave. Dramatic. Ineffective.”

“I don’t want your power,” she said. “I hurt people.”

“You hurt one person,” he said. “They hurt more by turning it into a show.”

“That’s not better.”

He shrugged. “Nothing’s better. It’s just… different flavors of worse.”

“Why me?” she demanded. “Really. No riddles.”

He looked at her, really looked, the joking gone. The train rocked.

“Because you run like you’re trying to get somewhere that isn’t on any map,” he said. “Because you hate being owned more than you love being adored. I thought that might matter.”

“Well, it sucks,” she said. “Tell your colleagues I resign.”

“That’s not how this works,” he said. “You put on a story, it leaves a mark. Cutting laces doesn’t scrape it off.”

“Then what does?”

“Writing your own version,” he said. “Or letting someone else write it for you. Apex has a draft ready.”

The train lurched; a crowd got on at the next stop, severing their line of sight. When the doors closed again, he was gone.

Mara rode the rest of the way staring at the reflection of her own plain sneakers.

By the time she reached home, a message from Apex was waiting in her email: formal, sympathetic, offering legal support and covering of “all medical expenses related to the unfortunate incident,” contingent on certain agreements.

Rosa read it over her shoulder.

“Say yes,” Rosa whispered.

Mara’s thumb hovered over the reply button.

She thought of Lila’s hospital bed. Of Ny’s smooth voice. Of Hermes telling her he’d invented half their tricks. Of the way it had felt, just for a second, to be faster than anything.

She closed the app.

“Not yet,” she said.

Rosa’s face hardened. “We don’t have ‘not yet’ money.”

“I know,” Mara said, and for once she didn’t try to argue, because Rosa was right. And Ny was right. And Hermes was right. They were all right and all wrong in ways that knotted her up.

In her room, she lay on the floor, looking at the underside of the bunk. The box under her bed felt like a live wire even with the laces cut.

She was done being pushed from sprint to sprint without deciding where the race ended. But right now, without the shoes, without a plan, she felt just like she had at the first meet: half a step behind and no one slowing down for her.

If there was a third way, she hadn’t seen it yet.

But the city was full of doors she’d never tried. And Hermes, damn him, wasn’t wrong about one thing.

She hated being owned. Even by her own fear.
step_outputs.12:
The next week crawled.

Rain came in, greasy and half-hearted, turning the sidewalks slick. Mara ran without timing herself, regular shoes slapping the pavement, lungs burning in a way that felt both honest and infuriating. No hum. No lanes stretching open. Just her and the grind.

At school, kids scrolled through glitchy clips of the invitational like it was a movie trailer. Someone had cut the footage into a TikTok with a trap beat and lightning emojis. Comments: SHE’S A GOD / SHE’S A CHEATER / EDIT IS FIRE / LILA DESERVED BETTER? / APOCALYPSE AD.

The athletic board emailed: “suspended from competition pending further review.” Coach didn’t meet her eyes when he posted it on the locker room bulletin board.

Apex’s formal offer thickened in her inbox, pages of legalese. They’d cover Lila’s bills. They’d position Mara as a face for “responsible performance tech.” In return: exclusivity, PR appearances, a statement about “trusting Apex’s safeguards.”

Rosa kept circling back to it.

“You don’t have to love them,” she said one night, rubbing her temples. “You just have to not make enemies of people with lawyers.”

“I already did,” Mara said. “When I picked the wrong shoes.”

“You picked,” Rosa said. “You think you have so many choices. You don’t.”

“I’m trying to fix it.”

“How?” Rosa snapped. “By saying no to everything until the rent disappears by magic?”

Mara shut her mouth. There was no answer that didn’t sound childish.

She went to see Lila again. Hospital beige, same humming machines. Lila’s color had improved; the cast made her look badass in a lopsided way.

“Hey,” Mara said, hovering.

“Hey,” Lila said. “Welcome to my glamorous recovery arc.”

They talked about small things: a math quiz, a show Lila had finally binge-watched. Skated around the stadium-sized thing between them.

Finally, Lila said, “Apex called my parents.”

Mara’s stomach dropped. “What did they want?”

“To pay for this,” Lila said, gesturing to the cast. “And to get a cute picture of me giving thumbs up with their logo in the background. ‘Proof they stand by athletes affected by this unfortunate glitch.’”

“That’s disgusting,” Mara said.

“It’s practical,” Lila said. “My parents are scared. Hospital bills are stupid. A check is a check.”

“What do you think?” Mara asked.

Lila looked at the ceiling. “I think I don’t care why the money shows up as long as it shows up,” she said. “And I hate that that’s true. I also think if they can buy me, they can buy you. And you’re more expensive.”

“I haven’t said yes,” Mara said.

“Yet,” Lila said. She studied her. “What are you waiting for?”

“Something that doesn’t feel like selling my name to the highest bidder or ghosting the mess I made,” Mara said. “Option C.”

“Always was greedy,” Lila said. But there was a ghost of a smile.

“I broke the shoes,” Mara blurted.

“Okay,” Lila said slowly.

“The laces, anyway,” Mara said. “It feels… different. Less haunted.”

“You think that fixes anything?”

“No,” Mara said. “But it means if I do something now, it’s me doing it. Not just… wearing him.”

“Then do something,” Lila said. “You’re agitating in place. It’s annoying.”

“You’re concussed.”

“I’m still right,” Lila said.

On the way home, Mara cut down side streets without thinking. The city was wet and reflective; headlights made rivers on the asphalt.

Under the elevated train, an old woman hunched over a folding table fixing shoes. A cobbler stall made of tarps and milk crates and a hand-painted sign: SOLES / HEELS / MIRACLES.

It was probably nothing.

Mara stopped anyway.

The woman glanced up. Her hair was iron gray, braided and coiled. Cigarette in the corner of her mouth. Thick glasses.

“You look like you broke something,” she said.

Mara blinked. “I have some shoes,” she said. “They’re… complicated.”

“Aren’t they all,” the woman snorted. She flicked ash into a chipped mug.

Mara pulled the box from her bag, suddenly sure she’d gone insane. She opened it.

The white-and-gold lay inside, laces severed, tongues lax. Even dead-ish, they looked wrong under the harsh bulb.

The woman’s eyebrows climbed. “Ah.”

“Ah what,” Mara said.

“Hermes,” the woman said, like an exasperated aunt spotting a familiar mess. “Always leaving his toys everywhere.”

Mara’s pulse tripped. “You know him?”

“We all know each other,” the woman said. “Some of us clean up. Some of us play.” She reached out; Mara hesitated, then let her take a shoe.

The woman turned it in her hands. In the reflection of her glasses, for a heartbeat, Mara saw not her own face but a crossroads at night, candles guttering.

“You can’t unwear a thing like this,” the woman said. “But you can retie it so it’s not driving.”

“I don’t want it driving at all,” Mara said.

“A little late for wanting nothing,” the woman said. “Power is like smoke; once you breathe it, good luck pretending your lungs are the same.”

“Are you… a god too?” Mara asked.

“I’m a lady fixing broken soles in the rain,” the woman said. “Ask fewer label questions.” Her mouth twitched. “Names are just brands.”

Mara huffed. “That’s what the other ones say.”

“They’re not wrong,” the woman said. She fished in a crate, came up with a bundle of laces: reds and yellows, frayed, cut from other shoes, bits of bus handles, caution tape woven in. Mortal leftovers.

“Here,” she said. “You want them to be yours? Lace them with your own shit.”

“That’s… very mystical advice,” Mara said.

“You want a TED Talk, go back to the nice tent,” the woman said. “Here you tie or you don’t.”

Mara sat on a crate. Her hands shook as she threaded one of the new laces through the holes.

“What am I supposed to think?” she asked.

“Think whatever you like,” the woman said. “Just know a pact is a two-way story. You been letting other people narrate.”

Mara remembered Ny’s practiced concern, Zeno’s calm control, Hermes’ amusement. They’d all told her versions of herself: prodigy, liability, chosen troublemaker.

“What if my version is worse?” she asked.

“Then at least it’s honest,” the woman said.

Mara finished lacing one shoe, then the other, the red-yellow weaving stark against the white.

“What do I owe you?” she asked.

“Bring them back if they blow up the world,” the woman said. “I’ll be here.” She tapped ash into the mug again. “And maybe stop trying to save everybody in one lap. Start smaller.”

Rain started in earnest. Mara tucked the shoes away.

On the pedestrian bridge near her building, Hermes waited, leaning on the rail. The city lights smeared behind him.

“New look,” he said, nodding at the box.

“New terms,” Mara said.

“Ooh,” he said. “Tell me.”

“If I wear them again,” she said, “it’s not for you. Not for Apex. Not for some cosmic custody battle. No more secret strings.”

He made a face. “That’s not really how—”

“I know I can’t unmake whatever this is,” she cut in. “But I can decide who gets to use it as a commercial. I’m not saying yes to Zeno’s script. I’m not saying yes to yours.”

“Stubborn,” he said approvingly.

“Good,” she said. “You want my speed? It’s public domain. No exclusive rights.”

Hermes laughed. “You’re talking copyright to a god of thieves.”

“Steal something else,” she said.

He looked past her, toward the hazy skyline. “Apex is doing a big launch,” he said. “New line, new slogan, ‘Safeguarding the Future of Performance.’ Cute, isn’t it?”

“I saw,” she said. The invites were all over her feed.

“They’re going to use what happened with you as justification,” Hermes said. “‘See? Wild power is dangerous. But under our brand, it’s safe.’ Close the loop. Nice, clean story.”

“Unless someone screws up the narrative,” Mara said.

His smile sharpened. “Careful. That sounds like volunteering.”

“I’m not stupid,” she said. “I’m not marching in there to burn it all down with my bare hands.”

“Good,” Hermes said. “That never works. Too flammable.”

“I just…” She exhaled. “If they want to use me as the reason everyone hands them the keys, then I’m going to talk first.”

He cocked his head. “You think they’ll hand you a mic?”

“They love a redemption arc,” she said. “Storm girl gets responsible. Nice optics.”

He laughed, delighted. “God, I like you.”

“I’m not doing it for you,” she said.

“Lie to yourself, sure,” he said. “But whatever you’re planning, don’t do it alone.”

“Who else is there?” she asked.

He shrugged. “You’ll be surprised who doesn’t like centralized authority. Even among our lot.”

“Will you help?” she asked.

He leaned in. For once, the joking dropped entirely. “I’ll open doors,” he said. “That’s what I do. What you walk through and what you break once you’re inside? That’s on you.”

“That’s all I want,” she said.

“Liar,” he said softly. “You want justice. That’s a bigger problem.”

He straightened. “They are going to hit you with very convincing arguments,” he said. “They’re not wrong about everything. That’s what makes them hard to fight.”

“I noticed,” she said.

He nodded once, like they’d made a deal even though she’d explicitly tried not to. Then he was gone, leaving only the smell of rain and hot pavement.

At home, Rosa was waiting with her arms crossed. “They called again,” she said. “Apex. They say launch event, they want you there. To show no bad feelings.”

“Good,” Mara said.

Rosa blinked. “Good?”

“I’ll go,” Mara said. “On my terms.”

“What terms? They are the ones with terms.”

Mara thought of the cobbler’s crooked sign, of Lila’s tired face. “Then I’ll mess with theirs,” she said.

Rosa looked at her like she was a child playing superhero. “Don’t make trouble we cannot afford.”

“We can’t afford not to,” Mara said, and surprised herself with the certainty in it.

In her room, she pulled the relaced shoes out of the box. The new laces looked rough, stubborn. Human.

She slipped one on.

The hum was back, but different. Fainter. Less like being dragged, more like getting a nod.

No voice in her head. Just her own pulse.

She walked a quick circle. The floor didn’t tilt. No visions. Just a sense of… routes. Like remembering a map she’d drawn herself.

The launch would be in two days, downtown. Cameras, athletes, Ny’s speeches. They’d invited her to stand there and smile, proof their world was intact.

She’d stand there.

And then, she thought, let’s see how intact it really is.
step_outputs.13:
The Apex launch looked like a festival had eaten a cathedral.

A stage rose in the middle of a closed-off downtown avenue, flanked by towering screens. Drones buzzed overhead. Music pounded from speakers tucked into sleek black trusses. Lines of kids in fresh Apex gear snaked around merch booths. Everywhere she looked, black and gold.

WEAR THE FUTURE, the banners said. TRUST YOUR GODS.

Mara stood at the edge of it all, credentials on a lanyard around her neck, a handler ushering her toward the VIP area.

“Just smile, yeah?” Kade said beside her. His voice was tired around the edges. “No surprises. They’ll say a few words about the investigation, you nod, done.”

“Right,” she said.

He glanced down. “Those aren’t our shoes.”

The white-and-gold, re-laced with stubborn red and yellow, were impossible to miss. They’d scuffed since she’d walked here; city grit marked them. They hummed gently, like a cat thinking about maybe purring.

“They’re mine,” she said.

Kade exhaled. “Mara…”

“You said no surprises,” she said. “Me wearing my own stuff shouldn’t be a surprise.”

He rubbed his forehead. “Just… don’t do anything that gets you sued.”

“You worried about me?” she asked.

“Yeah,” he said. “Stupid, right?”

He peeled off toward the media pen. Mara climbed the steps to the stage.

Ny and Zeno stood near a podium, conferring with people holding clipboards. Ny’s white sneakers gleamed; Zeno’s suit was the color of storm clouds. When they saw Mara, Ny’s smile calibrated itself instantly.

“So glad you came,” Ny said, air-kissing the space near Mara’s cheek. “You look strong.”

Zeno’s gaze dipped to her shoes, paused at the mortal laces. Something flared in his eyes, then banked. “Remember what we discussed,” he said minimally.

“We didn’t agree,” Mara said. Quietly, so only they heard.

He tilted his head. “You agreed to be here. That’s a start.”

Handlers moved them into position. The stage faced a sea of faces and phones. Athletes in Apex gear lined up at the sides like a living catalog. Media jostled in their pen. Lila’s hospital bills, Mara’s suspension, the glitch vids—all of it buzzed under the surface.

Hermes was somewhere; she could feel it like a draft under a door. Near a lamppost, maybe, or in the food truck line. Watching.

“Ladies and gentlemen,” the announcer boomed. “Welcome to the Apex FutureProof launch!”

The crowd roared.

Zeno took the mic first. He spoke about responsibility, about resilience. He mentioned “the unfortunate incident” at the invitational, how Apex’s new regulated performance line would “ensure that what happened that day can never happen again.”

“Unregulated tech and… other forces,” he said, voice carefully neutral, “have no place in modern sport.”

The screens behind him showed glitchy images smoothed into clean lines. “Apex FutureProof: Power with safeguards.”

Ny stepped in, all grace. “We believe in our athletes,” she said. “We don’t exploit them. We protect them.” She gestured toward the line of sponsored kids. “Today, we welcome new faces to the Apex family.”

Polite applause.

“And,” Ny said, smile widening, “we’re honored to be joined by someone you all know. A young woman who’s been at the center of so much conversation. Mara Ortiz.”

A camera snapped to her; her face appeared huge on the screen. The crowd noise shifted—curious, uncertain.

Mara stepped up to the mic. The white-hot stage lights hit her. The hum in her shoes rose, waiting.

She was supposed to read the statement they’d emailed: about trusting Apex, about moving forward together.

Her phone, tucked in her pocket, held the script. She didn’t take it out.

“Hi,” she said.

It came out too loud, raw.

She exhaled, tried again.

“I’m Mara,” she said. “You’ve seen the videos.”

A ripple of laughter, uneasy.

“They asked me to tell you everything’s fine,” she said. “That what happened was a glitch their tech will fix. That I believe in their safeguards.”

Ny’s smile tightened. Zeno’s eyes darkened.

Mara kept going.

“I’m not gonna say that,” she said.

The hum in the crowd shifted. A drone buzzed lower, camera whirring.

“I ran in shoes I didn’t understand,” she said. “I loved how it felt. I liked feeling untouchable. And people got hurt. that’s on me.” Her voice shook, but she didn’t let it crack. “But the answer isn’t handing everything scary to the shiniest company and letting them gatekeep who gets to be fast. Or strong. Or seen.”

“Miss Ortiz—” Zeno started.

She talked over him, heart pounding. “I met gods,” she said. “Real ones. They wear sneakers now. They’re in boardrooms and back alleys and, yeah, probably on this stage.”

A nervous titter in the crowd. Some thought it was a bit.

“They all want the same thing,” she said. “For you to look at them. To believe they’re the only way you get what you want.”

Ny stepped closer, fingers near the mic. Hermes’ voice brushed her ear, amused: You sure?

“Don’t worry,” Mara said. “I’m not telling you to burn your shoes or throw away your phones. I’m not your mom.”

A few real laughs.

“I’m just saying: when someone gives you power with their logo on it, ask what they’re taking back. Don’t let them turn your hope into their altar. Not the old gods. Not the new ones.”

Ny tried to take the mic. Mara took a step sideways, out of reach.

“This is off-script,” Ny hissed, too low for the crowd.

“Yeah,” Mara said into the mic. “It is.”

Zeno gestured sharply toward some tech in the wings. The nearest screen flickered, sound crackling. Something tried to cut her feed.

The shoes tingled.

Routes, Hermes had said.

Mara ran.

Not away. Across.

She bolted along the length of the stage. The hum surged as she moved; each step was a small refusal. The screens’ signals stuttered but didn’t die—she could feel Apex’s tech wrestling with something older.

She dropped off the edge of the stage onto the wide strip of asphalt they’d closed for the event. Security startled, grabbed for her; she slipped past by inches. Everything narrowed to the feel of the ground under her feet.

“Kid,” Hermes’ voice snapped from somewhere above. “Don’t go full martyr.”

“I’m not,” she muttered.

She ran a tight loop in front of the stage, a rogue orbit. Kids cheered, thinking it was part of the show. The cameras tried to track her; their auto-focus smeared.

As she passed clusters of onlookers, she slowed just enough to brush hands, shoulders. A little girl in knockoff Apex sneakers; a teen boy in off-brand skates; an older woman in orthopedic walkers. Each contact sent a faint spark up through them—not lightning, not transformation. Just a flash of I could.

On the screens, the planned footage glitched. Instead of Zeno’s talking points, fragments of what was actually happening jumped through: close-ups of people’s faces as they felt that tiny jolt, as their eyes widened.

“Shut it down,” Zeno barked into his mic. The PA screeched.

Ny moved to recenter. “Technical issue, folks,” she called, voice sugared. “Let’s—”

Mara circled back to the front of the stage, breathing hard, and stopped. She didn’t try to take the mic again. She just looked up at the nearest camera and, for a heartbeat, let herself be huge on the screen.

“I’m not your warning,” she said, more to Zeno and Ny than to the crowd now. Her voice carried anyway. “You don’t get to use me to scare people into your box.”

Zeno stepped forward, jaw tight. “What exactly do you think you’ve accomplished?” he said quietly. Up close, he smelled like expensive cologne and faint ozone.

“Messing up your story,” she said. “A little.”

“It’s still our stage,” Ny murmured. “Our cameras. We can spin this in six minutes.”

“I know,” Mara said. “You’re good at this.”

She wasn’t naive. Half the crowd would leave with a version where Apex had given a troubled girl room to vent. The other half would think it was a stunt.

But some would remember the flicker in their legs when she brushed past. The way their discount shoes had buzzed. The way Ny’s smile had cracked.

That was enough. It had to be.

Security closed in. Hermes appeared at her elbow like a bad idea.

“Time to go,” he said cheerfully.

Zeno’s eyes narrowed. He saw Hermes now, really saw him. Recognition flared like lightning. Old grudges.

“This is your doing,” Zeno said.

Hermes smirked. “Please. I wish I was that organized.”

Ny glared at him. “Always running interference.”

“Always running,” he said.

Mara slipped between them before they could start a pissing contest measured in thunderstorms.

“I’m leaving,” she said. “You’re gonna do what you do. I just wanted people to see you flinch.”

Zeno’s nostrils flared. “We can make this very difficult for you.”

“You already did,” she said. “I’m still here.”

Ny’s gaze, for a second, wasn’t god or brand—it was a tired athlete looking at a stubborn kid. “You could’ve had everything,” she said softly. “Our way.”

“I’d rather have not-everything my way,” Mara said.

Hermes barked a laugh. “God, marry me,” he muttered.

“Shut up,” she said.

They walked off the stage. Security let them, more confused than anything. The crowd’s attention splintered; some watched her go, some turned back to the spectacle as Apex rebooted the program.

By the time Mara reached the barricade at the end of the block, the screens behind her were playing a polished spot again, pretending nothing had happened.

Kade caught up, breathing a little hard. “You really don’t like easy paths, huh,” he said.

“Did you?” she asked.

He looked away.

“Tell Lila they’re still paying,” Mara said. “No strings for her. She got hit by a system, not a god.”

“I’ll try,” he said. “No promises.”

“Tell them if they don’t, I’ll keep talking,” she added.

“That, uh, might be more persuasive,” Kade said, half-smiling.

Hermes leaned on the barricade, watching the crowd. “They’ll recover,” he said. “They always do. They’ll cut your rant into a promo about ‘embracing the conversation.’”

“I know,” Mara said.

“Still worth it?” he asked.

She thought of Zeno’s face when she went off-script. Of Ny’s cracked composure. Of kids who’d felt that instant of shared something.

“Yeah,” she said. “A little.”

“You didn’t free the world,” Hermes said. “Just nudged it.”

“I’m seventeen,” she said. “Nudging’s my level.”

He grinned. “Fair.”

Rosa found her a few blocks away, worry and anger written all over her. “Qué hiciste?” she demanded. “I saw on livestream—”

“I didn’t sign,” Mara said.

Rosa closed her eyes, exhaled. “Of course you didn’t.”

“I’m sorry,” Mara said.

“You’re stubborn like your father,” Rosa muttered. “He also thought he could argue with gods.” But she reached out, squeezed Mara’s shoulder. “We will figure it out. Without selling your name for a commercial. Okay?”

“Okay,” Mara said. It hurt, but it was a good hurt.

A week later, nothing was neatly resolved.

Apex spun the launch as bold transparency. Clips of Mara’s speech circulated with #OwnYourGame; some edits made her sound like she endorsed them. Others painted her as ungrateful. Conspiracy forums called her a demigod. The federation quietly kept her suspended “pending further clarification.”

Lila came home, arm in a real cast now. They texted in fits and starts.

You’re still dumb, Lila wrote once.

yeah, Mara replied.

also it was kinda badass, Lila added. don’t let it go to your head.

too late, Mara wrote.

Rosa picked up an extra shift. Nico drew comics of “Storm Girl” fighting “Shoe Gods” in the margins of his homework.

Hermes drifted in and out: on a street corner, on a bus, once jogging beside her in a drizzle.

“Regretting not signing?” he asked that time.

“Sometimes,” she said. “Regretting saying yes to you.”

“Fair,” he said.

“But,” she added, “I like running on my own terms.”

He nodded. “You know, that’s all any of us ever wanted. Before the temples. Before the logos.”

“Then stop selling,” she said.

He laughed like that was the funniest thing he’d heard in centuries.

One evening, Mara laced up—her white-and-gold with mortal-thread laces, scuffed, imperfect—and went out at dusk. The city was in that in-between light where everything looked more honest.

She trotted down familiar streets. Past the laundromat, where Lila waved with her cast. Past the bodega, past the closed-up lot where Lace & Legend had been and not been. She crossed at a light that stayed green a half-second longer than it should have, and didn’t overthink it.

On the bridge over the expressway, she picked up speed. The shoes responded, but they didn’t drag her; they just met her halfway. Her breath found its old four-count. Her stride settled into that place where effort and ease blurred.
