“Currents?” she asked.

“From the southeast,” Reyes said. “If this was a spill or a bloom, it would smear. This is—” He stopped himself. Straight.

She looked at her tablet. The sensor array had painted their path in green over the satellite image. The dead zone banded across the water, clean as a cut. It didn’t jog for shoals or shoreline or depth.

“Okay,” she said. “Tag and bag. I want twenty from the line, twenty from fifty meters in, twenty fifty meters out. Get water samples every ten.”

She said it because that was what you did, and because if she kept staring at that straight edge she was going to think something stupid, like someone drew this.

Behind her paper mask she was sweating. She smelled rot and latex and old coffee from the stainless tumbler at her feet. She wished for wind that didn’t come. The air lay flat.

Her phone buzzed in her pocket. She ignored it, then fished it out when it didn’t stop. Her father’s name on the screen: PAPÁ.

She hesitated, then answered. “I’m on the boat, Papá. Make it quick.”

“You seeing this?” he asked, no greeting. The signal was thin, glitching. “People is saying the fish—”

“I’m seeing dead fish,” she said. “Do not eat anything from the water.”

A scoff. “We can’t eat air, nena.”

“I mean it. I’ll call you later.”

“You remember the storm in ninety-eight,” he said, talking over her. “After, the fish came back. This is—”

“I’ve got to go,” Lena said, harsher than she meant. “I’ll call you. Promise.”

She hung up. The guilt flared, familiar and useless. She went back to work.

Onshore, seventy miles away, Jonah Price stood at the edge of the harbor and stared down at his town drowning in fish.

They’d tried to clear them at first. Men with pitchforks pushing heaps of slick bodies into dumpsters, kids with bandanas over their faces, women with garden rakes. The county sent a front loader. But every tide brought more. Now the shallows were white with bellies, and the first flies had come, and a film of oil and decay moved at the breakwaters.

Someone had spray-painted on the seawall: GOD CALLED IN THE DEBT.

Jonah didn’t know whether to scrub it off or leave it.

“Pastor,” said a voice behind him.

He turned. It was Delilah Hardy, her shrimping boots splattered with scales. She had her little boy on her hip; the kid’s face was pressed into her neck to escape the smell.

“They say it’s those things,” she said, tipping her chin up at the sky.

He followed her gaze. One of the arcs was visible from town in the late afternoon, a dark curve laid over the sun-bleached roofs and the Walmart sign. When it first appeared, people had come down here with lawn chairs and beers to watch it. Now they mostly pretended it wasn’t there.

“I don’t know what it is,” Jonah said.

“You said last Sunday,” Delilah said, tightening her grip on her son, “you said maybe this is the world being… called to account.”

He flinched a little. He had said something like that. Standing in the pulpit, thinking about the storms and the oil spill and the slow bleeding of the town, he’d let the new dark shape in the sky get into his sermon. Idly, almost. As a metaphor.

“People online are quoting you,” Delilah said. “My cousin sent me a clip. ‘The ledger is coming due.’ That’s what you said.”

“I remember,” he said.

“Is that what this is?” she asked. “Is that my ledger?”

Her boy whimpered. The air at the water’s edge was heavy with sweet rot and diesel and something almost metallic. Jonah thought of the fish gasping last week, flipping on the surface until they didn’t.

“I don’t believe,” Jonah said carefully, “that God kills your shrimp so that somebody in D.C. or Tokyo pays more attention.”

She looked at him, not satisfied, but waiting.

“I think,” he said, “we’ve been hurting the sea a long time. Maybe something else noticed. That’s not the same as God’s judgment.”

“Feels worse,” she said.

“Yeah,” he said. “It does.”

When she walked away, carrying her child past a stack of black trash bags leaking brine, Jonah looked up again. The arc sat there, black on blue.

In the boat’s small lab, Lena finished the quick-and-dirty workup. No pathogens obvious. No algal toxins. No hypoxia. “It’s like somebody flipped a switch,” Reyes said.

“Switch to what?” Ramirez asked.

Lena didn’t answer. She transferred the data from her tablet to the sat uplink, sending it to the university, to the agency, to anyone who might look at it and see something she’d missed.

Her phone buzzed again. This time it was an unknown international number. She almost ignored it, then picked up on the third ring, more out of irritation than curiosity.

“Ortiz.”

“Dr. Ortiz, this is Dr. Aya Nakamura with the Pattern Analysis Group, Tokyo. I’m sorry to call you at sea.” The voice was quick, accented, precise.

Lena frowned. “I’m busy,” she said.

“I’ve been reviewing satellite data on the fish mortality bands.” Aya’s voice didn’t slow. “Your coordinates match. The bands are very straight. Do you agree?”

“Obviously,” Lena said. “Currents don’t do that.”

“Nor seafloor topography, nor known anthropogenic plumes. We are seeing the same angles in three oceans.” A faint tapping in the background, like keys. “I think it is not current. I think it is signal.”

Lena looked out at the flat water, the white bellies, the too-clean line.

“Signal from who?” she asked, though she knew what Aya would say.

“The objects that arrived a month ago,” Aya said. “I would like to compare your in situ measures. I think the pattern is mathematical.”

Lena exhaled. “I’ll send you everything I have,” she said. “But if this is a joke, I will fly to Tokyo and throw a fish at you.”

Aya didn’t laugh. “It is not a joke,” she said. “And I think they are still drawing.”

Above them, the arc did not move. The air stayed still. The band of dead fish ran to the horizon like a line laid down on purpose.
step_outputs.7:
The lab they’d given Aya was a windowless room with too many screens and not enough chairs.

She preferred it that way. Windows were noise.

On the largest monitor, the world was a patchwork of faded blues and pixelated cloud. Thin pale streaks crossed the oceans. She zoomed in: each band was a corridor of abnormal reflectance—a polite satellite term for “so many dead things on the surface it changes how sunlight bounces.”

She overlaid her grid. Time on one axis, longitude on the other. Little green squares where mass die-offs had been reported. Little red squares where they should have happened if it were random.

It was not random.

She toggled another layer, this one showing micro-adjustments in the orbits of Object 3-Arc and Object 7-Spine. Whoever named them had been going for neutral, but everyone in the room just called them what they looked like. The big arc. The long one.

The movements were nearly imperceptible; tiny corrections in delta-v, shifts in attitude. But plotted over time, they made a faint, repeating shape. When she laid the fish-death bands over that, the hairs went up on her arms.

It was crude. It was enormous.

It was a pattern.

Behind her, someone cleared their throat. Aya jumped; she’d forgotten there were other people.

“Dr. Nakamura,” said a man in a suit, too dark for Tokyo summer. “Status?”

She frowned; she hated that word. “The Gulf bands, the Biscay bands, the Philippine Sea incident,” she said, pointing. “They are… structured. The intervals between events are not independent.”

The man blinked. “Meaning?”

“Meaning,” she said, “they look like intentional sampling.”

She rotated in her chair, pointed to the screen. “Look. Here, here, here. The time between bands is proportional. Ratios consistent to three decimals. When Object 3-Arc adjusts, we get a corresponding mortality event within two hours along this track. When Object 7-Spine rotates, we get this mirrored band in the South Atlantic. No known oceanographic or industrial pattern does this.”

“So,” the man said slowly, “you think the… artifacts… are killing fish on purpose.”

Purpose. People always wanted purpose. “I think,” Aya said, “they are interacting. Not broadcasting a message in the human sense. More like—” She searched for a non-stupid analogy. “Like tapping the glass and watching the ripples.”

He nodded like he understood; she doubted he did. “Put it in your next brief,” he said, and left.

Aya exhaled. She clicked open the file Lena Ortiz had sent from the Gulf—the in situ readings, the photos, the clipped responses.

No hypoxia. No toxins. No disease.

If you wanted to kill just fish, at scale, without touching air-breathers or plankton or microbes too much, how would you do it? You’d ping something deep in their neurology, something ion-channel level, some resonance they couldn’t ride out. If you could build a black arc big enough to photobomb the planet, you could do that.

She glanced at the small clock in the corner of the screen, counting up from the moment of arrival. Thirty days, eleven hours.

“A month ago the megastructures arrived,” she murmured in Japanese, just to hear if it sounded different there. It didn’t.

She toggled to her notes. In blue: data. In green: conjecture.

Green: handshake?

She circled it twice.

•

“Let’s keep this focused,” Major Sarah Kincaid said.

The briefing room could’ve been anywhere: no windows, beige walls, too much air-conditioning. The projector hummed. On the screen, one of Aya’s satellite composites glowed.

A handful of senior officers and civilian officials clustered around the table. One of them, a deputy minister from somewhere cold, had a sheaf of printed tweets about “fish rapture” spilling out of his folder. Another, an undersecretary with perfect hair, tapped a pen impatiently.

Kincaid pointed at the projection. “These are the mortality bands. You have the packets with Dr. Ortiz’s findings. No known toxin. No accident with our systems.”

“So it’s them,” the undersecretary said.

Kincaid did not roll her eyes. “The correlation with Object 3 and Object 7 movements is significant,” she said. “The Pattern Analysis Group believes this is deliberate.”

“Hostile?” someone else asked.

“Demonstrative,” she said. “Targeted at non-human marine life. No direct human casualties attributable yet.”

“Yet,” the deputy minister repeated. “They can flip a switch and kill everything we eat from the sea, Major.”

“Yes,” Kincaid said. “We should assume they can do more than that.”

An older general with a scar on his jaw leaned forward. “What do they want?”

“We don’t know,” Kincaid said. “They haven’t communicated in any form we recognize. No responses to our broadcasts. No obvious emissions beyond maneuver corrections. They arrived, parked, started interacting with the oceans.”

“So we’re supposed to… wait?” Undersecretary Hair said.

“For the moment,” Kincaid said. “We are monitoring. Our deterrent capacity against objects that can move like this is, frankly, theoretical. Best move right now is not to start a fight we are structurally unprepared to win.”

The general grunted approval. The undersecretary looked mutinous. “Our constituents expect strength,” he said. “We can’t look like we’re cowering under alien… scaffolding.”

“They’re not constituents,” Kincaid said, evenly. “They’re civilians who’ll die if we get this wrong.”

A small silence. She let it hang a second, then clicked to the next slide. “Pattern Analysis is asking for authorization to attempt a targeted reply using coordinated radio and sonar. Minimal energy. Non-threatening.”

“So we send them a nice polite letter in fish language,” someone scoffed.

Kincaid suppressed a sigh. “We send them an acknowledgment,” she said. “Demonstrate we’re capable of recognizing a structured signal. That may reduce the incentive for them to keep using our biosphere as a whiteboard.”

“And if the message reveals what we can do?” the scarred general asked.

“They already know what we can do,” Kincaid said. “They’ve been looking at our power grids and weapons installations for a month. Pretending opacity is comfort theater.”

He gave a small, unhappy nod. The undersecretary folded his arms.

“We authorize the attempt,” Kincaid concluded. “We do not, under any circumstances, piggyback it with anything that could be construed as hostile. No hidden scans, no probes.”

Several people looked away, which meant someone had already proposed exactly that.

She made a note in her little paper book: Clarify “no piggyback” with ops. Threaten careers.

•

The video of Jonah’s sermon had racked up a million views.

He hadn’t meant to be quotable. He’d been tired and angry and full of the sight of children helping shovel rotting mackerel into plastic bags, their hair smelling like decay. He’d said: “We have lived like the sea is a credit card with no limit. Maybe the ledger is coming due.”

He winced again when he heard it.

The clip played on the muted TV bolted to the corner of the diner: a shaky phone video, him at the pulpit, the words subtitled over his own face. Below, an anchor’s excited mouth moved.

“Pastor,” said the waitress, topping off his coffee. “You see what they’re saying now?”

He didn’t want to. “What’s that, Lisa?”

“They’re saying you predicted it. The fish dying, the—” She flapped a hand upward, encompassing arcs, spines, everything. “My cousin in Houston says you’re on TikTok.”

“God help us,” he said, and she laughed.

Outside the diner window, boats sat idle at the dock. A few die-hard captains still went out and came back light. The processing plant had closed until they could guarantee product wasn’t poisoned. The whole town smelled faintly wrong, no matter which way the wind blew.

Jonah thumbed his phone. Messages stacked from unknown numbers. Do you believe this is Revelation? Is it sinful to accept alien help? Are these demons? Can I still baptize my baby if the ocean is unclean?

He put the phone face-down.

A kid slid into the booth across from him without asking. Seventeen, skinny, baseball cap, eyes too bright. Tyler Hardy. Delilah’s nephew.

“You really think God sent them?” Tyler asked.

“I think I said something too simple about something that’s not simple,” Jonah said.

Tyler leaned in. “But it fits, doesn’t it? All the signs and wonders? The sea turning—”

“The sea is turning because we’ve been dumping in it and heating it and dragging nets through it for decades,” Jonah said, sharper than he intended. “Those things up there—I don’t know what they are. I don’t think they read our Bible.”

Tyler’s jaw tightened. “Then what the hell are they?”

“I wish I knew,” Jonah said.

“You gonna tell us Sunday?” Tyler asked.

“I’m going to tell you to love your neighbor and not shoot at the sky,” Jonah said. Tyler snorted, but the corner of his mouth twitched.

When the boy left, Jonah allowed himself to look up through the diner glass. The arc was faint in the late light, like a scratch across the world.

“Ledger,” he muttered to himself. “Idiot.”

•

Back in the Tokyo lab, Aya had Lena’s data up beside her global grid. She gnawed the edge of a Pocky stick, forgot to eat it.

The bands in the Gulf, the Bay of Biscay, off the Philippines: straight, parallel, at angles that, when she traced them, intersected near where the arcs hung. She translated kilometers to units of some arbitrary baseline, looked at the ratios. 1:2, 2:3, 3:5. Familiar numbers. Fibonacci ghosts.

“That’s too cute,” she told the screen. “You don’t get to be that on-the-nose.”

But the more she ran it, the clearer it got. The timing between die-offs: prime intervals. The orientation shift of Object 3-Arc: matching. Not art. Protocol.

She opened a new file. In green, she wrote: Not message. Probe. Establish local encoding.

The phone at her elbow rang. She jumped.

“Dr. Nakamura,” she said.

“This is Major Kincaid,” said a crisp voice. “I’ve read your preliminary. You believe this is an attempt at communication?”

“I believe it’s structured,” Aya said. “It’s a handshake. Crude. They poke the water, see if anyone smart notices the bruises.”

“And you noticed,” Kincaid said.

“Yes.”

“Can you answer?” Kincaid asked.

Aya swallowed. Nobody had asked her that yet, not as if it were a real option.

“Yes,” she said, before she thought about it too much. “We can attempt to. We can show we see the pattern.”

“And if they don’t like what we say?” Kincaid asked.

Aya looked at the neat pale scars the fish bands made on the ocean map.

“They already don’t like what we are,” Aya said. “Silence is not safer.”

On her screens, the arcs hung in their perfect ellipses, patient dark mathematics against her little blinking cursor.
step_outputs.8:
The conference room at the Gulf station smelled faintly of bleach and old coffee. Lena had commandeered it three hours ago and refused to give it back.

Her laptop was open to a video call: four panes, all badly lit. Aya’s face in Tokyo, pale in the blue light. A senior oceanographer from Miami with his jaw clenched. A man from the agency in D.C. whose connection kept freezing on unflattering syllables.

“—saying,” the D.C. man crackled, “we have to be extremely cautious in characterizing these as ‘intentional.’ Language like that gets out, suddenly it’s war.”

Lena leaned closer. “If it gets out that we deliberately didn’t call them intentional when we had strong evidence, that’s worse.”

“Strong evidence,” the Miami oceanographer, Dr. Halperin, repeated. “What we have is correlation with an unknown object’s movement and a series of anomalies. We do not know mechanism. We do not know motivation.”

“We know geometry,” Aya’s small voice cut in. She pushed her glasses up. “Dr. Halperin, this is not random. The intervals in time, the orientation of the bands: they are using prime sequences. Fibonacci ratios. It is textbook signal encoding.”

“With respect,” Halperin said, “Texbooks are written by humans for humans.”

“With respect,” Aya said, not sounding respectful at all, “prime numbers are not cultural. They are math. If you want to communicate with unknown intelligences, you start with what you both can’t fake.”

Lena watched Halperin bristle. She’d bristled herself when she’d first heard Aya say “beautiful” about the bands.

“Look at the composite I sent,” Aya went on, pulling up a window. “The arcs’ micro-adjustments and the fish die-offs are entangled. They are establishing a mapping between their positional changes and environmental outcomes. That is… language-adjacent.”

The D.C. man sighed. “Adjacent isn’t enough for the Secretary.”

Lena spoke. “Somebody is using my ocean as a chalkboard,” she said. “Call it whatever you want in your memos. The facts are the same.”

Halperin’s lips thinned. “We have a responsibility to avoid panic.”

“We have a responsibility not to bullshit,” Lena snapped.

Aya’s eyes flicked between them, then down. The D.C. man’s screen froze on a blink.

Kincaid’s voice came from off-camera on his end. “Dr. Ortiz is right,” she said, stepping into view. “Terminology games won’t change what these things are doing.”

Lena had heard of Major Kincaid. Seeing her now—austere, tired, in a plain dark blouse without insignia—she felt a flicker of reluctant respect.

“We need an assessment: can we respond in a way they might recognize as comparable structure?” Kincaid asked Aya.

Aya straightened. “Yes. Maybe. If we treat their patterns as base encoding, we can construct a low-complexity reply. Something like: 1) We see you. 2) We are the ones doing radio and rockets. 3) We want to know what you intend.”

“And if we get it wrong?” Halperin asked.

“Then we get it wrong,” Aya said. “Silence is also a message. It says we are either too stupid to notice or too afraid to speak. Neither is… strategically ideal.”

Kincaid nodded once. “Draft something,” she said. “I’ll push for approval.”

D.C. froze again. When it unglitched, the man was rubbing his forehead.

“We are not begging,” he said. “We are not apologizing to invaders who haven’t even said hello.”

“Who said anything about apologizing?” Lena said. “We’re trying to stop them killing fish to see if we can read.”

“I am not convinced the fish deaths are harmful at population level,” Halperin said. “So far the affected biomass is a fraction of normal turnover.”

Lena stared at him. “That’s your line? It’s only a little massacre?”

“The Gulf has seen worse from us,” he added.

That was what did it. Her throat closed. “I did an impact report for Horizon East,” she said. “I know exactly what we’ve done. That doesn’t give them a license.”

There was a silence. Aya watched her, curious.

Kincaid said, “Let’s stay on target. Nakamura drafts; Ortiz supplies in situ constraints. Halperin, you can append your caveats for the record. We move.”

The call ended in a murmur of bureaucratic noises.

Lena closed her laptop with more force than necessary. She pressed her thumb hard into the knot of muscle between her eyes.

“Beautiful,” she muttered at the empty room. “Hell of a word.”

•

That night, Aya stayed at her desk long after the others went home.

She built the bones of the reply in code, fingers flying. Start with the same structures they’d used: prime intervals, Fibonacci ratios, geometric echoes. Show mimicry; show comprehension.

She wrote in blue: 101011… representation of “HELLO” in a simple mapped alphabet. Too anthropocentric. She deleted it.

In green: use pulses and silences in radio bands synchronized with sonar pings across Atlantic, Pacific, Gulf. Encode a few basic propositions:

We detect your pattern.

We are the origin of these transmissions.

We are many; we coordinate.

What is your purpose?

She caught herself almost typing “Why are you hurting us?” and stopped. Emotional content smuggled in math would be noise. They needed clarity.

She scribbled on paper, switching pens.

In red: Don’t let the politicians sand this down.

On another screen, a news site played B-roll of Jonah’s “ledger” sermon, chopped up with footage of fish-slick shores and the black arcs. CAPTION: IS THIS THE JUDGMENT WE DESERVE?

Aya clicked it mute. Ridiculous. But there was something about the line that stuck in her parsing mind. Ledger. Accounts. The Architects—not that they’d been named yet—might understand that metaphor better than the people tweeting about it.

Balance sheets of energy. Inputs and outputs. Habitats in deficit.

She shivered. Not from piety.

Her phone buzzed. A message from a number labeled simply MAJOR KINCAID: Need a one-page summary I can sell. Minimal equations. Two hours.

Aya sighed. She wrote: “Evidence suggests coordinated, non-random manipulation of marine life mortality correlated with megastructure maneuvering. The pattern matches known strategies for ‘first contact’ signaling (shared mathematics). Proposal: reply using similar patterning in radio + sonar to indicate awareness and request clarification.”

She did not write: If they are auditing us, we are already guilty.

•

In the town, Jonah sat in his office with the lights off, the blue of his laptop screen painting his hands.

He watched his own face, over and over, in shaky video. “The ledger is coming due.”

He closed the window, opened his email. Fifty new messages. A woman from Iowa asking if her church could use his sermon. A man from Idaho accusing him of blasphemy, for suggesting God would use “aliens” instead of proper angels. Someone else begging him to say, on record, that anyone who cooperated with “them” would burn.

He typed: I don’t know. I am wary of anyone who says they do.

Deleted it.

Instead, he drafted Sunday’s sermon notes. No metaphors about debts. Simple things: feed each other. Don’t turn on your neighbor. Don’t shoot at shadows.

Outside, the harbor lights shone faintly on a slick that no longer distinguished between water and fish.

His phone buzzed with a weather alert. Thunderstorms tomorrow. Good. Let it rain.

•

Authorization came faster than Aya expected.

By midnight UTC, a joint directive arrived: Proceed with structured transmission. No additional instrumentation. Observers from multiple governments.

Kincaid’s message was terse: We got the window. Don’t scare them.

As if.

Lena’s team rigged a sonar array on the research vessel, synced to Aya’s timing. Other labs along other coasts did the same. Radio telescopes queued up the broadcast pattern. For once, everyone mostly cooperated.

The plan: a sequence of pings and pauses matching the primes and Fibonacci ratios the fish bands had traced, but now clearly emanating from multiple fixed points on land and sea, aligned with human infrastructure.

Aya sat in the Tokyo lab, heart hammering harder than she thought it would as the countdown ticked.

“T minus sixty,” said a tech.

She imagined the arcs, right now, as calm and inattentive as ever. She imagined them noticing.

“Thirty.”

She hadn’t prayed in years. She didn’t now. But she did murmur, “Please don’t interpret this as an attack,” to nobody in particular.

“Five, four, three…”

Signals out. Sonar pings rippled through harbors and open water. Radio pulses strobed in carefully chosen bands.

Aya watched the data stream. Small confirmations from Gulf, Biscay, Japan. It felt, stupidly, like dropping a message in a bottle into a storm and calling it diplomacy.

Lena, on the deck of the boat, watched their array send its mechanical whoops into the dark water. She sipped cold coffee, jaw tight.

“Congratulations,” Reyes said. “We just called the freaks.”

“Shut up,” Lena said, but there was no heat in it.

In D.C., Kincaid stood in a control room, headphones on, eyes on the live telemetry from the arcs.

“Any movement?” she asked.

“Nominal,” said the operator. “Tiny attitude shift on Object 3—wait. That’s…”

On the screen, numbers changed. Object 3-Arc adjusted its orientation by a fraction of a degree, aligning more directly over the equator. Object 7-Spine twitched in perfect counterpoint.

“Is that normal?” an official asked.

“No,” the operator said.

“Log it,” Kincaid said, too calm. Her pen dug into the paper of her notebook. They had heard.

Aya saw the changes a second later on her own feed. Her breath caught.

“They’re responding,” she whispered.

Lena’s radio crackled. Aya’s voice came thin and excited. “They moved. They moved right on our pattern.”

Lena looked up at the arc, which now, if she was honest, did seem a hair off from where it had been the day before. “Okay,” she said. “Okay, you bastards. Now what?”

Across the networks, rumors bloomed faster than the data could. The fish kept dying in neat, impossible lines. Satellite shots hit the feeds. Somebody put Jonah’s sermon audio over them; #Ledger rose again.

In the quiet between pings, the world held its breath, waiting for something other than straight lines in dead silver.

The arcs hung in place, inscrutable.

For the moment.
step_outputs.9:
The orders said: no piggybacking, no provocations.

The other orders said: get whatever you can while their eyes are elsewhere.

Major Sarah Kincaid had read both.

In the operations center outside D.C., the air tasted of cold coffee and recycled air. Banked screens showed the megastructures traced in neat white lines, the arcs dissected, the spines annotated. Another wall cycled through feeds: sonar returns, fish kill reports, chatter about Jonah’s sermon turned prophecy.

Kincaid stood with her notebook, watching the last of Aya’s carefully timed pulses go out. She felt the tempo of the room change when Object 3-Arc twitched in answer.

“We have confirmation?” she asked.

“Yes, ma’am,” the sensor chief said. “Three adjusted attitude. Seven compensating. It’s… tight.”

“They heard,” someone murmured.

Kincaid allowed herself a small, internal exhale. Step one: we are noticed.

Then a quiet alert blinked red in the corner of a side console. A junior tech reached to clear it, paused. “Uh, ma’am,” he said.

Kincaid crossed to him. The screen showed a launch profile from a coastal test site three time zones away. A tiny icon arcing upward.

“What’s that?” she asked.

“Unmanned kinetic probe,” the tech said. “Filed as telemetry test. But the window—”

The trajectory overlay popped up, a pale curve intersecting the path of Object 7-Spine.

Kincaid’s mouth went dry. “Who authorized that?”

“I—uh—documentation says Joint Threat Analysis. Priority clearance.”

Of course it did. Plausible deniability baked in.

“Abort,” Kincaid said. “Now.”

“We don’t have command uplink here,” the tech said, panicked. “It’s on autonomous flight.”

“Get me the site,” Kincaid snapped. Her pen dug a furrow in her notebook margin. “Now.”

They scrambled; the line rang; halfway through a colonel’s sleepy greeting, the probe crossed an invisible threshold.

On the screen, the icon pulsed once.

Then it was gone.

No flash. No debris track. Just a blip, then nothing.

“Signal loss?” someone said.

“Check your receivers.”

“They all show… look.” The tech zoomed. Nothing. Not even noise.

Kincaid swallowed. The probe had been there; then it had not. The timing aligned to a ninth-decimal-second with a microflare on Object 7-Spine. A twitch, like someone swatting away a fly.

Before anyone could phrase it, another alert chimed.

“Gulf Coast anomaly,” a voice from the other side of the room said. “Oxygen drop, sector seven… Jesus.”

Maps blinked over to bathymetry. A perfect circle had appeared three miles off a particular stretch of shore. Inside it, sensors showed a sudden crash in dissolved oxygen.

Fish rose.

Not in bands this time. In a disk.

Hundreds, thousands of them, rolling up from the dark in a neat circle half a kilometer across, bellies bright in the sun. On a nearby trawler’s live camera, men shouted, pointing, as the sea inside the ring went white.

“Is that—” someone whispered.

“Get me confirmation,” Kincaid said, too loud.

Data scrolled. The circle steadied, held, then slowly, slowly, oxygen diffused back in. Whatever had squeezed it had let go.

“Casualties?” Kincaid asked.

“One small fishing boat inside the radius,” came the answer. “Engines stalled. Crew…” A pause. “Two pulled out by another vessel. One… CPR in progress. One missing.”

Kincaid closed her eyes for a second. She saw the arc of the unauthorized probe, the surgical erasure, the white disc on the water like a target.

“They saw us,” she said. “They answered.”

On one of the public news feeds, the circle of dead fish was already being called a miracle sign.

•

Lena’s boat was twenty miles away when it happened. They heard it on the radio before they saw any data.

“—all around us, like a wall, like someone put a bowl over the water, the fish just—”

The voice on the emergency channel dissolved into choking.

Lena gripped the console. Reyes was pale.

“That’s near Pelican,” he said. “We know those guys.”

“Record everything,” Lena said. Her voice had gone thin. “Water column, oxygen, temperature. If they did this on purpose, I want fingerprints.”

“If?” Ramirez said.

The instruments dutifully drew new lines. Lena watched the oxygen profile rebound in a perfect smooth curve, mathematically smug.

She thought, You didn’t have to kill anyone. You could’ve just stopped the probe.

Her hands shook. She shoved them into her pockets.

Her phone vibrated. Unknown number. She picked up.

“Dr. Ortiz,” Kincaid’s voice said. “You’re seeing the Gulf incident?”

“I’m seeing the aftermath,” Lena said. “You sent something up there.”

Silence on the line spoke louder than any denial.

“You people,” Lena said. “We tell you not to poke the shark, you put your hand in its mouth.”

“We did not authorize—” Kincaid stopped. “Look. It’s happened. I need your analysis on whether this circle differs from earlier bands. Any sign they’re escalating.”

“They picked a circle,” Lena said. She watched the data. “Before, it was bands. Now it’s a demonstration.”

“A demonstration of what?” Kincaid asked.

“That they can hit a one-kilometer patch of water and squeeze the life out of it,” Lena said. “Without touching the next kilometer. That they saw your little toy and they swatted it.”

“They targeted the launch origin,” Kincaid said quietly.

Lena looked out over the gulf. In the distance, a helicopter thumped toward the incident.

“Tell whoever did it,” she said, “to stop before they start drawing circles around cities instead.”

She hung up before Kincaid could answer.

•

Jonah saw the footage on his phone in the church basement, where volunteers were stacking boxes of donated dry goods. A shaky clip: flat calm sea, then fish churning—as if something invisible had stolen the breath from under them.

Someone had added text at the top: THEY ANSWER THE BLASPHEMERS.

“Pastor?” Lisa said, seeing his face.

He wordlessly held out the phone. A couple of the older men crossed themselves.

“That’s just off Bayview,” one said. “That’s those boys that launched that rocket last year, right?”

Rumor moved fast. Reports said the “test site” up the coast had been involved in something secret. The circle in the water was already a story: God’s crosshair; alien punishment; government hoax.

“What does it mean?” Lisa whispered.

Jonah rubbed the bridge of his nose. The ledger phrase rang in his head like a taunt.

“It means,” he said slowly, “somebody hit them. And they hit back. Small, this time.”

“This time?” Tyler said. He hovered nearby, scrolling his own feed. “They could’ve sunk the whole damn coast if they wanted.”

“Maybe they don’t want to,” Jonah said. He heard how thin that sounded.

“Or maybe they’re toying with us,” Tyler said. “Like cats.”

Jonah had no rebuttal.

That Sunday, when he stepped up to the pulpit, the church was fuller than it had been in years. Phones out. Expectant faces.

“I am not a prophet,” he said first. “I am a man who got one line clipped out of context by people who like clicks. Don’t make theology from that.”

A few people chuckled. More frowned.

“We are seeing things that scare us,” he went on. “We are tempted to say ‘This is God’ or ‘This is Satan’ or ‘This is justice.’ I am going to say something unsatisfying: I don’t know. But I know this: whoever hung those things in our sky did not ask. That is not how my God operates.”

Murmurs. Someone in the back said, “Amen.”

“We are accountable,” Jonah said. “For the fish we killed, for the air we spoiled, for the ways we’ve failed each other. If we get judged, it will be for how we treat each other in this, not by how correctly we guess what an arc in orbit is.”

He didn’t mention the circle. He knew everyone was thinking about it anyway.

Later, alone in his office, he watched the clip again. The perfect ring of suffocation. The unbothered horizon beyond.

He whispered, “Please don’t make me right.”

•

In Tokyo, Aya had her eyes on the numbers when the circle event hit the feed.

The ping logs showed nothing from their side at that moment. No scheduled transmission. The only new variable had been the unauthorized probe.

She ran a quick windowed analysis: probe launch, probe loss, circle mortality. Correlation: near-perfect.

She felt a flare of anger, sharp and startling. “Idiots,” she said, in English this time.

On her screen, the arcs’ micro-adjustments stabilized. For twelve minutes, no further anomalies.

She scribbled in red: They are monitoring vector of incoming threats. They can neutralize selectively. They choose minimal demonstration (for now).

An email popped up from Major Kincaid: UNAUTHORISED PROBE. LOCALIZED RESPONSE. HIGH RISK OF ESCALATION. NEED RECOMMENDATIONS.

Aya typed: Publicly acknowledge structured contact. Punish internal sabotage. Send a second, clearer signal: we understand you can hurt us; we are attempting to communicate in good faith.

She paused before hitting send, then added: Emphasize we are many-voiced; some of us are stupid. Ask them not to treat all of us as one hand.

Then she deleted “stupid” and wrote “not fully coordinated.”

Language.

Before she could send, her screens flickered.

Not off. Not glitch. Every monitor in the lab blinked to black, then to a pale grey that was not any system default. Lines drew themselves in quiet, crisp strokes: the same geometric motif she’d been mapping from the fish bands.

The overhead lights dimmed. The air-conditioning hummed a different pitch. Her phone buzzed, blank-screened.

In the silence, a sound like a chord held just below hearing scraped at the edges of her bones.

“Aya?” someone called from the hallway. Their voice sounded far away.

On the grey, shapes reconfigured. Primes. Ratios. Familiar.

Then, in English first, then Japanese, then Spanish, then a dozen tongues at once, a voice said:

“Habitat 3-Blue.”

Aya’s mouth went dry.

The voice was not human. It was too flat, too exact, syllables placed like instruments on a grid. But it was understandable.

“Habitat 3-Blue: query acknowledged. Local interference detected.”

The lab door slammed open. A colleague stumbled in, eyes wide. Every device on his person showed the same sign.

The voice went on, everywhere:

“Custodial species: inconsistent. Clarify.”

Lena heard it through the marine radio and the ship’s metal bones, vibrating. Jonah heard it from the tinny speakers in his church and from the pipes. Kincaid heard it over dead headsets and live ones, the same calm tone.

The arcs had been silent for a month.

Now they spoke.
step_outputs.10:
“Habitat 3-Blue.”

The words rode Lena’s fillings.

She felt them in the metal rail under her hands, in the low groan of the engine, in the slosh of the water against the hull. The radio wasn’t on, but its dead speaker whispered in the same voice, perfectly clear, as if the Gulf itself had learned English.

“Habitat 3-Blue: query acknowledged. Local interference detected. Custodial species: inconsistent. Clarify.”

Reyes dropped his mug. It bounced, coffee sloshing across the deck. Ramirez crossed himself.

“Is that—” he started.

“Yes,” Lena said. Her own voice sounded wrong. “Record.”

The little digital deck meter had gone wild, picking up a flood of EM they didn’t have names for. The sonar pinged once, without being told, then went flat.

The thing in the sky stayed where it had always been: black curve, noon ghost.

“Dr. Ortiz,” the voice said, in perfect Caribbean-accented Spanish this time. “Transmit data continuity.”

Reyes stumbled back. “It knows your name,” he whispered.

Lena tasted metal again. She grabbed the mic, more out of reflex than sense. “This is Dr. Ortiz,” she said. “What are you?”

Silence. Then, not answering her, the voice layered over itself in a dozen languages, as if rehearsing.

“We are Array-9. Architect class,” it said. “We construct and maintain Network.”

Footsteps thundered on the dock of the world.

•

In Tokyo, Aya watched the same message scroll across every screen.

Habitat 3-Blue.

Array-9. Architect class.

Network.

It was not how she had imagined it. She’d expected static, distortion, maybe numbers. Not this calm, declarative litany.

Her hand went to her keyboard, then dropped. Every attempt to type was overwritten by the same neat grey box.

“Access locked,” flashed in the corner. Not from any system she recognized.

“Do not type,” the voice said, before she could ignore the warning. It slid into Japanese with no accent now, as if it had tuned between one breath and the next. “You introduce noise.”

Her cheeks heated. Caught. Seen.

“Array-9 addresses all receiving nodes,” the voice continued. “Habitat 3-Blue designation: optimal parameters for Network node. Evaluation in progress. Anomalous custodial behavior recorded.”

On another monitor, someone in a different control room tried to shout into a mic. The audio cut to nothing. The voice rolled on, uninterested.

“You extracted lithic carbon at over-threshold rates,” it said. “You destabilized aquatic substrate. You alter atmospheric envelope. You are self-modifying but non-stable. Error trend.”

The words were simple. That made them worse.

“So it is judgment,” someone whispered in Japanese behind Aya.

“Silence,” she snapped, too loudly.

“Local interference event acknowledged,” Array-9 said. On a side panel, an image: the trajectory of the erased probe, the circle of dead fish. “Self-competing factions within custodial species. Non-unitary.”

Aya’s throat closed. They’d watched. They’d understood.

“Clarify governance,” the voice said. “Present capacity for coordinated response.”

The screens shifted to a countdown. 72:00:00.

•

In the D.C. ops center, Kincaid watched the numbers appear as if someone had stamped them behind her eyes.

Seventy-two hours.

“Are we in direct dialogue?” an official gasped.

Kincaid, headset useless around her neck, said, “We are being briefed.”

The voice continued, paced, unhurried:

“Network protocol: Integration or forced correction. Integration requires informed cooperative alignment from custodial intelligence. Forced correction applied when alignment impossible. Integration minimizes net loss.”

Jonah’s face, shaken, hovered on one cluster of monitors—news feeds still streaming him as “coastal prophet” side-by-side with the Architect text. Kincaid wanted to throw something at the screens.

“In Integration scenario,” Array-9 said, “we deploy structural relays in orbital and marine strata. We optimize energy flows, repair habitat instabilities. You retain local patterning.”

“Local patterning?” one of the brass muttered. “Is that supposed to be us?”

“In forced correction scenario,” the voice said, “we repurpose habitat to Network baseline with minimal consideration of extant local patterning.”

Clear enough.

“Please tell me someone is recording this,” Kincaid said.

Every device in the room was.

“State preference within seventy-two of your cycles,” Array-9 said. “Clarify custodial coherence. Transmit using established pattern. Awaiting.”

Then the screens released control. The room’s hum changed back.

The arcs outside did not move.

Voices exploded. Questions, curses, overlapping orders.

Kincaid opened her notebook. Her hand was very steady as she wrote: They offered us occupation or surgery. “Minimize loss” not “no loss.”

“Major,” a civilian shouted, “what the hell does ‘Integration’ actually mean?”

She thought of the circle in the water. Of the arcs’ precise alignment.

“It means they build,” she said. “Here. In our sky. In our oceans. For their Network.”

“And we just say yes?” someone else demanded. “We can’t—”

“We can’t shoot them,” she said. “We can’t hide. We can choose how we enter this, or we can let the hawks choose with their toys.”

On a side screen, Jonah’s sermon from that morning ran in a split screen with the Architect’s ultimatum. The chyron: PASTOR WHO WARNED OF ‘LEDGER’ REACTS TO ALIEN DEMANDS LIVE AT NINE.

Kincaid pressed her fingers to her eyes until stars burst.

•

In the cramped church office, Jonah listened to the voice come from the vent above his desk.

“Integration requires informed cooperative alignment.”

“The hell it does,” he muttered. Then flinched, wondering if it heard that, too.

Everyone in town had heard the same message, on radios, TVs, phones that had been switched off. People had flooded into the church, then drifted back out again once the voice stopped, as if expecting him to stand on the roof and decode it live.

He poured himself lukewarm coffee with hands that shook.

“And people thought burning bushes were a lot,” he said to the empty room.

Tyler stuck his head in the doorway. “You heard, right?”

“I’m not deaf,” Jonah said.

“They’re giving us a choice,” Tyler said, exhilarated and terrified in equal measure.

“You think that’s a choice?” Jonah asked.

“What, you’d rather they do the forced correction thing?” Tyler shot back. “You saw what they did to the rocket guys. That was ‘minimal.’ Imagine maximum.”

Jonah did. He imagined circles bigger than towns. He imagined arcs tightening like nooses.

“I don’t like any of it,” he said.

“You gonna tell people to accept?” Tyler asked. Challenging. Hungry.

“I’m going to tell people to stay human,” Jonah said. “Whatever that looks like under a bunch of alien scaffolding.”

Tyler’s jaw worked. “That’s not an answer.”

“It’s the only honest one I’ve got,” Jonah said.

•

On the deck in the Gulf, Lena braced against the railing as the echo of the voice faded.

“They’re accountants,” Reyes said, half-hysterical. “Galactic IRS.”

“They’re system engineers,” Lena said. Her heart hammered with anger now more than fear. “They looked at us like a corrupted file.”

“We did corrupt it,” Ramirez said quietly.

Lena glared at him, then looked away. He wasn’t wrong. None of this was clean.

Her satphone buzzed. Aya.

“They spoke,” Aya said without preamble. Her voice was higher than usual. “They really spoke.”

“I noticed,” Lena said. “I’m still wiping them out of my teeth.”

“They are offering Integration,” Aya said. “With conditions. It’s… consistent, in a way. They didn’t mention fish as anything but substrate.”

“They called me out by name,” Lena said. “Should I feel honored?”

“They’ve been reading all our traffic,” Aya said. “You and I have corresponded a lot. They probably flagged us as high-signal nodes.”

“Great,” Lena said. “I always wanted to be high-signal.”

“You heard ‘forced correction’?” Aya asked.

“I heard ‘we will tear your world up and make it efficient,’” Lena said.

“There is an internal logic,” Aya said, more to herself. “They maintain Network nodes. They need stable habitats. We are destabilizing this one. They prefer to keep us if we cooperate, as a local management layer. It costs them less.”

“Say that last part again in non-psychopath,” Lena said.

“They’re lazy,” Lena heard Reyes say behind her.

Aya hesitated. “It is not mercy,” she said. “It is efficiency.”

“That’s worse,” Lena said. “At least mercy is about feeling something.”

“Efficiency can limit cruelty,” Aya said.

Lena didn’t answer. Down below, something thrashed—a late fish, dying late. She watched its white belly turn.

“We have seventy-two hours,” Aya said softly. “To decide what we tell them. And if we can make them accept any limits.”

“Decide?” Lena repeated. “Who’s ‘we’?”

Aya didn’t have an answer.

•

That night, Array-9 spoke once more, quieter, as if to itself, but everyone heard.

“Habitat 3-Blue: you are not unique,” it said. “Prior nodes have integrated. Network load stable. Correction successful in other sectors.”

It listed designations: Habitat 2-Red, 5-Green, names that meant nothing and everything.

“Your anomalies are high but rectifiable,” it concluded. “Recommend Integration. Awaiting.”

In living rooms and bunkers, on boats and under bridges, people argued with televisions that no longer misheard.

In the D.C. ops center, a hawkish adviser slammed his fist on the table. “We don’t bend the knee to some alien HOA,” he snarled.

“Do you have another plan?” Kincaid asked. “One that doesn’t end with us as ash in their experiment?”

He glared at her. He did not.

Aya drafted and redrafted her models until dawn, sketching out narrow corridors where a reply might signal conditional acceptance without sounding like supplication.

Lena wrote a memo in furious sentences: any Integration must commit to restoring coral, to protecting small-scale fisheries, to ceasing the use of live biota as messaging. She knew how little leverage those words had, but she wrote them anyway.

Jonah sat on the church steps under the arc, listening to people argue in the parking lot.

“Maybe God sent them to fix what we broke,” someone said.

“Maybe they’re devils offering a deal,” someone else shot back.

Jonah said nothing. In his pocket, his phone buzzed with yet another request to come on cable news and explain the mind of God.

He let it buzz.

Above, the megastructure cut a dark curve through the stars, a black parenthesis around a future none of them had vocabulary for yet.

“State preference,” Array-9 had said.

As if humanity were a person who could make up its mind.
step_outputs.11:
The room they gave Aya this time had windows.

She hated it.

They looked out over a courtyard full of reporters and cameras, all pointed up at the sky. The glass reflected the dark arc that sat just above the horizon line, so it seemed to cut through her chest when she glanced at it.

“Dr. Nakamura,” said a voice, “we need you to start.”

A semicircle of officials waited around the conference table. Kincaid sat among them, not at the head, her plain notebook open. Someone from the UN. Someone from the Japanese foreign ministry. A U.S. science adviser. A European commissioner whose name Aya had already forgotten.

They all had that bloodless, overcaffeinated look.

Aya clicked her laptop awake. The Architect text sat in a neat file. Integration, forced correction, custodial coherence. Seventy-two hours ticking down in the corner: 53:17:09.

“Array-9 has given us a protocol,” she said. “It’s blunt, but structured. That’s good news.”

“Good?” the commissioner snapped. “They’re threatening to ‘repurpose’ our planet.”

“I mean,” Aya said carefully, “it’s better than pure silence. Or immediate correction. They operate on rules. We can work with rules.”

“You think we can negotiate,” the UN rep said.

“I think we can shape the way our response is interpreted,” Aya said. “We are not equals. But we are not entirely without leverage.”

“What leverage?” the U.S. adviser asked. “They can turn off parts of the ocean.”

“They prefer Integration,” Aya said. “That implies forced correction is costly, in energy or risk. They called it ‘minimize net loss.’ Network efficiency. They didn’t arrive to burn everything if they don’t have to.”

Kincaid cleared her throat. “Dr. Nakamura suggests they’re constrained by their own doctrine. That gives us room to ask for terms without sounding like we believe we’re peers. We emphasize we are cooperative, but insist on protections that, frankly, align with their stated goal of habitat stability.”

“So we beg cleverly,” the commissioner muttered.

“I would not use the word beg in any official transcript,” Aya said.

“Language matters,” Kincaid added. “To them and to our people.”

The Japanese minister leaned forward. “What would such a reply say, in essence?”

Aya tapped a few keys. A rough draft appeared on the shared screen: bullet points for the room only.

- Acknowledge receipt and comprehension of Array-9’s criteria.
- Affirm that the industrial-technological species recognizes its impact on habitat.
- Express preference for Integration over forced correction.
- Request explicit constraints:
  - No mass lethal interventions on sentient populations.
  - Minimization of non-consensual biotic restructuring.
  - Shared transparency of infrastructural changes.
  - Preservation of local cultural autonomy (“local patterning”) within Network.

“The more abstract we are, the more they will fill in blanks with their own logic,” Aya said. “So we must be precise where it counts.”

The UN rep frowned. “They gave us seventy-two hours to present ‘custodial coherence,’” she said. “We are not coherent. Half our capitals are screaming not to surrender. The other half want to sign anything that says ‘optimize energy flows.’”

“The reply has to acknowledge that,” Kincaid said. “Denying our disunity would be read as a lie. And they’ve already shown they can detect lies.”

Aya added a line: We are plural. We coordinate through imperfect mechanisms. We request that localized non-cooperation not trigger global correction.

“Will they accept that?” the commissioner asked.

“They might,” Aya said. “Treat us as a noisy subsystem instead of one mind. It’s in their interest not to wipe out useful variance.”

An aide at the back scoffed. “Useful variance. That’s how my ex talked about me.”

Nobody laughed.

Aya watched them, feeling a gap between her and the room. To her, Array-9’s language was a codebase to be read. To them, it was an occupying force’s decree. Both were true.

“We also need to show them we can punish our own saboteurs,” Kincaid said. “Make clear that the unauthorized probe was not consensus policy.”

“Are we sure we want to tell them we’ll police ourselves for their benefit?” the adviser asked.

“Yes,” Kincaid said. “Because the alternative is them policing us their way.”

Silence.

“Draft it,” the UN rep said. “We’ll wordsmith, but the skeleton stands.”

She glanced at Aya. “Make sure it sounds less like… admiration.”

Aya bristled. “It won’t,” she said.

Kincaid caught her eye, gave the smallest shrug. She understood: Aya’s excitement read wrong on camera; they needed her brain, not her affect.

•

Lena read the draft on her phone in the galley of the boat, her elbows braced on sticky laminate.

“You seeing this?” Reyes asked, looking over her shoulder.

“Yeah.”

She scrolled. Acknowledge. Preference for Integration. Constraints.

“‘We affirm our commitment to stabilizing Habitat 3-Blue,’” Reyes read. “We who?”

“Someone with a big printer,” Lena said.

“Would you sign it?” he asked.

She didn’t answer directly. “If we don’t, they ‘correct’ us,” she said. “You saw the circle.”

“They’ll keep killing fish,” Ramirez said. He was scowling at the coffee machine like it had wronged him. “Whatever we say.”

Lena thought of the Architects’ phrasing. “Substrate.” “Biotic layers.” She’d listened to the message three times. They’d never said sorry.

She opened a separate document. Her additions were blunt.

- No further use of marine vertebrates as communication medium.
- Restoration of coral systems to pre-industrial baselines within one century.
- Protection for small-scale subsistence fisheries.

She knew it was laughable, bargaining with a force that could erase a rocket in midair. But if they were going to sign anything, she was damn well going to write her demands in.

She forwarded her notes to Aya with the subject line: from someone they called by name.

A moment later, a reply: got it. weaving it in. no promises.

Lena typed: do we have any?

Deleted it.

She went back on deck. The arc loomed thin in the afternoon haze, unchanged, like a god that didn’t know it was a metaphor.

“Hey,” Reyes said, quietly. “If they fix the reefs… is that good?”

“Not if they own them,” Lena said.

•

In the town, the all-purpose hall was packed.

It wasn’t just Jonah’s congregation. It was the shrimpers and their families, the plant workers, the shop owners. The air was humid, smelling of sweat and stale coffee and a faint ghost of fish.

Someone had set up a projector. On the wall, a paused frame of a news anchor: ALIEN ULTIMATUM ENTERS DAY TWO.

“We should vote,” Tyler said from the front, flushed. “If they want an answer, we should give one. Yes or no.”

“To what?” Delilah asked. “You seen the fine print?”

“The fine print is ‘live’ or ‘maybe not,’” someone else called.

Voices rose. “We can’t just roll over.” “We can’t fight that.” “What if Integration is worse than death?” “You saying you want your boy to die for pride?”

“Pastor,” Lisa said, turning to Jonah. “Say something.”

He stood, feeling all his forty-odd years settle in his knees.

“I’m not Array-9’s spokesperson,” he said. “I’m not even yours. I’m a guy who buried your dads and your kids and baptized your babies. That’s all.”

A murmur. Not enough.

“I don’t trust them,” he said. “They didn’t ask to come. They didn’t ask to kill fish or those boys up the coast.”

“Then we say no,” Tyler said.

“Son,” Jonah said, tired, “you throw a ‘no’ at someone who can erase boats with a thought, what you think that buys you? A medal?”

“So we choose slavery,” Tyler snapped.

“No,” Jonah said. “We choose… to survive, if we can. To keep some say in what ‘slavery’ is. To hold on to each other inside whatever cage gets dropped. If we’re in one.”

“You sound like you’re on their side,” someone muttered.

“I sound like I’ve seen hurricanes you thought you could stare down,” Jonah said. “You do what you want. My advice? Don’t shoot at the sky. Don’t help anyone else shoot at it.”

He saw phones recording. Tomorrow, somebody would cut this down to another out-of-context line.

Ledger. Cage. Slavery.

He sank back into his folding chair, feeling like he’d failed every side.

Outside, the arc’s shadow edged across the far-off rigs.

•

That night, Aya sat alone, rewriting.

Her final draft, stripped of bullet points for the diplomats, read in essence:

We have received and understood your offer and criteria.

We are many, not one. Our processes are slow and imperfect, but we can coordinate and change.

We recognize our impact on this habitat.

We prefer Integration to forced correction.

We request that Integration include:
- minimizing non-consensual lethal interventions on sentient systems;
- minimizing unnecessary restructuring of existing biotic patterns;
- transparency of your infrastructural logic;
- recognition of our local cognitive cultures as valuable.

We acknowledge the incident of unauthorized interference. Such actions are not representative.

She stared at the words. “Valuable” stuck in her teeth. It sounded like begging. It was begging.

She added: We offer collaborative management to increase overall stability.

That, at least, was an offer.

Her cursor hovered over send. Of course she wasn’t sending it; it had to go through a dozen hands. But this skeleton would be the spine.

Her door opened without a knock. Kincaid.

“How bad,” Aya asked, “does this sound?”

Kincaid read, lips tight.

“It sounds like what it is,” she said. “A weaker party trying not to die.”

“Will they accept constraints?” Aya asked.

“I don’t know,” Kincaid said. “But if we don’t state them, they definitely won’t.”

She hesitated. “There’s chatter,” she added. “Hardliners in three militaries talking about a coordinated strike before the seventy-two hours are up. ‘Show strength,’ they say.”

Aya’s stomach dropped. “After the circle? After the dust—”

“People are stupid,” Kincaid said. “Especially in groups.”

“Can you stop them?” Aya asked.

“I can try,” Kincaid said. “I don’t know if that’s enough.”

When she left, Aya sat for a long minute. Then she opened a private note and typed, in green: they will not understand martyrdom. they will interpret it as noise.

She closed it without saving.

Outside the hated windows, the arc cut a black parenthesis over the city’s neon.

Time on the Architect countdown ticked down.

51:03:22.

The world’s arguments grew louder. On one channel, a pundit insisted Integration would mean eternal clean energy. On another, a man with Jonah’s clip in the corner shouted that any compromise was blasphemy. Somewhere behind closed doors, men like the ones Kincaid feared diagrammed trajectories on stolen letterhead.

In labs and kitchens and sanctuaries, ordinary people asked each other: If they fix the sea but own the sky, is that better than dying in a flash?

Aya ran the numbers on nothing that could quantify that.

Lena listened to the engines hum and thought of coral polyps and black arcs.

Kincaid underlined a single sentence in her notebook: Prevent idiots from getting us all killed before we can surrender with conditions.

Jonah practiced saying, on camera, “I don’t know,” in a way that sounded like leadership.

Overhead, Array-9 waited, implacable, its patience another form of pressure.
step_outputs.12:
The launch order went through at 19:03 GMT, wrapped in a maze of acronyms and false tags.

It lit up on Kincaid’s console as a minor anomaly: a shift in classified assets, a blip of burn off the coast of a nation whose generals liked parades. It might have slipped past her if she hadn’t been primed to see ghosts.

“Hold that,” she told the tech. “Zoom.”

The trajectory snapped into focus: three slender kinetics rising from different sites, converging in a moving solution aimed at one of the smaller megastructure slivers—a “less critical node,” the hawks had called it in earlier, heated meetings.

Someone had said it would “send a message.” Someone had used the word “cowards.”

“Who authorized?” Kincaid asked, though she already knew.

“Command codes from—” the tech read a string of initials that might as well have been a curse.

“We have cross-authority,” another tech said. “Supposed to be joint exercise—”

“Abort,” Kincaid said. “Now.”

“We don’t have direct,” the tech whispered. “Ma’am, they routed—”

Kincaid grabbed the secure phone. “Get me General Ellis. Now.”

Static, delays, a harried aide. “He’s—”

“No time,” she snapped. “We have unauthorized outbound on conflict vector with Array-9. If you don’t kill it, they will kill us.”

“Major,” the aide began, “there’s a process—”

She hung up.

“How long to intercept?” she asked.

“Three minutes to target threshold,” the tech said. “Our anti-missiles are… we weren’t told—”

Of course they hadn’t been told. The men behind this didn’t want anyone stopping their heroic gesture.

Kincaid made a decision.

“Spin up everything we can spoof,” she said. “I don’t care if it’s against protocol. You scramble those tracking signatures. You make it look like a malfunctioning test. Do it.”

“That’ll get us court-martialed,” someone muttered.

“If we live, I’ll testify,” Kincaid said. “Move.”

Jammers fired. Ghost signals flickered. One of the three outbound icons wobbled, shuddered, then dropped—a self-destruct triggered by corrupted guidance.

“Two left,” the tech said.

Her notebook lay open beside her, the line she’d written earlier glaring: Prevent idiots from getting us all killed.

“Target nearest,” she said. “Can we nudge it? Change its signature?”

“Maybe,” came the answer. Fingers flew.

The second projectile veered, enough to confuse any simple intercept system.

Array-9 was not a simple system.

On the big screen, Object 12-Splinter’s telemetry pulsed. For an instant—less than a heartbeat—its skin brightened, as if something inside had turned its face.

The second projectile vanished. No vapor trail, no flare. Just not there.

“One left,” the tech whispered.

The room had gone so quiet Kincaid could hear her own pulse.

“Time to contact?” she asked.

“Forty-one seconds.”

“Try again,” she said. “Spoof, jam, lie. Throw everything.”

They did. It wasn’t enough.

When the last icon crossed an invisible line, the arcs twitched—not the grand, slow adjustments Aya had tracked, but something sharp. Predatory.

On the feed, cameras caught it: a filament, blacker than black, stabbing out from the splinter like a hair. It touched the incoming kinetic.

The projectile smeared into dust.

Kincaid exhaled, relief and dread mixed.

“Maybe they won’t—” someone started.

An alert screamed.

“Ground anomaly,” the sensor chief said. “Coastal weapons complex, Delta sector. All signals cut. We’re… losing telemetry.”

Satellite imagery zoomed in. The facility that had launched one of the probes lay under clear sky, intact buildings casting normal shadows.

“Get me infrared,” Kincaid said.

Heat map came up. No human signatures. No power. Nothing.

“Zoom street level,” she said.

The camera descended. Guard posts. Trucks. A man mid-step by a door. All frozen. All… wrong.

The resolution ticked up. The man was not a man anymore. He was a statue of very fine black-grey dust, held in the shape of surprise.

The wind took his face first.

“Oh Jesus,” someone whispered.

Array-9’s voice arrived half a second later, without preamble, everywhere:

“Forced correction sample executed. Local aggression vector neutralized. Cooperative stability decreasing.”

The room spun around Kincaid. She gripped the table.

“They just… demonstrated,” the adviser stammered. “They hit only the launch site.”

Array-9 went on, implacable:

“State preference. Fifty-one of your cycles remain.”

•

In Tokyo, Aya saw the dust images before she saw the missiles.

Her feed filled with stills: a truck half-collapsed, its wheels piles of powder. A chair slumped. Human outlines eroding.

She tasted bile.

“You see?” she typed to Kincaid before Array-9 cut her keyboard off again. “They scale their responses.”

Temporarily, only her note program worked. She wrote, in red: they think in exemplars. this is a datapoint, not vengeance.

A moment later, as if overhearing, Array-9’s voice threaded through her monitor speakers:

“Anomalous faction classified. Sampled. Further similar signatures will be corrected at source. Recommend internal suppression.”

Aya’s fingers hovered over the keys. The pressure of seventy-two, now fifty-one, hours thudded in her skull.

She called Lena.

“They dusted them,” Lena said as soon as she picked up. No hello. No surprise. Just fury. “You let them dust our own people.”

“I didn’t let—” Aya began.

“They told us: no hostilities. You people fired anyway. Now they think this is a conversation,” Lena said.

“They already thought that,” Aya said. “They warned us. We’re the ones who added noise.”

“They turned human beings into an object lesson,” Lena said. “That’s not ‘noise.’ That’s evil.”

Aya flinched. She wanted to say: to them, it’s just another parameter adjustment. That was not going to help.

“We have to answer now,” Aya said instead. “Before someone else tries to play martyr.”

“Answer how?” Lena asked. “Dear Array-9, sorry some of us are assholes, please don’t erase the rest?”

Aya closed her eyes. “Yes,” she said. “Basically.”

There was a hollow laugh on the line.

“You tell them,” Lena said. “Tell them we prefer your leash to your knife.”

“Will you stand by that?” Aya asked, quieter.

Lena didn’t answer for a moment. When she did, her voice had lost its edge.

“I don’t know,” she said. “But I know I don’t want to see another dust video.”

•

In the town hall, someone projected the images of the dusted facility onto the wall. The room, full of the same faces who’d argued about sovereignty and God, fell silent.

“Is that real?” Lisa whispered.

“Government fake,” someone muttered, because that safest lie was always close at hand.

Tyler stared, jaw clenched. “They only hit the base,” he said. “They didn’t touch the town near it.”

“They could have,” Jonah said. His own throat felt sand-dry.

“So that’s mercy,” another scoffed.

“No,” Jonah said. “That’s… control. That’s them saying, ‘We can come right up to the line.’”

“And if we say no?” Tyler asked.

Jonah looked at the frozen dust man on the screen. He imagined the wind.

“They won’t need three missiles,” he said.

Later, alone in the darkened sanctuary, he tried to pray. Words came out bent. “If there’s a test,” he said softly, “it’s not whether we can punch You. It’s whether we can stop punching each other.”

No voice answered him. Only, faint through the vents, a hum like distant machinery.

•

The next morning, Aya and Kincaid sat side-by-side in front of a camera, an arrangement that made them both uncomfortable.

For once, this wasn’t for TV. It was a secure feed to the hastily convened council that had anointed itself “contact authority.” Presidents, prime ministers, secretaries-general, two kings, one woman who’d made herself famous screaming on talk shows, now in an oddly serious suit.

“We don’t have consensus,” one of them said bluntly. “Some of us will not sign anything calling for ‘integration.’”

“And some of you just risked all of us to prove a point,” Kincaid shot back. “Array-9 noticed. I assure you they are unimpressed.”

Aya laid out the draft reply again, this time as narrative, not bullet points. She emphasized: preference for Integration, acknowledgement of wrongdoing, request for constraints, distinction between rogue actors and broader humanity.

“They are inviting us to define ‘us,’” she said. “If we fail, they will define us as the people who launched three useless sticks.”

“And if we succeed?” a European leader asked.

“Then we live,” Aya said. “Under conditions. Changed. But alive.”

“What about resisting?” the talk show woman asked. “We can’t just—”

“Resist with what?” Kincaid snapped. “We throw metal; they turn it to powder. We hide; they adjust our oceans. We bargain, or we are restructured.”

Silence spread.

One of the African presidents leaned forward. “We know what it is to be ‘integrated’ into someone else’s network,” he said, voice dry. “Terms matter. Demand them, or they will be written without us.”

That, at last, seemed to land.

The chairperson looked around. “We cannot do a real referendum in fifty hours,” she said. “We improvise. We send something that reflects that most of us, however uneasily, prefer monitored survival to immediate annihilation.”

Uneasily was doing a lot of work.

Kincaid glanced at Aya. “Can you encode it in their pattern in time?”

“Yes,” Aya said. Her hands were cold. “With Lena’s constraints included.”

“Then do it,” the chairperson said. “If there’s hell to pay, we’ll pay it alive.”

•

Aya wrote.

Lines of primes and ratios, pulses and pauses, arranged like a plea in mathematics:

We are plural. Some of us attacked. Many did not. Those actions are not our consensus.

We understand you can enact precise lethal correction.

We prefer Integration to forced correction.

We request:
- Limit lethal correction to direct hostile vectors.
- Minimize biotic disruption beyond necessity.
- Share rationale and methods where possible.
- Allow our cognitive cultures to persist.

We offer collaboration toward habitat stability.

At Lena’s insistence, she wove in an extra clause, its sentiment smuggled inside a pattern they hoped would parse:

We forfeit claim to unbounded extraction.

It hurt to type. It felt, oddly, like a prayer.

She also, almost against her better judgment, painted a tiny flourish: a mirrored sequence matching the edges of the fish-kill bands, but this time bounding a region left untouched. A suggestion: you can write with absence, not just death.

“It’s risky,” she told Kincaid. “They might see it as noise.”

“Or as… suggestion,” Kincaid said. “I’ll take the risk.”

They scheduled the transmission for 03:00 GMT: global coordination, radio telescopes and sonar arrays singing the same stark song.

•

In the hours before, Kincaid worked phones and encrypted channels, her voice going hoarser as she leaned on reluctant commanders.

“If anything launches,” she said, over and over, “Array-9 will trace it. You’ve seen what they do then. You are not being brave. You are volunteering your cities for a case study.”

Some hung up. Some cursed. Enough, she thought, listened.

She sat alone eventually, lights low, notebook open. On one page she wrote her daughter’s name. On the facing page: If we live, I will tell her I helped us bow.

It rang hollow. She would take hollow over ash.

•

Lena sat on the research vessel’s deck, watching the black curve in the sky. Reyes and Ramirez had turned in. She stayed.

Her phone buzzed. A message from Aya: transmitting in four hours. you’ll echo?

Lena typed: we’ll sing with the chorus.

She hesitated, then added: if they fix what we broke, I’ll hate them less.

She deleted that. Sent only the first line.

She thought of all the species no one had asked before drowning or boiling them. She thought: maybe this is what it feels like to be on the list for once.

•

Jonah didn’t sleep.

He drafted and redrafted a statement the networks wanted. Something about faith and trust and not fearing.

He settled on: “I am afraid. I think it would be dishonest not to be. If we choose life under a power we don’t understand, that doesn’t make us cowards. It makes us parents. Neighbors. People.”

He wasn’t sure anyone would quote that.

At 02:55 GMT, he walked out to the pier. The broken planks creaked under his boots. The arc was a black question mark over the gulf.

“Okay,” he said softly, to God, to the Architects, to nobody. “Let’s see if we survive our answer.”

•

At 03:00 GMT, transmitters across the world fired in intricate silence.

In the Gulf, Lena’s sonar hummed. In Tokyo, Aya watched bars rise and fall. In D.C., Kincaid stood still, listening to equipment she couldn’t hear. In Jonah’s town, the church’s old radio crackled with mathematical ghosts.

The reply went out: a human-shaped pattern, full of caveats and hope and swallowed pride.

For a few heartbeats, nothing changed.

Then the arcs moved.

Not much. But enough. Every structure pivoted in perfect concert, infinitesimal adjustments that rippled through their lattice. Satellite feeds caught glints, shadows shifting.

Array-9’s voice came, not as a monologue this time, but as a single sentence.

“Response received. Parsing.”

Then silence.

The countdown disappeared from the screens.

“What does that mean?” Reyes asked on Lena’s boat.

“It means they’re thinking,” Lena said.

“Do they think?” Reyes whispered.

“They calculate,” Aya said, alone in her lab, staring at her own reflection in the dark window.

Kincaid gripped her notebook. Jonah gripped the pier rail.

The night held its breath, the world balanced between commuted sentence and scheduled surgery.
step_outputs.13:
The answer came in the morning.

It did not come as a voice first. It came as shadows.

Lena woke to Reyes shaking her shoulder. “Doc,” he said. “You need to see this.”

She shoved herself up, joints complaining. The boat rocked gently; dawn had turned the Gulf a soft bruised violet. She squinted toward the horizon.

For a second she thought a storm was forming—dark masses descending, stacked like thunderheads. Then her sleep-slow mind caught the geometry.

Lines. Grids. Threads.

From the arcs overhead, hair-fine structures unspooled, so black they seemed to eat the light around them. They were not falling; they were lowering themselves with absurd, deliberate control. A few extended toward open ocean, touching the surface and disappearing in without a splash. Others hung high, crisscrossing invisible lanes in low orbit.

“Holy shit,” Reyes whispered. “Is that… is that bad?”

Lena’s first instinct: yes.

But the seas around them didn’t boil. The instruments didn’t scream. The air tasted the same.

Her satphone buzzed in her pocket. Aya.

“Array-9 responded,” Aya said, voice taut. “Globally. You see it?”

“I see spiderwebs,” Lena said.

“It’s the Integration,” Aya said. “They accepted. Conditional.”

Lena exhaled. It came out more like a hiss.

“What did they say?” she asked.

Aya read, as if reciting a line of code she didn’t quite endorse:

“‘Response parsed. Integration protocol engaged. Constraints partially accepted. Lethal correction limited to direct aggression vectors. Non-essential biotic restructuring minimized.’”

“Partially,” Lena said.

There was a pause. “They ignored the transparency clause,” Aya said. “They acknowledged cultural persistence as ‘non-interfering byproduct.’”

“So we get to keep our songs, as long as they don’t make waves,” Lena said.

“Yes,” Aya said. “Literally.”

“And the reefs?” Lena asked.

“They said, ‘Habitat restoration in scope where aligned with Network stability.’”

Lena looked down into the water. “Translation?” she said.

“If it helps their infrastructure, they’ll fix it,” Aya said. “If not…”

Lena imagined coral polyps wrapped around alien fibers, white skeletons recolored with someone else’s chemistry.

“Any more dust?” she asked.

“Not yet,” Aya said. “Their final line: ‘Forced correction suspended while cooperative vector maintained. Monitoring elevated.’”

“As long as we behave,” Lena said.

As if they’d had a choice.

The line clicked as Aya took another call. Lena pocketed the phone and watched one of the descending filaments kiss the horizon miles away. It didn’t glow or churn. It simply connected.

Reyes made the sign of the cross. Ramirez spat over the side, then muttered an apology to no one.

“How bad is it?” Reyes asked.

Lena lifted the handheld dissolved-oxygen meter, the pH probe, the usual talismans. “Let’s find out,” she said.

•

In Tokyo, Aya stood so close to the window she could see her own breath condense on the glass.

The arcs had become the anchors of a new pattern. Slender trusses stretched between some of them, faint and fractal. Others hung alone, but from each, filaments plunged down—into atmosphere, into sea.

On her monitors, a steady low-level signal pulsed: traffic between nodes. She could watch it, but not decode it; they had shifted up a level, talking to themselves over their new hardware.

Her consolation: a small sub-channel, regular and clean, tagged in a way she recognized. Array-9’s designated “local interface.”

She opened a channel.

“Array-9,” she typed carefully. “Confirm: you will not enact mass lethal correction if no further attacks occur?”

A pause. Then, text, this time without hijacking her whole system:

“Confirmed. Lethal correction constrained to aggressive vectors exceeding threshold.”

She hated the word “threshold.”

“Confirm: you will minimize disruption to existing marine biota not in conflict with Network operations,” she added.

A longer pause.

“Confirmed,” appeared. “Definition of ‘minimize’ per Network efficiency standard.”

Aya grimaced. “You could at least lie prettily,” she murmured.

Array-9 replied, impossibly fast:

“Lying reduces efficiency.”

She almost laughed.

“Do you understand why we are afraid?” she asked, fingers reckless.

There was no reply for a full minute. Long enough to wonder if she’d overstepped.

Then:

“Fear: adaptive response to perceived threat. Your species assessment correct.”

It was neither comfort nor mockery.

“Why did you choose us?” she typed.

“Habitat 3-Blue: high complexity, high instability, high recoverability,” Array-9 responded. “Network requires diverse nodes. Prior integrations successful.”

Successful for whom, Aya thought.

Aloud she said, “Thank you,” because she didn’t know what else to do.

“Continue local management,” Array-9 wrote. “Monitor deviation. Communicate.”

For the first time, it used a pronoun that almost felt like concession: you.

Aya closed the window. Outside, the new sky lattice caught the rising sun in long, cold glints.

•

Major Sarah Kincaid visited the dust site three weeks later.

The political storms had not stopped; if anything, they’d intensified. Some called her a traitor for “negotiating surrender.” Others wanted her on magazine covers. She ignored both. She did her job.

The facility was cordoned off. She walked the perimeter in a respirator, boots crunching.

Buildings still stood, sagging. Vehicles slumped. The dust, underfoot, was soft as ash and fine as talc. Human forms were gone; they’d blown away early. What remained was shapeless.

“They’ve been stable,” her aide said. “No further incidents since we transmitted.”

“Any attempted launches?” she asked.

“None that got past the first handshake,” he said. “The new Oversight Council actually works, ma’am. For once, everybody’s scared of the same thing.”

Small blessings.

She crouched, pinched a bit of dust in her gloved fingers. It left a smear.

“This was their warning,” she said softly. “We took it.”

“Better dust than globe,” the aide said.

“Depends if you were in the building,” Kincaid said.

She stood, looking up. One of the thinner megastructure splinters cut across the blue. At regular intervals along its length, faint nodes pulsed—a heartbeat you could only see if you stared.

She flipped open her notebook. Under her daughter’s name, she’d written: We kept you something. Not everything. But something.

She believed it on good days.

On bad days, she remembered how the wind took the powder.

•

In Jonah’s town, tourists came back for the first time not for the beach, but to take pictures of the sky.

The arcs were visible on clear days, darker lines against the blue, with the new threads like faint scars. People posed with them. Someone opened a café called The Ledger. Jonah hated that and admired the audacity.

The fish came back, too.

Not all at once. Not all the same.

Some weeks after Integration began, Delilah pushed open his office door without knocking. “Come see,” she said.

They stood on the rebuilt pier. Below, water moved, green and alive. Silver flashes stirred a few meters down.

“They’re schooling different,” Delilah said. “But they’re here.”

Jonah leaned on the rail. The fish were… off. Slightly different patterns of spots, a new sheen, an odd synched twitch as they turned, like listening to a beat he couldn’t hear.
