  - Leon: Flirts with him for sport initially; later, oddly kind, brings him chips.

Sample lines:

- “If this is a cult and I’m not invited, I will sue.”
- (Seeing Crimson branding) “Oh, they’ve got a logo. That’s never good.”
- “We’re not victims, babes, we’re protagonists. Start acting like it.”
- (To Eleanor) “You don’t get to call it ‘donation’ when you’re picking the cast behind our backs.”

# Leon

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:

- Turned in his late teens several decades ago; stopped counting properly around Britpop.
- Migrated between towns under different names; now “Leon Hart,” quietly embedded.
- Works nights at a kiosk coffee stand by the station and does odd jobs.
- Personality:
  - Dry humour, understated.
  - Fed up with vampire melodrama; prefers quiet, boring survival.
  - Feels responsible for keeping things “ethical,” resents being unpaid supernatural compliance officer.
- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Drinks tea obsessively as a sensory placebo; complains it does nothing but keeps doing it.
  - Collects lost-and-found items from the kiosk (gloves, scarves).
  - Hates being cold even though it doesn’t affect him much anymore.
- Flaws:
  - Passive. Let Crimson Service creep into grey areas without stepping in hard enough.
  - Avoidant like Immy, but with more centuries of practice.
  - Can be condescending to humans: assumes they’ll screw it up.

Physicality:

- Looks 17-ish: pale, tired-pretty, permanent shadows under his eyes.
- Dark hair, usually tucked under a beanie or hood.
- Dresses in layers; neutral colours; nothing flashy. Moves carefully, like he’s used to not drawing attention.
- When he forgets himself, he’s too still; no fidgeting, unblinking. That’s when he looks wrong.

Motives, allegiances, relationships:

- Motives:
  - Stay off the radar of both human authorities and vampire enforcers.
  - Maintain his “ethical feed” to avoid hunting.
  - Story-specific: shut down Eleanor’s rogue scheme before it burns everything.
- Allegiances:
  - Bound by some agreement with Beckwith and whoever runs the more official vamp operation.
  - Begrudgingly allies with Immy & co when he realises he’s outpaced.
- Relationships:
  - Mr Beckwith: co-conspirator; Leon has a complicated almost-friendship with his guilt.
  - Immy: sees too much of his younger self in her; respects her spine.
  - Callum: recognises the value of his anxiety-logic.
  - Aaliyah: baffled, slightly charmed by her theatrics.

Sample lines:

- “I don’t sleep in coffins. They’re terrible for your back.”
- “If I wanted to kill you, I wouldn’t text first. That’s… tacky.”
- (Looking at Eleanor’s files) “Humans shouldn’t need fangs to be terrifying, apparently.”
- “I’m not your spooky mentor. I’m the idiot who thought a memorandum of understanding would fix this.”

# Mr Daniel Beckwith

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:

- Early 30s, Science teacher. Grew up locally, left for uni, boomeranged back when the job market laughed at him.
- Teaches Chemistry and random PSHE; runs Science Club that three kids attend.
- Personality:
  - Earnestly nerdy, fumbling.
  - Too trusting of systems and “partnerships.”
  - Deeply conflict-averse; intellectualises guilt.
- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Overuses “right” at the end of sentences.
  - Owns three identical grey cardigans.
  - Draws terrible diagrams on the board and apologises to them.
- Flaws:
  - Went along with Crimson Service because it sounded like funding and opportunity.
  - Didn’t push back hard enough at red flags; convinced himself oversight was enough.
  - When cornered, initially more worried about his job than the kids.

Physicality:

- Slim, slightly hunched from years over lab benches.
- Perpetual five o’clock shadow he never quite manages to shave clean.
- Glasses, ink-stained fingers, scuffed staffroom mug saying “World’s Okayest Teacher.”
- Moves nervously; drops things; laughs in small bursts.

Motives, allegiances, relationships:

- Motives:
  - Wanted to help bright students; got out of his depth.
  - Wishes fervently to fix this without a scandal.
- Allegiances:
  - To the kids, ideally, but compromised by his deal with Leon/the vamp bureaucrats.
- Relationships:
  - Leon: strange colleague; semi-friend. Owes him.
  - Immy: sees her potential; feels he’s betrayed her trust most.
  - Eleanor: initially impressed; later frightened by her certainty.

Sample lines:

- “It’s all, um, very above board, I assure you. Mostly. I mean— it is.”
- “Imogen, you have to understand, the funding— no, that’s not… that’s not the point. Sorry.”
- “I thought if there were rules, it would be safe. I was wrong.”

# Eleanor Price

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:

- 17, head girl, UCAS poster child.
- From a middle-class family in the “nice” part of town; parents on PTA, talk about “maximising opportunities.”
- Personality:
  - Razor-focused, high-achieving, good at smiling in ways that make adults relax.
  - Believes deeply in meritocracy; despises “wasted potential.”
  - Treats people like resources on a spreadsheet.
- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Keeps a colour-coded planner; ticks items off with a flourish.
  - Says people’s names a lot in conversation—weaponised politeness.
  - Adjusts her blazer and badge when stressed.
- Flaws:
  - Zero tolerance for mess or refusal; sees hesitation as weakness.
  - Has convinced herself ends justify means; no one has successfully challenged that.
  - Sees poorer / less “driven” students as needing direction, even if forced.

Physicality:

- Tall, neat brown hair in a low ponytail or immaculate bob.
- Always in regulation uniform plus badge and lanyard; shoes polished.
- Perfect posture; controlled movements, calculated eye contact.
- Smile that never quite reaches her eyes when she’s angry.

Motives, allegiances, relationships:

- Motives:
  - Get into a top uni; leave town; leave mark.
  - Crimson Service is her legacy project; ethics warped by pressure and opportunity.
  - Thinks: if some kids’ blood can serve greater good, and they’re “strong enough,” why not?
- Allegiances:
  - To her constructed self-image as indispensable leader.
  - To an abstract “system” of excellence, not to individuals.
- Relationships:
  - Teachers: adore her; trust her too much.
  - Peers: respect/fear; very few close friends.
  - Immy: barely on her radar initially; later, sees her as a troublesome anomaly.
  - Beckwith: a tool; disappointing when he wavers.

Sample lines:

- “We’re all asked to give something, Imogen. Some of us are brave enough to say yes.”
- “Language like ‘harvest’ is unhelpful. It’s allocation.”
- (When confronted) “You think the world will thank you for being difficult? I’m offering you purpose.”
- “Do you know how many zeros are on the grant letter? You’d rather let that vanish than part with a few millilitres?”

These profiles should give us enough grain and contradiction to keep the cast feeling like people rather than roles when we move into drafting.
step_outputs.6:
The text came just after midnight.

I was in bed but not in any meaningful way on the path to sleep. The duvet was up, lamp off, headphones in, laptop balanced dangerously on my stomach. On-screen, a man in a PVC trench coat was giving a monologue about destiny to a woman in suspiciously impractical lingerie. A bat screeched somewhere off-camera; lightning flashed even though five seconds ago that scene was indoors.

Nightbite 3: Blood Rebooted. My comfort film. Objectively dreadful. Perfect.

My phone buzzed against my wrist. I flinched, stabbed pause on the trackpad, and squinted at the notification bar. Group chat. Aaliyah had sent a video from some house party: bodies, bad strobes, her doing duckface with a bottle of something luminous.

I typed, under the video:

I: ur liver ok?

The little “Aaliyah is typing…” bubble appeared, vanished, reappeared.

A: pls, I’m networking

C: statistically speaking, that luminous blue is 40% antifreeze

Callum’s avatar: an owl with glasses. Of course.

I smiled into the dark. The only light was the laptop and the sodium-orange seep from the street outside my curtains. The flat hummed: the boiler’s faint groan, the fridge fighting for its life.

The phone buzzed again. New notification. Not the chat.

Unknown Number
🩸🩸🩸

No text. Just three neat blood drops in a row.

I stared at it for a second, then snorted. Even my scam texts were trying too hard.

I screenshotted it and dropped it into Cryptids & Chemistry.

I: new admirer

C: oh good, the murderers are emoji literate

A: !!! aesthetic

A: is that, like, minimalism marketing? “come to our show or die”? should I be flattered I didn’t get one

I: menstruation awareness month but make it ominous

C: don’t click any links they send or donate any of your organs etc etc

There weren’t any links. Just the three drops, sharp red against black.

I tapped the number. Just digits. No profile picture, no name. WhatsApp couldn’t decide if it existed.

My thumb hovered over “Block.” Felt dramatic. Also, a little cursed, like those films Callum hates where the girl deletes the warning and then the demon eats her intestines first.

“You’re not getting me with that,” I muttered to the empty room. I muted the thread instead.

Through the thin ceiling I could hear the muffled whine of the nail drill from downstairs; Mum must’ve taken a late walk-in. Midnight biab emergency. Glam Claws never slept.

I went back to Nightbite. On-screen, the vampire anti-hero climbed into a coffin in slow motion while nu-metal howled. I could recite the next line. I didn’t get that far.

The laptop screen flickered. Once. Twice. The audio stuttered like it was buffering, though I’d downloaded it years ago.

My bedside lamp blinked on and off on its own.

“Oh, piss off,” I told the universe.

The lamp steadied. The laptop sulked itself back into motion. The coffin lid slammed; the vampire whispered, “Darkness is a gift.” The usual.

My phone, on the duvet, lit up my peripheral vision. I tried to ignore it. Failed.

Unknown Number
Read 00:01

For a second, stupidly, I thought: It can see me.

Of course it said “Read.” I’d tapped into the thread. That was all. My brain was just helpfully overlaying creepiness like a filter.

I flung the phone to the far side of the bed anyway, on principle.

Outside the half-open curtains, the glow from the high street looked wrong. Too still. I slid my headphones off one ear and listened.

No scooter engines. No drunk laughter. No distant bass from the bar two doors down. Just the faint roar of the sea and a gull doing a noise like a demon being blendered.

I shifted up onto my elbows, pushed the curtain with two fingers.

Bayview High Street at 00:03 on a school night: shuttered shops, sickly streetlights, puddles. The Glam Claws sign buzzed, pink tubes flickering. Across the road, the arcade letters that still said A CA E because the R and D never got replaced.

Under the dead letters stood a boy.

He was directly under the broken A, where the light didn’t quite reach. He had no umbrella. Hood up. Hands in pockets. Not moving.

I told myself he was waiting for someone. People waited. That was normal. People stood like just… standing. Motionless. In the rain that had started needling down without me noticing.

He looked up towards our building, as if he’d heard me think. The angle of his face caught the light. Pale. Too pale.

I jerked the curtain closed and dropped back onto my pillow, heart doing a double-kick.

“Okay,” I whispered. “You’re being mental. It’s a guy at a bus stop that doesn’t have a bus.”

I picked up my phone again. No new messages. The blood drops sat there. Condescending.

I: (to chat) so

I: anyone else get goth spam

The typing bubbles pulsed as I watched.

C: yeah actually wtf

C: just 3 drops, no text?

A: rude. zero serial killers in my dms

C: maybe it’s random

C: or someone’s idea of a joke

I: it’s a bit 2016 for a joke tbh

A: ur all cowards, I would simply reply “k x”

I considered it. Three blood drops back. Or three neutral thumbs. Or an aubergine, just to confuse whoever was behind it.

I didn’t reply.

Mum knocked twice on my wall, our signal for “I’m locking up, go to sleep.” The nail drill whine clicked off. The hallway light went dark, one strip at a time, like the building was shutting its eyes.

“Night,” I called.

“Night, love,” came muffled through plaster.

I closed the laptop, shoved it down beside the bed, plugged my phone in. It lit the room for a moment, harsh and blue.

The blood emoji thread sat above everything else. I scrolled up. Just the one message. No previous. No profile picture. No nothing.

“It’s nothing,” I told myself. “It’s probably Aaliyah, bored in a bathroom queue.”

That made me feel better. It also made me a bit annoyed with her, which helped.

I set the phone face-down. The room exhaled.

Sleep didn’t land so much as creep in around the edges. Last thought: I’d tell them tomorrow, we’d figure out which little psychopath had everyone’s numbers, and that’d be that. Another stupid story we’d make fun of in the canteen.

Down on the street, a police car went past, blue lights washing the ceiling for half a second. I registered it as a colour without meaning. Turned over. Let the dark take me.

In the morning, my alarm shrieked at 7:00. I slapped at it until it shut up and lay there with that instant fog of vague dread that usually meant “double Maths.” Then the previous night unspooled: the text, the boy under the arcade sign, the lamp.

I reached for my phone. Notifications stacked up: Instagram, school app, three from Cryptids & Chemistry, one from Mum (“Breakfast in the fridge”) and, in the middle:

Unknown Number
🩸

Just one this time.

Below it, timestamped 02:14, a story notification from Kyle Davies:

kyle.dvs added to their story.

I tapped it. Spinning lights. A can of something cheap. His trainers on a curb. Caption: “Dead.”

I checked the time again. 02:14.

On the bus, half an hour later, his seat was empty.
step_outputs.7:
The rain turned the playground into a shallow, grim soup. Half the school was crowded under the thin overhang by the canteen, steaming gently in damp blazers and cheap aerosol.

I sloshed off the bus and joined the queue of misery.

“Morning,” Callum said, appearing at my elbow with his hood up and his bag zipped to his chin. His glasses were spattered. “You look like death.”

“Rude. Any mysterious suitors in your inbox?”

He hesitated. “Yeah.”

He fished his phone out, keeping it angled away from the rain. The same unknown number. The same three blood drops from last night. Underneath, a new one from 6:02 a.m.:

🩸

“Copy-paste job,” he said. “Zero effort.”

“Lazy murderers,” I said. “I also got the deluxe one-blood follow-up.”

He exhaled slowly. “Right. Great.”

We squeezed through the doors. The warm corridor smelled of wet fabric and Lynx Africa. Everyone was shaking hoods, combing hands through hair, shoving each other off radiators.

On the noticeboard, someone had Blu-Tacked a bright red poster:

COMING SOON: THE CRIMSON SERVICE
BE THE DROP THAT STARTS THE WAVE
ELITE VOLUNTEERING OPPORTUNITIES
LAUNCH ASSEMBLY TODAY

Three stylised blood droplets in a row. Neat little logo.

“Oh, for f—” I started.

“Coincidence,” Callum said, instantly, which is what he says when he’s sure something is not a coincidence.

“Okay, but that slogan,” I said. “‘Be the drop that starts the wave.’ That’s definitely how you get a plague.”

He tugged my sleeve. “Assembly. Now. I want to see how bad this is.”

Bayview High’s hall looked particularly lifeless in strip-light grey. The stage curtains were a faded maroon that somehow made everything else seem beige. We filed in by tutor group, wet trainers squeaking, chairs scraping.

Aaliyah slid into the seat behind me, smelling faintly of coconut and regret. “Darling,” she hissed. “Why did no one tell me there’d be a goth rebrand? Crimson Service? Very cult. I approve.”

“You should be in bed,” I whispered. “You look like your mascara’s filed for divorce.”

“It’s a look,” she said. “So. Emojis?”

“You got one?” I twisted round.

“No.” She pouted. “Do I not look like prime murder material? I’m offended.”

“Count your blessings,” Callum muttered.

Up front, Headteacher Dr Lewis did her microphone tap. “Good morning, Bayview.”

A ragged “morning” came back.

“Today, I’m very excited to introduce a new initiative in partnership with some wonderful organisations.” She did the smile she always did when grant money was involved. “This is a chance for some of our students to really shine. To tell you more, please welcome our Head Girl, Eleanor Price.”

Polite clapping. Eleanor walked on stage like the concept of posture had hired a PR team. Blazer immaculate, badge straight, hair smooth. She smiled, pressed the clicker.

Slide one: THE CRIMSON SERVICE. Three drops. Same neat logo as the poster. Same as my phone, minus the smugness.

“Thank you, Dr Lewis,” Eleanor began. Clear, warm voice. “The Crimson Service is an elite volunteering and leadership programme, unique to Bayview. We’ve been selected to pilot this in partnership with several important medical charities.”

The next slide: pictures of happy people in lab coats. Stock photo heaven.

“Members of Crimson Service,” she said, “will have the chance to support lifesaving research and treatments. You’ll gain experience that will stand out on future applications and show that you are the kind of person who doesn’t just talk about making a difference, but does it.”

“Oh my God,” Aaliyah whispered. “She rehearsed that in the mirror for three years.”

I watched the slides. Looked harmless enough if you stripped away the colour red. Bullets about “community impact,” “leadership workshops,” “exclusive opportunities.” The logo hovered in the corner like a tooth.

Eleanor clicked again. “Selection is by invitation only. We have looked carefully at attendance, grades, behaviour, and involvement in school life. If you are approached today, please consider it carefully. Not everyone will have this chance.”

Flattering poison.

My phone buzzed once against my leg.

I risked a glance. Not a text; Cryptids & Chemistry.

C: this screams organ harvesting

I bit back a laugh.

Aaliyah poked me. She held up her own phone. No new anything. Her drawn-on winged eyeliner twitched.

Dr Lewis wrapped it up with something about “opportunity” and “pride.” Dismissal row by row.

As we shuffled out, Eleanor and two prefects stood strategically by the doors, holding crisp white envelopes. She handed them out with laser accuracy.

“Thank you, Benjamin. We’d love to have you on board.”

“Amelia, this would be perfect for you.”

Her eyes flitted over faces, cross-checking some internal list.

Then: “Imogen.”

I nearly kept walking, assuming there was another Imogen. There isn’t.

She stepped in front of me, blocking the flow, and handed me an envelope. “We’ve identified you as a strong candidate,” she said, with that politician smile. “Have a read.”

“Oh,” I said. Eloquence. “Thanks.”

Callum was behind me. She pressed one into his hand too. “We’ve had our eye on you for a while,” she said.

“That’s not creepy at all,” he blurted. She pretended not to hear.

Aaliyah reached out hopefully as we moved past.

Eleanor’s smile didn’t widen. “We’ll be in touch,” she said. No envelope.

We spilled into the corridor.

“I hate her,” Aaliyah announced. “On principle. Give that here.” She snatched my envelope, tore it open.

“Aaliyah—”

“Relax, your destiny awaits.” She unfolded the letter, read aloud in a pompous voice as we walked.

“Dear Imogen, based on your exemplary potential, you have been selected for consideration for the Crimson Service. This is an exclusive opportunity to demonstrate leadership through discreet participation in life-saving donation programmes…”

She slowed. “…‘discreet participation’?”

“Sexy,” I said.

“There’s an information meeting this evening, Lab 3, seven p.m. Bring this letter and an open mind,” Aaliyah finished. She wrinkled her nose. “Corporate cult vibes. I love it.”

Callum had his open too. “Same wording,” he said. “Plus a health questionnaire. ‘Have you ever fainted giving blood? Are you currently on any medications?’”

“They’re just filtering out wimps,” Aaliyah said.

“It’s… targeted,” he said.

We stopped by the stairs. The corridor surged past us.

“So. Honest thoughts,” I said. “On a scale from school charity scam to full occult sacrifice?”

“It’s a scheme,” Callum said. “They get free student labour, maybe some PR. The emoji thing is… tacky. Probably someone thought it was clever branding.”

“But the texts came before they announced this,” I said. “And not to everyone. And you got them.”

He chewed his lip. “Correlation is not causation.”

“You’re wobbling,” I said.

“I’m… open to data,” he said.

Aaliyah snatched both letters and tucked them under her arm. “We’re going,” she said. “Obviously.”

“You didn’t even get invited,” I said.

“Rude. I’m your emotional support extrovert,” she said. “And if it is a cult, they always need someone to replace the High Priestess when she trips.”

“Looking forward to that transition plan,” I said. “Assuming this isn’t just Eleanor’s personal LinkedIn farm.”

As we argued, someone brushed past us. Mr Beckwith, clutching a stack of exercise books and a mug that said “World’s Okayest Teacher.” His cardigan sleeve was pushed up; a strip of red Crimson Service lanyard peeked out from under it.

“Sir,” I called. “What’s Crimson Service? Actually?”

He jumped slightly, slopped coffee on his hand. “Oh, ah, Imogen, hi. It’s—well, it’s all in the letter, right? Fantastic opportunity. Great partners. Very safe. Very controlled. Right.”

“You’re involved?” Callum asked.

“Just as staff liaison,” he said too fast. “Science, blood, that sort of thing.”

“‘That sort of thing,’” I repeated.

“Yes. Anyway, bell’s about to go.” He hurried off, almost colliding with the janitor.

We watched him go.

“Very safe,” Aaliyah mimicked. “Very controlled. Right. He’s hiding a basement of bodies.”

“He can’t even control his mug,” I said. “If this is his cult, we’re fine.”

The bell shrieked.

In Maths, I couldn’t focus. The Crimson logo floated behind the equations. At break, two Year 10s compared their envelopes near the vending machine like trading cards. By lunch, whispers had started:

“Only top sets got picked.”

“My mate who does rugby got one.”

“Eleanor’s favourites, innit.”

By the lockers, a girl from my Science class rolled up her sleeve to show a small square of white plaster in the crook of her elbow. “Mum took me to give blood last week,” she said proudly. “I put it on my form. Got Crimson invite this morning. Told you it mattered.”

Little patterns.

In the canteen queue, I checked my phone under the table. New school app message about the Crimson launch. Mum asking if she should save me a plate. A DM from an old primary school friend I never answered.

And from Unknown Number, time-stamped 10:26:

Attendance appreciated.
🩸

My chest felt tight for a second.

I typed back: who is this

The three dots appeared. Blinked. Vanished.

“Immy?” Callum nudged me. “Earth to Clarke. You’re holding up the line.”

“Oh.” I grabbed a tray. “Sorry. Just being recruited into the blood Illuminati.”

“You’re not actually going, right?” he whispered.

“Obviously we’re going,” Aaliyah said from behind him. “If they’re giving out free tote bags, I’m taking two.”

I looked at the message again. The neat single drop.

If it was Aaliyah, she was committing to the bit. If it was Eleanor somehow, we’d have material for weeks. If it was neither, and it knew my name and timetable and the way my stomach dipped reading it—

“Seven o’clock,” I said. “Lab 3. We go. We see. If it’s boring, we leave.”

“And if it isn’t?” Callum asked.

“Then,” I said, “we at least get a good story.”

The three dots blinked once more on my screen, then disappeared for good.
step_outputs.8:
By seven o’clock the rain had given up and left everything slick. The science block looked wrong in the dusk, all its windows black squares instead of sleepy rectangles full of bored teenagers.

“This is how every Netflix series starts,” Callum said, shoving his hands deeper in his pockets. “Season one: ironic kids. Season two: trauma.”

“Season three: they recast me with someone taller,” Aaliyah said. “Come on, the door’s open.”

It was. Lab 3’s yellow strip of light cut across the corridor.

Inside, someone had tried very hard.

The blinds were down. The harsh strip-lights had been swapped for softer lamps. At the front, a laptop and projector cast the Crimson Service logo huge on the board. Three perfect blood drops over an abstract wave.

Chairs were set out in neat rows. A tray of supermarket biscuits sweated under cling film. Bottles of water formed a sterile little battalion.

About a dozen students sat scattered: a couple of rugby boys, two top-set girls with revision cards in their laps, Jaya from my Chemistry class twisting a bracelet off her wrist and on again, Kyle’s mate Liam hunched low.

No Kyle.

“Welcome,” Eleanor said from the front, as if we’d walked into a spa instead of a lab that usually smelt of Bunsen gas and despair.

She clocked us. Her gaze paused a fraction too long on Aaliyah, who technically wasn’t invited.

“Please find a seat,” she said. “We’ll start in just a moment.”

Aaliyah gave her a wide, fake-innocent smile and chose the middle row. I sat beside her; Callum took the aisle, like he wanted a clear escape route.

Mr Beckwith hovered near the whiteboard, shuffling papers. He looked like he wanted to be invisible.

At the back of the room stood the boy from under the arcade sign.

Same pale skin, same too-straight posture, hood down now. In the forgiving lamplight, he looked less horror-film and more…annoyed. Hands in the pockets of his dark jacket, shoulders slightly hunched away from everyone.

His eyes flicked over us and moved on, as if he was counting.

A flicker of recognition jolted through me. My brain supplied: stalker. Then: bus stop. Then: you are being ridiculous.

“Thank you all for coming,” Eleanor began. “Firstly, congratulations. You’re here because we see something in you. Not just grades, but character.”

Aaliyah mouthed “vomit” at me.

“The Crimson Service,” Eleanor went on, clicking to a slide of smiling volunteers in red T-shirts, “is about leadership through action. We’re partnering with exciting medical charities to support life-saving work. That includes awareness campaigns, events, and for some of you, the opportunity to participate directly in donation programmes.”

The word hung.

“Donation,” Jaya repeated quietly.

“Blood,” one of the rugby lads said, too loud.

Eleanor smiled. “Some programmes do involve giving blood, yes. Completely voluntary, fully supervised. Many of you will be eligible at seventeen. Until then, there are other roles.”

“So we’re being recruited as walking blood bags,” Aaliyah whispered. “Iconic.”

I elbowed her.

Beckwith stepped forward. “It’s all properly regulated,” he said. “You’ll have information, consent forms for parents. Nothing happens without, um, proper procedure.”

The pale boy at the back shifted, as if that phrasing irritated him.

Why was he here? He wasn’t in uniform. No lanyard.

“Is that your cousin?” Aaliyah whispered. “He’s got your playlist.”

“I’ve never seen him in my life,” I whispered back.

Eleanor clicked to another slide: bullet points about “skills,” “training,” “references.” She talked smoothly, every sentence polished.

I half-listened, half-watched the room.

The kids here weren’t random. Most were the ones teachers smiled at in corridors. The reliable ones. The “you’ll go far” nods.

I caught Callum’s profile: jaw tight, eyes flicking between the biscuits and the exits.

On the table near the door sat a stack of forms and a plastic box of pens. On the forms, I could see the words Health Questionnaire in bold.

“Some of our partners,” Eleanor said, “work in experimental therapies. They need carefully screened young donors. People like you, strong and prepared to do more than just repost petitions.”

That made something in me bristle. Like the implicit: If you say no, you’re lazy.

Jaya raised her hand. “Is this, like… the NHS?”

“A range of organisations,” Eleanor said. “Some public, some private. All thoroughly vetted.”

I glanced at Beckwith. Sweat at his hairline. He nodded along like he was being paid per nod.

A hand went up at the back. Not rugby. The pale boy.

“Yes?” Eleanor said.

He stepped forward a bit. His voice, when it came, was low and flat. Local accent blurred around the edges.

“You should emphasise it’s voluntary,” he said. “Strongly. And that no one will be penalised for declining.”

Something taut flashed behind Eleanor’s eyes. “Of course,” she said. “As Mr Beckwith said. Voluntary.”

“But selective,” the boy said. “Right.”

It wasn’t really a question.

“Thank you, Leon,” Beckwith said quickly. “Everyone, this is Leon Hart. He’s one of our external coordinators. He’s, er, here to help ensure best practice.”

Leon. Not Arcade Boy. Leon Hart.

He looked about seventeen. I knew he wasn’t a student—I would’ve noticed. His skin had that odd undertone of someone who’d never seen the sun in person, only on TV.

“Hi,” Aaliyah whispered under her breath. “I’m uncomfortable and intrigued.”

“Later we’ll have a chance for questions one-to-one,” Eleanor said, reclaiming the rhythm. “For now, please take a form. If you’re interested, fill in the basic details. We’ll send more information home.”

Chairs creaked as people shuffled up. The biscuits were finally breached.

Aaliyah tugged me up with her. “Come on,” she said. “We’ll get data.”

“Just don’t sign my life away,” I said.

At the table, Eleanor smiled directly at me. “Imogen,” she said, like she enjoyed saying names. “I’m glad you came. People like you are exactly who we need.”

“People like me,” I echoed.

“Bright. Underutilised. Principled.” She slid a form towards me. “We’re offering a chance to turn that into something concrete.”

“I like being abstract,” I said. “Less paperwork.”

Her smile didn’t flicker. “Have a read.”

Callum took his with two fingers, as if it might bite. Aaliyah reached for one; Eleanor’s hand paused.

“This is invitation-only,” she said.

“She’s with me,” I said before I could stop myself.

Eleanor considered that. “You can sit in,” she said to Aaliyah. “Observe. For now.”

Power. Tiny, stupid power, in an after-hours lab with bad biscuits. I suddenly understood why she liked this.

We sat again. I scanned the form.

Name. Date of birth. Medical conditions. “Have you ever experienced dizziness or fainting after blood tests or injections?” “Are you currently taking any medication?” “Do you have any conditions that affect your blood?”

The last question: “Would you be willing to be contacted about future donation opportunities and research participation?”

I left it blank.

Jaya’s pen hovered at that line too. Her leg jiggled under the table.

At the front, Leon leant against the bench, arms folded. Under the too-soft lights, his eyes looked almost black. When someone laughed at the “donation” line, he didn’t.

Eleanor wrapped up. “If you’d like to proceed, leave your form with us before you go. If you’re unsure, take it home. We’ll follow up.”

Follow up. Like debt.

As people began to stand, I saw Jaya sway.

Her hand went to her forehead. The pen skittered from her fingers.

Leon was there before she could fall, crossing the space in a blur so fast my brain stumbled. One second against the bench; the next, beside her, steadying her elbow.

It might’ve been adrenaline. I might’ve imagined the speed.

“Probably just hot,” Eleanor said quickly. “Do you need some air?”

“I’m fine,” Jaya mumbled, cheeks flushing. “Didn’t eat.”

“Sit,” Leon said, voice low. He guided her back into the chair with a care that didn’t match his bored expression.

Up close, I saw his fingers against her wrist. His jaw clenched once, hard.

He looked up. Our eyes met. Something unreadable passed there: surprise at being seen.

I looked away first.

“Nothing to worry about,” Beckwith said too cheerfully from the front. “Happens all the time in labs. Right, grab a biscuit on your way out, don’t forget your consent forms.”

Slowly, like they were waking from a spell, people drifted to the table. Some left forms, some folded them into blazer pockets.

We hung back until the crush thinned.

“Thoughts?” Aaliyah said brightly.

“Ten out of ten red flags,” Callum said. “That questionnaire is more invasive than my GP. And who ‘follows up’ on voluntary stuff like that?”

“I’m giving it a solid seven for production values,” Aaliyah said. “Minus three for no snacks that aren’t beige.”

Leon was by the door now, talking quietly to Beckwith. Eleanor collected forms into a folder with practised hands.

I took a step towards the bin with my blank one.

My phone buzzed.

Unknown Number
Pending.
🩸🩸

A cold ribbon unrolled along my spine.

“Immy?” Callum asked.

I showed them.

Aaliyah’s joking smile slipped. “Okay, that’s…” She glanced at Eleanor. “She’s sending those, right? This is, like, her weird branding?”

“How would she have my number?” I said. “And why not just say it to my face?”

“Well, she likes drama,” Aaliyah said, recovering. “Queen of ominous stationery.”

“You can always decline,” Callum said. “They said we can say no.”

I looked up. Eleanor was watching us. Not with a glare, not with friendliness. Assessing.

Leon’s gaze flicked from her to me to my phone. His mouth tightened, just a fraction.

“Yeah,” I said, folding the form in half, then in half again. “They said.”

We left it on the table. Walked out into the corridor that suddenly felt too long.

Behind us, the door clicked shut.
step_outputs.9:
Kyle wasn’t in form the next morning.

His tutor, Mr Hughes, read out “authorised absence” like it was a spell that made concern optional. Liam muttered something about “must’ve crashed after Sam’s,” then went quiet when he saw no one was laughing.

By break, someone said Kyle hadn’t been on Insta since that 2 a.m. story. Someone else said his mum had put in the year group WhatsApp that he’d “gone to stay with family.” Both could be true. Neither made my stomach stop knotting.

My phone buzzed in my blazer pocket three times during double English. I ignored it until Mrs Bray turned to the board.

From: Unknown Number
Non-compliance complicates things.
🩸🩸🩸

I stared at the words until they blurred. When I blinked, I could almost see Eleanor’s neat handwriting around them.

I jammed the phone face-down under my copy of Macbeth. On the page, Lady M was doing her “screw your courage to the sticking-place” bit. I was not feeling especially screwed or courageous.

At lunch, Callum and Aaliyah cornered me by the vending machines.

“New creepy fan mail?” Aaliyah asked. “Because I got nothing and I’m starting to think they don’t value me.”

I showed them.

Callum swore under his breath. “Okay. That’s… specific.”

“Non-compliance,” Aaliyah read. “That’s the least sexy sext ever.”

“It’s not funny,” Callum said. “They’re tracking who signs up.”

“I am aware it’s not funny,” she said. “I’m choosing to fight dread with camp.”

“Look at the timing,” I said. “We didn’t hand in forms. Now this.”

“They could’ve seen,” Callum said. “Eleanor was right there. But why anonymous? Why not send a follow-up email like a normal tyrant?”

“Because anonymous is scarier,” Aaliyah said quietly.

We turned, in unison, to the Crimson Service posters plastered along the corridor. Those little blood drops now looked less like cheerful branding, more like tally marks.

“Maybe it’s a prank,” I said, forcing the words out. “Like, someone in on it. Testing commitment. Annoying, but not… you know.”

“A prank that references compliance?” Callum said. “That’s not Year 10-level banter.”

“Sixth-form boys enjoy the illusion of power,” Aaliyah said. “Trust me, I’ve done plays with them.”

In Science, Beckwith was more distracted than usual. He spilled copper sulfate on the floor, laughed too loudly, wrote “DONNER” on the board instead of “DONOR” and scrubbed it out like it had personally betrayed him.

When the bell went, I hung back.

“Sir,” I said. “Quick question.”

He flinched like I’d thrown something.

“Yes, Imogen?”

“The Crimson forms,” I said. “Who sees them? Who sends…communications?”

“Communications?”

“You know.” I tried to sound casual. “Texts. Follow-ups.”

He rubbed his forehead. “All official contact goes through the school channels. Letters home. The app. Nothing… nothing personal, right? That would be inappropriate.”

“So you haven’t been texting me emojis from a burner?” I said.

His eyes widened behind his glasses. “What? No, of course not.”

“Okay. Just checking.” I watched his reaction like Callum would. “And if someone was?”

“I—if there’s something odd, you should bring it to Dr Lewis,” he said. “Or me. But it’s probably just someone having a laugh. Don’t let people spook you.”

“I’m not spooked,” I lied. “It’s just… weird.”

He hesitated. “If you’re uncomfortable, you don’t have to be involved with Crimson. It’s voluntary.”

“That’s not what the texts say,” I said before I could stop myself.

“What texts?” he asked, too quickly.

We stared at each other for a beat. He broke first.

“I have a meeting,” he said. “Hand in your homework Thursday. Right.”

I stepped out into the corridor, pulse up.

Callum and Aaliyah were waiting by the stairs.

“Well?” Aaliyah said. “Is Sir our mysterious dom?”

“No,” I said. “He’s terrified of everything. Including me and the word ‘text.’”

“So either he knows and can’t say,” Callum said, “or he doesn’t and someone’s freelancing with Crimson branding.”

“Either way,” I said, “I don’t like that I qualify as ‘non-compliant’ on anyone’s spreadsheet.”

We headed towards the lockers. A group of Year 10s were crowded around one boy, Tom, who was rolling up his sleeve to show off a little white plaster in the crook of his elbow.

“Gave blood with my mum last week,” he said. “Crimson Service invited me this morning. Boom.”

“Look at you, human Capri-Sun,” someone said.

I exchanged a look with Callum.

“Pattern,” he murmured.

“Strong, healthy, high attendance, no meds,” Aaliyah recited. “Like you,” she nudged me. “Like you,” she nudged him. “Like me, except they didn’t pick me, the cowards.”

“Maybe they think you’d bite back,” I said.

“Accurate,” she said.

On the way out of school, I saw him again. Leon, by the gate, talking low to Beckwith.

“…have to be clearer,” he was saying. “Voluntary. No pressure. That’s the agreement.”

“They know that,” Beckwith muttered. “Mostly.”

Leon’s eyes tracked a group of kids walking past, flicking down to bandages, up to faces. Assessing.

Then his gaze landed on me.

I made the mistake of holding it. His eyes were dark, almost black in the grey afternoon, and tired in a way that didn’t match seventeen.

He looked away first this time. That made me feel both victorious and stupid.

“I’m going to follow him,” I said as we turned onto the high street.

“I’m going home,” Callum said. “I’m going to eat toast and pretend we live in a benevolent universe.”

Aaliyah stretched her arms. “I have rehearsal. Also I’m one detention away from indefinite grounding. But you two enjoy your stalking date.”

“Stalking observation,” I said. “For science.”

Callum groaned, but he came with me.

We hung back as Leon walked down towards the seafront. He moved like he knew the town without looking. Past the chippy, past the vape shop, towards the row of shuttered arcades.

“You are aware we’re bad at this,” Callum whispered.

“Yes,” I said. “But he doesn’t know that.”

Leon stopped briefly outside the old A CA E, looking up at the dead letters, then crossed to the other side where a mobile blood donation van was parked. Its side read GIVE BLOOD, SAVE LIVES in cheerful NHS font.

He didn’t go in. He leaned against a lamppost opposite, watching the door like he was on break.

“Maybe he’s just waiting for someone,” Callum said.

“In front of a blood van,” I said. “With my life, yes.”

We ducked into the doorway of the Pound Saver two shops down.

“Okay,” Callum said, “I will admit: the aesthetics are damning.”

The van door opened; a woman in scrubs stepped out, smoking. Leon said something to her. She laughed, swatted his arm, went back inside.

“Is he flirting with the blood nurse?” I whispered.

“Professional networking,” Callum said.

Leon checked his phone. His face tightened. He looked straight over at us.

I flinched back into the doorway.

“He saw us,” I hissed.

“Of course he saw us,” Callum said. “We’re literally in a doorway staring at him like Victorian ghosts.”

When I risked another glance, Leon was gone.

My heart thumped. “Okay. Fine. That’s fine.”

We walked home the long way, skirting the seafront. The air tasted of salt and chips and something metallic underneath, or maybe that was my brain overcommitting.

“I don’t like this,” Callum said. “I like patterns. I don’t like being in one.”

We parted at the corner near my building.

“Text me if you get anything,” he said.

“If I get murdered, I’ll let you know,” I said.

On the stairs up to our flat, the Glam Claws sign buzzed at my back. Mum was in the kitchen, in the pink polo with GLAM CREW on it, counting coins out of the tip jar.

“You’re late,” she said without looking up.

“Got trapped in a leadership seminar,” I said, dropping my bag. “You know how it is.”

“Did they give you a lanyard?” she asked warily.

“Not yet,” I said. “I think that’s stage two of the indoctrination.”

She snorted. “As long as you’re not signing up to work for free for the posh kids.”

“Would I do that?” I asked.

“Yes,” she said. “If they made it sound intellectual.”

Later, in my room, I sat cross-legged on my bed with the Crimson leaflet. The words blurred. “Elite.” “Opportunity.” “Impact.”

My phone lay beside it, black screen.

I opened a new note and started writing names.

People with plasters. People with invites. People with texts.

Immy (🩸🩸🩸 + 🩸 + “Non-compliance…”)
Callum (🩸🩸🩸)
Jaya (invite + nearly fainted)
Tom (blood donor + invite)
Kyle (🩸? last story 2:14 / now MIA)
Others from Lab 3.

It was messier than any conspiracy thread on Callum’s forums. But there was something there.

My phone buzzed.

My heart jumped so hard I nearly threw it.

Unknown Number
Next steps soon.
🩸🩸🩸

I swallowed. Fingers hovering.

I typed: stop messaging me

Dots appeared. Disappeared.

Then nothing.

I set the phone down, palms sweaty.

Somewhere below, the shop’s shutter rattled down. The flat seemed to contract, quieter, as if listening.

I lay back, staring at the crack in the ceiling that looked like a river on a map.

It was just some overachieving psycho with a bulk-text app and a God complex.

It had to be.

On the bus the next morning, Kyle’s seat was still empty. Liam scrolled through his phone, jaw clenched.

“He hasn’t opened my snaps,” he muttered to no one.

I checked Cryptids & Chemistry.

C: kyle’s still not online?

A: maybe his mum hid his phone, mine did that once, I almost chewed through the door

C: the school app says “authorised”

I: “authorised” by who tho

As if on cue, another notification from Unknown Number slid down over our thread.

Tonight.
Midnight.
Lab 3.
🩸🩸🩸

The bus shuddered over a pothole. No one else seemed to feel it.
step_outputs.10:
The message sat there like a dare.

Tonight.
Midnight.
Lab 3.
🩸🩸🩸

The bus juddered, kids shouted, someone in the back played tinny drill out loud. I stared at the words as if they might rearrange into something sensible.

When I looked up, I caught Leon standing at the school gate as we pulled in, hood up despite the half-hearted sun. He wasn’t looking at me; he was scanning faces as people trudged past, like he was counting again. Like the text had gone to a specific list and he was checking who’d show.

“Don’t,” Callum said, sliding into the seat beside me. He’d clearly seen my expression.

“I didn’t say anything,” I said.

“You’re doing your ‘tempted to do something obviously stupid’ face.”

A notification slid down over the last one.

C: we’re not actually going to midnight science, right?

I: define “we”

A: babes if there’s a midnight lab cult and we *don’t* go, I’ll have to move schools for shame

I: there it is

Callum rubbed his eyes. “If we go, we tell someone. An adult. A real one. Dr Lewis. Or your mum. Or the police.”

“And say what?” I asked. “‘Hi, some emojis invited me to a blood party, can you arrest the font?’”

He grimaced. “Fine. We gather data. Then maybe police font.”

We drifted through the day, everything taking on that over-sharp edge it gets when you’re counting hours to something you’re pretending not to be scared of.

In Chemistry, I tried to catch Jaya’s eye. She looked wan, a faded version of her usual precise self. The plaster on her elbow was gone, replaced by a faint yellow bruise.

“You okay?” I whispered as Beckwith banged on about covalent bonds.

She shrugged. “Just tired. My mum made a big deal out of the donation. Keeps telling people at church. I feel like I should have a medal or something.”

“Did you get any Crimson stuff after?”

“Yeah,” she said. “They sent another questionnaire. And that meeting. I’m not sure.” She hesitated. “Did you get the weird texts?”

“Maybe,” I said. “Probably spam.”

“Yeah,” she said. “Probably.”

At lunch, I shoved chips around my tray.

“Option A,” I said. “We go tonight, it’s dramatic and boring, we go home and roast it.”

“Option B,” Callum said, “we go and it’s not boring, and we die.”

“Option C,” Aaliyah said, “we don’t go, and spend the rest of our lives wondering if there was a hot vampire and we missed him.”

“There is a hot vampire,” I said. “He works nights and glowers.”

“So we’re agreed,” she said. “Option Chaos.”

Callum inhaled. “If anything looks seriously off, we film. And we run. And we call actual authorities. We do not split up. We do not drink anything they hand us. We do not sign anything.”

“Okay, Dad,” Aaliyah said.

“Call me when you’re not in a ditch,” he said.

By seven, my mum was in her pyjamas on the sofa, watching a dating show where everyone had veneers and unresolved issues.

“Science club thing,” I said, hovering by the door with my bag.

She raised an eyebrow. “Since when do you go to science club?”

“Since there’s snacks,” I said. “I’ll be back by ten.”

“If you end up in McDonald’s, text me,” she said. “And don’t sign up for anything unpaid past five p.m.”

“Would I do that?” I asked.

“Yes,” she said.

Outside, the evening had that flat blue light that makes everything look like a set. Callum and Aaliyah were waiting by the corner shop; Aaliyah had swapped her blazer for a massive tartan coat like a villain.

“You told your mum?” Callum asked.

“Partial truth,” I said. “You?”

“My nan thinks I’m at your house,” he said. “So don’t die, it ruins my alibi.”

We slipped round the back of school, over the grass that tried and failed to be a field. A side door to the science block was cracked open.

“No,” Callum whispered. “That’s too on-the-nose. At least make us pick a lock.”

Inside, the corridors were lit by emergency strips, greenish and thin. Our footsteps echoed. Posters for exam prep and safe internet use glowed faintly.

Lab 3’s door was shut. Light seeped underneath.

“Listen,” Aaliyah whispered.

Muffled voices. Eleanor’s, crisp. Beckwith’s, fluttery. Another tone, flat: Leon.

I gently pushed the door. It opened a few inches. Enough.

Inside, about ten students—fewer than this morning—sat in a half-circle. No biscuits. No slides. Just clipboards and a steel trolley with sealed packs, tubes, wipes. Clinical.

Eleanor stood by the trolley. Beckwith hovered. Leon leaned against the far bench, arms folded.

“…completely confidential,” Eleanor was saying. “Your contribution tonight will kickstart something incredible.”

“This is too much,” Beckwith said, low. “We should wait for the full sign-off.”

“We have it,” Eleanor said, in that tone that brooked no argument. “If we don’t move now, we lose the slot, and do you want to explain that to our partners?”

I flinched when I saw Kyle.

He was there. Sat at the end of the row, hoodie sleeves shoved up. He looked wrong. Not hurt, not chained, just… hollow. Eyes unfocused, skin a waxy shade that didn’t suit a seventeen-year-old who played football.

“Immy,” Callum breathe-whispered behind me. “He’s here. He’s fine.”

He didn’t look fine.

“Alright,” Eleanor said, louder now. “Those of you doing preliminary sampling, we’ll start with small draws. Purely diagnostic. If at any point you feel unwell, we stop. You’re in control.”

She said it like a script, like a box to tick.

Leon’s jaw clenched. “Emphasise,” he said. “They can say no. No one’s obliged to be here. You invited them, you didn’t summon them.”

A boy laughed. “Sounds like vampires.”

Leon’s gaze snapped to him so fast it was almost funny. Almost.

I pushed the door another inch. It creaked.

Leon’s head turned. Our eyes met through the gap.

He moved.

One second he was by the bench. The next he was at the door, hand flat against it, stopping it opening further, stopping it slamming shut.

I choked on my breath.

“For someone trying not to get caught sneaking,” he murmured, “you’re terrible at doors.”

Up close, his eyes weren’t black. Dark brown, almost amber where the light caught. They looked tired. And, annoyingly, a bit concerned.

“You shouldn’t be here,” he said.

“Neither should you,” I shot back automatically, then realised that made no sense.

“Is someone out there?” Eleanor called.

Leon didn’t look away from me. “Just me,” he said. “Door was ajar.”

A beat. Then she went back to her script.

Callum tried to pull me back. Aaliyah leaned over his shoulder, eyes huge.

Leon’s voice dropped. “Are you invited?”

“Clearly not,” I whispered. “I like crashing.”

“This isn’t…” He exhaled. “Go home.”

“Are you draining them?” I heard myself say. “Like Kyle. Is that why he looks like extra in a zombie film?”

His expression did something complicated. “I’m not,” he said. “I’m here to stop worse.”

“Doing amazing so far,” I muttered.

He glanced over his shoulder at the room. “This part is legit,” he said. “Samples. Minimal. There’s a bigger problem.”

“Like the texts?” I asked.

His eyes narrowed. “What texts?”

I realised I’d given something away he hadn’t known. My stomach swooped.

“You really don’t know?” I said.

“I wouldn’t ask if I did,” he said. “What texts?”

Inside, I heard Eleanor say, “Kyle, you’re up first, if you’re ready.”

He moved like a puppet, took the chair by the trolley. Beckwith fumbled a tourniquet.

Leon swore under his breath, a language that wasn’t English, then pushed the door fully open, stepping through before I could decide whether to follow.

“Stop,” he said, louder now. “You’re not drawing that much from him. He’s already borderline.”

Eleanor’s smile went cool. “He agreed.”

“He’s had three sessions in as many days,” Leon said. “He’s not baseline. He’s supply.”

The word hung.

Beckwith froze, needle halfway uncapped.

“What’s going on?” one of the lads asked.

“It’s fine,” Eleanor said. “Leon is being overcautious.”

Leon looked at the faces in the room, at us in the doorway. Something in him crunched.

“Everyone leave,” he said. “Now.”

Nobody moved.

“This is ridiculous,” Eleanor said. “You are a consultant, not in charge.”

“I’m the one answerable if this crosses a line,” he said. His voice had dropped into something older, heavier.

For a second, maybe three, something in his expression slipped. The lamplight hit his teeth. And there, between the polite white, a flash of too sharp.

Gasps. Nervous laughter. Beckwith whispered, “Leon, for God’s sake.”

“Vampires,” the rugby boy said. Trying it out.

“Leon,” I said, without meaning to, “the texts.”

He turned his head slightly towards me. “This isn’t me,” he said. There was more in it than denial. Offence.

My pulse pounded. “Okay,” I said. “Then who thinks they can schedule me for a midnight science sacrifice?”

“What?” Eleanor snapped. “No one is sacrificing anyone.”

I pulled my phone from my pocket, thumb shaking, flicked to the messages, held it up. “Then explain this,” I said.

Tonight. Midnight. Lab 3. 🩸🩸🩸

Beckwith made a strangled noise.

“That is not from us,” Leon said. No hesitation.

“Then who?” Callum asked from behind me.

Silence, for a beat too long.

Eleanor’s gaze flicked to my screen. Something like annoyance crossed her face before she smoothed it out.

“Probably a joke,” she said. “Someone being dramatic. We can look into it.”

Leon took a step towards her. “You didn’t authorise that?”

“I do not use emojis,” she said, with faint contempt. “If I wanted you here, Imogen, I’d have asked you properly.”

It wasn’t a denial, not really.

We stood there in a stupid tableau: me with my phone up like a crucifix, Leon coiled, Beckwith sweating, Eleanor poised, Kyle pale.

“Everyone out,” Leon said again, quieter. “We’re done for tonight.”

“Leon,” Eleanor started.

“You want to explain to them what you are?” he asked, just as quiet.

The air in the room seemed to thicken.

Eleanor’s jaw flexed. She looked at the students. She calculated.

“Fine,” she said. “Thank you all so much for your interest. We’ll be in touch with next steps via the usual channels. Please don’t share any details yet; we want to make a proper announcement.”

Half-dismissal, half-command.

The kids filed out, confused, muttering. Kyle moved slower, like he was wading through something heavy. As he passed me, I caught a whiff of antiseptic and something metallic.

“You okay?” I whispered.

He blinked at me as if I was a light too bright. Didn’t answer. Kept going.

When the last of them had gone, Leon shut the door.

“Right,” he said. “Tell me everything about those texts.”

And suddenly it was just us in Lab 3: the ethical vampire, the guilty teacher, the power-hungry prefect, and three idiots who should’ve stayed home.
step_outputs.11:
Leon didn’t sit. He perched on the edge of a lab stool like he might lose patience and leave mid-sentence.

“Start from the top,” he said. “What messages, what times, who got them.”

His voice wasn’t loud, but it had weight. Beckwith hovered by the trolley, twisting a wipe packet in his hands until it split.

I held onto my sarcasm like it was buoyant. “I thought you didn’t do emojis.”

“I don’t,” Leon said. “Because I’m not twelve. Talk.”

So I did. Because being told off by a maybe-vampire in a school lab was somehow less scary than the thought of someone unknown planning me like a meeting.

“The first one came Sunday night,” I said. “00:01. Three drops, no text. Callum got it too. Some others. Then single drops, then ‘Non-compliance complicates things.’ Then the invite for tonight. All from the same unknown number.”

“And those went to Crimson candidates?” Leon asked.

“As far as I can tell,” Callum said. He’d got his notes app out. Of course he had. “I’ve got four confirmed: me, Immy, Tom, this Year 10 from my bus. Same pattern.”

“The language,” Eleanor said, stiff. “Reads like a silly prank. ‘Non-compliance.’ No one associated with Crimson would be so…on the nose.”

“That’s your objection?” I said. “The phrasing?”

Her eyes flashed. “If I wanted leverage, I wouldn’t send unsanctioned messages from an unregistered number. I’d talk to you.”

“Comforting,” I muttered.

Beckwith cleared his throat. “Right, so, hypothetically,” he said, “if someone is using that, ah, imagery, without authorisation, that’s a breach. Of guidelines.”

Leon looked tired. “It’s more than a breach of guidelines. It’s stupid and dangerous.”

“Dangerous how, exactly?” Aaliyah asked. “Beyond the obvious ‘threatening children’ thing. And the anaemia.”

Leon hesitated. “You already know too much.”

“Understatement of the century,” Callum said.

Leon swore under his breath again. “Fine,” he said. “Short version: We don’t hunt. Not here. Not like that. We have agreements. Legal-ish. Monitored donations only, from informed adults. School programme was a pilot. Limited, voluntary. It was never supposed to escalate.”

“And yet,” I said, gesturing at the room, the trolley, the echoes. “We have Kyle: the trilogy.”

“I’ve been arguing with her,” Leon said, tipping his head at Eleanor. “She’s keen to impress. Our…contact is impatient. And someone’s taken it on themselves to speed up recruitment.”

“I am following the framework I was given,” Eleanor snapped. “You asked for reliable sources. I identified them. Efficiently.”

“You also started planning midnight sessions without telling me,” Leon said. “And someone is sending threats to kids who haven’t signed. That’s not efficient. That’s feral.”

“Do not call me feral,” she said, so cold it could have frozen the Bunsen burners.

“Enough,” Beckwith said weakly. “We’re all on the same side.”

I laughed. I couldn’t help it. “Which side is that, sir? The one where you nearly drained a boy in front of us? Or the one where Emo Dracula here gets all huffy about brand guidelines?”

Leon shot me a look. “I’m trying to stop anyone getting drained,” he said. “And my name is Leon, not Emo Dracula. That’s offensive to both of us.”

“Prove it,” I said. “About stopping it. Because from where I’m standing, the only people acting like this is messed up are us.”

“All due respect,” Aaliyah said, folding her arms, “I don’t trust anyone in a cardigan or with a prefect badge right now.”

Eleanor inhaled sharply. Beckwith looked down at his cardigan like it had betrayed him.

Leon pinched the bridge of his nose. “Okay,” he said. “Here’s what I know. There is a group out there—the unregulated side—who will pay a lot for certain kinds of blood. Young, healthy, above-board paperwork if they can get it. Easier to hide in plain sight. Eleanor has been approached through ‘partnership’ channels. I’ve been trying to keep it inside the ethical lines. I didn’t know about the texts.”

“So the emojis are…marketing?” Callum said faintly.

“Control,” Leon said. “Whoever’s sending them is marking people. Testing how suggestible they are. Who comes when called.”

“Like a loyalty programme,” I said. “Collect three drops, get a free draining.”

“Stop,” Beckwith said. “Please.”

“If you didn’t send them,” I said to Leon, “and you didn’t,” to Eleanor, “that leaves, what, some mystery admin with a God complex and my number?”

Eleanor’s mouth thinned. “There are only a handful of people with access to the invite list and contact details,” she said. “Me. Mr Beckwith. The office. Our external coordinator.”

“External coordinator?” Callum echoed.

“The buyer,” Leon said flatly. “Although we don’t call them that. We call them a ‘partner institution.’ They have their own person. I’ve never given them direct access to students.”

“Until now,” I said.

“You, specifically” —Leon pointed at me— “were not on any supply list. We avoid staff kids, vulnerable backgrounds, anyone who’d be missed by more than a stat line. You’re trouble.”

“Wow,” I said. “Honoured.”

“So why is she getting texts?” Aaliyah said. “If you’re so careful.”

“Because someone’s freelancing,” Leon said again. “And if I can’t prove we’re keeping it clean, someone from higher up will come down and clean it their way. You don’t want that.”

“Define higher up,” Callum said.

“No,” Leon said.

Eleanor tapped her nails on the trolley. “We’re wasting time. Whoever is sending those messages is undermining the integrity of Crimson. We find them, we cut them out, we move on.”

“‘Integrity of Crimson,’” Aaliyah repeated, sotto voce. “I’m begging you to hear yourself.”

“We’re not moving on,” I said. “Not until Kyle stops looking like a corpse and my phone stops impersonating a horror trailer.”

“This is above students,” Beckwith said. “I should— we should inform Dr Lewis.”

“And say what?” Eleanor said. “‘We almost accidentally industrialised teen bloodletting but don’t worry, it was voluntary’?”

Beckwith wilted.

“So we handle it,” Leon said. “Quietly. We trace the texts. That number must be linked to someone.”

“You can’t trace it,” Callum said. “It’s using one of those online-sender things. I tried.” He flushed when we all looked at him. “What? You think I wouldn’t check?”

Leon’s brows went up. “Useful,” he said, almost on reflex.

Callum blinked, thrown by the compliment. “It’s not bulletproof anonymous,” he said. “But we’ll need access to logs. School network. Phone records.”

All eyes turned, inevitably, to Beckwith.

“I can’t,” he said. “That’s—I’d get sacked.”

“You’re going to get more than sacked if an investigator finds goth spam in your safeguarding audit,” I said.

A long, horrible moment where everyone was thinking about worst-case scenarios in their own flavours.

Finally, Aaliyah sighed theatrically. “Right,” she said. “Since all the adults in the room are morally compromised or undead, here’s what we’re going to do.”

“Excuse me?” Eleanor said.

“You’re excused,” Aaliyah said. “Immy’s getting the texts. That makes her bait. Not ideal, but narratively satisfying.”

“Thanks,” I said.

“We lean into that,” she went on. “If they think she’s scared, they’ll push. We push back. We catch whoever’s sending them somewhere we control. Preferably not with needles.”

“I’m not using a child as bait,” Leon said automatically.

“She’s fifteen,” Aaliyah said. “That’s, like, three years from uni offers. Practically decrepit.”

“I’m in the room,” I said.

“We also,” Callum said carefully, “secure some evidence. Screenshots, files. In case we do need to go to someone external. Police. Or the terrifying safeguarding woman with the lanyard collection.”

Eleanor looked offended. “I am not the villain you think I am,” she said. “I am trying to make a flawed system work. If some idiot has decided to turn my programme into a joke with emojis, I want them gone as much as you do.”

“Cool motive,” I said. “Still counts.”

Leon looked at me. Really looked. “You’re scared,” he said, as if surprised.

“I’m not auditioning for your tragic heroine fan club,” I said. “Of course I’m scared. Some stranger has my number and a calendar.”

“It’s not higher up,” he said slowly. “They’re not subtle. This feels…petty. Local.”

“Which narrows it down to the entire sixth form,” Aaliyah muttered.

“My money’s on whoever did the Crimson design,” I said. “You can’t trust people that into branding.”

Silence again.

Then Callum, reluctantly: “We know Eleanor has the names and numbers. We know Beckwith does, but he doesn’t have the spine. We know there’s an external contact who might have leaned on them for ‘more supply.’ If that person or someone they talked to got access…”

“The texts mention ‘non-compliance,’” I added. “That’s… administrative. That’s someone who sees a spreadsheet, not a vampire in an alley.”

Eleanor’s cheeks flushed. “I would never threaten a student. I don’t need to. They listen.”

“Alright, Jesus,” I said.

Leon hopped off the stool. “I’ll talk to my side,” he said. “See if anyone else has noticed this number. Beckwith”—the name came out like a warning—“you quietly see if there have been any weird requests from the ‘partner institution.’ No emails you don’t normally get. No extra attachments.”

“And us?” I asked.

“You,” Leon said, pointing at me, “block that number.”

“I tried,” I said. “It keeps coming through. It’s like it’s not…normal. It shows as unknown every time.”

“Then don’t answer,” he said. “Don’t follow instructions. Don’t come here at midnight alone like an idiot again.”

I bristled. “I didn’t come alone. I brought backup idiots.”

“Me,” Aaliyah corrected.

“Us,” Callum said faintly.

Leon’s mouth twitched, like a half-dead smile tried to surface and thought better of it. “You have very brave friends,” he said. “Or very stupid ones.”

“Often the same thing,” I said.

He moved towards the door. “I’ll be in touch,” he said over his shoulder. “Not with emojis.”

“And if the psycho is listening right now?” I asked. “If they’re in this room?”

Eleanor bristled. Beckwith paled. Aaliyah raised her hand like she’d confess to a prank, just to break the tension, then dropped it.

“Then,” Leon said, pausing in the doorway, “they should know this: if they escalate, they’ll have to deal with me.”

It was dramatic. It shouldn’t have worked. But his eyes had that flat, old look again, and I believed him.

The door swung shut behind him.

Beckwith sat down heavily on a stool. “I’m going to be sick,” he said.

“Not on the samples,” Aaliyah said. “We might need those for court.”

Eleanor straightened her blazer. “You three stay out of this,” she said. “You’ve interfered quite enough.”

“You’re welcome,” I said. “By the way, your pilot scheme is bleeding people.”

I walked out before she could answer.

In the corridor, the emergency lights hummed. Outside, the night was thicker than it had any right to be.

My phone buzzed in my pocket.

Unknown Number
You were told to come alone.
🩸

“Leon?” Callum asked.

I showed them.

“Okay,” Aaliyah said. “New rule. We don’t go anywhere alone. Not toilets, not canteen, not emotional breakdowns. Buddy system.”

“Agreed,” I said, even as my skin crawled.

Because someone knew where I was, and they weren’t in the room we’d just left.
step_outputs.12:
The school never looks more cursed than on a big evening event.

By six, Bayview’s hall had been attacked with fairy lights and borrowed dignity. The Crimson Service banner hung over the stage. Parents milled, governors hovered, staff wore their good cardigans. The biscuits had been upgraded to tiny quiches.

I’d managed about three hours of sleep. Every creak of the flat had been the unknown number crawling through the walls.

The messages since Lab 3 were short and spiteful.

You were told to come alone. 🩸
You like being difficult. That can be fixed. 🩸🩸
Tonight we prove who’s serious. Lab 3 after the showcase. Selected only. 🩸🩸🩸

I’d screenshotted them all. Callum had printed them and kept the pages in a plastic wallet like cursed revision notes.

Now we were at school with an audience, and the air hummed with low-grade smug.

“They went all out,” Aaliyah said, surveying the hall. She’d swapped uniform for a black dress and Docs, using “it’s an evening performance” as excuse.

Dr Lewis fluttered past, cheeks flushed. “Isn’t this exciting?” she said. “Such a vote of confidence in our students.”

“Thrilled,” I said.

“We’re so proud of Eleanor,” she added, moving on.

Eleanor stood near the front, immaculate, greeting parents like a minor royal. If she was rattled from our midnight encounter, it didn’t show. She caught my eye, gave a small, neutral nod, as if we were co-workers.

Leon lurked by a side door in a shirt that actually fit, looking like he’d rather be set on fire. Beckwith hovered near the stage, gripping a folder like a flotation device.

Callum sidled up, clutching his cursed plastic wallet.

“I’ve been thinking,” he said.

“That’s never ended badly,” I said.

“Whoever’s texting you,” he murmured, “they knew you weren’t alone in Lab 3. And they want you there after this. That’s… that’s someone on the inside. With eyes.”

“On-brand,” I said. “Evil, but observant.”

“It might not be Eleanor,” he said. “If I were sending illegal threats, I wouldn’t also be onstage accepting a certificate.”

“She has lieutenants,” Aaliyah said. “Little prefect minions. Anyone handling the tech? That’s who I’d be.”

“Speaking of,” I said. “Positions.”

We’d gone over it on the walk here: messy, not a heist, but something.

- Aaliyah at the AV desk. She’d charmed the actual AV kid into letting her help.
- Callum near the fire alarm panel; he’d memorised the layout like it was a dungeon map.
- Me floating as a Crimson “candidate,” so I could track who moved when.

Leon was our unofficial nuclear option. He’d said, “I won’t do anything obvious unless there’s no alternative.” Which I’d translated as: there is an alternative level, and it has fangs.

Parents and students filtered in. The hall filled. The lights dimmed, leaving everyone’s faces in that washed-out blue.

Eleanor took the stage to polite applause.

“Good evening,” she began, confident. “Thank you all for coming to celebrate the launch of the Crimson Service. Tonight, you’ll hear about the incredible work our students are already doing to support vital research.”

She clicked the remote. The projector hummed. On the screen behind her, the Crimson logo appeared.

A beat too long.

Then, instead of the planned slide deck, a folder of files popped open. Big, black letters at the top of the screen:

CRIMSON SERVICE – CANDIDATE PRIORITISATION

Somewhere at the back, Aaliyah went, “Oops,” into the AV mic, which was still on. It echoed around the hall. Half the teachers winced.

The cursor moved, highlighting lines seemingly of its own accord:

Prime stock – O negative, healthy, low absenteeism.
Non-compliant: follow up. 🩸🩸🩸
Selection window: midnight.

The hall murmured. Dr Lewis stepped forward. “If we could just—technical glitch—”

Another window opened: screenshots of my messages from the unknown number, enlarged for the back row.

Non-compliance complicates things. 🩸🩸🩸
You were told to come alone. 🩸
Selected only. 🩸🩸🩸

The words were huge and obscene in the school hall, hovering over Eleanor’s rigid shoulders.

Teachers turned like a flock. Parents’ heads snapped towards me, toward the screen, back again.

“Kill it,” Eleanor hissed. “Now.”

At the AV desk, the real AV kid slammed keys. “It’s not—it’s not—”

Aaliyah leaned over and oh-so-helpfully unplugged the laptop. The screen went black. A guillotine.

Silence. Thick.

“I’m so sorry, everyone,” Eleanor said, voice level by visible force. “That file is internal draft language and does not reflect—”

The murmur rose. Words like “prime stock” don’t go down well with parents.

Dr Lewis grabbed the mic. “There appears to have been an error with the presentation materials. We will—”

She was cut off by the shriek of the fire alarm.

Callum, by the panel, looked extremely guilty.

Red strobes flashed. Sprinklers didn’t, thank God; the system wasn’t that functional.

“Everyone, please proceed calmly to the nearest exit,” Dr Lewis shouted, flipping to Teacher Fire Drill Voice.

The crowd began to shuffle, complain, grab bags. In the confusion, a handful of students at the back—Crimson invitees—checked their phones, eyes flicking to me, to the side door.

My phone lit up.

Lab 3. Now.
🩸🩸🩸

“Showtime,” I muttered.

“Do not go,” Callum said, appearing at my elbow. “You promised no splitting up.”

“I’m not splitting,” I said. “You’re coming.”

“Excellent,” he said faintly.

Aaliyah skidded over, hair wild. “They’re blaming the AV kid,” she reported. “We should flee before they realise I’m art.”

Leon materialised like a bad mood. “That wasn’t subtle,” he said.

“Neither is kidnapping,” I said. I held up my phone.

He glanced. His jaw hardened. “That’s our rat,” he said. “They won’t resist the lab, not now. They’ll want to grab what they can before this blows.”

“Who’s ‘they’?” Callum asked.

“Stay behind me,” Leon said, which wasn’t an answer.

The hall emptied into the dark. Teachers in hi-vis vests waved torches, counting heads. We peeled off down a side corridor while everyone else queued for the playground.

“Flaw in the plan,” Callum whispered. “Everything about the plan.”

The science block was lit only by emergency strips again, pulsing faintly. Lab 3’s door was ajar. Classic.

“Of course,” Aaliyah muttered. “They love a door ajar.”

Voices inside, low and urgent.

“…no time,” someone said. Not Eleanor. Older, male, unfamiliar. “Take what you can. We’ll relocate. They’ve seen too much.”

“I told you not to use the damn emojis,” Eleanor snapped. “You’ve made us look insane.”

The blood went cold in my hands.

We edged closer. Leon gestured us back, but I needed to see.

Through the gap: Eleanor, pale but composed, standing near the trolley. Beside her, a man in an ill-fitting suit with a visitor’s lanyard flipped backwards. His hair was slicked, his skin sallow. He held a cool-box. Actual cartoon villain kit.

Two students were in there: Jaya, looking scared; Tom, trying to look hard and failing. Both had that floaty expression I’d seen on Kyle.

“I didn’t make you text children threats,” the man said. “I simply provided a system. You wanted commitment. You wanted numbers.”

“Not like this,” she said.

“You knew what the contract was,” he said. “Prime samples. Efficiently sourced. You were very keen on ‘impact’ on our call, Miss Price.”

Leon pushed the door fully open.

“No more,” he said.

The man spun. His gaze slid over Leon, tried to place him, failed. “Who the hell—”

“External compliance,” Leon said. “You’re done.”

He stepped between the man and the kids with lazy, precise movement. For a second, I could almost see how he might have been before: frightening because he didn’t need to be.

“I have authority,” the man blustered. “You’re just a local asset.”

“Try me,” Leon said.

The man’s hand dipped into his coat. A flash of metal. Not theatrical fangs; a stubby syringe.

He lunged—not at Leon, but at Jaya.

I didn’t think. I shoved the door open and barreled into him.

We both went sideways. The syringe clattered, rolled. Callum kicked it reflexively. It slid under a cupboard.

“You absolute idiot,” the man hissed, trying to scramble up.

Leon grabbed him by the collar and slammed him against the bench with inhumanly casual force.

The man’s feet scrabbled. “You can’t—”

Leon bared his teeth. Properly. No pretending. They were wrong. Too long, too sharp. The emergency light made them glint.

“Oh, he can,” Aaliyah breathed.

The man’s bravado evaporated. “I—I’ll report you up the chain,” he stammered.

“I hope you do,” Leon said. “I’d like a word.”

Eleanor looked like her operating system had crashed. She stared at the syringe, at the cool-box, at the kids.

“I thought—” she started.

“That you’d be the hero,” I said. The words came out harsher than I intended. “That hurting the right kids for the right reasons made you special.”

“Nothing actually—” she tried.

“Prime stock,” I said. “Non-compliant. Selection window. That’s you. That’s your language.”

Her mouth snapped shut.

Footsteps thundered in the corridor. Adults. Dr Lewis shouting, “Everyone out! Has anyone checked the labs?”

“Perfect timing,” Aaliyah muttered.

Leon leaned in close to the man’s ear. “You’re leaving,” he said. “Now. And you’re telling your bosses this route is burnt. If anyone comes back, they’ll find me.”

The man nodded so fast he blurred.

Leon let go. He bolted, stumbling, out the side door that led to the car park, clutching his empty cooler. Gone into the dark.

“Eleanor,” Beckwith gasped from the doorway, sudden, taking in the scene: the trolley, the kids, Leon, us.

Dr Lewis appeared behind him. “What is going on?”

The room froze.

Eleanor opened her mouth. For once, nothing came out.

“They were about to take blood from underage students without proper consent,” I said. My voice didn’t shake. Small victory. “We stopped it.”

“That is a serious allegation,” Dr Lewis said, automatic.

“There’s evidence,” Callum said quickly, shoving his folder at her. “Files. The slides. The texts. Check the USB. The AV kid’s still crying.”

Jaya made a small sound. “They said it was for research,” she whispered. “They said it would look good for uni.”

Dr Lewis’ face did something I’d never seen before: the mask cracked. Genuine horror.

“Everyone out of this room,” she said, tight. “Now.”

Her eyes swept over Eleanor, over Beckwith.

“To my office,” she said to them. “Immediately.”

Eleanor’s smile was gone. She looked…young. And furious. At us? At herself? I couldn’t tell.

She brushed past me. For a second our shoulders touched.

“You’ve ruined everything,” she hissed.

“If your ‘everything’ involves cool-box traffickers,” I said, “good.”

She stalked out.

Beckwith trailed, grey.

Leon started to follow, then stopped by us.

“You shouldn’t have rushed him,” he said to me.

“You’re welcome,” I said.

He almost smiled. “Reckless,” he said. “Effective.”

“Also, fire alarm,” Callum said. “We committed at least three health and safety violations for this.”

Dr Lewis turned. “You three. Office, tomorrow morning. We will need statements.”

Aaliyah gave me a tiny thumbs up. “We’re so expelled,” she whispered.

“No,” I said. “They can’t. Not when—”

My phone buzzed.

Unknown Number
🙂

A single, smug smiley. No blood.

Leon saw it. His jaw ticked. “It’s not over,” he said quietly.

For once, I didn’t have a joke.

Out in the corridor, the alarm still wailed. In the hall, the fairy lights dripped. Outside, parents waited in the drizzle with half-understood outrage.

We had stopped something. We had not tied it up with a bow.

And someone out there was still enjoying the show.
step_outputs.13:
The fire alarm finally cut out at half past eight, leaving a ringing hush. Parents were hustled away with vague apologies about “technical issues.” The Crimson banner sagged.

By nine, Mum had me caged at the kitchen table with a mug of tea I hadn’t asked for.

“What happened?” she said. No TV voice, no jokey deflection. Just the quiet that meant she was scared.

I’d rehearsed with Callum and Aaliyah on the walk home: half-truth, half-omission.

“There was a problem with that new charity thing,” I said. “Couple of teachers got ahead of themselves. They were taking blood without the right forms. Someone hacked the slideshow. It got messy. We… told Dr Lewis.”

“‘Got messy’ how messy?” she asked.

“Not me-messy,” I said. “Promise.”

She watched my face, looking for the flicker. There wasn’t one. I wasn’t lying, technically. Just leaving out undead compliance officers and emoji stalkers.

She exhaled, shoulders dropping. “You don’t have to impress them,” she said. “You know that, yeah? They’ll take and take and call it opportunity.”

It hit harder than any text.

“I know,” I said.

“Eat something,” she said, ruffling my hair like I was five. “You look like a crisp.”

I took my tea and toast to my room. Closed the door. Checked my phone.

No new unknowns. No smiley. The last one sat there like an unwanted sticker.

In Cryptids & Chemistry, Aaliyah was already spinning.

A: tell me we’re getting a Netflix doc
C: I’d take “stern letter from academy trust”
I: no fame until my breakout role as “anonymous source”

I hovered, then typed:

I: u guys home ok?

C: yep
C: nan thinks alarm was “drill that went wrong”
A: my mum thinks it was an art installation
A: she’s not entirely wrong

I: can’t believe they didn’t arrest us for cyber war crimes

C: they’re too busy panic-lawyering

A: my condolences to eleanor’s ucAS statement

We were supposed to be in Dr Lewis’ office at ten the next morning for “formal statements.” Suspensions hung in the air like a possibility no one wanted to say.

I slept like someone had unplugged me.

In the grey light of morning, school felt hungover. Kids clustered in whisper knots; rumours sprouted.

“They were selling our blood online.”

“Apparently Eleanor’s in prison.”

“My mum says we’re all getting tested.”

In reality: Eleanor was “suspended pending investigation.” Mr Beckwith looked like he’d aged a decade overnight. Lawyers had been mentioned in staff emails. Governors were “reviewing safeguarding protocols.”

The Crimson posters were gone. Torn rectangles of blue-tack the only trace.

In Dr Lewis’ office, the blinds were half-closed. She looked tired in a way that made me feel bad for her for the first time ever.

“We are treating this extremely seriously,” she said. Her usual iron tone had frayed. “You did the right thing bringing those files to light.”

I waited for the “but.”

“But,” she said, “pulling the fire alarm without authorisation is still a breach—”

“It was an emergency,” Callum said, voice surprisingly firm. “They were about to move students off-site with a man who wasn’t on your visitor log.”
