He slid a copy of the visitor sheet he’d grabbed under the door last night onto her desk. He’d circled the name of the man in the bad suit: RICHARD MAY, PARTNER REP.

Dr Lewis stared at it. “Where did you get this?”

“It fell,” he lied baldly. “I picked it up.”

She pinched the bridge of her nose. “I have never been so disappointed in some of my staff,” she said.

It took me a second to realise she meant them, not us.

“You will not be punished,” she said. “For this. Officially.” Her eyes flicked to me. “Unofficially, please try not to hijack my assemblies again.”

“No promises,” Aaliyah said, then coughed. “Sorry. Yes, miss.”

Afterwards, in the corridor, the three of us leaned against the lockers like our bones had dissolved.

“So,” Aaliyah said. “We’re heroes.”

“We’re liabilities,” Callum said. “Heroes are liabilities with better PR.”

“I think I’m going to throw up,” I said.

“We can do it as a team,” she said. “Coordinated nausea.”

Leon was waiting in Lab 3.

The door was propped open now, lights on, everything aggressively normal. No trolley. No cool-box. Just periodic tables and last year’s volcano models.

He stood by the window, hands in his pockets, looking out at the grey playing field like it offended him.

“You survived,” he said as we walked in.

“Not through lack of trying,” I said.

Beckwith was there too, perched on a stool. He looked wrung out.

“Dr Lewis has reported everything up,” he said. “To the trust, to the regulators. The… external partner has been cut off.” He swallowed. “I’m on… leave, while they investigate. Which is fair.”

“You going to tell them about the vampire clause?” Aaliyah asked.

He flinched. Leon rolled his eyes.

“No one’s mentioning the words you people keep saying too loudly,” Leon said. “Officially, it’s ‘inappropriate medical procedures’ and ‘rogue contractor.’ They don’t actually want the full story.”

“That makes four of us,” Callum murmured.

Leon looked at me. “Thank you,” he said. “For the slides. The alarm. The tackling. All of it.”

“Don’t get soppy,” I said. “You’ll ruin your aesthetic.”

He ignored that. “The buyer won’t be back,” he said. “He’s been… discouraged.”

A flash of something flickered behind that: satisfaction edged with something darker.

“And the texts?” I asked.

“We traced what we could,” he said. “Our charming partner set up the system. But someone local was sending them. Using his access.”

“Well?” Aaliyah said. “Name and shame.”

“Office manager,” Leon said. “The one who does the mail merges. She thought it was ‘motivational.’ She didn’t write the threats, just sent what she was given, didn’t question it. Says she deleted everything once she got nervous.”

“That’s it?” I said. “Killer stationery vibes and zero critical thinking?”

“Told you,” Callum said. “Admin.”

Leon shrugged. “The texts with… flavour were May. The smiley.” His lip curled. “He thought it was funny.”

“Nothing about him is funny,” I said.

“He’s being handled,” Leon said. “My side and yours, for once, in agreement.”

“Cool,” Aaliyah said. “Love a bipartisan takedown.”

Silence settled for a moment.

“So what now?” I asked.

“Now,” Beckwith said, “we rebuild trust. We make sure nothing like this can happen again. We—”

“You resign,” Aaliyah suggested.

He winced. “Possibly.”

Leon said, “Now we shut down the school scheme. For good. No more pilots. If I need supplies, I go back to anonymous adults with proper forms and less drama.”

“You’re welcome,” I said.

He tilted his head. “I meant it. You three are… annoyingly effective.”

“We aim to irritate,” Aaliyah said.

Callum chewed his lip. “So there are more of you,” he said to Leon. “Out there.”

“Yes,” Leon said. “Most of them extremely boring. I am, tragically, considered rebellious.”

“That is tragic,” I said.

Leon’s mouth twitched. “If anything like this stirs again,” he said, “I might need help. Someone who recognises certain patterns. Preferably someone who doesn’t immediately post them on TikTok.”

“I’m not your intern,” I said.

“Obviously,” he said. “You’d unionise.”

A beat. We were all, inexplicably, smiling a little.

“Give me your number,” he said. “Properly. No creepy unknowns.”

I hesitated. “You’re not going to send me blood emojis?” I asked.

“God, no,” he said. “If you ever see three of those again, it won’t be from me.”

I tapped my number into his battered old Nokia—of course he had a brick—and watched him save it as “Imogen (Do Not Drink).”

On my screen, a new contact request popped:

Leon (Definitely Not A Vampire)

“You’re hilarious,” I said.

“It’s aspirational,” he said.

We left them there, two compromised adults in a bright lab, and stepped out into the corridor.

The day had brightened a bit. Through the glass doors we could see kids on the field, shrieking, footballs flying. As if nothing had nearly turned into a farm.

“Do we tell anyone?” Callum asked, as we loitered by the stairs. “About the vampire bit.”

“Who’d believe it?” Aaliyah said. “Plus, imagine the safeguarding form. Too much admin.”

“I think,” I said slowly, “we hold it. For now. We keep an eye. We annoy anyone who starts talking about ‘prime stock.’ We don’t walk into midnight labs alone.”

“Buddy system forever,” Aaliyah said, hooking her arm through mine.

“Statistically speaking,” Callum said, “if anything weird happens again, it’ll find us.”

“Great,” I said. “Love that for us.”

Later, after a day of half-hearted lessons and full-throated gossip, after giving a sanitised statement to an actual police officer (“No, I didn’t think the texts were serious at first. Yes, I’m aware that was dumb.”), after Mum squeezed me so tightly my ribs complained, I lay on my bed with my phone on my chest.

A notification pinged.

Leon (Definitely Not A Vampire):
If you see anything else odd, text. No heroics.
🩸

Just one drop.

I snorted despite myself. Typed back:

I:
that is not reassuring branding
also: terms & conditions apply

Three dots. Then:

Leon:
noted

I locked the chat. Left it there.

I was about to toss the phone aside when another notification slid down, from a number I didn’t recognise.

Unknown:
👍

Just a thumbs up. No name, no thread history.

Every muscle in my body went tight.

“Not funny,” I told the ceiling.

I stared at it for a long moment. Waited for more. Nothing came.

I screenshotted it. Forwarded it to Cryptids & Chemistry.

I:
plz tell me one of you thinks you’re hilarious

Aaliyah:
not my brand
send me address, I’ll fight them

Callum:
could be someone fat-fingering a group chat
or may
or
okay I’m spiralling

I:
new rule, no unknown emoji ever

I didn’t reply to the unknown. I blocked it, knowing that might not mean much.

Then I put the phone face-down.

In the dark, I could still see the first message in my mind. The neat three drops. The moment when it had been just another joke.

I thought about Kyle, pale and quiet but back in class, his mum holding his arm a little too tight at pick-up. About Jaya, deleting an email with shaking fingers. About Eleanor, somewhere with her planner and no title, replaying the moment her perfect story cracked.

I thought about my mum’s line: they’ll take and take and call it opportunity.

I thought about how, for half a second when the envelope had landed in my hand, I’d felt flattered.

That was the bit that lodged under my ribs.

“Never again,” I told myself.

The street outside flicked from day to night. Glam Claws sign buzzing, gulls screaming, buses sighing.

My phone buzzed once on the duvet. My heart kicked.

It was just Callum.

C:
if we get murdered by a thumbs up emoji I’m suing microsoft

I:
that’s windows
this is google’s fault probably

A:
breathe, cryptids
u ruined a blood cult, you can handle a hand

I exhaled. Smiled, slightly.

Outside my window, under the half-lit A CA E sign, a figure in a beanie stood for a moment, hands in his pockets, looking up. When the bus passed, blocking my view for a second, he was gone.

I didn’t freak out.

“See you soon, maybe,” I said quietly. “But only if I text first.”

No grand moral. No neat ending.

Just me, my stupid heart, my cursed phone, my friends, a possibly ethical vampire, and a town that would absolutely turn this into a rumour by Monday.

It would do.

chapter_raw_judge_text.1:
[Analysis]

Chapter 1 of this piece is a confident, well-executed opening that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Immy's voice is the chapter's strongest asset. It's genuinely specific rather than generically "quirky teen" — the Nightbite 3 loyalty, the muted-instead-of-blocked decision, the "You're not getting me with that" muttered to an empty room. These feel like real character texture rather than performed personality. The voice is British without being a slang showcase, dry without being smug. The self-awareness ("Your brain was just helpfully overlaying creepiness like a filter") is exactly the right register — she's smart enough to know she's scared, too proud to admit it cleanly.

GROUP CHAT DYNAMICS: The Cryptids & Chemistry exchange is one of the chapter's best moments. The three voices are distinct: Callum's "statistically speaking" and the owl avatar, Aaliyah's "aesthetic" and theatrical offence at not receiving the text, Immy's sardonic deflection. The dialogue feels genuinely observed rather than constructed. Callum's "At least send a link. Terrible user journey" (from the plan) doesn't appear verbatim but the spirit is captured in "oh good, the murderers are emoji literate." The chat feels like actual teenagers rather than adult writers performing teenagers.

PACING AND ATMOSPHERE: The chapter builds unease effectively without overplaying it. The lamp flickering, the too-still figure under the arcade sign, the "Read 00:01" beat — these are deployed with restraint. The figure under the arcade is the chapter's most effective horror moment: brief, ambiguous, not over-explained. The chapter correctly doesn't resolve whether it's sinister.

PROSE QUALITY: Generally clean and specific. "The fridge fighting for its life" is good. "A gull doing a noise like a demon being blendered" is excellent — funny and slightly wrong in the right way. "Sleep didn't land so much as creep in around the edges" is a nice turn. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard: "the building was shutting its eyes" is a touch precious, and "the dark take me" is a mild cliché. But these are minor.

FORCED POETRY/PURPLE PROSE: Largely avoided. The metaphors that appear are grounded in Immy's voice rather than authorial intrusion. "The sodium-orange seep from the street" is good sensory writing, not purple. The chapter doesn't overreach.

FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter hits its beats faithfully — the Nightbite viewing, the group chat dynamic, the midnight text, Immy's dismissive reaction, the figure outside, the "Read 00:01" unease, the morning bus with Kyle's empty seat. The plan called for a "quiet end" and the chapter delivers: the single blood drop at 02:14 and Kyle's story notification land with exactly the right understated dread. The chapter doesn't oversell its horror.

WEAKNESSES: The lamp flickering is a slightly generic horror beat — it's the one moment that feels borrowed rather than earned. The "It can see me" thought is a bit on-the-nose for a character who's otherwise more oblique about her fear. The chapter could perhaps do slightly more to establish the setting's specificity (the "faded English seaside town" is gestured at but not fully rendered — we get the arcade sign and the high street but the seaside itself is only implied by a distant sea roar). These are minor complaints.

KYLE SETUP: The ending with Kyle's story notification is well-handled — it's the chapter's most effective structural move, converting what seemed like ambient weirdness into something with stakes. "Dead" as a caption, then the empty bus seat, is economical and effective.

CONSISTENCY WITH PROFILES: Immy is exactly as described — sardonic, avoidant, using humour as deflection, horror-nerd specific. Callum and Aaliyah are briefly but accurately sketched in the chat. The chapter doesn't yet introduce Leon, Beckwith, or Eleanor, which is correct for this opening.

Overall this is a strong, controlled opening chapter that earns its tonal balance between comedy and unease. It's not a masterpiece — there are small generic beats and the setting could be more fully rendered — but it's genuinely good YA dark comedy writing.

[Scores]

Nuanced Characters: 15
Emotionally Engaging: 15
Compelling Plot: 14
Coherent: 17
Weak Dialogue: 4
Tell-Don't-Show: 4
Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5
Amateurish: 3
Purple Prose: 3
Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4
Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16
Characters Consistent with Profile: 16
Followed Chapter Plan: 17
Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
chapter_raw_judge_text.2:
[Analysis]

Chapter 2 of this darkly comic British YA piece is a confident, well-executed instalment that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the premise. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER

Immy's voice is the chapter's strongest asset. It's consistent, specific, and genuinely funny without being try-hard. Lines like "Flattering poison" (a single-sentence paragraph that lands cleanly), "Eloquence" as self-deprecating commentary on her own response, and the running commentary on Eleanor all feel earned rather than performed. The humour is rooted in character observation rather than quip-for-quip's sake. Callum's "Coincidence" followed by the parenthetical explanation of what that actually means is a nice character beat. Aaliyah's theatrical energy comes through well, though she risks tipping into pure comic relief here — her "emotional support extrovert" line is good, but she's slightly less three-dimensional than the profile suggests she should be.

Eleanor's introduction is handled well. The "politician smile," the laser-accurate envelope distribution, the weaponised politeness of using names — these are shown rather than told, and they match her profile precisely. The detail of her pretending not to hear Callum's "That's not creepy at all" is a small, sharp character moment.

Mr Beckwith's brief appearance is excellent — "Very safe. Very controlled. Right." with the coffee spill and the lanyard peeking out does a lot of work efficiently.

PLOT AND STRUCTURE

The chapter follows its plan closely: assembly introduction of Crimson Service, Eleanor's presentation, the envelope distribution, Aaliyah's exclusion (and her theatrical offence), Beckwith's nervous appearance, and the escalating text. The pacing is good — it moves through the school day with appropriate rhythm, using the canteen queue and locker scenes to show how the Crimson Service gossip spreads organically.

The detail of the girl with the plaster in the crook of her elbow is a nice piece of foreshadowing that doesn't announce itself. The "little patterns" observation from Immy is appropriately understated.

The chapter ends on a genuinely unsettling note — the three dots appearing and vanishing after Immy texts back is creepy in a very contemporary, recognisable way. The final image of the dots blinking once more then disappearing is effective.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is mostly strong. It sounds like teenagers without being a parody of teenagers. The group chat reference ("this screams organ harvesting") is well-placed. The Aaliyah reading-aloud-pompously scene works. However, some exchanges are slightly functional — moving plot information rather than revealing character. The "So. Honest thoughts. On a scale from school charity scam to full occult sacrifice?" exchange is good but the responses are a touch on-the-nose in their exposition of each character's position.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter's main weakness is that it occasionally tells us what to think rather than letting us arrive there. "Flattering poison" is good, but "Little patterns" as a section break feels slightly too neat — Immy flagging her own detective work for the reader. The metaphor "Eleanor walked on stage like the concept of posture had hired a PR team" is clever but slightly overwrought — it's the kind of line that calls attention to itself. The opening "grim soup" playground image is fine but not particularly fresh.

The chapter is also perhaps slightly too comfortable — the unease is present but the comedy consistently wins, which is appropriate for this stage of the story but means the chapter doesn't quite achieve the "quiet uneasy" ending the plan called for. The final beat is good, but the chapter as a whole feels more funny than unsettling, which may be a slight miscalibration given that the plan wanted the weirdness to grow.

The Aaliyah-not-getting-an-invite thread is handled well emotionally — her hurt is real and not played purely for laughs — but it could have been pushed slightly further given the profile's emphasis on this being "real and messy."

CRAFT

The prose is clean and controlled. No purple passages. The sensory details (Lynx Africa, wet blazers on radiators, the faded maroon curtains) are specific and grounding without being laboured. The chapter earns its lightness — the jokes don't neutralise the threat, they glance off it, as intended.

Overall, this is a well-crafted chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains consistent characterisation, and advances the plot with appropriate economy. Its weaknesses are minor: occasional over-signposting, one or two slightly strained metaphors, and a comedy-to-unease balance that leans slightly too comfortable for where the story needs to go.

[Scores]

chapter_raw_judge_text.4:
[Analysis]

Chapter 4 is a strong piece of YA dark comedy writing that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the earlier chapters. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Immy's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The sardonic deflection ("If I get murdered, I'll let you know"), the self-aware cowardice ("I'm not spooked," I lied), and the way she uses humour as armour all feel authentic to the profile. The exchange with her mum is particularly well-handled — it's warm, economical, and reveals character without exposition. The mum's line "if they made it sound intellectual" is a small gem that tells us a lot about how well these two know each other. Callum's anxiety-logic ("I like patterns. I don't like being in one") is pitch-perfect. Aaliyah's "I'm choosing to fight dread with camp" is exactly right for her character. Eleanor is present only as a shadow here, which is appropriate for this chapter's function.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is generally strong and naturalistic. The banter feels earned rather than performed. "Human Capri-Sun" is a genuinely funny line that feels like something a teenager would actually say. The Beckwith scene is well-executed — his evasiveness is shown through behaviour (spilling copper sulfate, writing DONNER, flinching) rather than just stated. The "too quickly" beat when he asks "What texts?" is nicely done. The dialogue occasionally tips into being slightly too witty in a coordinated way — Aaliyah's "least sexy sext ever" and "mysterious dom" feel slightly scripted — but this is a minor issue and within the register of the piece.

PLOT AND PACING: The chapter does exactly what the plan requires: escalates from unease to active threat, introduces the "non-compliance" text, shows Immy beginning to track patterns, and ends with the midnight summons. The surveillance of Leon by the blood van is a good scene — it advances the mystery while providing some comic relief (the Pound Saver doorway, "Victorian ghosts"). The chapter moves well without feeling rushed.

SHOWING VS TELLING: The chapter is predominantly showing. Beckwith's guilt is conveyed through physical behaviour. The threat of the texts is shown through Immy's physical reactions (heart jumping, palms sweaty) rather than narrated. The note-taking scene is a good device for organising information without an exposition dump.

METAPHOR AND PROSE: The prose is clean and controlled. There are a few moments of light metaphor that work: "read out 'authorised absence' like it was a spell that made concern optional" is sharp. "Tally marks" for the blood drop branding is effective. The line "the air tasted of salt and chips and something metallic underneath, or maybe that was my brain overcommitting" is good — it's doing double duty (atmosphere + character voice). There's no purple prose or forced poeticism. The writing knows its register and stays in it.

COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and well-structured. The three-act shape (school day → Beckwith confrontation → Leon surveillance → home → escalating texts) flows naturally. The information is parcelled out at the right rate.

DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The balance is well-managed. The humour never fully neutralises the threat — the "Non-compliance complicates things" text lands with genuine menace, and the final midnight text on the bus is properly unsettling. The mum scene provides genuine warmth that makes the subsequent dread more effective.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly too comfortable in its own competence. There are no real surprises in terms of craft — it executes the plan faithfully but doesn't exceed it in any striking way. The Leon surveillance scene, while fun, is a little predictable in its beats (they're bad at it, he sees them, he disappears). The ending with the midnight text is effective but the chapter's final image — the bus shuddering over a pothole while no one else notices — is a slightly familiar "protagonist alone in the crowd" beat. The note-taking list device is functional but a little on-the-nose as a way to summarise the mystery so far.

FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter follows its plan closely. All the major beats are hit: Kyle's absence, the "non-compliance" text, Callum's pattern-tracking, the Beckwith confrontation, the Leon surveillance, the midnight Lab 3 text. The chapter doesn't quite deliver the "Immy's fear breaks surface" beat as dramatically as the plan suggests — her fear is present but still fairly controlled, which is consistent with her character but slightly undersells the plan's stated purpose.

Overall, this is confident, well-crafted YA writing that serves its story well. It's not transcendent, but it's genuinely good — specific, funny where it should be, unsettling where it needs to be, and true to its characters.

[Analysis]

Chapter 3 is a confident, well-executed piece of YA dark comedy that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the premise. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER

Immy's voice is consistent and genuinely distinctive. The sardonic interiority ("like he was being paid per nod") feels earned rather than performed. Her observation of Eleanor's "tiny, stupid power" is one of the chapter's best moments—it's psychologically precise and reveals Immy's class awareness without announcing it. The line "I like being abstract. Less paperwork" is exactly the kind of deflection-as-wit the character profile calls for.

Callum's "ten out of ten red flags" and the two-fingers-on-the-form detail are good character shorthand. Aaliyah's "I'm uncomfortable and intrigued" and her running commentary land well. Eleanor's name-weaponising ("Imogen," said with relish) is a nice touch consistent with her profile.

Leon's introduction is handled with restraint. The blur of movement when Jaya sways, the jaw-clench at her wrist, the "something unreadable" eye contact—these are effective horror beats that don't overplay themselves. His interjection about voluntary participation being "strongly" emphasised is a smart way to show his ethical discomfort without exposition.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong. It feels like actual teenagers rather than screenwriters' approximations. The exchange between Immy and Eleanor over the form is particularly good—Eleanor's "Bright. Underutilised. Principled" is chilling in its accuracy, and Immy's deflection is character-true. The whispered asides between Immy and Aaliyah are natural without being too quippy.

One weakness: Beckwith's lines are slightly too on-the-nose in their nervousness ("It's all properly regulated... You'll have information, consent forms for parents. Nothing happens without, um, proper procedure"). The "um" and the trailing off are fine, but the dialogue doesn't quite capture the specific texture of a compromised adult trying to sound reassuring—it reads a little generic.

PLOT AND PACING

The chapter moves well. The setting is established efficiently (the swapped strip-lights for lamps, the sweating biscuits, the sterile water bottles—all good specific details). The presentation scene doesn't drag. The Jaya fainting moment is well-placed as a horror beat that's deniable but unsettling. The final text ("Pending. 🩸🩸") lands with appropriate menace and the chapter ends on a genuinely uneasy note rather than a false resolution.

The chapter follows its plan closely: Eleanor's presentation, Aaliyah inserting herself, the forms, Leon's intervention with Jaya, the "pending" text. The plan's beat about "one boy jokes 'What is this, vampire scouts?'" is absent, replaced by a rugby lad's blunter "Blood" comment—a reasonable substitution that avoids a slightly too-on-the-nose joke.

SHOW VS TELL

Mostly strong. The horror is conveyed through implication: Leon's speed, his jaw-clench at Jaya's wrist, Eleanor's assessing gaze. The class dynamics are shown through Eleanor's "underutilised" comment and Immy's bristling response. There are occasional moments where the narration tells us what we've already seen ("Power. Tiny, stupid power"), but these are brief and feel like Immy processing rather than the author explaining.

PROSE QUALITY

The prose is clean and specific. "The biscuits were finally breached" is a nice dry line. "Bottles of water formed a sterile little battalion" is a good image. "A cold ribbon unrolled along my spine" is the one moment that tips slightly toward cliché—it's not terrible, but it's the most generic image in the chapter. The opening paragraph ("the rain had given up and left everything slick") is efficient and atmospheric without being overwrought.

TONAL BALANCE

The chapter manages the dark comedy balance well. The humour (Aaliyah's commentary, Callum's exit-route positioning) doesn't defuse the genuine unease—it runs alongside it, which is exactly right for the genre. The moment where Aaliyah's "joking smile slipped" at the text is a good tonal shift, showing the humour as coping mechanism rather than authorial deflection.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth. Everything lands more or less as intended, which means there's little of the productive messiness the plan calls for. The plan mentions Immy's "half mocking / half snagged" internal state more explicitly than the chapter delivers—we get the snagging but the mockery is mostly externalised through Aaliyah. Immy's interiority could be a touch more conflicted.

The "Arcade Boy / Leon Hart" recognition moment is handled a bit quickly—the plan suggests this is a significant beat (the figure by the shuttered arcades from Chapter 1), but the chapter doesn't quite milk the recognition for its full unease.

Overall, this is a well-crafted chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains consistent characterisation, and balances tone effectively. It's not quite masterclass level—the prose occasionally defaults to competent rather than distinctive, and the chapter's smoothness works slightly against the intended productive messiness—but it's genuinely good YA dark comedy writing.

chapter_raw_judge_text.6:
[Analysis]

Chapter 6 is a tense ensemble scene set in a school lab, functioning as a confrontation/strategy chapter where the various factions—Immy, Callum, Aaliyah, Leon, Eleanor, and Beckwith—hash out what they know and what to do next. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Immy's voice is the chapter's strongest asset. Her sardonic deflection ("I held onto my sarcasm like it was buoyant"), her reflexive jokes under pressure ("Like a loyalty programme. Collect three drops, get a free draining"), and her moments of genuine fear breaking through ("Of course I'm scared. Some stranger has my number and a calendar") are all consistent with her profile. The line "I'm not auditioning for your tragic heroine fan club" is particularly good—it's defensive, self-aware, and character-specific.

Callum's anxiety-logic manifests naturally: he's already checked the number, he has a notes app out, he thinks in terms of evidence and external escalation. His flushing at Leon's compliment is a nice small beat. Aaliyah's theatrical bravado ("Previously, on My Terrible Decisions" energy) comes through in her "buddy system" declaration and her sotto voce commentary. Leon's exhausted, unglamorous competence is well-rendered—he's not cool, he's tired and responsible and slightly condescending. Eleanor's cold rationalism and her genuine offense at being called feral are consistent with her profile. Beckwith's wilting, cardigan-betrayal moment is brief but effective.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is the chapter's primary vehicle, and it largely works. The exchanges have rhythm and feel like distinct voices rather than one author speaking through multiple mouths. "Language like 'harvest' is unhelpful. It's allocation" (from the character profiles) doesn't appear here, but Eleanor's "I am not the villain you think I am. I am trying to make a flawed system work" captures her self-justifying rationalism well. Leon's "I'm not your spooky mentor. I'm the idiot who thought a memorandum of understanding would fix this" (from profiles) doesn't appear, but his equivalent lines here—"I didn't know about the texts" and his flat correction of "Emo Dracula"—land similarly.

Some dialogue is slightly on-the-nose in its exposition function: the "external coordinator?" / "The buyer. Although we don't call them that" exchange is a bit mechanical. But this is a chapter that has to convey a lot of information, and it mostly does so through character-specific speech rather than pure info-dump.

PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter plan called for: strategy in Immy's bedroom, cramped, mum knocking, Leon among horror DVDs. The chapter instead takes place in the school lab. This is a significant deviation from the plan. The plan also called for Callum finding a hidden folder on a shared drive with rosters and notes, and discovering messages scheduling a "midnight collection." None of this happens in the chapter as written—instead, the chapter is primarily a confrontation/negotiation scene. The plan's key beats (Aaliyah insisting on confronting Eleanor, Eleanor stonewalling, Callum's IT discovery, the showcase revelation) are largely absent or restructured.

The chapter does accomplish some of the plan's goals—suspicion of Eleanor is articulated, motives are hinted at, the external coordinator is named as a suspect—but it's doing so through dialogue rather than through the discovery mechanics the plan outlined. The ending text ("You were told to come alone. 🩸") is a good escalation beat, though it doesn't appear in the plan either.

This is a notable structural deviation. The chapter feels like it's doing Chapter 5's aftermath and Chapter 6's setup simultaneously, compressing what should be two distinct movements.

SHOW VS. TELL

The chapter is mostly dialogue-driven, which means it's showing through speech rather than telling through narration. The few descriptive beats—Beckwith twisting the wipe packet until it splits, Eleanor's cheeks flushing, Leon's "half-dead smile tried to surface and thought better of it"—are economical and effective. The final image ("the night was thicker than it had any right to be") is the chapter's one reach toward atmospheric prose, and it's a mild cliché but not egregious.

PROSE QUALITY

The prose is clean and controlled. There are no purple passages or forced metaphors. "I held onto my sarcasm like it was buoyant" is a good line—specific and character-revealing. "His eyes had that flat, old look again" is efficient horror. The chapter avoids the trap of over-writing the tension.

TONAL BALANCE

The dark comedy balance is well-maintained. The humor never fully neutralizes the threat—the final text lands as genuinely unsettling after the chapter's banter. Aaliyah's "Not on the samples. We might need those for court" is funny and in-character. The chapter earns its lightness because it's rooted in how these specific people cope.

WEAKNESSES

The main weakness is structural: the chapter deviates significantly from its plan, losing the bedroom scene's intimacy and the IT discovery's plot momentum. The confrontation with Eleanor is present but doesn't "misfire" in the way the plan described (Aaliyah losing her temper publicly, them looking unhinged). Instead, Eleanor is present and engaged in the same room, which changes the dynamic considerably. The chapter also doesn't advance the plot as much as it should—by the end, we know roughly what we knew at the start of the scene, plus the external coordinator detail and the final text. The discovery of Eleanor's files, which was meant to be the chapter's key plot turn, doesn't happen.

The "You were told to come alone" text is a good ending, but it raises a question the chapter doesn't quite earn: if the antagonist is watching, why weren't they watching earlier? The chapter's internal logic is slightly strained here.

chapter_raw_judge_text.7:
[Analysis]

Chapter 7 is the climactic chapter of this darkly comic British YA story, and it largely delivers on its considerable ambitions. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Immy's voice is well-maintained throughout. The sardonic commentary ("Thrilled," she said), the self-aware humour as defence mechanism, and the moments where the jokes fail her ("For once, I didn't have a joke") all feel authentic to the established character. Her line "If your 'everything' involves cool-box traffickers, good" is exactly the kind of slightly-too-sharp comeback the profile promises. Callum's anxiety-logic ("we committed at least three health and safety violations") and Aaliyah's theatrical bravado ("We're so expelled") are consistent with their profiles. Leon's "Reckless. Effective." is perfectly calibrated to his character: dry, grudging, precise.

Eleanor's unmasking is handled with some nuance. Her "You've ruined everything" is petulant rather than villainous, and the moment where she looks "young. And furious" gestures toward the complexity the plan promised. However, the chapter doesn't quite deliver on the plan's ambition to show Eleanor's motives surfacing with real depth. The exchange between her and the unnamed man does some of this work ("You were very keen on 'impact' on our call"), but Eleanor's interiority remains somewhat thin. The plan called for her mask to crack and her logic to surface; we get glimpses but not the full confrontation Immy was supposed to have with her.

PLOT EXECUTION

The chapter follows its plan closely. The AV sabotage, the fire alarm, the split to Lab 3, the confrontation with Eleanor and the unnamed partner, the partner's flight, the arrival of authority figures—all land in roughly the order planned. The "messy, not a heist" quality is well-executed: the AV kid crying, Callum kicking the syringe reflexively, Aaliyah's "Oops" into a live mic. These feel like genuine teenage improvisation rather than slick genre plotting.

The plan called for Immy to clock Eleanor with a lab stool, Leon to block a jab, and Callum to uselessly wave a fire extinguisher. The actual chapter replaces this with Immy barreling into the man and the syringe clattering away. This is arguably more grounded and less slapstick, though it loses some of the planned comic texture. The fire extinguisher gag is referenced only in Callum's retrospective comment, not dramatised. This is a minor deviation but the replacement works.

The unnamed partner is deliberately shadowed, which is consistent with the plan ("more implied than explored"), but he's perhaps a little too cartoonish—"ill-fitting suit," "slicked hair," "sallow skin," "actual cartoon villain kit" (Immy even names this). The self-awareness partially saves it, but the character is thin.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong. The exchanges feel natural and character-specific. "Try me" from Leon is perhaps a touch generic, but it's earned by context. The Eleanor confrontation dialogue is the weakest section: "You've ruined everything" / "If your 'everything' involves cool-box traffickers, good" is snappy but doesn't quite achieve the emotional weight the plan promised. The plan specified Eleanor defending her logic ("some people's blood 'does more good'"), and while the man's dialogue gestures at this, Eleanor herself doesn't get to articulate her warped worldview in the confrontation. This is a missed opportunity for the nuance the character profile promises.

TONE AND COMEDY

The darkly comic balance is well-managed. The fairy lights and tiny quiches, Aaliyah's "Oops" into the live mic, the "cursed revision notes" description of Callum's printed screenshots—these are funny in ways rooted in character and situation rather than quips for their own sake. The comedy doesn't neutralise the threat; the syringe lunge is genuinely alarming, and the final unknown smiley emoji lands with appropriate unease.

PROSE QUALITY

The prose is clean and specific. "The hall murmured. Dr Lewis stepped forward" is efficient. "The words were huge and obscene in the school hall, hovering over Eleanor's rigid shoulders" is a strong image. "Her operating system had crashed" is a well-chosen metaphor for Eleanor's character. There's no purple prose to speak of, and the occasional poetic reach ("The blood went cold in my hands" is slightly clichéd but functional) doesn't tip into excess.

The bullet-pointed plan recap mid-chapter is a stylistic choice that works in context—it's consistent with Immy's list-making character and the chapter's planning-gone-messy theme—but it's a slight risk that pays off.

COHERENCE

The chapter is coherent and well-paced. The two-front structure (hall chaos / Lab 3) is handled without confusion. The timeline is clear. The ending—the smiley emoji, Leon's "It's not over"—is appropriately unresolved without feeling like a cheat.

WEAKNESSES

The main weaknesses are: Eleanor's confrontation scene doesn't fully deliver on the character's promised complexity; the unnamed partner is thin; the planned lab stool moment is softened into something less memorable; and the final emoji from the unknown number (a smiley face) is slightly anticlimactic as a closing threat, though it's consistent with the story's refusal to over-dramatise.

The chapter is not a masterpiece, but it's a very competent, often genuinely good piece of YA dark comedy that executes its plan with intelligence and mostly earns its tonal balance.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Immy's voice is well-maintained throughout. The sardonic deflection ("define 'we'"), the self-aware horror-nerd framing, and the way she processes fear through quips all feel consistent with the profile. The line "Would I do that?" / "Yes" exchange with her mum is economical and warm, showing the relationship without sentimentalising it. Callum's catastrophising-but-correct anxiety ("If anything looks seriously off, we film. And we run.") and Aaliyah's theatrical bravado ("Option Chaos") are both on-profile. Leon's exhausted, unglamorous competence comes through well in the door scene.

Eleanor is perhaps the weakest in this chapter — she functions more as a plot mechanism than a character with interior life. Her "I do not use emojis" line is good, but we don't get much of the genuine-believer quality the profile promises. She's more smoothly sinister than complexly motivated here.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong. The group chat exchange is naturalistic without being try-hard. The door scene between Immy and Leon is the chapter's best passage — the rhythm of their exchange ("Neither should you" / "that made no sense") feels genuinely earned rather than scripted. Leon's "If I wanted to kill you, I wouldn't text first. That's… tacky" from the profile doesn't appear, but the actual dialogue serves similarly. The "supply" line landing in the room is a good beat.

Some dialogue is slightly on-the-nose: "This is too much," Beckwith said, low. "We should wait for the full sign-off." This is functional but a bit expository. Eleanor's lines occasionally tip into villain-speak rather than the polished-but-warped true-believer the profile describes.

PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter plan called for: Immy snapping and cornering Beckwith (this is compressed/skipped — she doesn't corner him separately, the confrontation happens in Lab 3), following Leon (they follow him to the lab rather than an alley scene), the alley revelation being replaced by a Lab 3 confrontation, and the key beat of Leon seeing the threatening texts and realising they're off-book. Most of this is achieved, though the structure is somewhat compressed. The plan's alley scene — with its comedy of hiding behind bins and being nearly run over by a mobility scooter — is absent, which is a loss of some of the planned dark comedy. The chapter substitutes a more dramatic Lab 3 confrontation, which works but loses some of the planned mundane-comedy texture.

The "Next trial: Clarke. Midnight selection. 🩸🩸🩸" text that was supposed to end Chapter 4 and drive Chapter 5 is here reframed as "Tonight. Midnight. Lab 3. 🩸🩸🩸" — a reasonable adaptation. The chapter ends with "Tell me everything about those texts" rather than the planned "Leon reads it over her shoulder and swears," but the functional beat is achieved.

SHOW VS TELL

The chapter is mostly showing rather than telling. Kyle's wrongness is conveyed through physical detail ("hollow," "waxy shade," "moved like a puppet") rather than stated. The moment Leon's expression "slipped" and the flash of teeth is handled with appropriate restraint. However, there are occasional tell-y moments: "Something in him crunched" is a bit vague and abstract. "The air in the room seemed to thicken" is a cliché.

PROSE QUALITY

The prose is clean and purposeful. Metaphors are generally grounded: "like a set" for the flat blue evening light, "like a puppet" for Kyle. These land. "Something in him crunched" is the weakest metaphor — it's reaching for something physical to describe an emotional shift but doesn't quite work. "The air in the room seemed to thicken" is a stock phrase that should have been cut. The opening — "The message sat there like a dare" — is simple and effective.

The line "three idiots who should've stayed home" as a chapter closer is good: self-aware, funny, appropriately deflating without being too cute.

TONE

The balance of dark comedy and genuine unease is well-managed. The comedy doesn't undercut the threat — Kyle's hollowness is genuinely unsettling, and Leon's "He's not baseline. He's supply" lands with real weight. The humour (Aaliyah's tartan coat "like a villain," Callum's alibi logic) keeps the chapter from becoming oppressive without trivialising the danger.

CREATIVITY

The chapter doesn't do anything wildly unexpected, but it executes its beats with specificity and character-rooted detail. The door scene is the most creative passage — using a physical threshold as the site of the first real Immy-Leon exchange is well-chosen. The "crucifix" phone gag is a nice touch. The chapter is more competent than surprising, but competence at this level is genuinely valuable.

WEAKNESSES

The main weaknesses are: Eleanor remains underdeveloped as a character with genuine interiority; the planned comedy of the surveillance sequence (bins, mobility scooter) is absent, making the chapter slightly less funny than planned; a couple of stock phrases slip through; and Beckwith's role is thin. The chapter is also slightly long for what it accomplishes — the middle section (lunch, evening prep) could be tighter.

chapter_raw_judge_text.8:
[Analysis]

This is Chapter 8, the finale of the novella, and it needs to accomplish several things simultaneously: deliver emotional resolution without false tidiness, maintain the voice, pay off character arcs, and leave some edges frayed. Let's examine how well it does each.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Immy's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The dry deflection ("Not through lack of trying"), the self-aware humour as armour, the moment where she admits the flattery of being "chosen" lodged under her ribs—these are all true to the character profile. The line "they'll take and take and call it opportunity" landing harder than any text is a genuinely good moment: it's the mum doing what the mum does (gallows humour, working-class pragmatism) and it resonates thematically without being underlined. Callum's anxiety-logic, his bold lie about the visitor sheet "falling," and his final spiral about the thumbs-up emoji are all consistent. Aaliyah's theatrical bravado ("Coordinated nausea," "bipartisan takedown") is well-maintained. Leon's dry exhaustion and the Nokia detail (of course he has a brick) is a nice character beat. Beckwith's wrung-out guilt reads true.

Eleanor's absence is handled correctly—she's "suspended pending investigation," reduced to a rumour, her planner and no title. The plan called for her to be present in the climax (Chapter 7) and then diminished here, which is what happens. The brief mention of her "replaying the moment her perfect story cracked" is appropriately unsentimental.

PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter follows the plan closely. The official narrative is vague and sanitised. Kyle is back but closed off—Immy's "you okay" gets a shrug and a walk-away, which is exactly the "no pat closure" the plan demanded. The meeting with Beckwith and Leon in Lab 3 happens. The emoji reclamation with Leon's single 🩸 and "terms and conditions apply" is executed almost verbatim from the plan. The unknown 👍 at the end is present and handled with appropriate ambiguity—Immy screenshots it, shares it, blocks it, but doesn't resolve it. The plan's instruction to have her "choose not to turn everything into a story" is partially honored: she does block it and put the phone face-down, though she does share it with the group chat, which slightly undercuts the "for once choosing not to" beat. This is a minor deviation.

The "emotional check" beat—Immy alone, thinking about how she'd felt flattered—is present and is the strongest moment in the chapter. "That was the bit that lodged under my ribs" is honest and specific.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong and character-specific. The Leon/Immy exchange about the Nokia and contact names ("Imogen (Do Not Drink)" / "Leon (Definitely Not A Vampire)") is charming and earns its lightness. The group chat exchanges feel authentic to how teenagers actually communicate. Aaliyah's "You resign" to Beckwith is a good beat. The dialogue rarely feels written-for-effect; it feels like these specific people talking.

One weakness: the "bipartisan takedown" line from Aaliyah feels slightly too clever, a bit of a reach for a 16-year-old in the moment, though it's within her theatrical register. The "admin" joke from Callum about the office manager lands well.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT AND EARNED LIGHTNESS/DARKNESS

The chapter earns its lightness because it's built on genuine unease. The thumbs-up emoji is a smart choice—it's ambiguous, slightly menacing, and the characters' reactions (Callum spiralling, Immy blocking it) feel real. The figure in the beanie at the end is a nice touch: Leon watching over, or something else? The ambiguity is appropriate.

The mum scene at the opening is the emotional anchor. It's brief but does real work. The half-truth Immy tells her mum, the mum's line about "take and take and call it opportunity," and the hair-ruffle—these are small, specific, and true. The chapter doesn't over-explain the emotional weight.

Kyle's brief appearance (pale, closed off, mum holding his arm too tight) is exactly right: it acknowledges that not everyone bounces back without dwelling on it morbidly.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter is slightly long for what it needs to do. Some of the Lab 3 scene could be tighter—the revelation about the office manager doing mail merges is a bit anticlimactic and slightly deflating in a way that feels more like a loose end than an intentional fraying. The plan called for "the adult partner bolts" in Chapter 7, and here we get "Richard May, Partner Rep" named and "handled," which is fine, but the office manager reveal feels like it's trying to explain too much of the mechanics when the chapter's strength is in the emotional register.

The line "Like someone had unplugged me" (about sleeping) is a mild cliché. "Bones had dissolved" is slightly overused. These are minor.

The final paragraph—"No grand moral. No neat ending. Just me, my stupid heart..."—is a bit self-conscious about its own refusal to be neat. It announces its own aesthetic intention rather than simply embodying it. The plan called for "a dry line acknowledging the unease is still there, but so is she. No grand moral, just a human shrug in the dark." The execution is close but slightly too explicit about what it's doing.

OVERALL

This is a strong, competent, and often genuinely good chapter. It honors the plan, maintains voice, earns its emotional beats, and resists false resolution. The dialogue is mostly excellent. The character work is consistent and specific. The weaknesses are real but minor: slight over-explanation in places, one or two self-conscious moments, and a slightly anticlimactic mechanical reveal. It's not a masterpiece, but it's well above average YA fiction and demonstrates real craft.

final_raw_judge_texts:
[Analysis]

This is a confident, accomplished piece of darkly comic British YA fiction that largely delivers on its premise. Let me assess it across the key dimensions.

VOICE AND STYLE
The first-person voice of Imogen is the story's greatest strength. It's consistently rendered, genuinely funny, and feels authentically teenage without being condescending. Lines like "Callum's avatar: an owl with glasses. Of course" and "Eloquence" after she says "Oh" to Eleanor land with real comic timing. The banter in the group chat feels natural rather than performed. The British specificity (Lynx Africa, BIAB nails, the arcade with missing letters) is well-deployed and adds texture without becoming a checklist.

The darkly comic tone is maintained throughout all eight chapters with impressive consistency. The humour doesn't undercut the genuine menace; rather, it coexists with it in the way good YA horror-comedy should. The "Nightbite 3: Blood Rebooted" framing device is clever and economical.

CHARACTER
Imogen is well-drawn: brave but not stupidly so, funny but not flippant, scared but functional. Her relationship with her mum is sketched economically but effectively. Callum and Aaliyah are distinct supporting characters rather than interchangeable sidekicks - Callum's data-driven anxiety and Aaliyah's theatrical bravado are consistent throughout. Leon is the most interesting character: the "ethical vampire" who's tired and morally compromised but not villainous. Eleanor is perhaps the weakest - she's more archetype than person, though the story acknowledges this somewhat.

The nuance in characters is moderate. Beckwith is a coward with a conscience, which is more interesting than pure villain. Eleanor's "you've ruined everything" moment is genuinely ambiguous - is she a true believer in her own narrative or a cynical operator? The story doesn't fully commit to exploring this.

PLOT AND PACING
The plot is well-structured across eight chapters. The mystery escalates logically: emoji texts → assembly → lab meeting → midnight confrontation → showcase disaster → resolution. The pacing is generally good, though chapters 4 and 5 feel slightly repetitive in their "we discuss what to do" structure. The resolution in chapters 7-8 is satisfying without being too neat - the thumbs-up emoji at the end is a smart choice that keeps the world open.

The "Richard May" villain is underdeveloped - he appears, gets slammed against a bench, and flees. For someone who's been the shadow threat throughout, his actual presence is anticlimactic. The office manager as emoji-sender is a clever subversion of expectations (administrative banality rather than supernatural malice), though it deflates slightly.

DIALOGUE
The dialogue is one of the piece's strongest elements. It's sharp, character-specific, and funny. "I do not use emojis. If I wanted you here, Imogen, I'd have asked you properly" is a perfect Eleanor line. Leon's "Imogen (Do Not Drink)" is genuinely charming. The group chat exchanges feel real. Occasionally the wit becomes slightly too polished - teenagers in crisis don't always have perfect one-liners - but this is a minor complaint in a genre that permits heightened dialogue.

WORLD-BUILDING
The world-building is light but effective. The seaside town setting (Bayview, the arcade, Glam Claws) is evocative. The vampire mythology is deliberately underexplained, which works - we get enough to understand the stakes without an info-dump. The "ethical vampire with agreements" concept is a fresh take on familiar material.

METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY
The prose is generally clean and controlled. There are some very good images: "the alarm still wailed. In the hall, the fairy lights dripped." The metaphors mostly land. Occasionally the voice reaches for something slightly too clever: "Sleep didn't land so much as creep in around the edges" is fine but slightly self-conscious. "The day had brightened a bit" after the climax is deliberately flat and works well.

There's minimal purple prose. The author knows when to be spare. The "forced poetry" problem that plagues some AI writing is largely absent here.

COHERENCE
The story is impressively coherent across eight chapters. Character motivations remain consistent. The plot threads are tracked carefully. The Crimson Service scheme makes internal sense. The resolution addresses the main conflicts without leaving major loose ends while preserving appropriate ambiguity.

EARNED MOMENTS
The lightness is well-earned throughout. The darkness (Kyle's hollowed-out appearance, Jaya's fear, the cool-box) is genuinely unsettling. The moment where Imogen admits she felt flattered by the envelope is the story's most honest beat and elevates it above pure genre exercise.

WEAKNESSES
- Richard May is a weak villain for such a prominent threat
- Eleanor's arc is underdeveloped; her "you've ruined everything" feels like it needed more setup
- Chapters 4-5 have some structural repetition
- The "ethical vampire" concept, while fresh, is never quite as explored as it could be
- Some of the wit is slightly too consistent - real teenagers have duller moments
- The resolution wraps up perhaps a touch too neatly despite the open ending gesture

PROMPT FAITHFULNESS
The story is entirely faithful to the prompt: darkly comic, British, YA, multi-chapter, and built around the midnight blood emoji text. It executes the brief with genuine skill.

chapter_raw_judges_text.1:
[Analysis]

Chapter 1 of this piece is a confident, well-executed opening that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Immy's voice is the chapter's strongest asset. It's genuinely specific rather than generically "quirky teen" — the Nightbite 3 loyalty, the muted-instead-of-blocked decision, the "You're not getting me with that" muttered to an empty room. These feel like real character texture rather than performed personality. The voice is British without being a slang showcase, dry without being smug. The self-awareness ("Your brain was just helpfully overlaying creepiness like a filter") is exactly the right register — she's smart enough to know she's scared, too proud to admit it cleanly.

GROUP CHAT DYNAMICS: The Cryptids & Chemistry exchange is one of the chapter's best moments. The three voices are distinct: Callum's "statistically speaking" and the owl avatar, Aaliyah's "aesthetic" and theatrical offence at not receiving the text, Immy's sardonic deflection. The dialogue feels genuinely observed rather than constructed. Callum's "At least send a link. Terrible user journey" (from the plan) doesn't appear verbatim but the spirit is captured in "oh good, the murderers are emoji literate." The chat feels like actual teenagers rather than adult writers performing teenagers.

PACING AND ATMOSPHERE: The chapter builds unease effectively without overplaying it. The lamp flickering, the too-still figure under the arcade sign, the "Read 00:01" beat — these are deployed with restraint. The figure under the arcade is the chapter's most effective horror moment: brief, ambiguous, not over-explained. The chapter correctly doesn't resolve whether it's sinister.

PROSE QUALITY: Generally clean and specific. "The fridge fighting for its life" is good. "A gull doing a noise like a demon being blendered" is excellent — funny and slightly wrong in the right way. "Sleep didn't land so much as creep in around the edges" is a nice turn. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard: "the building was shutting its eyes" is a touch precious, and "the dark take me" is a mild cliché. But these are minor.

FORCED POETRY/PURPLE PROSE: Largely avoided. The metaphors that appear are grounded in Immy's voice rather than authorial intrusion. "The sodium-orange seep from the street" is good sensory writing, not purple. The chapter doesn't overreach.

FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter hits its beats faithfully — the Nightbite viewing, the group chat dynamic, the midnight text, Immy's dismissive reaction, the figure outside, the "Read 00:01" unease, the morning bus with Kyle's empty seat. The plan called for a "quiet end" and the chapter delivers: the single blood drop at 02:14 and Kyle's story notification land with exactly the right understated dread. The chapter doesn't oversell its horror.

WEAKNESSES: The lamp flickering is a slightly generic horror beat — it's the one moment that feels borrowed rather than earned. The "It can see me" thought is a bit on-the-nose for a character who's otherwise more oblique about her fear. The chapter could perhaps do slightly more to establish the setting's specificity (the "faded English seaside town" is gestured at but not fully rendered — we get the arcade sign and the high street but the seaside itself is only implied by a distant sea roar). These are minor complaints.

KYLE SETUP: The ending with Kyle's story notification is well-handled — it's the chapter's most effective structural move, converting what seemed like ambient weirdness into something with stakes. "Dead" as a caption, then the empty bus seat, is economical and effective.

CONSISTENCY WITH PROFILES: Immy is exactly as described — sardonic, avoidant, using humour as deflection, horror-nerd specific. Callum and Aaliyah are briefly but accurately sketched in the chat. The chapter doesn't yet introduce Leon, Beckwith, or Eleanor, which is correct for this opening.

Overall this is a strong, controlled opening chapter that earns its tonal balance between comedy and unease. It's not a masterpiece — there are small generic beats and the setting could be more fully rendered — but it's genuinely good YA dark comedy writing.

[Analysis]

Chapter 2 of this darkly comic British YA piece is a confident, well-executed instalment that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the premise. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER

Immy's voice is the chapter's strongest asset. It's consistent, specific, and genuinely funny without being try-hard. Lines like "Flattering poison" (a single-sentence paragraph that lands cleanly), "Eloquence" as self-deprecating commentary on her own response, and the running commentary on Eleanor all feel earned rather than performed. The humour is rooted in character observation rather than quip-for-quip's sake. Callum's "Coincidence" followed by the parenthetical explanation of what that actually means is a nice character beat. Aaliyah's theatrical energy comes through well, though she risks tipping into pure comic relief here — her "emotional support extrovert" line is good, but she's slightly less three-dimensional than the profile suggests she should be.

Eleanor's introduction is handled well. The "politician smile," the laser-accurate envelope distribution, the weaponised politeness of using names — these are shown rather than told, and they match her profile precisely. The detail of her pretending not to hear Callum's "That's not creepy at all" is a small, sharp character moment.

Mr Beckwith's brief appearance is excellent — "Very safe. Very controlled. Right." with the coffee spill and the lanyard peeking out does a lot of work efficiently.

PLOT AND STRUCTURE

The chapter follows its plan closely: assembly introduction of Crimson Service, Eleanor's presentation, the envelope distribution, Aaliyah's exclusion (and her theatrical offence), Beckwith's nervous appearance, and the escalating text. The pacing is good — it moves through the school day with appropriate rhythm, using the canteen queue and locker scenes to show how the Crimson Service gossip spreads organically.

The detail of the girl with the plaster in the crook of her elbow is a nice piece of foreshadowing that doesn't announce itself. The "little patterns" observation from Immy is appropriately understated.

The chapter ends on a genuinely unsettling note — the three dots appearing and vanishing after Immy texts back is creepy in a very contemporary, recognisable way. The final image of the dots blinking once more then disappearing is effective.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is mostly strong. It sounds like teenagers without being a parody of teenagers. The group chat reference ("this screams organ harvesting") is well-placed. The Aaliyah reading-aloud-pompously scene works. However, some exchanges are slightly functional — moving plot information rather than revealing character. The "So. Honest thoughts. On a scale from school charity scam to full occult sacrifice?" exchange is good but the responses are a touch on-the-nose in their exposition of each character's position.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter's main weakness is that it occasionally tells us what to think rather than letting us arrive there. "Flattering poison" is good, but "Little patterns" as a section break feels slightly too neat — Immy flagging her own detective work for the reader. The metaphor "Eleanor walked on stage like the concept of posture had hired a PR team" is clever but slightly overwrought — it's the kind of line that calls attention to itself. The opening "grim soup" playground image is fine but not particularly fresh.

The chapter is also perhaps slightly too comfortable — the unease is present but the comedy consistently wins, which is appropriate for this stage of the story but means the chapter doesn't quite achieve the "quiet uneasy" ending the plan called for. The final beat is good, but the chapter as a whole feels more funny than unsettling, which may be a slight miscalibration given that the plan wanted the weirdness to grow.

The Aaliyah-not-getting-an-invite thread is handled well emotionally — her hurt is real and not played purely for laughs — but it could have been pushed slightly further given the profile's emphasis on this being "real and messy."

CRAFT

The prose is clean and controlled. No purple passages. The sensory details (Lynx Africa, wet blazers on radiators, the faded maroon curtains) are specific and grounding without being laboured. The chapter earns its lightness — the jokes don't neutralise the threat, they glance off it, as intended.

Overall, this is a well-crafted chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains consistent characterisation, and advances the plot with appropriate economy. Its weaknesses are minor: occasional over-signposting, one or two slightly strained metaphors, and a comedy-to-unease balance that leans slightly too comfortable for where the story needs to go.

[Analysis]

Chapter 4 is a strong piece of YA dark comedy writing that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the earlier chapters. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER: Immy's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The sardonic deflection ("If I get murdered, I'll let you know"), the self-aware cowardice ("I'm not spooked," I lied), and the way she uses humour as armour all feel authentic to the profile. The exchange with her mum is particularly well-handled — it's warm, economical, and reveals character without exposition. The mum's line "if they made it sound intellectual" is a small gem that tells us a lot about how well these two know each other. Callum's anxiety-logic ("I like patterns. I don't like being in one") is pitch-perfect. Aaliyah's "I'm choosing to fight dread with camp" is exactly right for her character. Eleanor is present only as a shadow here, which is appropriate for this chapter's function.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is generally strong and naturalistic. The banter feels earned rather than performed. "Human Capri-Sun" is a genuinely funny line that feels like something a teenager would actually say. The Beckwith scene is well-executed — his evasiveness is shown through behaviour (spilling copper sulfate, writing DONNER, flinching) rather than just stated. The "too quickly" beat when he asks "What texts?" is nicely done. The dialogue occasionally tips into being slightly too witty in a coordinated way — Aaliyah's "least sexy sext ever" and "mysterious dom" feel slightly scripted — but this is a minor issue and within the register of the piece.

PLOT AND PACING: The chapter does exactly what the plan requires: escalates from unease to active threat, introduces the "non-compliance" text, shows Immy beginning to track patterns, and ends with the midnight summons. The surveillance of Leon by the blood van is a good scene — it advances the mystery while providing some comic relief (the Pound Saver doorway, "Victorian ghosts"). The chapter moves well without feeling rushed.

SHOWING VS TELLING: The chapter is predominantly showing. Beckwith's guilt is conveyed through physical behaviour. The threat of the texts is shown through Immy's physical reactions (heart jumping, palms sweaty) rather than narrated. The note-taking scene is a good device for organising information without an exposition dump.

METAPHOR AND PROSE: The prose is clean and controlled. There are a few moments of light metaphor that work: "read out 'authorised absence' like it was a spell that made concern optional" is sharp. "Tally marks" for the blood drop branding is effective. The line "the air tasted of salt and chips and something metallic underneath, or maybe that was my brain overcommitting" is good — it's doing double duty (atmosphere + character voice). There's no purple prose or forced poeticism. The writing knows its register and stays in it.

COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent and well-structured. The three-act shape (school day → Beckwith confrontation → Leon surveillance → home → escalating texts) flows naturally. The information is parcelled out at the right rate.

DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The balance is well-managed. The humour never fully neutralises the threat — the "Non-compliance complicates things" text lands with genuine menace, and the final midnight text on the bus is properly unsettling. The mum scene provides genuine warmth that makes the subsequent dread more effective.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly too comfortable in its own competence. There are no real surprises in terms of craft — it executes the plan faithfully but doesn't exceed it in any striking way. The Leon surveillance scene, while fun, is a little predictable in its beats (they're bad at it, he sees them, he disappears). The ending with the midnight text is effective but the chapter's final image — the bus shuddering over a pothole while no one else notices — is a slightly familiar "protagonist alone in the crowd" beat. The note-taking list device is functional but a little on-the-nose as a way to summarise the mystery so far.

FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter follows its plan closely. All the major beats are hit: Kyle's absence, the "non-compliance" text, Callum's pattern-tracking, the Beckwith confrontation, the Leon surveillance, the midnight Lab 3 text. The chapter doesn't quite deliver the "Immy's fear breaks surface" beat as dramatically as the plan suggests — her fear is present but still fairly controlled, which is consistent with her character but slightly undersells the plan's stated purpose.

Overall, this is confident, well-crafted YA writing that serves its story well. It's not transcendent, but it's genuinely good — specific, funny where it should be, unsettling where it needs to be, and true to its characters.

[Analysis]

Chapter 3 is a confident, well-executed piece of YA dark comedy that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the premise. Let me work through the key elements critically.

VOICE AND CHARACTER

Immy's voice is consistent and genuinely distinctive. The sardonic interiority ("like he was being paid per nod") feels earned rather than performed. Her observation of Eleanor's "tiny, stupid power" is one of the chapter's best moments—it's psychologically precise and reveals Immy's class awareness without announcing it. The line "I like being abstract. Less paperwork" is exactly the kind of deflection-as-wit the character profile calls for.

Callum's "ten out of ten red flags" and the two-fingers-on-the-form detail are good character shorthand. Aaliyah's "I'm uncomfortable and intrigued" and her running commentary land well. Eleanor's name-weaponising ("Imogen," said with relish) is a nice touch consistent with her profile.

Leon's introduction is handled with restraint. The blur of movement when Jaya sways, the jaw-clench at her wrist, the "something unreadable" eye contact—these are effective horror beats that don't overplay themselves. His interjection about voluntary participation being "strongly" emphasised is a smart way to show his ethical discomfort without exposition.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is generally strong. It feels like actual teenagers rather than screenwriters' approximations. The exchange between Immy and Eleanor over the form is particularly good—Eleanor's "Bright. Underutilised. Principled" is chilling in its accuracy, and Immy's deflection is character-true. The whispered asides between Immy and Aaliyah are natural without being too quippy.

One weakness: Beckwith's lines are slightly too on-the-nose in their nervousness ("It's all properly regulated... You'll have information, consent forms for parents. Nothing happens without, um, proper procedure"). The "um" and the trailing off are fine, but the dialogue doesn't quite capture the specific texture of a compromised adult trying to sound reassuring—it reads a little generic.

PLOT AND PACING

The chapter moves well. The setting is established efficiently (the swapped strip-lights for lamps, the sweating biscuits, the sterile water bottles—all good specific details). The presentation scene doesn't drag. The Jaya fainting moment is well-placed as a horror beat that's deniable but unsettling. The final text ("Pending. 🩸🩸") lands with appropriate menace and the chapter ends on a genuinely uneasy note rather than a false resolution.

The chapter follows its plan closely: Eleanor's presentation, Aaliyah inserting herself, the forms, Leon's intervention with Jaya, the "pending" text. The plan's beat about "one boy jokes 'What is this, vampire scouts?'" is absent, replaced by a rugby lad's blunter "Blood" comment—a reasonable substitution that avoids a slightly too-on-the-nose joke.

SHOW VS TELL

Mostly strong. The horror is conveyed through implication: Leon's speed, his jaw-clench at Jaya's wrist, Eleanor's assessing gaze. The class dynamics are shown through Eleanor's "underutilised" comment and Immy's bristling response. There are occasional moments where the narration tells us what we've already seen ("Power. Tiny, stupid power"), but these are brief and feel like Immy processing rather than the author explaining.

PROSE QUALITY

The prose is clean and specific. "The biscuits were finally breached" is a nice dry line. "Bottles of water formed a sterile little battalion" is a good image. "A cold ribbon unrolled along my spine" is the one moment that tips slightly toward cliché—it's not terrible, but it's the most generic image in the chapter. The opening paragraph ("the rain had given up and left everything slick") is efficient and atmospheric without being overwrought.

TONAL BALANCE

The chapter manages the dark comedy balance well. The humour (Aaliyah's commentary, Callum's exit-route positioning) doesn't defuse the genuine unease—it runs alongside it, which is exactly right for the genre. The moment where Aaliyah's "joking smile slipped" at the text is a good tonal shift, showing the humour as coping mechanism rather than authorial deflection.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth. Everything lands more or less as intended, which means there's little of the productive messiness the plan calls for. The plan mentions Immy's "half mocking / half snagged" internal state more explicitly than the chapter delivers—we get the snagging but the mockery is mostly externalised through Aaliyah. Immy's interiority could be a touch more conflicted.

The "Arcade Boy / Leon Hart" recognition moment is handled a bit quickly—the plan suggests this is a significant beat (the figure by the shuttered arcades from Chapter 1), but the chapter doesn't quite milk the recognition for its full unease.

Overall, this is a well-crafted chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains consistent characterisation, and balances tone effectively. It's not quite masterclass level—the prose occasionally defaults to competent rather than distinctive, and the chapter's smoothness works slightly against the intended productive messiness—but it's genuinely good YA dark comedy writing.
