Jax keyed shipwide. “We’re down. We refuel, patch what we can, and see if there’s anyone here who doesn’t send reports to HelioDyne for pocket change. No one goes wandering off alone.”

Stitch glanced toward medbay. “What about Lyra?”

Hale answered before Jax could. “She stays.”

Lyra, propped on the med cot, looked up. “They’ll find me.”

“They’ll look,” Hale said. “But this place leaks. That’s good and bad.”

Lyra’s fingers tapped her thigh in a steady rhythm. The symbiote along her side was subdued; its pale tendrils lay flat.

“I can feel them,” Lyra said. “Like thunder that hasn’t started yet.”

“Voss?” Jax asked.

Lyra nodded. “Closer. And the Marshal. He’s… torn.”

“Accurate,” Jax said.

Mara leaned in the doorway, arms folded. “We’ll throw off as much of their trail as we can. You just keep that thing from chewing through my hull.”

Lyra’s gaze flicked to her. “You’re angry at yourself,” she said. “It makes you sharp.”

“Stay out of my head,” Mara said.

“I’m not trying,” Lyra said softly.

Hale put a hand on Lyra’s shoulder. “Rest. We’ll shout if the sky falls.”

“Small sky,” Stitch muttered. “Fewer pieces to dodge.”

On the station, Prospector’s Rest smelled like burnt oil, stale air, and the sour tang of recycled sweat. Lights buzzed. A vendor’s screen looped an ad for a mining outfit that had gone bust years ago.

Jax, Mara, and Stitch stepped into this tired gravity with the gait of people who’d seen worse.

A thin man with a station pass on a lanyard met them at the end of the umbilical. He wore two different boots and a harassed expression.

“Dockmaster?” Jax asked.

“One of them,” the man said. “Name’s Kell. You got credits?”

“We’ve got enough,” Mara said. She flashed a balance on her slate that was technically true if no one counted upcoming fuel burn.

“We charge extra for drama,” Kell said, eyeing their hull scars.

“No drama,” Jax said. “Just gas and quiet.”

Kell snorted. “You docked the wrong end of the galaxy.”

They walked the ring: past shuttered storefronts, a flickering news screen replaying core-world politics nobody here cared about, a cramped bar with a neon sign that read SAL N in sputtering pink. A handful of miners hunched over drinks, shoulders like rocks.

Jax clocked exits, cameras, the way people’s eyes slid off when they saw Mara’s old corporate tattoo. Word traveled even in forgotten places.

In a corner booth, a lanky woman in a grease-stained flight suit played cards with a man whose arm was clearly printed steel. Mara murmured, “That’s Ada. Runs jobs no one writes down. Might sell us a route or a lie.”

“Later,” Jax said. He needed information first.

He ordered a cheap drink at the bar just to pay for loitering. The owner, a broad man with a biosculpted nose that had gone out of style decades ago, poured.

“You seen any heavy corporate ships come through?” Jax asked casually. “HelioDyne spiral. All clean and shiny.”

The barman’s lip lifted. “Ain’t yet. Hear one’s burning hard this way, though. Got a ping to reserve a berth. Noise ‘bout some stolen miracle gone wild.”

“Yeah,” Jax said. “Miracles. Always trouble.”

“You law?” the barman asked, eyeing Jax’s stance.

“Not anymore,” Jax said.

Mara drifted back from a quick circuit. “Station’s got blind spots,” she said quietly. “Service tunnels. Old ore ducts. If we need to move someone unseen, we can. We can also get shot in the spine and no one’ll find us for a month.”

“Comforting,” Jax said. “Let’s get what we came for.”

Fuel was arranged with Kell: too expensive, but they didn’t argue much. Cleaning out the Jane’s lines, rebalancing her reactors—Stitch hustled between ship and station with focused energy.

On the second trip back, Stitch nearly ran into Marshal Briggs.

Briggs looked wrong on Prospector’s Rest. His uniform was too clean, his bearing too straight. He filled the corridor with something that wasn’t quite threat, not quite safety.

“Easy there,” Briggs said, grabbing Stitch by the shoulders before he could topple.

Stitch froze. “Uh. Hi.”

Jax arrived two breaths later, calm on the outside, thoughts tangling.

“Marshal,” he said.

“Rourke,” Briggs said. He released Stitch, looked Jax over. “This place suits you.”

“Trashy?” Jax said.

“Untidy,” Briggs said. A ghost of something like a smile.

Mara appeared behind Jax, posture taut.

“You got here fast,” Jax said.

“Could’ve been faster if I’d jumped when HelioDyne yelled,” Briggs said. “I took my time. They didn’t like that.”

“Appreciate it,” Jax said.

Briggs’s eyes flicked toward the dock where the Jane lay. “She aboard?”

Jax didn’t pretend. “Yeah.”

“Hiding a classified asset,” Briggs said, voice low. “Obstructing an investigation. Harboring stolen corporate property. You’re stacking charges like cards.”

“You going to play them?” Jax asked.

Briggs sighed, rubbed the bridge of his nose. “I read the files they sent. The words they use for her. I also saw the unredacted report from Outpost Twelve. She’s dangerous, Jax.”

“I know,” Jax said. “So’s HelioDyne.”

“I should take her,” Briggs said. “Hand her to a neutral facility. Start an inquiry. Make a fuss.”

“And when HelioDyne leans on the inquiry?” Hale’s voice cut in. She joined them, arms folded. “And on you?”

Briggs looked at her. “Doc.”

“Marshal.”

He hesitated. There was a war behind his eyes: duty vs. the evidence in front of him.

“I can’t endorse you running,” Briggs said finally. “But I’m not dragging your girl out by force so Voss can carve her up. I can put in a ‘jurisdictional dispute’ and stall. That’s the best I can give you.”

“Sometimes stalling’s all we need,” Jax said.

Briggs’s jaw tightened. “Voss is ten minutes out. She’s bringing her own enforcement. You hole up here, there’s going to be a fight.”

“We’re not holing up,” Mara said. “We’re… staging.”

“Staging what?” Briggs asked.

“Something unsanctioned,” Jax said.

Briggs shook his head. “You never were this much trouble in uniform.”

“I was,” Jax said. “You just didn’t see it.”

They parted with no promises.

Back on the Jane, Jax pulled everyone into the cramped galley.

“Voss is docking soon,” he said. “Briggs is going to slow-walk her, but she’ll throw papers and guns. We can’t match force. We can make a mess.”

“Mess I can do,” Stitch said.

“Here’s the play,” Jax said. “Prospector’s has old maintenance ducts tied into central power, atmosphere, security. Mara’s map-hacking. Stitch is going to plant some surprises. We make sure Voss’s toys glitch. We make her overreach in front of witnesses.”

“Use her own arrogance,” Hale said. “I like it.”

“We’ll route Lyra through the ducts to a secondary dock,” Mara said. “If things go bad in the main hall, we put her on a different hull and break contact.”

“Whose hull?” Rian asked.

“Ada’s, if she bites,” Mara said. “She’ll smuggle anything if the price is right.”

“And if she sells Lyra to the highest bidder?” Hale asked.

“Then we don’t let her,” Jax said. “This isn’t clean. No version of this is clean. But I’d rather gamble on smugglers and miners than hand Voss a quiet victory.”

Stitch raised a hand. “Just checking: we are aware this is all half-baked and will probably explode in our faces?”

“Yes,” Jax said. “Get your rigging. We don’t have long.”

He found Lyra in medbay, curling the blanket in her fingers.

“You’re moving me,” she said before he spoke.

“You felt me decide?” Jax asked.

She shrugged. “You think loud.”

“We’re going to try to get you off this station before HelioDyne locks it down,” he said. “You’ll go with Stitch through the ducts. Hale, if she can. Mara’ll guide.”

Lyra looked past him, through the bulkhead, like she could see the station’s guts. The sym stirred, intrigued.

“She’ll come,” Lyra said. “Voss. With papers and soldiers. She thinks she’s right.”

“Are you scared?” Jax asked.

“Yes,” Lyra said. “But not of them.”

“Of what, then?”

“Of what I’ll do if you let them touch me,” she said simply.

Jax held her gaze. “We’re not letting them touch you.”

“You might die,” Lyra said again.

“Occupational hazard,” he said.

Lyra’s mouth twitched. “Stitch is afraid of ducts.”

“He’s more afraid of leaving you,” Jax said.

That got a full, brief smile.

The station shuddered as Voss’s gunship locked on. You could feel the difference: HelioDyne metal kissing tired Prospector’s steel. The hum of richer power. The lights brightened for a heartbeat, compensating.

“Showtime,” Rian said over comms.

In the concourse, Aurelia Voss emerged flanked by armored security. Her uniform looked absurdly crisp against peeling paint. She moved like she owned the oxygen.

Prospector’s locals watched from doorways and bar stools. Kell hovered, twisting his lanyard.

“Station Administrator,” Voss said, inclining her head. “We are here under emergency authority. There is a stolen HelioDyne asset on these premises. We will retrieve it.”

Kell swallowed. “We don’t want trouble.”

“Then you’ll have none,” Voss said. “Unless someone causes it.”

Briggs stepped forward, badge visible. “This station is under Marshal oversight. Any operation goes through me.”

Voss smiled with half her mouth. “Of course, Marshal Briggs. You’ve been most cooperative. Shall we?”

Up in a crawlspace above the concourse, Stitch lay on his stomach, fingers hovering over a jury-rigged switch wired into a maintenance panel. Beside him, Lyra crouched, symbiote pressed close, eyes wide.

“Okay,” Stitch whispered. “When Mara says ‘cut,’ I flip this. Their fancy guns go hiccup. Then we move.”

Lyra nodded. “I can feel their helmets. Buzzing.”

“Please do not pop their brains,” Stitch whispered. “We’re going for embarrassing, not massacre.”

Down below, Jax and Mara walked out to meet Voss with deliberate casualness. Hale stayed half a step back.

“Captain Rourke,” Voss said. “I’m disappointed.”

“I get that a lot,” Jax said.

“Place is perfect, though,” Voss went on. “A dead station for dead ends.”

Mara’s voice murmured over the crew channel, tight. “Positions set. On my mark.”

Voss held out a slim packet. “Emergency writs. Authority to reclaim Project Lyric, lethal force authorized if necessary. You are all hereby ordered to stand down.”

Her security fanned, visors down, rifles gleaming. The locals tensed.

Jax didn’t take the papers. “You want her, you explain to all these good people what ‘reclaim’ looks like.”

“Cut,” Mara whispered in Stitch’s ear.

Stitch flipped the switch.

Half the concourse lights died. Vents coughed. A sharp crack ran through the soundscape as HelioDyne rifles sparked, powered armor HUDs glitching. Voss’s security staggered, cursing.

A murmur rippled through the crowd. Someone laughed.

Voss’s jaw tightened. “Fix it,” she snapped to her tech.

In the ducts, Lyra closed her eyes, breathing slow; the sym flexed, tasting the electromagnetic confusion like spice.

“Go, go,” Stitch hissed. He wriggled forward; Lyra followed.

Below, Briggs stepped between Voss and Jax.

“Emergency or not,” he said, voice carrying, “you start shooting civilians on my watch, I shut you down.”

Voss studied him. Calculated. “Marshal, we have a threat—”

“You have a child you tortured in a box,” Hale said, loud enough for everyone. “Might want to include that in your threat assessment.”

Eyes turned. Phones and slates lifted, recording.

Jax watched Voss clock each lens. Saw fury spark under the professionalism.

Good, he thought. Get mad. Make mistakes.

“Where is she?” Voss asked, veneer cracking.

“Gone,” Jax said. “Somewhere you don’t own.”

It wasn’t true yet. But the ducts echoed with Stitch’s scrambling and Lyra’s softer movements. And sometimes, in the thin places, saying a thing was the first step toward making it real.
step_outputs.12:
The duct was tighter than Stitch remembered.

“Left,” Mara’s voice crackled in his ear. “Then down two levels. Watch the drop.”

“I am watching,” Stitch whispered. “Whole lot of watching and very little liking.”

Lyra moved behind him with eerie grace for someone half full of sedatives and scar tissue. The symbiote rippled along her side, tendrils sliding over metal silently, like it was tasting, mapping.

“They’re confused,” Lyra murmured. “The helmets. Their lights went dark.”

“That’s the idea,” Stitch said. Sweat traced his spine. “We are pro confusion. We are Team Confuse.”

Back in the concourse, confusion was edging toward something sharper.

HelioDyne’s rifles flickered, HUDs rebooting. Locals muttered. Someone in the bar yelled, “Get that corporate shit off my station!” which did not help.

Aurelia Voss’s composure had cracked just enough to show steel underneath. She handed her malfunctioning rifle to a subordinate without looking. “Switch to sidearms. Manual. We don’t rely on temperamental tech.”

Marshal Briggs stood between her line of security and the gathered stationers, palm up. “Stand down,” he said. “We will proceed without frightening everyone into a stampede.”

“Marshal,” Voss said, voice like a polished edge, “you are hampering an emergency retrieval.”

“And you are escalating an already tense situation,” Briggs said. “Last I checked, my badge means something on this ring.”

“That depends,” Voss said softly, “on whether you want it to mean anything tomorrow.”

Jax watched her. Watched Briggs. Watched the way Voss’s people, manual weapons now drawn, started to push the crowd back in a practiced wedge.

Hale stepped up beside Jax. “She’s going to force this,” she said under her breath.

“I know,” Jax said.

The locals weren’t fools. They saw the writs, the guns, the Marshal blockade. Some backed away toward the corridors. Others stayed, stubborn. Prospector’s Rest didn’t have much pride left; what remained bristled.

Voss turned slightly, voice pitched to carry.

“The asset you are hiding is a threat,” she called. “This is not a debate. Project Lyric has already caused the deaths of forty-three people at Outpost Twelve. HelioDyne is responsible for ensuring it doesn’t happen again. Anyone assisting in harboring it will be treated as complicit.”

Murmurs. Forty-three dead. That landed.

“Tell them the rest,” Hale said, louder. “Tell them those people died because you used a child as a weapon.”

Voss’s gaze snapped to her. “Doctor Hale. You’ve always enjoyed dramatics.”

“You vivisect children,” Hale said. “I retire dramatically.”

Jax saw phones still up, recording. Good. Let her show.

Voss took a breath, smoothed her expression. “Last offer,” she said. “Hand over the asset. Walk away.”

Jax was suddenly, bone-deep tired of offers.

“No,” he said.

It was quiet. Simple. It carried.

Something in Voss’s face set. She nodded once to her security.

And that was the moment Lyra and Stitch dropped into the wrong corridor.

They hit the deck in a spray of dust. Stitch cursed, catching himself on torn grating.

“This isn’t Dock Six,” he hissed into his mic.

“You missed the second left,” Mara said, grim. “Backtrack and—”

Lyra grabbed Stitch’s sleeve. “Too late.”

Boots thundered around the bend: two HelioDyne security, visors still rebooting, sidearms drawn.

“Down!” Stitch yelped.

Lyra didn’t drop. The sym snapped awake.

Fear knifed through her; she wrestled it, but it was fast—caught the edges. Tendrils lashed out, quicker than thought, smacking the guns from the guards’ hands, slamming one into the bulkhead. He crumpled. The other stumbled, hands to his helmet, screaming.

The corridor lights flickered as Lyra’s power grazed systems, raw and wild.

“Lyra!” Stitch shouted. “Stop. Stop.”

She squeezed her eyes shut, sucking air. “I’m trying.”

The symbiote froze, mid-surge, tendrils trembling. The standing guard, disarmed but breathing, stared, terrified.

In that sliver of hesitation, more boots pounded from behind.

“Run,” Stitch hissed. He dragged Lyra down a side ladder, the sym scrabbling for purchase.

She left the screaming man alive. Barely. It cost her.

Back in the concourse, Voss’s people moved in, guns up. Briggs stepped in front of Jax, fury finally cracking his professional calm.

“You fire here,” Briggs said, voice ringing, “and I swear I will have you in restraints before your lawyers learn to spell ‘reckless endangerment.’”

“I am retrieving stolen property,” Voss said.

“You are pointing live weapons at unarmed civilians on a station hanging together with duct tape,” Briggs snapped. “Stand down.”

Jax saw the calculation flicker. Voss’s jaw worked.

Then her wrist comm chirped. She listened, eyes tightening, then looked past Briggs to Jax.

“She’s in your ducts,” Voss said. “My men just encountered your pet experiment.”

Jax didn’t let a flinch show. “Sounds like your problem.”

Voss smiled without warmth. “Not for long.”

To her squad: “Teams Alpha and Beta, flush the maintenance levels. Non-lethal if possible. If not, neutralize.”

Briggs grabbed her arm. “You do not get to deploy ‘neutralize’ on a child in my jurisdiction.”

She looked down at his hand.

“Marshal,” she said quietly, “remove your hand.”

For a moment, he didn’t.

That was the fulcrum.

Then he did. Slowly. A choice, and not the one he wanted to make.

“Briggs,” Jax said. “Don’t do this.”

Briggs looked at him, something cracking behind his eyes.

“I have orders,” he said. But it sounded like he was apologizing.

Stitch’s voice erupted over the crew channel. “We have company. Lyra did a thing. Non-fatal! Mostly.”

Lyra’s breath came ragged over comm. “She’s sending more. I can feel them. Like teeth.”

“Reroute to Dock Six,” Mara said. “Ada’s prepping a hatch.”

“I don’t think we’ll make—”

Gunfire snapped through Stitch’s mic. Not directed, panicked. Screams. Metal struck.

In the concourse, some of Voss’s security turned at the sound. Voss’s composure slipped another inch.

“This is escalating,” Hale said. “Do you want your footage now, Voss? Because you’re earning it.”

Jax made a decision that felt like stepping off an edge.

He raised his voice. “Everyone recording, listen up,” he called to the station. “HelioDyne put a child in a box and used her to kill. They are here to take her back so they can do it quieter. That’s the emergency.”

Eyes swung to him. To Voss. To Briggs.

Stitch came back on, panting. “We’re pinned. Two corridors down from where we should be. They keep coming.”

Lyra spoke, low. “I can stop them.”

“No,” Stitch said. “Not like Outpost Twelve. We talked about this.”

“They won’t stop,” Lyra whispered. “They won’t stop and they’ll take me.”

In the concourse, Voss moved. She signaled a flanking pair to sweep around Jax’s group. Tension snapped; a stationer shoved back, a rifle swung, a shot went off into the ceiling. Dust and sparks rained.

Briggs swore. “Enough!”

He yanked his sidearm, firing a warning shot that cracked like a judge’s gavel.

“Everyone freeze!” he barked. “HelioDyne, weapons down. Locals, stand down or get arrested. Captain Rourke, call off whatever circus you’ve got in my ducts.”

Jax met his gaze. “I’d love to. Get Voss to call off hers.”

Voss’s eyes burned. “Marshal, if you cannot control this, I will.”

She reached for her own weapon.

Lyra, cornered in a narrow junction, felt that motion as if it were inches from her throat. Fear surged, white-hot. The sym convulsed.

“Lyra, no,” Stitch said. Too late.

It wasn’t the crude blast of Outpost Twelve. She remembered that. She steered.

Power bent. Switches tripped. Every HelioDyne weapon and visor in that quadrant screamed with sudden overload, shocking wrists, frying optics. Men and women dropped their guns as they sparked, yelping.

In the concourse, Voss’s sidearm spit smoke and died in her hand. Her squad’s rifles clattered uselessly. The crowd gasped.

Lyra sagged against the wall, nose bleeding. The sym went slack, spent.

“Manual only,” she whispered, half to herself. “I only took the guns.”

Stitch grabbed her. “Attagirl. That’s… that’s precise terror.”

Over comm, Hale exhaled a low, reverent, “She’s learning.”

Voss stared at the dead thing in her hand, then at Jax. Behind her, her tech scrambled with a portable console, cursing.

“They’ve disabled our systems,” the tech said. “It’s—it’s not just an EMP, it’s targeted—”

“Project Lyric is evolving,” Voss snapped. The professional facade was stripped now. “All the more reason—”

“Reason to treat her like a person who doesn’t want to be your gun,” Hale said.

Briggs holstered his pistol, as if the decision came with the motion.

“Ms. Voss,” he said, loud, clear. “You are hereby ordered to suspend your retrieval operation pending review of HelioDyne’s compliance with sentient-biotech statutes and use-of-force protocols. This station is under temporary Marshal protection. You will confine your personnel to your vessel.”

Voss turned slowly. “You don’t have that authority.”

“I do,” Briggs said. “I’ve just used it. You can appeal. Later.”

“You’re ending your career,” Voss said.

Briggs’s mouth twitched. “I’ve had a good run.”

Her security looked between them, uncertain now that their toys were dead and cameras were everywhere.

She could have pushed it. Could have started a fistfight, tried to grab Jax, Hale, anyone. For a moment, Jax thought she might.

Instead, Voss straightened her jacket with sharp, angry precision.

“This isn’t over,” she said.

“No,” Jax said. “It’s not.”

She signaled her people. “Fall back.”

They withdrew, humiliated but intact, toward the airlock. Locals jeered under their breath. Phones stayed up.

In the ducts, Stitch and Lyra slumped against a bulkhead.

“We did it?” Stitch asked, incredulous.

“For now,” Lyra said. She wiped her bloody nose with her sleeve. “She’s already thinking of other ways.”

Mara’s voice came in, taut. “Change of plan. Ada won’t take you; she doesn’t want this heat. We bring you back to the Jane and burn out fast while they’re tangled in paperwork and pride.”

“That’s not far,” Stitch said. “Three turns and a ladder.”

“Move,” Jax said. “Before Voss thinks of shooting something else.”

They emerged through a maintenance hatch near their berth. Hale was waiting with a med kit; Mara with a pistol; Jax with his decision writ on his face.

Lyra looked smaller out of the ducts, shoulders curled. The sym lay quiescent along her side like a pale shadow.

“I tried,” she said to Jax. “I didn’t break them. Just their machines.”

“You did good,” Stitch said firmly.

Jax studied her a long beat. “You did better than I have on some days,” he said.

Behind them, Briggs approached, looking older by the minute.

“I’ve put in a provisional report,” he said. “Allegations against HelioDyne included. It will cause noise.”

“It’ll get buried,” Mara said.

“Maybe,” Briggs said. “Maybe not all of it.” He met Lyra’s eyes. “You have my word, as long as I’m wearing this badge—and maybe after—I won’t sign anything that sends you back.”

Lyra searched his face as if she could read the truth there without powers.

“Thank you,” she said.

Voss stood at the mouth of her airlock, watching them go with the expression of someone memorizing debts.

Jax jerked his chin toward the Jane. “Let’s go before she finds a workaround.”

As they hustled up the ramp, Rian called from onboard, “Engines spooled and sulking, Captain. We light up, we’re visible from half the sector.”

“Then we won’t linger,” Jax said.

Lyra paused at the threshold, hand on the Jane’s worn frame. She closed her eyes.

“She feels… kind,” Lyra said.

“She leaks,” Stitch said. “Don’t romanticize it.”

Jax glanced back once, meeting Voss’s glare across the gap. They both knew this wasn’t victory. Just a stolen breath.

Then he sealed the hatch.

“Strap in,” he said. “We’re riding.”

The Calamity Jane tore free of Prospector’s Rest like a punched thought, engines flaring. Behind them, a station full of shaky footage and a Marshal’s seditious report. Ahead, open dark.

On the receding ring, Aurelia Voss watched them go, filing that, too.
step_outputs.13:
The stars slid by like slow sparks.

Out beyond Prospector’s Rest, the dark felt thinner, the usual chatter of beacons and station pings gone. The Calamity Jane ran fast and as quiet as she could, which wasn’t very. Her patched hull hummed, engines a low growl.

Jax sat in the cockpit, chin propped on one hand, watching the vector line creep across the screen. Rian lounged in the pilot’s chair, boots for once on the floor.

“We clear?” Jax asked.

“For certain frontier values of ‘clear,’” Rian said. “HelioDyne gunship’s still docked at Prospector’s, last ping. Probably licking its wounds. Briggs’s cutter broke the other way. No one hot on our tail. Yet.”

“Yet,” Jax said.

The word had gotten a lot of work lately.

Rian glanced at him. “You know she’ll come back. Different writs. Different badge. Same spiral.”

“I’m counting on it,” Jax said. “If they’re busy chasing us, maybe they’re a little less busy boxing more kids.”

Rian made a face. “That is either profound or deeply stupid.”

“Probably both,” Jax said. “Hold our course. Wake me if anyone calls us names on open comm.”

“Copy that.”

Jax left him there and went walking.

The Jane felt different. Same cramped corridors, same scuffed bulkheads, but the air held something like a held breath easing. The crew moved softer. They’d chosen, and the choosing was done. The consequences would stretch on, but the moment itself had passed.

In the galley, Stitch and Mara were at the table. A half-played game of cards lay between them. Stitch was losing, badly.

“Cheater,” he muttered.

“Learn to count,” Mara said without heat.

Jax poured himself a bulb of water. “How’s the fuel look?”

“Light,” Mara said. “Prospector’s gouged us and you jumped like your ass was on fire. We’ve got enough to get to the Driftline or one of the smaller co-ops if we glide smart. After that, we’re bartering.”

“Barter’s honest work,” Jax said.

“Better than organ donation,” Stitch added.

Mara looked at Jax for a long second. “Briggs’ll be crucified,” she said. Not accusation. Just fact.

“Yeah,” Jax said.

“He knew it,” she said. “Still did it.”

“Yeah.”

“You think it’ll change anything?” Stitch asked.

Jax took a sip of water that tasted faintly of old pipes. “I think now there’s records. Footage. A Marshal’s report that doesn’t match the spiral’s story. That’s something. Might not save anyone right away.” He shrugged. “Might save someone later.”

“Might get him killed,” Mara said.

“Probably,” Jax said.

They let that sit.

“You did good work in those ducts,” Jax said to Stitch.

Stitch perked a little. “Saved the day with badly insulated wires. My brand.”

“Messed up the route,” Mara pointed out.

“Character-building detour,” Stitch said.

“Both true,” Jax said. “Get some rest when you’re done losing.”

“Rude,” Stitch said.

Mara watched Jax as he turned to go. “We’re really keeping her,” she said. Quiet.

“Yeah,” Jax said.

“You’re painting a target on all of us.”

He gave a tired half-smile. “Liked is boring.”

He left before he could say something softer. He didn’t trust his mouth.

Medbay was dim, just enough light to pick out outlines. Hale sat on a stool, reading from a slate. Lyra lay on the cot, not hooked to anything now. The symbiote had retracted, hugging close to her skin like a patterned scar.

Hale looked up as Jax stepped in. “No seizures, breathing steady, vitals boring,” she said. “We like boring.”

Lyra rolled her head, watching him. “You never answered me,” she said.

“Haven’t talked yet,” Jax said.

“Earlier,” Lyra said. “I asked if you’d throw me out an airlock if I turn into what they wanted.”

Hale raised an eyebrow. “I missed that part of the consult.”

Jax leaned against the doorframe. “That what’s been keeping you up?”

“And the ceiling flickers,” Lyra said. “But mostly that.”

The sym shifted, as if listening.

Jax chose his words slowly. “If the choice is between HelioDyne carving you up and turning you into a factory line, or anyone using you like that,” he said, “then yeah. I won’t let them have you.”

“That’s not an answer,” Lyra said.

“It’s the only one I’ve got that isn’t pretty and useless,” Jax said. “You ask me to swear you’ll never hurt anyone again, I can’t. You ask me to swear no one ever hunts you, can’t do that either. I can swear we won’t hand you over. And if it goes bad, it’ll be our bad, not theirs.”

Lyra absorbed that. No flinch. “Okay,” she said.

“That’s it?” Stitch’s voice came from the hall. He leaned in, chewing on a cable tie. “You’re okay with that?”

Lyra shrugged, a small, precise movement. “He didn’t lie.”

Hale snorted. “Low bar for trust.”

Lyra shifted to sit up. Hale moved to help; Lyra waved her off, managing on her own.

“I want to stay,” Lyra said. “On your ship. At least until…”

“Until?” Jax prompted.

“Until I know how to not be what they made,” she said. “And then I’ll decide.”

“You’re a terrible passenger,” Hale said. “But medically, better than sending you to a colony that doesn’t know what’s coming.”

“You can’t keep her,” Mara said from the door. “Not like a pet.”

Lyra turned her head. “You don’t get to talk about keeping people.”

Mara took that, winced. “Fair.”

Jax looked at Lyra. “If you stay, it’s not as cargo,” he said. “Not as a weapon. As crew. Pain-in-the-ass, potentially catastrophic crew, but crew.”

“What’s the job description?” Lyra asked.

“Don’t kill anyone by accident,” Stitch said quickly.

“Help Stitch not blow out our relays,” Hale said.

“Help us see trouble coming before it docks,” Mara added.

“Make my life harder,” Rian’s voice called from the overhead. “It’s tradition.”

Jax almost smiled. “And you help us remember why we pissed off the biggest bully in the belt,” he said. “In case we get stupid.”

Lyra considered. The sym, sensing her mood, settled.

“I can do that,” she said.

“Then we’ve got an agreement,” Jax said.

Hale rose, stretching her back. “I’m going to strong-arm Rian into a nap before he flies us into a sun,” she said. To Lyra: “Shout if your brain feels funny.”

“My brain always feels funny,” Lyra said.

“Funnier,” Hale said, and left.

Mara lingered. “Lyra,” she said.

Lyra looked at her, eyes dark and unreadable.

“I signed those manifests,” Mara said. No preamble. “Not just yours. That doesn’t get unwritten because I pulled one box the other way.”

“No,” Lyra said.

Mara nodded, jaw tight. “I’m not asking you to forgive me. I’m telling you I intend to spend what’s left of my life breaking their lines instead of drawing them.”

Lyra studied her. “Okay,” she said.

“That’s it?” Mara sounded almost offended.

“What else do you want?” Lyra asked. “That’s your job. My job is to decide what to do with that.”

Jax caught Mara’s eye. There was something like relief there, buried under the sting. She left without another word.

Stitch stepped in fully and flopped onto the chair Hale had vacated, spinning it around, arms folded on the back.

“So,” he said. “Important crew question, Lyra.”

“Yes?”

“Favorite snack,” Stitch said. “We run on democracy and terrible food.”

She blinked. “I don’t know.”

“Well,” Stitch said, “project for the week.”

She rested her head back against the bulkhead. “I’ve never had a week before,” she said softly.

Stitch opened his mouth, closed it, then just nodded.

Jax slipped out, leaving them.

In the corridor, he almost walked into Briggs’s badge in his mind. He stopped at his small quarters, opened the drawer, pulled out the old Marshal shield. The metal was dull, edges worn.

He sat on the bunk and turned it in his fingers.

“You’re late,” he said to it. “But you showed up.”

On some station, Briggs was probably already being called in, asked to explain his “overreach.” Voss was planning responses, new paths. HelioDyne’s lawyers were drafting narratives.

Out here, in this small pocket of stubborn air, a girl with a monster stitched into her skin was learning how not to be one.

Jax put the badge back. Not thrown away. Not on his chest. Just there.

Rian’s voice crackled from the intercom. “Captain to the cockpit. We’ve got a fork in the road.”

Jax pushed up, went.

At the console, the nav display showed two pulsing options. One toward a bigger border station with more resources and more eyes. One toward a drifting co-op colony with less of both.

“Big rock,” Rian said, tapping the first. “We might disappear in the crowd. Also HelioDyne’s sure to have feelers there.”

“Little rock,” he tapped the second. “Off the beaten path. Freehold, kind of anarchic. Might take us in. Might sell us out.”

Mara leaned over the back of his chair. “We can’t stop moving,” she said. “They’ll look for patterns.”

Stitch’s voice piped in from below, faint: “Vote for little rock! Go full frontier.”

Hale’s tone followed, dry: “The bigger station’s medbay doesn’t make it less corruptible.”

Lyra, from medbay, quiet but clear over comm: “Whichever way you go, I’ll make it worse. You know that.”

Jax looked at the twin points of light. Two doors, same house of wolves.

He thought about Calico’s dry air, about the crate’s thump. About opening it. About saying no to Voss, yes to running. About Briggs standing up until his knees shook.

He thought about Lyra’s small, steady “Okay” when he told her the truth.

“We’re not picking safe,” he said. “We’re picking who we’re willing to owe.”

He tapped the path to the drifting co-op. The line bent.

Rian huffed. “Always knew you were a romantic.”

“Shut up and fly,” Jax said.

The Jane rolled her nose, engines easing into the new course. Ahead lay a little station made of welded dreams and bad decisions, the kind of place where people believed in making their own rules until someone bigger showed up.

Perfect for fugitives, he thought. Perfect for starting trouble.

As Prospector’s Rest shrank behind them, a message came through—tight-beam, low-power. Unstamped. Jax opened it out of reflex.

Briggs’s voice, low.

“Rourke. For what it’s worth, I wrote her down as ‘individual’ in my report. Not ‘asset.’ That’ll make some clerk choke. You keep her alive. I’ll keep making noise until they take this badge off me. Try not to make me regret betting on you.”

Jax listened twice. Then he archived it, not deleted.

He stared out at the stars, a scattered herd.

“Lyra,” he said on shipwide. “That crate you came in? It’s still in our bay. You want me to keep it, toss it, nail it to the nose as a warning?”

A pause. Then Lyra’s voice, thoughtful. “Keep it,” she said. “I like seeing it open.”

“Copy that,” Jax said. “Open it stays.”

In the cargo bay, the HelioDyne crate sat empty, door ajar, restraints hanging slack. The hazard sigils were scuffed, meaningless now. Stitch had tagged it with a sticker: a tiny cartoon skull with a flower.

The Calamity Jane carried on, patched and humming, a stubborn scar in motion.

They were broke, marked, and outmatched. HelioDyne would be coming. The Marshals would be arguing. The frontier would keep grinding people down.

And a girl who had been property had a bunk, a ship under her feet, and a captain who’d chosen one hard truth over a thousand easy lies.

Out here, that counted as hope. Not the pretty kind.

The kind you had to keep moving.

chapter_raw_judge_text.1:
[Analysis]

Chapter 1 of this Space Western establishes the world, crew, and inciting incident with considerable craft. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The prose voice is well-calibrated for the genre: dry, economical, with Western-flavored cadences that don't tip into parody. Lines like "Waystation Calico looked like it had been built drunk and maintained out of spite" are genuinely effective scene-setting—specific, tonal, and efficient. The description of the station as "caught mid-explosion and never finished the job" is a strong image that earns its place.

Character differentiation is handled well through dialogue and behavior rather than exposition. Rian's cockiness ("I've kissed uglier ports"), Stitch's nervous humor, Mara's clipped efficiency, and Jax's sardonic pragmatism all emerge organically. The chicory detail ("tastes like consequence" is implied by the character profile and the prose handles it without over-explaining) works as characterization.

The pacing is controlled. The chapter moves through docking, negotiation, loading, and departure without feeling rushed or padded. The decision to take the job is rendered with appropriate moral weight—Jax's internal calculation is shown through behavior (the math of fuel, food, parts) rather than stated directly.

The callback to Jax's past—"He thought of a different crate, years back, that he'd signed over in a similar dock. He had never seen what was inside that one. He had never asked."—is the chapter's most effective moment. It plants the thematic seed without sermonizing, and it's earned by the character profile's backstory.

The ending lands cleanly. Stitch's voice cracking through the speakers with that ellipsis-laden line delivers exactly what the prompt requires, and the chapter has done enough work to make it feel like consequence rather than contrivance.

WEAKNESSES:

The HelioDyne rep is purely functional—a plot delivery mechanism with no texture. This is acceptable for a minor character but slightly missed opportunity for world-building flavor.

Some of the prose leans on familiar Space Western furniture without fully transforming it. "Recycled sweat and cheap antifungal," "miners in stained coveralls"—these are competent but not surprising. The world feels lived-in but not quite singular.

Mara's behavior—pushing harder than seems logical for the job—is noted in the plan as a deliberate tell, but in execution it reads as slightly too subtle. The "elbow brush" moment is good, but her motivation for pushing this particular job isn't seeded with quite enough tension to make a rereader feel the dramatic irony.

The dockmaster scene is efficient but the bribe exchange ("here's docking, waste, and your bribe") is a bit on-the-nose in its winking self-awareness—it slightly undercuts the gritty realism the prose otherwise maintains.

Hale's warning about HelioDyne biotech is handled well but briefly; her character voice is the least distinct of those who appear.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully and hits all the required beats. The prose avoids purple excess while maintaining atmosphere. Dialogue is functional and character-appropriate throughout. The chapter accomplishes what a first chapter should: grounds the reader, establishes stakes, and ends on a hook that feels earned.

[Scores]

Nuanced Characters: 15
Emotionally Engaging: 14
Compelling Plot: 14
Coherent: 18
Weak Dialogue: 5
Tell-Don't-Show: 4
Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7
Amateurish: 3
Purple Prose: 3
Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3
Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15
Characters Consistent with Profile: 17
Followed Chapter Plan: 18
Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19
chapter_raw_judge_text.4:
[Analysis]

Chapter 4 is a strong piece of character-driven tension that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The dialogue is the chapter's greatest asset. Each character speaks in a distinct register that feels earned rather than assigned. Stitch's "Let's not use the words that make the murder jelly agitated" is genuinely funny and character-consistent. Hale's interjections are surgical and dry. Jax's final exchange with Briggs—"I'm harboring a girl"—lands with appropriate weight without being overwrought. The chapter avoids the trap of having characters deliver moral speeches; instead, positions emerge through clipped exchanges and reactions.

Lyra's characterization is particularly well-handled. Her matter-of-fact delivery of "Then you should kill me" as a contingency plan is chilling precisely because it's not melodramatic—it reflects someone who has genuinely internalized herself as a system to be managed. The line "She said 'back' like 'buried'" is one of the chapter's best moments: economical, image-rich, and emotionally precise.

The reveal of Mara's complicity through Lyra naming her is well-staged. The plan called for this beat, and the execution is clean—Lyra drops "A Kade" without accusation, and the room's reaction does the work. Mara's response ("I signed whatever they gave me") is appropriately damning without being a confession speech.

The chapter follows its plan closely: Lyra's instability is demonstrated (the sym bristling at tension), Mara's partial confession lands, Briggs escalates, and Jax makes the decisive choice to run. The plan's note that this is "the moment he crosses from 'avoiding trouble' into 'harboring stolen property' knowingly" is honored.

WEAKNESSES:

Some metaphors are workmanlike rather than distinctive. "Like someone had peeled back a panel in his own hull" is competent but predictable for a space setting—it's the kind of metaphor that writes itself. Similarly, "The name hit the bay like a dropped tool" is functional but not particularly fresh.

The chapter occasionally tells us emotional states rather than showing them. "Something twist tight in his chest" is a cliché. "Something desperate in her eyes" is vague. These are minor but recurring lapses in a chapter that otherwise shows well.

Rian's characterization is slightly thin here—he gets a few lines but doesn't quite register as the morally compromised figure the plan describes. His grinning terror at "outlaw it is" is fine, but the plan's note about him slipping a ping (which happens in this chapter per the plan) is absent. The ping is actually placed in Chapter 5 per the plan, so this is not a failure, but Rian feels underutilized.

The pacing in the middle section—the options debate—runs slightly long. The back-and-forth of "option one, option two, option three" feels a touch mechanical, like a screenplay beat being checked off rather than organic crew dynamics.

Hale's "Finally" after Jax decides to run is a small but effective moment, though it risks being a bit too on-the-nose as a validation beat.

The chapter's ending—"The Jane's course arced away, off the path of law and into the thin places between"—is slightly purple and reaches for a poetic register the rest of the chapter wisely avoids. "Thin places between" is evocative but feels imported from a different, more lyrical story.

OVERALL: This is confident, well-crafted genre fiction with strong dialogue, consistent characterization, and good structural discipline. Its flaws are mostly minor—occasional clichés, a few tell-don't-show lapses, one slightly purple closing line. It doesn't quite reach the level of genuinely surprising prose, but it executes its plan with skill and emotional honesty.

[Scores]

chapter_raw_judge_text.8:
[Analysis]

Chapter 8 is the concluding chapter of this Space Western, and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: deliver a quiet, earned denouement that avoids triumphalism while still providing emotional resolution. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. Lines like "Until I know how to not be what they made" and Lyra's "He didn't lie" as a basis for trust are genuinely sharp—they carry character weight without over-explaining. Stitch's "I've never had a week before" exchange lands with real emotional economy. Mara's confession to Lyra and Lyra's response ("What else do you want? That's your job. My job is to decide what to do with that.") is particularly well-handled: it refuses easy forgiveness without being melodramatic. The dialogue throughout feels lived-in and character-specific.

The chapter follows its plan closely. Briggs's message, the empty crate, Lyra's decision to stay, Rian's partial redemption, Mara's unabsolved confession, the nav choice—all are present and handled with appropriate weight. The plan called for "messy, not neat" and the chapter delivers that.

Character consistency is strong. Jax's emotional constipation expressed through acts rather than words is maintained. Stitch's nervous warmth, Hale's dry practicality, Mara's self-flagellating directness, Rian's deflection through humor—all track with their profiles. Lyra's "Okay" as acceptance of a brutal truth rather than a comforting lie is exactly right for her character.

The thematic work is mostly shown rather than told. The empty crate as image, Briggs writing "individual" instead of "asset," Jax keeping the badge but not wearing it—these are concrete details doing thematic work without sermonizing.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter occasionally tips into slightly too-neat aphorism. "Not the pretty kind. The kind you had to keep moving" is the most egregious example—it's the kind of closing sentiment that sounds profound but is actually fairly generic. Similarly, "Two doors, same house of wolves" is a metaphor that doesn't quite cohere (doors in a house of wolves?). "A stubborn scar in motion" applied to the ship is reaching.

The nav-choice scene, while functional, is a bit schematic—each crew member gets a line, almost like a roll call. It's efficient but slightly mechanical, and the stakes feel lower than they should given everything that's happened.

Rian's arc in this chapter is thin. The plan called for him to apologize "without excuses" and Stitch to "half accept it," but this is barely present—Rian mostly just delivers quips. His guilt over the ping, which was a significant character beat in the plan, is essentially unaddressed in this chapter.

The Briggs message at the end is a nice touch but slightly convenient—it wraps things up a little too tidily for a story that has otherwise resisted tidiness. The "individual not asset" detail is good, but the message itself risks being the "soft lie" the story has been avoiding.

Some of the prose reaches for poetry and lands in cliché: "stars slid by like slow sparks" is a weak opener. "A scattered herd" for stars is strained. These are minor but accumulate.

The chapter is coherent and follows the plan well. The emotional beats are mostly earned. The lightness (Stitch's snack question, Rian's quips) feels genuinely integrated rather than forced. The darkness (Mara's unabsolved guilt, the ongoing threat) is maintained without being overwrought.

Overall, this is a competent-to-good concluding chapter that delivers on most of its promises, with some minor lapses into convenient resolution and occasional strained metaphor.

chapter_raw_judge_text.5:
[Analysis]

Chapter 5 of this Space Western is a strong piece of craft that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the character profiles. Let me work through the key elements critically.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter's central tension—the crew's response to Voss's offer—is handled with genuine skill. The ensemble debate feels organic rather than staged. Each character speaks in a voice consistent with their profile: Rian's self-serving pragmatism ("walking away alive is a seductive little devil"), Stitch's emotional directness, Hale's blunt moral clarity, Mara's guilt-laden awareness. These aren't speeches; they're people under pressure revealing themselves in short, pressured bursts.

Voss is handled well. She's not cartoonish. Her use of "asset" and "technical procedures" and the Outpost Twelve reveal are genuinely chilling precisely because they're delivered with bureaucratic calm. The line "She thinks she's the only adult" (filtered through Lyra's psionic perception) is a sharp, economical character note.

The Stitch-Lyra scene in the medbay is the chapter's best moment. It earns its lightness. The exchange about bravery versus poor judgment, and Lyra's observation that she can't tell when people's feelings are real, lands with genuine pathos. Stitch's response—"I mostly feel hungry and anxious"—is funny and humanizing without deflating the tension. This is the "well-earned lightness" the plan called for.

The Outpost Twelve reveal is handled with restraint. Lyra's account ("They all stopped") is more disturbing for being understated. The plan called for Lyra to confide in Stitch about this incident, but the chapter smartly has it emerge in front of the whole crew via Voss's provocation, which is actually more dramatically efficient.

Jax's decision to refuse Voss is earned. The callback to the "muted 'Help'" is effective without being overwrought. His final line—"We'll make damn sure everyone sees what they're trying to drag back into a box"—is appropriately unglamorous as a rallying cry.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter has some moments of slightly too-neat aphorism. "It felt like a stone he'd picked up chapters ago and finally had to throw" is a self-aware meta-reference that breaks the fictional dream slightly—"chapters ago" is a jarring intrusion of authorial voice. Similarly, "In the thin, quiet dark between stars, sometimes that was all you had" is the kind of closing sentiment that gestures at profundity without quite achieving it; it's the chapter's weakest line, a bit of purple reaching.

The plan called for Rian's secret ping to be sent in this chapter—a crucial character beat showing his cowardice. This is absent from the chapter as written. The plan also specified that Voss intercepts the ping and alters course, and that Briggs receives stronger orders. The chapter has Voss arriving via FTL shortcut, which covers the plot function, but Rian's betrayal—one of the most morally interesting beats in the plan—is entirely missing. This is a significant deviation that weakens Rian's arc and removes a key source of internal crew tension.

The plan also specified Lyra "confides in Stitch that there is truth in what Voss said: she lost control once; people died" and that "she's not sure she should be free either." The Outpost Twelve moment partially covers this, but Lyra's self-doubt about her own freedom is not really present—she seems more clearly on the side of wanting freedom than the plan intended.

Some of the dialogue tags and beats are slightly mechanical: "Mara's jaw worked," "Hale's mouth thinned"—these are functional but not particularly fresh physical tells.

The "sandwich named after us" line from Rian feels slightly too jokey for the moment, though it's in character.

OVERALL: A competent, often genuinely good chapter that maintains voice and character consistency, delivers real emotional beats, and handles the moral complexity with appropriate restraint. The missing Rian betrayal is the most significant structural gap. The prose is clean and mostly avoids purple excess, with one or two lapses at chapter's end.

chapter_raw_judge_text.7:
[Analysis]

Chapter 7 is the action climax of the story, and the model executes it with considerable skill while showing some minor weaknesses worth examining carefully.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter manages the difficult task of splitting action between two locations—the concourse confrontation and Lyra/Stitch in the ducts—without losing coherence. The intercutting is handled cleanly, and the reader always knows where they are spatially and emotionally. This is genuinely competent structural work.

Character consistency is strong throughout. Stitch's nervous humor ("We are Team Confuse," "That's… that's precise terror") feels authentic to his profile without becoming a tic. Voss's composure cracking in controlled increments—handing off the rifle without looking, the jacket-straightening at the end—is exactly the kind of behavioral specificity the character profile called for. Briggs's agonizing moment of removing his hand from Voss's arm, then his eventual stand, is the chapter's emotional fulcrum and it lands with appropriate weight. Hale's "You vivisect children. I retire dramatically" is a sharp, character-true line. Jax's "No" being described as "quiet, simple, carrying" is a good instinct, though the narration slightly over-explains it.

Lyra's pivotal action—targeting only the weapons, not the people—is the moral and dramatic center of the chapter and it works. The distinction between "crude blast of Outpost Twelve" and the controlled, targeted disruption shows her growth without making it feel unearned. The detail of her nose bleeding afterward, the sym going slack, grounds the power in physical cost.

The dialogue is generally strong. "I've had a good run" from Briggs is earned and appropriately understated. Voss's "This isn't over" / Jax's "No. It's not." exchange is a cliché, but it's deployed with enough self-awareness that it functions as genre acknowledgment rather than lazy writing.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter plan called for a "messy, grubby" shootout that is "not balletic." The actual confrontation is somewhat cleaner and more choreographed than promised. The EMP/targeted disruption is elegant rather than messy. The plan mentioned "bodies on the deck, station holed, everyone now on someone's blacklist"—the chapter delivers a more triumphant outcome than intended, with Voss withdrawing relatively intact and no significant casualties mentioned. This is a meaningful deviation from the plan's tonal commitment to cost.

The plan specified Rian's guilt about the ping being addressed in this chapter ("Rian blurts out his earlier contact; expects rage. Instead gets a flat 'We'll live with that later' from Jax"). This beat is entirely absent. Rian appears only briefly at the end to report engines are ready. This is a notable omission that weakens his arc.

Some of the narration edges toward over-explanation. "That was the fulcrum" after Briggs removes his hand is the most egregious example—the moment is already clear from the action; naming it as a fulcrum is the author intruding. Similarly, "It was quiet. Simple. It carried." after Jax's "No" tells us how to feel about something we've already felt.

The line "Lyra, cornered in a narrow junction, felt that motion as if it were inches from her throat" is a slightly strained metaphor—she's sensing Voss reaching for a weapon, not a physical threat to her throat. It's not terrible but it's imprecise.

"Like a punched thought" describing the Jane's departure is the kind of associative leap that doesn't quite land—it's reaching for poetry and arriving at confusion.

The chapter follows the plan's major beats (Voss boarding, confrontation, Lyra's targeted disruption, Briggs's stand, Voss withdrawing) but softens the consequences and omits the Rian beat. The overall tone is slightly more triumphant than the plan's "messy, high-stakes standoff" with "bodies on the deck."

The Lyra/Stitch duct sequence is well-handled and the symbiote action is visceral without being gratuitous. The "attagirl" from Stitch is perfectly in character.

Overall, this is a competent, often quite good action chapter with strong character work, clean structure, and mostly effective dialogue. Its main failures are a slightly too-clean resolution compared to the plan's stated tonal goals, the missing Rian beat, and occasional moments where the narration over-explains its own effects.

chapter_raw_judge_text.3:
[Analysis]

This chapter executes its central task—the opening of the crate and the revelation of Lyra—with considerable skill. The writing is tight, purposeful, and largely avoids the pitfalls of purple prose or forced metaphor. Let me work through the key dimensions.

CRAFT AND STYLE

The prose is clean and economical. Sentences are short where tension demands it, slightly longer in moments of reflection. The voice is consistent with the Space Western register established in the plan: dry, image-grounded, not overwrought. Phrases like "his past, asking politely" and "choices narrowed, hard as the walls" are earned rather than decorative. The description of Lyra—"Hair in ragged tufts around implanted ports. Tubes threaded into her veins from an IV hub"—is clinical and disturbing in exactly the right way, avoiding sentimentality while landing the horror.

There are a few moments where the writing reaches for effect and slightly overshoots. "Cold as medbay steel" is a competent but not particularly fresh simile. "Like a feeler testing air" works. The line "something pressing under his teeth, the urge to kick this decision farther down the road" is a slightly awkward mixed metaphor—pressure under teeth doesn't naturally connect to kicking something down a road—but it's a minor stumble.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. Each character speaks distinctly: Stitch's nervous humor ("He sounds nice"), Rian's self-interested pragmatism over comms, Hale's blunt authority, Mara's controlled defensiveness. Briggs's brief appearance—"I know your voice, Rourke. No rank. Just name."—is economical and effective. Lyra's first words ("This… isn't Prospector's Rest") are perfectly calibrated: not a plea, not exposition, just disoriented fact-checking that reveals her conditioning. The dialogue rarely over-explains; characters talk past each other in ways that feel real.

CHARACTERS

The characters are consistent with their profiles. Jax's conflict-avoidance followed by decisive action when cornered is well-rendered. His internal logic—"Only choice we got left is who we can look in the eye after"—is in character: moral without being preachy, practical in framing. Mara's tell when Lyra mentions the manifests is handled with appropriate restraint; Jax clocks it and files it without confrontation, which is exactly right for his character. Hale's "Decide" is perfectly in voice. Stitch's "We are guilty. We took the job" is a nice moment of unexpected clarity from the nervous kid.

Lyra's introduction is strong. The detail of her trying on "Lyra" after giving her designation number is quietly devastating and avoids melodrama. The symbiote's behavior—bristling at Mara's tone, tightening when Lyra is scared—is shown rather than explained, which is the right call.

PLOT AND STRUCTURE

The chapter follows its plan closely. The morning argument, the forced issue (crate shuddering, the pale tendril), the opening, Lyra's reveal, Mara's tell, and Briggs's escalating pressure all land in the right order. The chapter's center—the choice to open—is dramatized through genuine conflict rather than a single heroic decision, which is more honest. The stall with Briggs is a smart beat: not a refusal, not compliance, just a delay that costs something.

The chapter ends on a strong note. Jax's final line to Briggs—"Something that calls itself Lyra. And doesn't want to go back in the box."—is the best line in the chapter. It's understated, it carries the weight of the whole chapter's moral argument, and it echoes the prompt's original hook without being cute about it.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter is not without flaws. The pacing in the middle section—the argument before the opening—runs slightly long. The back-and-forth between Mara and Hale covers similar ground twice. Rian's comms interjections, while in character, occasionally feel like they're there to remind us he exists rather than to advance anything. The "old habit" internal note about kicking decisions down the road is slightly on-the-nose for a chapter that otherwise trusts subtext. The moment where Jax thinks "Lie, he thought. Or half of one" is a minor tell-don't-show lapse—the reader can already see it.

OVERALL

This is a confident, well-executed chapter that delivers on its plan without being mechanical about it. The revelation of Lyra is handled with restraint and specificity. The moral stakes are dramatized through action and dialogue rather than speechifying. The characters are distinct and consistent. Minor prose stumbles and a slightly over-extended argument sequence keep it from being exceptional, but it's solidly above average work.

chapter_raw_judge_text.2:
[Analysis]

Chapter 2 of this Space Western is a tightly constructed piece of tension-building that largely succeeds at its stated goals. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions.

CRAFT AND STYLE

The prose is clean and controlled, with a strong sense of physical space. The cargo bay feels real: "night-cycle dim, pale across the stacked containers." The writing avoids purple prose almost entirely. When it reaches for imagery, it tends to land: "the little spike where the word sat," "something old and bitter moved" behind Jax's ribs. These are earned rather than decorative.

The pacing is well-managed. The chapter builds pressure through accumulating small details—the thump, the scrape, the biosign, the Marshal broadcast, the audio clip, and finally the fingers pressing through the crate wall. Each beat escalates without feeling mechanical. The final image of five fingers pressing through the matte surface is genuinely unsettling and well-placed as a chapter-closer.

There are occasional moments where the metaphor strains slightly. "Something old and bitter moved" is evocative but vague in a way that feels slightly evasive rather than precise. "The crate sitting like an accusation" is a competent simile but not a surprising one. These are minor issues in an otherwise restrained prose style.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is one of the chapter's stronger elements. Each character speaks distinctly: Stitch's nervous humor ("Lawsuit's kind of a side quest then"), Rian's self-interested pragmatism ("Maybe we don't just crack it like a party keg"), Hale's clinical precision, Mara's corporate-inflected caution. Jax's "Put a pin in 'not yet'" is a good closing line—understated, character-consistent, and funny without undercutting the tension.

The dialogue occasionally tips into functional exposition—characters explaining their positions rather than revealing them through behavior—but this is a structural necessity in a chapter that is largely a debate scene, and the writer handles it with enough variation in voice to keep it from feeling like a symposium.

CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Characters are well-aligned with their profiles. Mara's instinct to invoke "protocol" and then catch herself is a nice moment of self-awareness. Stitch's "Jurisdiction?" snap is perfectly in character. Hale's clinical delivery of the "Help" audio is chilling in the right way. Rian's "Nope" when he hears the word is a small, perfect beat. Jax's conflict between Marshal instinct and self-preservation is rendered through behavior rather than internal monologue, which is the right choice.

The one slight weakness: Jax's internal conflict is gestured at ("Marshal thinking had gotten him burned") but not quite dramatized with the specificity the character profile promises. The guilt about the prior HelioDyne transfer is present as a shadow but doesn't quite press on the scene the way it might.

CHAPTER PLAN ADHERENCE

The chapter follows its plan closely. The debate, the Marshal broadcast, the audio clip with "Help," the cargo-cam moment with the fingers—all present and executed. The plan called for a "cargo-cam glitch: a flash of something—like fingers—pressing from inside before static," and the chapter delivers this as the closing image, though without the static/glitch framing, which actually makes it more effective. The plan's note about Stitch hearing a whisper but not repeating it is handled slightly differently (Hale plays the audio for everyone), which is a reasonable adaptation that serves the scene better.

EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT AND DARKNESS

The chapter earns its darkness. The word "Help" landing in the cockpit, the automated sedative drip putting whoever-it-is back to sleep, the fingers pressing through the wall—these are genuinely disturbing details that don't feel exploitative. The moral weight of "if we don't open it" is present without being sermonized. Jax's "If you're in there, we're watching. Hang on" is a quietly devastating moment—he's talking to a box he won't open. That's the right kind of uncomfortable.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter is slightly long for what it accomplishes. Some of the middle section—the crew debate—could be tightened without losing character definition. The Marshal broadcast arrives conveniently and is handled efficiently, but it's a fairly standard thriller device. The chapter doesn't do anything formally surprising; it executes its plan competently and with skill, but it doesn't transcend it. The "something old and bitter moved" line is the kind of vague interiority that the plan explicitly warned against ("avoid over-explaining" but also avoid the opposite: vague gesturing at emotion).

Overall, this is confident, professional genre writing with strong character voices, effective tension management, and a clear sense of what it's doing. It's not a masterpiece, but it's a very good chapter.

chapter_raw_judge_text.6:
[Analysis]

Chapter 6 of this Space Western is a competent, often engaging piece of genre fiction that largely delivers on its chapter plan while demonstrating both genuine strengths and some notable weaknesses.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter successfully compresses multiple moving parts—the station setting, crew preparation, Briggs's arrival, Voss's entrance, and the beginning of the confrontation—into a coherent sequence. The pacing is generally well-managed, moving from arrival to setup to confrontation without feeling rushed or padded.

Character voices are largely consistent with their profiles. Stitch's nervous humor ("Please do not pop their brains. We're going for embarrassing, not massacre") lands naturally. Briggs's weary authority and moral conflict come through in his dialogue without being over-explained. Jax's dry economy ("Not anymore," when asked if he's law) is well-executed. Hale's blunt advocacy ("You have a child you tortured in a box") is appropriately sharp and serves the scene's function of turning witnesses against Voss.

The Lyra-Jax exchange in medbay is the chapter's emotional high point. "Of what I'll do if you let them touch me" is a genuinely good line—it captures her dual nature (vulnerable and dangerous) without melodrama. Jax's response ("Occupational hazard") and the brief smile it earns feel earned rather than manufactured.

The setting of Prospector's Rest is efficiently sketched. "SPECTOR'S RE, flickering between colors" is a nice specific detail. The bar, the dockmaster with two different boots, the card-playing smuggler—these populate the world without becoming travelogue.

The chapter follows its plan closely: arrival, setup, Lyra among people (abbreviated but present), Jax-Briggs corridor confrontation, Mara's continued arc, Voss's arrival, and the beginning of the standoff. The plan's beats are hit with reasonable fidelity.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter's biggest structural problem is that it's doing a lot of setup work and the payoff is deferred to Chapter 7. This is appropriate for a penultimate chapter, but it means the chapter ends on a somewhat anticlimactic note—the EMP trick works, Lyra is moving through ducts, and Jax delivers a line ("Gone. Somewhere you don't own") that is more bravado than resolution. The final paragraph's observation ("sometimes, in the thin places, saying a thing was the first step toward making it real") is the chapter's most overtly "literary" moment and is also its weakest—it's a bit of authorial intrusion that tells us how to feel about Jax's bluff rather than trusting the scene.

Some of the metaphors are functional but not particularly fresh. "The station's umbilical snaked out to meet their hatch like a hungry thing" is a slightly strained comparison—umbilicals are standard docking equipment; calling it "hungry" is a reach that doesn't quite pay off. "Voss moved like she owned the oxygen" is a cliché of the "powerful person enters room" variety. "A war behind his eyes" for Briggs is similarly stock.

The Mara confession beat from the chapter plan (telling Stitch she signed off on shipments of "units") is absent. This is a notable deviation—the plan specifically called for Mara to confess more to Stitch, with it landing heavy. Instead, Mara is purely functional in this chapter, managing logistics and giving orders. Her emotional arc is stalled here.

The "Ada" character is introduced (the smuggler with the printed-steel-armed card partner) and then never used in this chapter—she's mentioned as a potential route but the conversation with her is deferred. This is fine narratively but slightly unsatisfying as a setup.

Lyra's psionic sensing of Voss and Briggs ("Like thunder that hasn't started yet," "She'll come. Voss. With papers and soldiers. She thinks she's right") is handled competently, but the "you think loud" exchange with Jax is a bit cute—it's the kind of line that works in a first draft but could be sharpened.

The dialogue is generally solid but occasionally slips into functional exposition delivery. The Briggs-Jax exchange covers necessary ground but some lines ("You're stacking charges like cards") feel like they're there to remind the reader of the stakes rather than emerging naturally from character.

The chapter's lightness (Stitch's humor, the brief smile from Lyra) is well-earned and appropriately deployed. The darkness (Voss's clinical authority, the weight of what's coming) is present but not yet fully landed—that's Chapter 7's job.

Overall, this is a solid, professional chapter that executes its plan competently, maintains character consistency, and moves the story forward. It's not transcendent—the prose rarely surprises, the metaphors are mostly functional rather than illuminating, and the missing Mara-Stitch beat is a real loss—but it's doing its job as a penultimate chapter with skill and without major failures.

final_raw_judge_texts:
[Analysis]

This is an ambitious, multi-chapter space western that takes a simple prompt—something moving in a crate—and builds it into a full narrative arc involving corporate malfeasance, a biotech-bonded child weapon, a fugitive crew, and a crisis of conscience. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The world-building is genuinely accomplished. Waystation Calico's description ("built drunk and maintained out of spite") establishes tone immediately and efficiently. The frontier-space aesthetic is consistent throughout—recycled air, patched hulls, corporate logos half-sanded off, dockmaster bribes. The HelioDyne corporate antagonist feels plausible and specific rather than generic. Prospector's Rest as a dying station with missing letters on its sign is evocative without being overwrought.

Character differentiation is solid. Jax has a coherent arc: ex-Marshal haunted by past inaction, now forced to choose. Hale is the moral spine, Stitch provides levity without being purely comic relief, Mara's complicity is revealed gradually and credibly. Rian functions as a voice of pragmatic fear. These are not deep characters, but they're distinct and consistent.

The dialogue is generally strong. Lines like "Lawsuit's kind of a side quest then, don't you think?" and "I've had a good run" land naturally. The banter feels earned rather than forced. Briggs's voice—weary, principled, conflicted—is particularly well-rendered.

Lyra is the most interesting character. Her matter-of-fact acceptance of her own potential execution ("Then you should kill me") is chilling and believable given her background. Her gradual emergence from sedation into personhood is handled with restraint. The symbiote as a physical manifestation of her emotional state is a clever device.

The pacing across eight chapters is generally well-managed. The escalation from "something's moving" to corporate confrontation to station standoff follows a logical progression. Chapter 2's audio reveal ("Help") is effective. The Outpost Twelve backstory is deployed at the right moment.

WEAKNESSES:

The metaphors are frequent and occasionally misfire. "The Jane settled into her burn toward Prospector's Rest" is fine, but "a stubborn scar in motion" at the end is the kind of closing image that sounds profound but doesn't quite cohere. "Two doors, same house of wolves" is a mixed metaphor that doesn't work. "A scattered herd" for stars is odd. These aren't catastrophic, but they accumulate.

The plot is not particularly surprising. The "corporation ships a person as cargo" premise is well-worn science fiction territory. The crew's moral awakening follows a predictable arc: initial reluctance, revelation, commitment. Voss is a flat antagonist—competent but without genuine menace or complexity. The resolution at Prospector's Rest (Lyra disabling weapons, Briggs making a stand, Voss retreating) is satisfying but not unexpected.

Some transformations feel slightly rushed. Mara's shift from corporate-minded pragmatist to someone making a genuine moral stand happens across a few chapters, but her backstory (she signed manifests, she tried to reroute Lyra) is revealed in a compressed way that doesn't fully earn the emotional weight placed on it. The crew's acceptance of Lyra as crew member by Chapter 8 is perhaps too tidy.

The "tell-don't-show" problem appears occasionally. "He felt the shape of HelioDyne's plan settle like lead" tells us Jax's realization rather than dramatizing it. "Something old and bitter moved" behind his ribs is vague emotional shorthand. These are minor but present.

The final chapter is slightly too neat—everyone gets their moment, Briggs sends a touching message, Lyra gets a bunk and a sticker on her old crate. The emotional resolution is earned in broad strokes but the execution is a bit tidy for the darkness that preceded it.

The Outpost Twelve backstory (forty-three dead) is introduced and then somewhat underplayed. Lyra's capacity for mass harm is stated but never truly felt as a threat to the protagonists, which reduces tension.

OVERALL ASSESSMENT:

This is competent, often engaging genre fiction with genuine craft in dialogue, world-building, and character differentiation. It's not a masterpiece—the plot is predictable, the antagonist is thin, some metaphors misfire, and the resolution is somewhat tidy—but it demonstrates real skill. The writing is confident without being purple, the characters are distinct without being deep, and the emotional beats mostly land.

chapter_raw_judges_text.1:
[Analysis]

Chapter 1 of this Space Western establishes the world, crew, and inciting incident with considerable craft. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The prose voice is well-calibrated for the genre: dry, economical, with Western-flavored cadences that don't tip into parody. Lines like "Waystation Calico looked like it had been built drunk and maintained out of spite" are genuinely effective scene-setting—specific, tonal, and efficient. The description of the station as "caught mid-explosion and never finished the job" is a strong image that earns its place.

Character differentiation is handled well through dialogue and behavior rather than exposition. Rian's cockiness ("I've kissed uglier ports"), Stitch's nervous humor, Mara's clipped efficiency, and Jax's sardonic pragmatism all emerge organically. The chicory detail ("tastes like consequence" is implied by the character profile and the prose handles it without over-explaining) works as characterization.

The pacing is controlled. The chapter moves through docking, negotiation, loading, and departure without feeling rushed or padded. The decision to take the job is rendered with appropriate moral weight—Jax's internal calculation is shown through behavior (the math of fuel, food, parts) rather than stated directly.

The callback to Jax's past—"He thought of a different crate, years back, that he'd signed over in a similar dock. He had never seen what was inside that one. He had never asked."—is the chapter's most effective moment. It plants the thematic seed without sermonizing, and it's earned by the character profile's backstory.

The ending lands cleanly. Stitch's voice cracking through the speakers with that ellipsis-laden line delivers exactly what the prompt requires, and the chapter has done enough work to make it feel like consequence rather than contrivance.

WEAKNESSES:

The HelioDyne rep is purely functional—a plot delivery mechanism with no texture. This is acceptable for a minor character but slightly missed opportunity for world-building flavor.

Some of the prose leans on familiar Space Western furniture without fully transforming it. "Recycled sweat and cheap antifungal," "miners in stained coveralls"—these are competent but not surprising. The world feels lived-in but not quite singular.

Mara's behavior—pushing harder than seems logical for the job—is noted in the plan as a deliberate tell, but in execution it reads as slightly too subtle. The "elbow brush" moment is good, but her motivation for pushing this particular job isn't seeded with quite enough tension to make a rereader feel the dramatic irony.

The dockmaster scene is efficient but the bribe exchange ("here's docking, waste, and your bribe") is a bit on-the-nose in its winking self-awareness—it slightly undercuts the gritty realism the prose otherwise maintains.

Hale's warning about HelioDyne biotech is handled well but briefly; her character voice is the least distinct of those who appear.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully and hits all the required beats. The prose avoids purple excess while maintaining atmosphere. Dialogue is functional and character-appropriate throughout. The chapter accomplishes what a first chapter should: grounds the reader, establishes stakes, and ends on a hook that feels earned.
