“If it’s about us watching each other,” she said, “we remove one variable. I’m on the couch, you’re in the bed, doors open. If anything… weird happens, we’re not breathing in each other’s faces when it does.”

“It’s going to wreck your back,” I said.

“It’s one night,” she said. “It’s a stupid little test. You like tests.”

The jab was fair.

“And if it’s nothing?” I asked.

“Then it’s nothing,” she said. “Then I can say, ‘See, my brain made a monster again,’ and you can argue with me in the morning.”

“And if it’s something?” I said.

“Then at least we’ll know it’s not just your proximity making me insane,” she said.

“You’re not insane,” I said, on reflex.

She raised an eyebrow. “That’s not the opinion of the State of Oregon.”

“That was years ago,” I said.

“It doesn’t feel like years,” she murmured.

She took a breath. “Please. One night. No pretending, no recording, no staring. We just… sleep separately. Like roommates after a fight.”

I hesitated. It felt like giving in to the narrative I’d been fighting. It also felt like the lowest-risk experiment in the world.

“Okay,” I said. “One night.”

Relief flooded her face so fast it hurt to see. “Thank you.”

She reached for my hand like she was going to squeeze it, then stopped herself halfway. Let it drop.

—

We made a show of it that evening. Mara dragged the old futon mattress into the living room and made a nest of blankets. “It’s like camping,” she said. “Without trees. Or friends.” She laughed at her own not-joke.

I set up the bedroom like normal. She poked her head in as I fluffed a pillow.

“If you wake up and feel weird,” she said, “call me. Don’t… wander.”

“I don’t wander,” I said.

“You’ve been standing in doorways,” she said.

I had never actually told her that. “How do you know that?”

She touched the doorframe, fingertips finding shallow divots I hadn’t noticed. “You leave marks,” she said.

We looked at each other. She shook it off with an effort. “I’m serious,” she said. “No heroics. Just yell.”

“Yes, ma’am,” I said, trying for levity. It came out thin.

She went back to her nest. I brushed my teeth, turned off the lights, left the bedroom door open. The hallway motion light pulsed faintly.

Lying alone in the bed, I felt more exposed than I had with her beside me. The empty half of the mattress might as well have been a sinkhole.

I told myself: This is good. We’ll get through one boring, dreamless night, and in the morning we’ll feel silly.

I closed my eyes.

—

Sometime deep, the quiet rearranged itself.

I surfaced to the creak of a floorboard. Not the house settling; a weight.

Eyes open.

The motion light in the hall glowed, painting a soft red frame. In it, a figure stood at the doorway.

“Ethan?” I called, before my brain caught up to the stupidity of that.

The figure stepped forward. The light didn’t follow. For a moment it was just shadow, denser than the dark.

Then Mara’s voice, from the living room. “Ethan?”

The shape by the door paused. Turned its head, as if considering.

I sat up too fast; the blood roared in my ears. The figure vanished—not melted, not theatrically, just gone when my eyes refocused. The hall was empty.

“Yeah?” I called back, voice high.

She appeared at the far end of the hallway, wrapped in a blanket. Real. Sleep-creased. “Did you call me?” she asked.

“No,” I lied. “Bad dream.”

She studied me from there. “Are you okay?”

“I’m fine,” I said. “Go back to sleep.”

“Don’t tell me what to do,” she said automatically. Then, softer: “You look wrong.”

“I’m just tired,” I said.

“Me too,” she murmured.

She shuffled back to the couch. The motion light flared and dimmed with her passing.

I lay down, heart pounding. Stared at the ceiling, at the doorway, at the absence in the hall. Tried to reconstruct the silhouette—had it been my height? Hers? An invented blob?

I didn’t sleep much after that.

—

In the thin blue of morning, I found Mara already awake, sitting cross-legged on the futon with a blanket around her shoulders, staring at the blank TV screen.

“How’d you sleep?” I asked.

She looked up. Her eyes were huge. “You came in here,” she said.

“No,” I said. “I didn’t.”

“You did,” she said. “You stood right there.” She pointed to the space by the couch. “You just… stood.”

I tasted metal again. “How long?”

“I don’t know.” Her voice cracked. “Long enough that I started to think if I moved you’d break.”

“I don’t remember,” I said. It felt like a confession and a plea.

“I know,” she said. “That’s why I think it’s you now.”

There was no accusation in it, only terror. That was worse.

Behind that terror, something else flickered in her eyes: recognition. Like she was seeing the shape of an old story slotting over a new one, and finding the edges match too neatly.

“I’m calling Haynes,” I said. “Today. For both of us.”

“Okay,” she said.

We both knew I should have called sooner.

In my head, a quieter voice said: Or you already did.
step_outputs.12:
We didn’t call Haynes.

I told myself I would. I even pulled up the number, thumb hovering over the green icon while Mara watched me from the couch, blanket around her shoulders like armor.

“Do it,” she said.

“I will,” I said. “I just have a call in five. I’ll do it after.”

She looked at me for a beat too long, then nodded. “Okay.”

I took my laptop into the second bedroom and let a marketing manager from Des Moines talk at me about engagement metrics while my brain screamed. By the time the call was over, there was another email, a Slack ping, a small fire. It was easy to shelve the call in the name of “just after this.”

By noon, the day had crusted over with normal. Call. Email. Snack. Pretend.

At two, I checked on Mara. She was in the bedroom, standing by my side of the bed, hand resting lightly on the headboard.

“What are you doing?” I asked.

“Counting,” she said.

“Counting what?”

Her fingers traced four shallow grooves in the wood I hadn’t noticed. “You dig in when you stand here,” she said. “You do this thing with your hand.”

“I don’t,” I said. The denial felt childish.

She looked at me, eyes tired. “I don’t think you know what you do when you’re not in there.” She tapped her own forehead.

“I’m calling Haynes,” I said again, guilty. “I swear.”

She shrugged. “Might as well invite everyone to the show.”

That stung, because it wasn’t fair, and because it was.

“I’m scared of what I’ll say,” I admitted. “I don’t know how to tell him this without sounding like we’ve both fallen into a subreddit.”

“Try: ‘I’ve been getting up at night and looming over my girlfriend while she’s awake,’” she said. “That’s a start.”

Her jokes were getting meaner. They were also not undeserved. I folded my arms. “We can’t keep… circling.”

“I know,” she said. “So let’s not.”

She walked past me, into the hall, and grabbed a roll of masking tape from the box by the door. On the way back, she snagged the little motion light.

“What are you doing?” I asked.

She tore a strip of tape with her teeth. “We’re going to make it less comfortable,” she said. “For whatever it is.”

“You read that on your ghost forum?” I tried to keep the derision out of my voice. Failed.

“I read it in my head,” she said. “Don’t humor me if you’re not going to commit.”

She stuck the motion light high on the wall across from the bed, aimed at the doorway. She taped a cardboard flap so it’d only trigger when something taller than the bed moved.

“If we’re up, it’s on. If something comes, we see it,” she said. “No more ‘I must’ve dreamed it.’”

“Unless it’s the cat we don’t have,” I said.

“Then we’ll get a cat,” she said.

She went to the dresser, picked up the mirror we’d yet to hang, and turned it to face the wall. “No extra angles,” she muttered.

“Mara,” I said carefully, “this is starting to feel like—”

“Like I’m doing ritual,” she said. “Yeah. If it’s just us, it’s silly. If it’s not, it’s insurance. You do your research, I’ll do mine.”

Her hands shook as she taped a paper eye over the alarm clock display. It was both absurd and heartbreaking.

“Tonight,” she said, “we don’t pretend. If one of us wakes up weird, we wake the other up. We don’t… freeze. We don’t watch.”

“And if I end up standing somewhere again?” I asked.

“You let me see you,” she said. “The real you. Not that—” she fluttered her fingers, searching—“visitor mode.”

“If I’m in visitor mode, how do I stop?”

Her mouth tightened. “Maybe you can’t. Maybe I can.”

Great. Comforting.

—

We made a point of being together that evening, as if proximity could muscle out intrusion. We cooked. She chopped too aggressively; I took the knife gently from her. We played a board game we’d never finished unpacking. We talked about anything but sleep.

At nine, she disappeared into the bedroom. When I followed, she’d changed into one of my old shirts and leggings, hair in a loose braid.

She had drawn a small, simple symbol on the headboard above our pillows: two short lines crossing, an eye in the center. It looked like a child’s ward against monsters.

“I know,” she said preemptively. “It’s nothing. But I don’t care.”

“I didn’t say anything,” I said.

“You thought it,” she said.

She lay down on her side facing me. “You gonna call Haynes before bed?” she asked.

I stared at the phone on the nightstand. “He’s not on call at ten p.m.”

“We have voicemail,” she said.

“I’ll do it tomorrow,” I said. The words felt thick.

She watched me. “Okay,” she said. “Tomorrow.”

We turned off the light. The room folded into familiar dark, now with the faint red eye of the motion sensor opposite the bed. The hall was a hollow throat.

“Remember,” she whispered, close enough that her breath warmed my mouth, “just say my name.”

“I remember,” I said.

We lay there, facing each other, pretending we weren’t waiting.

—

Sometime after two, I surfaced.

It wasn’t like before, the slow bleed between states. It was abrupt, as if someone had cracked ice over my head.

My eyes were closed. My body felt wrong.

I was on my side, facing Mara. I could feel the heat of her, the soft puff of her breath on my lips. I could also feel—distinct, separate—the urge to stay perfectly still. To see how long she’d watch me before she caught me not sleeping.

Pretend, something inside me suggested. Just stay. Let’s see.

I recognized it this time. Not a voice, exactly. A pressure wearing words.

“No,” I whispered, surprising myself.

Her breath hitched. “Ethan?”

I opened my eyes. She was inches away, eyes wide open, glossy in the faint light. Not blank. Terrified.

“You’re here,” she said.

“Yeah,” I said. My throat was sand. “I’m here.”

From the hall, a creak. Then the softest click: the motion sensor flicking on.

Red light bloomed on the wall behind her. It carved her face into planes. Over her shoulder, I saw the doorway.

A figure stood there.

My brain did dumb math: height, width, familiarity. It was me. It was my shape, my hoodie from yesterday, my slump. It shouldn’t have been; I was in the bed, heart detonating.

“Don’t pretend,” Mara whispered.

The other-me at the doorway tilted its head. In the red wash, its eyes were holes.

“Say my name,” she whispered urgently, fingers digging into my arm.

“Mara,” I said, but I was looking at him.

The shape stepped forward. The motion light stayed on, tracking.

“Ethan,” it said.

My name, in my voice, flat.

Mara made a strangled sound. “No,” she said. “You don’t—”

The thing moved wrong. Joints bending a fraction too far, like a bad video with dropped frames. In two jerks it was at the foot of the bed, then beside her.

I tried to sit up. My body locked.

The hand that reached toward her cheek was mine.

“Almost,” it said.

“No,” I said, or tried to. My chest clenched.

Mara shoved at it—at me. “Get out,” she hissed. “You don’t get him.”

Its head snapped toward her, something like amusement flickering in the dead gaze.

“Always you,” it said. My mouth forming words I hadn’t thought.

It pressed my—its—hand over her mouth. Not hard enough to hurt, but firm. Intimate. Proprietary.

Rage ripped through me. I pushed, not with arms that didn’t move, but with whatever part of me was being edged aside.

Mine, I thought. Not in the sense of possession, but of responsibility. This is my body. This is my voice. You don’t get to use them on her.

For a second, pressure built. Like two magnets forced together the wrong way.

The hand on her mouth faltered. Mara tore away, gasping.

“Ethan!” she barked. “Pick. You pick.”

I latched onto my name like a rope.

“Stop,” I croaked. The word came out through both mouths at once, layered.

The other-me froze. The motion light flickered.

Mara scrambled away, putting the headboard symbol between her and us like it was a shield. “You leave him,” she said to the wrong me. “Take me if you have to. You already know how.”

The shape turned toward her fully. Tilted its head. If something like this could look thoughtful, it did.

“Already did,” it said.

Then the light blew.

Black.

I don’t know how long there was nothing.

—

When I came back, I was on the floor, shoulder against the nightstand. The real lamp was on, buzzing faintly. My throat hurt. My chest felt bruised inside.

Mara was in the corner of the bed, pressed against the wall, knees to her chest. Her face was streaked with tears. She held the lamp cord in one hand like a weapon.

“You with me?” she asked.

I swallowed. “I think so.”

She scanned my face the way I’d been scanning hers for nights. “Say something only you would say.”

“I didn’t call Haynes,” I said. “Because I was scared of how it would sound.”

“Okay,” she said, a sharp exhale that was almost a laugh. “That’s sad, but that’s you.”

“What happened?” I asked.

She shook her head. “It tried. You fought. That’s… that’s the headline.”

“I saw—”

“I know,” she cut in. “Me too.”

We sat in the buzzing light.

“We need to get out of here,” I said.

She closed her eyes. “It’s not the house.”

“I know,” I said. That was the problem.

“Tomorrow,” she said. “We pack tomorrow. We go… somewhere. My mom’s. Motel. Anywhere.”

“Okay,” I said. This time I meant it in a marrow-deep way.

She climbed down off the bed, legs trembling, and came toward me slowly, like I was a strange dog.

“Can I touch you?” she asked.

“Yes,” I said.

She put her hand against my cheek. Her palm was warm. I leaned into it like a starving thing.

“If it gets back in,” she said quietly, “I’ll know.”

“What will you do?” I asked.

Her eyes were endlessly tired. “Whatever I have to.”

I wanted that to comfort me. It didn’t.

Later, when the lamp stayed on and we lay there not sleeping, Mara’s breathing uneven and my muscles twitching with phantom resistance, I thought about the choice she’d laid out: pick.

I’d tried. I’d pushed. I’d won something, for now.

But under the crackle of the bulb and the soft tick of the house, I could feel it sulking at the edges.

It wasn’t done.

And in a small, rotten corner of myself, I registered another truth I hated: a piece of whatever that was had liked wearing me. And a piece of me had liked the clarity—no guilt, no doubt, just action.

Almost, it had said.

Not yet, I thought back.
step_outputs.13:
We didn’t leave tomorrow.

Tomorrow came with dishes in the sink and an email from my boss and a low, animal exhaustion that made the idea of packing up our lives feel like moving a mountain with a spoon. The world outside the house went on as if nothing had shifted: trucks on the road, a dog barking somewhere down the hill, the mail folded wrong.

“We said we’d go,” Mara said, mug clutched in both hands. She hadn’t taken her meds the night before. The bottle sat between us like an accusation.

“We will,” I said. “I just… we should have a plan. Call your mom. Figure out what we tell people. Cancel the lease.”

“We tell them we have black mold and a demon,” she said. “They’ll understand.”

“Mara.”

She set the mug down. Her hands were shaking. “I can’t sleep here again,” she said. “I can’t sleep with you again. Not until—”

“Until what?” I asked. “I get a certificate that says ‘free of contamination’?”

“You joke like it makes you human,” she said. “It doesn’t.”

That stung. “I fought it,” I said. “Last night. You saw. I pushed it out.”

“For now,” she said. “It’s patient.”

“You sound like those posts,” I snapped.

“And you sound like Nate,” she shot back, fast and ugly.

We both froze.

“I didn’t mean that,” she said, instant regret.

“Yeah,” I said. “You did.” The hurt went in and sat down.

She closed her eyes. “I’m scared.”

“I am too,” I said. It was the easiest truth.

She looked at me like she was trying to see under my skin. “I know,” she said. “That’s what it likes.”

We moved around each other for the rest of the day in careful arcs. I sent a terse email about taking sick leave. She opened and closed boxes without putting anything in. The call to her mother hovered between us; neither of us made it.

By late afternoon, a sullen rain had started, tapping at the windows. The light went flat. The house felt smaller.

“I don’t want to be alone,” she said.

“Me neither,” I said.

“In the same room, I mean,” she said. “I don’t want to sleep with you, but I don’t want to sleep away from you.”

“We won’t sleep,” I said. “Problem solved.”

She huffed a humorless breath. “Cute.”

“Let’s do the couch,” I said. “Movie on. Lights on. If we pass out, we pass out there. Neutral ground.”

“No bed,” she said.

“No bed,” I agreed.

We dragged blankets into the living room like kids making a fort. Put on a streaming show we’d seen before so there’d be no surprises, just bright colors and canned jokes. We left the lamps on, their yellow light fighting with the cold blue from the TV.

She sat pressed against my side, arms wrapped around her knees. Every time I shifted, she tensed, then forced herself to relax.

“I hate this,” she said quietly.

“I know,” I said.

“I hate that I’m waiting for you to do something,” she said. “I hate that I’m waiting to see if your eyes go wrong.”

“I hate that you have a reason to,” I said.

Onscreen, someone fell through a sitcom coffee table. We didn’t laugh.

Around midnight, her head dropped on my shoulder. At one, her hand slackened on my sleeve. At one-thirty, her breathing slipped into a slow rhythm.

“You should go to bed,” I whispered, more habit than anything.

“Don’t,” she murmured, not really awake. “Stay.”

“I’m right here,” I said.

That was the last thing I remember saying as myself.

—

Sleep took me sideways this time, not down. One second: couch, lamp, the hum of the TV menu. Next: standing.

It had gotten good at that.

I was at the edge of the living room, in the threshold to the hall. Mara was a blue shape on the couch, wrapped in blankets.

Don’t move, I told my body.

It moved.

Step by quiet step, padding closer. The lamps seemed dimmer, their light oily, not reaching me. The air thickened.

I watched my own hand reach out, hover over Mara’s face. She stirred, lashes fluttering.

Say her name, I thought, desperate, a fish slamming against glass.

“There you are,” my mouth whispered.

She woke.

Her eyes opened onto my face inches from hers. For a heartbeat, all I saw in them was raw, animal fear. No reason, no rationalization. Just the body recognizing threat.

“Ethan,” she breathed.

And here’s the part I will never stop inventorying, never stop cutting apart: I smiled.

I felt it. A slow curl of lips, a weightless, alien pleasure in how small she looked. Like a rabbit you could close your hand around.

Then—mercifully—that wrong satisfaction sparked my rage. It gave me leverage.

“No,” I said, or thought. “No. No.”

It buckled. The hand that had started to trace her cheek jerked. Nails dug into my own palm.

She saw it. “Pick,” she whispered again. Not pleading this time. Commanding.

“I’m trying,” I said. Both of us.

My knees hit the floor. The room snapped back into focus, colors too sharp. I was me, panting, staring at my own hands as if they might still move without permission.

Mara was on her feet, backs up against the armrest, wild-eyed. She grabbed the heavy ceramic possum mug from the table and held it like a weapon.

“Is it you?” she demanded.

“Yes,” I said. My throat burned. “It— I don’t—”

She watched a second longer, then believed me, or decided to. The mug lowered an inch.

“Okay,” she said. “Okay.”

“We have to go,” I said. “Now.”

“In the middle of the night?” she asked.

“Yes.”

The rain had turned to a hard, steady sheet. The highway would be slick. My eyes felt full of sand. None of it mattered.

“We throw some shit in bags, we get in the car, we drive to—”

“To where it hasn’t already been,” she said. Not really cynical; just resigned.

“Anywhere with people,” I said. “Anywhere we’re not alone.”

She nodded slowly. “Okay.”

We moved fast then. Pajamas under coats. Tossed chargers, wallets, a handful of shirts into a duffel. She grabbed her sketchbook and meds. I took the laptop, my keys.

In the bedroom, the little warded eye she’d drawn on the headboard stared back. The grooves in the wood by my spot looked deeper.

“You good?” I called.

“No,” she called back. “But I’m ready.”

We met at the front door. I reached for her bag.

“Let me,” I said.

She hesitated.

“You have to trust me,” I said. I meant: I need you to.

She let me take it. That small act of faith hurt more than any accusation.

I opened the door. Rain blew in, needling my face. The porch glistened.

“Come on,” I said.

She didn’t move.

Her gaze was fixed on my face, searching. Not theatrical. Not dramatic. Just a woman who has been here before.

“Say something,” she said. “Say… tell me about the dumb toaster.”

“What?” A stupid, human prompt.

“The toaster,” she said. “You read reviews for an hour and then bought one that only half works and you pretend it’s fine.”

“It’s a good toaster,” I said, automatic defensiveness kicking in. “You put the bread in wrong.”

She exhaled, something easing. “There you are,” she said.

I almost laughed.

Behind her eyes, I saw calculation. If it comes back, I’ll know. If it comes back, I’ll stop it.

We stepped out into the rain.

For a second, as I locked the door, a treacherous thought slid through me: It’s not the house. It’ll come with us. This is pointless.

It didn’t sound like fear. It sounded like mild annoyance, like about the weather.

I stiffened.

“What?” Mara asked.

“Nothing,” I said. Too quick.

She watched me for a long beat. The rain plastered her hair to her forehead. “Maybe we wait till morning,” she said finally.

“What? No. You just—”

“You look dead on your feet,” she said. “I don’t want you driving us into a tree because we’re running from a maybe.”

“It’s not a maybe,” I said.

“Then it’ll still be here at eight a.m.,” she said, and the fatalism in her voice made something twist.

“I can drive,” I said. “I’m fine.”

She stepped back over the threshold instead of forward. “I’m not,” she said. “I can’t. I’m… I can’t.”

I should have pushed. Instead I followed her in, because that’s what I do: adapt. Adjust. Rationalize.

We left the bags by the door. Sat back on the couch with our coats still on. The lamps were harsh. The house listened.

“We’ll leave at first light,” I said.

She nodded, eyes on me.

“Will you… watch me if I fall asleep?” she asked, voice barely there.

The question landed between us like a weight.

“No,” I said. “I’ll wake you up.”

Her mouth twitched. “Good answer.”

She curled on the far end of the couch, not touching me. I sat upright, fists clenched on my knees, staring at the TV until the shapes blurred.

I meant to stay awake.

I didn’t.

—

I don’t remember closing my eyes. I remember opening them.

Mara was asleep, face turned toward me, features soft in the lamp glow. She looked young again. Trusting, despite everything.

My neck hurt. My back ached. I realized I was leaning forward, forearms on my thighs, hands hanging between my knees.

I also realized I hadn’t blinked in a long time.

Her eyelids fluttered. She was surfacing.

Close your eyes, a calm, new part of me suggested. She’ll feel better if she thinks you’ve been out.

I hesitated.

She stirred, lashes lifting. Our gazes met.

For a second, something like relief flashed across her face—he’s awake, he’s here.

Then I felt it: a small, deliberate movement of my own muscles that I didn’t exactly order.

My eyes didn’t soften. My expression didn’t change.

I just kept looking at her.

She froze.

“Ethan,” she whispered.

I didn’t answer.

Not aloud.

Inside, I scrambled. Say her name. Smile. Blink.

Nothing happened.

Her throat bobbed. “If you’re fucking with me—”

I watched the tiny pulse in her neck, the way her fingers dug into the blanket.

She understood, in increments. First the wrongness. Then the pattern. Then the recognition.

“Oh,” she said, tiny.

I felt my lips part.

“She thinks I’ve been sleeping,” I heard myself say.

The words were quiet. They didn’t sound like a movie demon. They sounded like me thinking out loud, matter-of-fact.

Mara’s eyes filled. “Get out,” she whispered. To me, to it, to both.

I tilted my head. Curious. There was no hurry in it. We had time.

In some dim corridor behind my own eyes, I beat my fists against the walls.

“Mara,” I tried. It came out in my head, not my mouth.

On the couch, she did something I didn’t expect.

She bared her teeth. Not a smile. A snarl.

“If you’re in there,” she said to me, ignoring my stillness, “you made a promise. Keep it.”

There was something like respect in the way we watched her then. She was not shrinking. She was choosing.

She slid her hand under the cushion, came up with the heavy ceramic mug. Her ugly yellow possum.

“I won’t do this again,” she said. “I’m not going to be scared of my own bed for another five years because you like an audience.”

We tilted our head, interested.

“Put it down,” my mind begged.

My hand didn’t move.

She raised the mug.

“I love you,” she said. “If you’re there, let go.”

The mug trembled in her grip.

For a hairline instant, I felt something slip, like a finger losing purchase. My chest burned.

I blinked.

“Don’t,” I gasped. It tore out of me, hoarse, mine.

She froze. Tears spilled. “Ethan?”

“Go,” I said. “Run.”

The clarity was bright and brief. Enough to know this: whatever lived in the gaps liked this body, this house, this us, and it was done being almost.

She shook her head. “Not without—”

The moment snapped.

My hand shot out, faster than she’d braced for, catching her wrist mid-swing. The mug clattered to the floor, didn’t break. Of course it didn’t.

We looked at each other.

Too late, the look said.

Mara’s breath hitched. “Okay,” she whispered. “Okay.”

She didn’t scream. She didn’t plead. She just watched me watching her, seeing exactly what had moved in behind my eyes.

“You picked,” she said quietly. “Or it picked for you.”

Then, softer, to me, maybe to us: “I’ll fix it.”

We loosened our grip on her wrist. Let her pull back. Gave her the illusion of space. It cost us nothing. There was nowhere for her to go.

She stood slowly, blanket falling away, and walked down the hall without turning her back. Into the bedroom. The bathroom door clicked. The lock turned.

We stayed on the couch.

“Careful,” I heard myself murmur, like a joke we might’ve made before.

Rain ticked. The lamp hummed.

Alone in my head, I understood something simple and terrible: it didn’t need the house. It didn’t need the dark. It needed the space between trust and action. The moment you decide to pretend a little longer.

“I caught her staring at me again,” I thought.

And somewhere in the house, maybe behind that bathroom door, maybe one day in some other dim room with some other tired man, something settled deeper behind my eyes and smiled.

She still thought, even now, that I’d been sleeping.

chapter_raw_judge_text.5:
[Analysis]

This chapter represents a strong execution of the plan's central pivot: Ethan consciously chooses to pretend sleep despite Mara's explicit warning, and the consequences arrive immediately and viscerally. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter's greatest achievement is its handling of Ethan's self-deception. The line "It wasn't a decision so much as a slide" is genuinely good — it captures how rationalization works, how the controlling mind reframes a moral failure as methodology. "The part of me that loves tests, loves knowing, stepped forward" is consistent with the character profile and doesn't feel forced. The narration earns its unreliability.

The "There you are" whisper is effective horror. The voice described as "someone had laid tracing paper over it and gone over the lines with a too-fine pen" is one of the chapter's better images — specific, strange, and not overwrought. The cold pressure on the eyelids and the sensation of the outline blurring are handled with appropriate restraint; the author doesn't over-explain the supernatural mechanics.

The morning scene is well-constructed. The bruise, the scratches, the avoidance — these are shown rather than told. Mara's line "Because every time I tell someone a fucked up thing happened at night, I end up in a room with fluorescent lights" is the chapter's best dialogue: it's character-specific, emotionally true, and advances the theme without being a speech. The dinner scene achieves genuine warmth before the horror reasserts itself, which is exactly what the plan called for.

The ending sequence — Ethan waking at the foot of the bed, the tilted head, the word "Almost" — is chilling and well-paced. The echo of the opening chapter's dynamic (now inverted) is handled without announcement, which is the right choice.

The research interlude works as a character beat (Ethan doing what Ethan does) and plants the thematic key ("It's about who pretends") without making it feel like an info-dump. The detail about the user's last post mentioning Mara's former city is a nice, restrained touch.

WEAKNESSES:

Some metaphors don't fully land. "The house exhaled" after the shower cuts off is a bit stock. "Between us, the air felt thick with something listening" as a closing line is functional but not distinguished — it's the kind of line that appears in competent horror without being memorable. The motion light described as "a seed of red like an animal eye" is slightly overwrought given the otherwise grounded prose style.

The dialogue in the dinner scene, while mostly strong, occasionally tips toward the expository. Mara's speech about fluorescent lights is excellent, but "Don't go full Paranormal Activity with your phone. Just be here. With me. On purpose." feels slightly too articulate, too neatly thematic for the moment — it's the author's thesis delivered through a character's mouth. Similarly, "Everybody says that until they're tired enough" is a bit aphoristic.

The scratches on Ethan's arm are introduced and then somewhat dropped — Mara doesn't react to them, and Ethan doesn't press the point, which feels like a slight missed opportunity for the morning confrontation.

The chapter follows the plan closely and faithfully. The key beats — Ethan ignoring the warning, the "There you are" whisper, the scratches and bruise, the webcam being unplugged (actually this detail from the plan is absent — the chapter doesn't include the unplugged webcam discovery, which was a planned beat), the online research, the dinner conversation, and the final sleepwalking episode — are mostly present. The missing webcam detail is a notable omission from the plan.

Overall, this is accomplished horror writing: character-driven, restrained where it needs to be, with genuine dread emerging from the relationship dynamics. It's not a masterpiece — some lines are merely competent, and the dialogue occasionally over-delivers thematic content — but it's well above average.

chapter_raw_judge_text.1:
[Analysis]

This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft and a clear understanding of what the story needs to accomplish. Let me work through the key elements.

VOICE AND NARRATION: The first-person past tense narration is well-calibrated. Ethan's voice is consistent with the profile: methodical, slightly self-congratulatory ("You have to pick the right moments with her"), and prone to reframing. The line "I'd read about it. Parasomnias, micro-awakenings. Her history in neat bullet points on a patient portal" is excellent—it captures his emotional arrogance and his tendency to medicalize her without being heavy-handed about it. The narration doesn't over-announce its own unreliability, which is exactly right.

PACING AND ATMOSPHERE: The opening scene is handled with restraint. The counting of breaths, the "thin curtain" of lashes, the radioactive red of the clock—these are specific and grounded without tipping into purple prose. The underwater dream fragment is used economically. The chapter earns its unease through accumulation rather than declaration.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. "This house is a maze of secrets" lands flat in a way that feels intentional—Mara is muted. The exchange about the staring ("Were my eyes open?" / "Yeah." / "Creepy. Sorry.") is naturalistic and carries subtext without forcing it. The "my brain isn't a pet" line is sharp and character-consistent. The dialogue avoids the common horror-fiction trap of having characters speak their themes aloud.

CHARACTER WORK: Both characters are rendered with specificity. Mara's chipped possum mug, her spider rescue, her "old lady hour"—these are the right kind of concrete details. Ethan's self-serving framing ("I'd framed it as 'peaceful' when I found the listing") is subtle but present. The chapter plants the seeds of his controlling tendencies without overplaying them.

METAPHOR AND POETRY: Most of the figurative language lands. "The fridge hummed in the other room" and "thoughts had gone syrup-slow" are clean and functional. "Like cutting my palms and sticking them in aquarium water" is a slightly odd simile—it's vivid but the logic is a bit strained (the image of aquarium water doesn't quite complete the thought about why googling sleep disorders at night is dangerous). It's the one moment where the prose reaches and doesn't fully grasp. "Radioactive red" is a touch overwrought for what is otherwise a restrained register.

ENDING: The final image—both of them potentially faking sleep, watching each other—is the chapter's best move. It echoes the prompt, establishes the central dynamic, and does so without announcing itself. The inversion of who is watching whom is planted here with real economy.

FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter follows the plan closely and well. The opening scene, the morning conversation, the mundane day, the subtle tension about her past, and the closing beat of Ethan waiting to catch her again—all present and executed. The "not like last time" unsaid line is handled exactly as planned.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes—the middle section (the afternoon, the streaming show, the porch light) could be trimmed without loss. The "missing poster" line, while good, is the kind of line that announces its own cleverness a little too loudly. The chapter is very competent but rarely transcendent; it's doing its job excellently rather than surprising us with unexpected angles.

OVERALL: This is accomplished, controlled horror writing that prioritizes character and atmosphere over cheap effect. It's not a masterpiece, but it's genuinely good work—the kind of opening chapter that makes a reader trust the author.

chapter_raw_judge_text.6:
[Analysis]

Chapter 6 is a strong piece of character-driven horror that largely succeeds at its core ambitions. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. The exchange between Ethan and Mara feels genuinely earned and human. Lines like "I know all the words, Ethan. I have a punch card" and "That's not the opinion of the State of Oregon" are sharp, specific, and character-revealing without feeling written. The back-and-forth about the forum post is particularly well-handled: Mara's admission that she didn't show it to him because she didn't want to be like Nate is a quietly devastating character moment that deepens both her psychology and the story's thematic concern with patterns repeating.

Ethan's narration maintains its unreliable quality effectively. His self-serving reframings are present but subtle: "I was going to say we're scaring ourselves with patterns" is exactly the kind of thing he'd tell himself he was going to say. The moment where he admits "No" about whether it felt like nothing is a small but significant crack in his rationalist armor.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The relationship fraying, Mara opening up about Nate (during conflict rather than as a neat monologue), the practical separation proposal, the night fragment with the figure in the doorway, and the morning revelation that Ethan came to stand over Mara—all are executed. The final line, "Or you already did," is a genuinely chilling intrusion that earns its place.

The sketchbook detail (bedroom door repeated, eyes peering through the crack) is excellent showing rather than telling. The doorframe divots Mara notices are similarly effective—small, concrete, unsettling.

WEAKNESSES:

Some of the horror beats are slightly telegraphed. The figure in the doorway scene, while competently executed, follows a fairly conventional horror grammar: the shape, the pause, the vanishing. It doesn't quite achieve the uncanny wrongness the best moments in this chapter reach. The "blood roared in my ears" is a minor cliché.

The chapter occasionally tells us emotional states rather than showing them: "Relief flooded her face so fast it hurt to see" is a bit on-the-nose, though not egregiously so. "Her eyes were huge" is similarly thin.

The forum post device is functional but slightly clunky—it's a necessary piece of exposition but the in-text reading of it slows momentum. The post's content is also a bit too on-the-nose as a summary of the story's mechanics.

The ending's final line ("Or you already did") is effective but perhaps slightly too neat—it announces its own cleverness a touch too clearly. The chapter plan called for Ethan's narration to have "slips" that feel "a touch too cold or collective," and this line does that, but it's a bit more explicit than the subtler intrusions the plan envisioned.

The "camping without trees or friends" joke lands adequately but the "not-joke" parenthetical is an unnecessary authorial intrusion.

CHARACTER CONSISTENCY:

Both characters are well-maintained. Ethan's emotional arrogance, his need to reframe, his reflexive "You're not insane" followed by her sharp comeback—all consistent. Mara's sharpened edges, her self-awareness about her own patterns, her magical thinking about rules—all present and believable. The moment where she reaches for his hand and stops herself is quietly perfect.

OVERALL:

This is accomplished literary horror writing. It's not flawless—some conventional horror beats, occasional telling, the forum post as a slightly clunky device—but the character work is genuinely strong, the dialogue is above average for the genre, and the chapter advances both plot and theme with economy and intelligence.

chapter_raw_judge_text.4:
[Analysis]

This chapter is a strong piece of character-driven horror that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter opens with a genuinely effective inversion of the prompt's dynamic: Ethan decides not to pretend this time, which creates immediate tension and differentiates this chapter from what came before. The confrontation scene is handled with real skill. The dialogue between Ethan and Mara feels earned and specific rather than expository. Lines like "Don't tell me that. Please" and "I changed my mind. I don't want to know" reveal character efficiently. The exchange about Nate is particularly well-handled: Mara's account of the watching, the "person suit" voice, the flat recording quality, all land with genuine dread without over-explaining.

The chapter follows its plan closely. The plan called for Mara to open up about her past during a night episode, for Ethan to be "quietly offended to be dropped into her old script," and for the chapter to end with Ethan breaking his promise. All of this is executed. The final image — the lamp popping out, Mara's hand on his chest, and Ethan forgetting the script and staying still — is genuinely chilling and earns its place.

Ethan's voice is consistent with his profile: the "clipboard" insult stings because it's accurate, his "I believe you believe what you're saying" is the exact kind of therapeutic condescension the character profile warned us about, and his internal admission that he wants her to be wrong is a moment of real self-awareness that the character profile said he'd occasionally allow. The line "I promise," I lied is blunt but effective — it doesn't over-explain, it just lands.

The mundane details work well: the heater clunking off at 2:43, the alarm clock's blue light, the burned pancakes, the fuzzy socks, the text with the ghost emoji. These ground the supernatural in the domestic without undercutting the dread.

WEAKNESSES:

Some metaphors are slightly strained. "Her breathing had gone symphonic" is a bit much for what it's describing — it's reaching for elegance and landing somewhere slightly awkward. "A black tongue at two a.m." for the hallway is vivid but feels like it's trying too hard in a chapter that otherwise earns its imagery through restraint. "Guilt settled on me like dust" is a cliché that the rest of the prose is too good to need.

The "he's almost open" callback to the audio recording from Chapter 3 is effective, but the parenthetical "he's almost open" (the plan says "he's almost there") is a small inconsistency that suggests the model may have slightly misremembered its own planted detail — though this is minor.

The morning scene (pancakes, fuzzy socks, the text) is well-observed but runs slightly long. The chapter plan called for a "daytime" section, but the dinner argument that follows covers similar emotional ground to the night conversation, making the chapter feel slightly repetitive in its middle section. The dinner fight doesn't add much that the bedroom conversation didn't already establish.

The dialogue is generally strong, but "We're on the same side" is a slightly on-the-nose line that feels like it's announcing its thematic weight. Similarly, "You always think there's a right thing to catch" is a bit too neatly articulated for a tired, scared person mid-argument.

The chapter plan specified that Ethan would review webcam footage and see himself sitting up with a "blankly hungry" expression — this is the chapter's most distinctive planned beat, and it's entirely absent. The chapter substitutes a different structure (no webcam this time, just direct confrontation), which is a legitimate creative choice but represents a significant departure from the plan. The plan's footage scene was meant to be the chapter's central horror set-piece.

OVERALL:

This is confident, controlled writing that serves the story's larger arc well. The character work is the strongest element. The horror is appropriately understated. The ending is effective. The weaknesses are real but not severe: some over-reaching metaphors, a slightly bloated middle, and the missing webcam footage scene from the plan. The dialogue is mostly excellent with a few moments of over-articulation. The chapter succeeds at its core task of deepening both the relationship and the supernatural threat simultaneously.

chapter_raw_judge_text.2:
[Analysis]

Chapter 2 is a strong piece of character-driven horror writing that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Ethan's voice is well-maintained throughout. The narration is first-person past tense, candid but self-serving in exactly the ways the profile specifies. Lines like "I let it go" after a moment of cowardice, the internal note "Quick yes. Noted," and the final admission that he "wondered if that was actually true. Or if we were both lying, equally, in different directions" all demonstrate a narrator who is self-aware enough to be interesting but not so self-aware as to be honest. His tendency to reframe his own behavior more kindly ("Not in a creepy way, I told myself. Just… appreciating") is precisely the kind of subtle self-serving narration the plan calls for.

Mara is also well-rendered. She's sharp, defensive, funny in a tired way, and her line "I'm trying really hard to only be myself" lands with genuine emotional weight. Her "dark joke" about him not remembering either is a nice piece of foreshadowing that feels organic rather than planted. The yellow possum mug appears as specified in the character profile. Her quick "Yeah. Totally" when he mentions Dr. Haynes is a small, precise detail that communicates volumes without over-explaining.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The kitchen argument feels genuinely lived-in. "Don't sound like a brochure" is an excellent line — specific, character-revealing, and funny in a bitter way. "If I levitate, I'll yell" is similarly good. The banter about being "rugged" in bed at night is well-calibrated: it's warm without being saccharine, and it serves the horror by making us feel what's at stake. The dialogue avoids the common pitfall of characters speaking in thesis statements about their relationship; instead, the subtext does the work.

HORROR MECHANICS

The chapter handles the horror beats with appropriate restraint. The second staring episode is more unsettling than the first because Ethan is now waiting for it, and the chapter earns that escalation. The final scene — both of them lying awake, six inches apart, neither moving — is genuinely creepy and thematically rich. The detail "if I move first, I lose something. I don't know what. I just know it feels like a game I didn't agree to play" is the chapter's best line: it captures the supernatural wrongness while also being psychologically true about how couples sometimes operate.

The chapter plan called for Ethan to set up audio recording at the end, and this is present. It also called for Mara to say "You do it too sometimes" — this is handled through the "dark joke" exchange and the morning cereal scene, which is a slightly softer version but functionally equivalent.

WEAKNESSES

There are a few moments where the writing reaches for effect and doesn't quite land. "The dark pressed up against the windows" is a mild cliché. "The bones of the previous tenant, my brain suggested helpfully" is a bit too cute — it's the kind of self-aware horror-adjacent joke that slightly deflates tension rather than building it. The line "My lungs forgot how" is a compressed metaphor that works but is on the edge of being too writerly for Ethan's established voice.

The chapter is also slightly long for what it accomplishes. The work scene in the middle (modem dropping, client copy changes) is meant to ground us in mundane reality, but it's thin and doesn't add much beyond the eyes-in-the-margin detail, which is the only genuinely good beat there.

The plan called for "a first slip in reality" where Mara says "You do it too sometimes" and Ethan edits it in his mind as defensiveness. The chapter handles this, but somewhat softly — Mara's "dark joke" is framed as a joke rather than a genuine accusation, which slightly undercuts the intended ambiguity. The plan also called for Ethan to set up audio recording at the end as the closing beat, but the chapter ends instead on the mutual-lying realization. The audio recording is mentioned only in passing ("Next time I'll film myself drooling so you can review the tape" — Mara's line, not Ethan's action). This is a deviation from the plan, though the actual ending chosen is arguably stronger.

OVERALL CRAFT

The prose is clean and controlled. The chapter avoids purple prose almost entirely. The pacing is good — the horror beats are spaced well, and the domestic scenes between them feel necessary rather than filler. The chapter successfully advances both the relationship deterioration and the supernatural unease without over-explaining either.

chapter_raw_judge_text.7:
[Analysis]

This chapter represents a significant escalation in the story's supernatural stakes while maintaining the character-driven grounding that the plan calls for. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter opens with a quietly devastating detail: Ethan's thumb hovering over the phone icon, the call he never makes. This is perfectly in character—his avoidance dressed as practicality ("I just have a call in five"). The "day had crusted over with normal" is a genuinely good line, capturing how mundane life becomes complicit in denial.

The grooves in the headboard is an excellent, specific horror detail. It's physical evidence of Ethan's nocturnal behavior that he can't rationalize away, and Mara's delivery—"I don't think you know what you do when you're not in there"—is sharp and earned. The detail of her tapping her own forehead is economical and precise.

Mara's practical ghost-proofing (motion sensor, mirror turned to wall, paper eye over the clock) is handled with exactly the right tone: "It was both absurd and heartbreaking." The chapter avoids mocking her rituals while also not endorsing them uncritically. Her line "You do your research, I'll do mine" is one of the better character moments—it acknowledges their different epistemologies without making either look foolish.

The supernatural confrontation is genuinely unsettling. The "other-me" appearing in the doorway while Ethan is in bed is a strong horror image, and the execution avoids over-explaining. "Joints bending a fraction too far, like a bad video with dropped frames" is a good, specific description that doesn't overreach. The layered voice—"Stop" coming from both mouths—is effectively creepy.

Mara's offer to sacrifice herself ("Take me if you have to. You already know how") is a powerful character moment that pays off her backstory without spelling it out. It's brave, self-aware, and tragic.

The ending is appropriately ambiguous and thematically coherent. Ethan's admission that "a piece of whatever that was had liked wearing me. And a piece of me had liked the clarity" is the chapter's most important line—it does exactly what the plan calls for, showing how Ethan's own psychology makes him a "perfect host."

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter occasionally slips into slightly over-written territory. "My chest felt bruised inside" is a bit vague. "I latched onto my name like a rope" is functional but not particularly fresh. "Rage ripped through me" is a cliché.

The confrontation scene, while effective, moves somewhat quickly through its beats. The "two magnets forced together the wrong way" simile is workmanlike rather than inspired. The entity's dialogue ("Always you," "Already did") is appropriately minimal but the "Almost" feels slightly too on-the-nose as a horror line—it's the kind of thing entities say in genre fiction.

The "say something only you would say" verification test is a familiar horror trope, though the specific answer Ethan gives (about not calling Haynes) is a nice, character-specific subversion of the cliché.

The chapter follows its plan closely—perhaps too closely in places. The plan called for "a flash of violence" and "a broken glass on the floor, a cut on Ethan's bare foot," which don't appear here. Instead, the confrontation is more direct and dramatic than the plan suggested. This is actually a reasonable creative choice, but it means the chapter is somewhat more bombastic than the plan's "slow, creeping unease" mandate.

The dialogue is generally strong but occasionally tips into exposition. "It tried. You fought. That's the headline" is a bit too neat as a summary line—it feels like the author tidying up rather than Mara speaking naturally.

The chapter's emotional arc is well-constructed: from avoidance through preparation through confrontation to a fragile, uncertain aftermath. The final image of Mara's palm against his cheek and his leaning into it "like a starving thing" is genuinely moving.

OVERALL ASSESSMENT:

This is a strong chapter that successfully escalates the horror while maintaining character integrity. It follows the plan's spirit if not every letter, and the supernatural confrontation is handled with more restraint than many writers would manage. The chapter's greatest achievement is keeping Ethan's psychology central even during the most overtly supernatural moment—his choice to fight back is rooted in his specific character, not generic heroism. Minor issues with occasional clichéd phrasing and a slightly rushed confrontation sequence prevent it from being exceptional, but it's solidly accomplished work.

chapter_raw_judge_text.8:
[Analysis]

This is Chapter 8 of a possession horror story, and it represents a significant departure from the chapter plan in ways that are both interesting and problematic. The plan called for a "quiet tone; no big exorcism," with Ethan calm and efficient in the morning, gaslighting Mara with concern, and ending with the inverted dynamic of him watching her sleep. Instead, the chapter delivers a dramatic midnight confrontation, a near-exorcism moment, a failed escape attempt, and a final possession sequence. This is a substantially different chapter than planned—more action-heavy, less psychologically subtle.

Let's assess what's actually on the page.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter has genuine momentum and some effective horror writing. The "toaster" exchange is the chapter's best moment—a clever, human test of identity that feels earned by the character work established in the profiles. Mara asking Ethan to prove himself through a mundane, specific memory is exactly the kind of grounded, character-driven horror the plan called for. It works beautifully.

The shift in narrative voice when the possession takes hold is handled with some skill. "She thinks I've been sleeping" echoing the opening prompt at the end is effective, and the "we" pronoun slipping in during the possession sequences is a nice technique. The ceramic possum mug as a weapon is a good callback to established character detail.

Mara's characterization is strong throughout—her snarl rather than shrinking, her "I'll fix it," her refusal to turn her back. This is consistent with her profile and feels earned.

WEAKNESSES:

The chapter fundamentally abandons its plan. The plan specified: quiet morning, Ethan gaslighting with concern, subtle shifts in his coldness, ending with him watching her sleep and the inverted prompt. Instead we get a dramatic midnight confrontation with near-violence, a failed escape, and an overt possession sequence where Ethan literally says "She thinks I've been sleeping" aloud. The plan's instruction to "leave us with the sense that whatever was staring out of Mara is now comfortably behind Ethan's eyes" through subtle, chilling understatement is replaced with explicit, theatrical possession.

The plan explicitly warned against "over-clean pacing where each chapter is a perfectly labeled beat" and reliance on dramatic confrontation. This chapter delivers exactly that—a climactic showdown with a clear arc: possession, resistance, near-escape, re-possession, standoff.

The narration becomes inconsistent. The story is in first-person past tense from Ethan's perspective, but during the possession sequences, the narration shifts to "we" and describes Ethan's internal experience from outside. This is a structural problem—if Ethan is possessed, who is narrating? The plan called for subtle slips in narration, not wholesale POV abandonment. The line "In some dim corridor behind my own eyes, I beat my fists against the walls" is the kind of purple metaphor the plan warned against.

Several metaphors don't land: "a fish slamming against glass" is clichéd for trapped consciousness. "Like a rabbit you could close your hand around" is effective but then immediately undercut by the explanation that follows. "The words were quiet. They didn't sound like a movie demon" is the author commenting on their own work, breaking immersion.

The dialogue has some strong moments (the toaster exchange) but also some weak ones. "Get out," she whispered. To me, to it, to both." This parenthetical is the author explaining what should be clear from context. "You picked. Or it picked for you" is a bit on-the-nose for a character who's been shown to be sharp and oblique.

The ending, while atmospheric, is over-explained. "Alone in my head, I understood something simple and terrible: it didn't need the house. It didn't need the dark. It needed the space between trust and action." This is the author delivering the thematic statement directly, which the plan explicitly warned against ("without a lore info-dump or moral sermon"). The final lines then repeat the prompt almost verbatim, which is effective but slightly mechanical.

The failed escape sequence feels like padding—they pack bags, go to the door, she changes her mind, they go back in. This is a narrative wheel-spin that exists to delay the inevitable confrontation rather than to develop character or theme.

The chapter is also significantly longer than the ~1000 words per chapter the plan specified, which contributes to some of the bloat.

OVERALL ASSESSMENT:

This is competent, sometimes effective horror writing that fails to execute its own plan. The plan called for quiet, subtle, psychologically complex horror; the chapter delivers theatrical confrontation. The character work is mostly solid, the horror has genuine moments, but the structural and tonal departures from the plan, combined with some over-explanation and purple moments, prevent it from reaching its potential.

chapter_raw_judge_text.3:
[Analysis]

Chapter 3 is a strong piece of horror-adjacent literary fiction that largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions.

VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY

Ethan's voice is well-maintained throughout. The self-serving rationalizations are present and convincing: "I didn't set out to spy on her. That's what I kept telling myself." The immediate pivot to "data required" and "that's my bias" is exactly the kind of self-aware-but-not-self-aware narration the plan calls for. His tendency to reframe surveillance as care ("Save her the embarrassment. Save me the conversation.") is subtle and effective. The line "Your worry sounds like a clipboard" is excellent characterization of both characters simultaneously—it tells us about Mara's perception of Ethan and confirms what we already know about him.

Mara is also well-rendered. Her fear behind the anger ("Not anger, not really. Fear.") is observed with appropriate nuance. Her "careful approximation of normal" is a good phrase. The detail of her arranging books by color "so anyone who needs to find something can suffer" is a nice character beat—the dry humor the profile promises. Her final confused "What?" when Ethan calls her name is genuinely unsettling because it's ambiguous.

FOLLOWING THE CHAPTER PLAN

The chapter plan called for: playback of the recording with the whisper "he's almost open," Ethan waking in the doorway, Mara's anxiety and hints of her past, a fight about meds/doctors, and the end beat of Mara saying "Please don't fake it. It notices." The chapter delivers the recording and the doorway scene well. However, the plan's end beat—Mara murmuring "Please don't fake it. It notices"—is not present. Instead, the chapter ends with Mara's hand on his chest and "I know you're awake," which is effective but different. The fight about meds/doctors is also absent; the conflict is more about the recording than about her mental health treatment. The hints about her past are minimal—less than the plan specified. These are meaningful deviations, though the chapter's actual ending is arguably stronger than the planned one.

HORROR MECHANICS

The horror works well here. "He's almost open" is genuinely chilling—the phrase is specific enough to be disturbing without being over-explained. The doorway scene is handled with appropriate restraint: Ethan waking on his feet, the lag of his limbs described as "like the lag on a glitchy video call" (a nice, character-appropriate metaphor), the red clock numbers cutting across Mara's cheek. The final scene—her hand flat on his chest, face inches from his, "I know you're awake"—is the chapter's strongest moment and earns its place.

DIALOGUE

The dialogue is mostly strong. "Your worry sounds like a clipboard" is excellent. "Delete it" repeated with escalating force works. The exchange about the recording feels real and uncomfortable. Some lines are slightly functional ("Can we not dissect every second, please?") but nothing is embarrassing. The dialogue carries character rather than just information.

METAPHORS AND PROSE STYLE

Most metaphors land. "The room folded in" is clean. "My heart with something made of glass" is slightly generic but not offensive. "Shadow stacked against my skin" is a bit awkward—"stacked" doesn't quite work spatially. "A soft animal in my chest" for relief is a touch precious. "Ink-dark" is a cliché. The prose is generally direct and controlled, avoiding purple excess, though there are occasional moments where it reaches slightly too hard.

TELL VS. SHOW

The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. The one area where it slips is the paragraph beginning "We tried to be normal after that"—the list of Mara's activities (books by color, charcoal painting, calling therapist) is slightly summary-mode, though it's brief and the details are specific enough to work. The observation "She was right. She was also wrong. Both felt true" is a bit of a tell, though it's in Ethan's voice and feels like his kind of self-justifying observation.

PACING AND STRUCTURE

The chapter has good rhythm. The recording scene, the morning confrontation, the interlude of normalcy, the doorway revelation, and the final night scene create a satisfying arc. The chapter doesn't overstay its welcome. The decision to delete the recording is a smart narrative choice—it removes evidence and creates complicity.

CREATIVITY

The "he's almost open" phrase is genuinely creative and unsettling. The doorway scene—Ethan waking outside the room watching Mara—is an effective inversion of the established dynamic. The chapter earns its darkness through accumulation rather than shock.

WEAKNESSES

The chapter's main weaknesses are: some deviation from the plan (missing the "Please don't fake it" beat and the meds argument); a few slightly generic metaphors; the summary interlude in the middle loses some momentum; and Ethan's self-awareness occasionally tips slightly too far into explicit self-critique rather than the more subtle unreliable narration the plan envisions. The line "I only had the distinct feeling that we weren't on one side of this thing watching it. We were inside it, and something on the edge of our bed was taking notes" is a bit too neat and explanatory as a closing observation—it tells us what to think rather than leaving us with the image.

final_raw_judge_texts:
[Analysis]

This is an ambitious, sustained piece of possession horror that demonstrates genuine craft in several areas while also revealing some notable weaknesses. Let me work through it systematically.

STRENGTHS:

The story's central conceit is well-executed: the mutual surveillance between partners, the way pretending to sleep becomes the vector for possession, is genuinely clever and thematically coherent. The line "it needs that pretending" is the story's best idea, and the author commits to it structurally. The ending, where the narrator himself becomes the watcher and reflects on the opening line, is satisfying and earned.

Character work is the story's strongest element. Mara is genuinely nuanced: her history with Nate, her distrust of medical institutions, her oscillation between wanting to be believed and fearing she's "making a thing of it," her dark humor as a coping mechanism. The possum mug as a character detail is excellent. Ethan's self-aware rationalism, his compulsive need to document and name things, his guilt about the recording, his admission that "a piece of me had liked the clarity" — these are psychologically real.

The dialogue is largely strong. Lines like "Your worry sounds like a clipboard" and "You trust systems. Must be nice" feel genuinely earned and character-specific. The banter has texture. The argument about the recording is a real argument between real people.

The pacing across the first four chapters is excellent — the slow escalation, the domestic friction, the way each night's incident is slightly worse than the last. The story earns its horror through accumulation rather than shock.

WEAKNESSES:

The prose occasionally tips into overworking its metaphors. "The house exhaled," "the hall was a hollow throat," "the day had crusted over with normal" — these are fine individually but accumulate into a kind of atmospheric straining. Some metaphors don't quite land: "oxygen clung thick and stale" is a bit confused (oxygen doesn't cling), and "the quiet rearranged itself" is vague rather than evocative.

The final two chapters (7 and 8) show signs of fatigue. The story begins to repeat its own beats — Ethan wakes up standing somewhere, Mara confronts him, he fights back, they almost leave but don't. By the third iteration of this cycle, the tension has diminished rather than escalated. The decision not to leave in Chapter 8 (Mara suddenly says "maybe we wait till morning" after being the one pushing to go) feels like a plot convenience rather than a character choice, and the story acknowledges this awkwardly.

The possession mechanics become somewhat muddled in the later chapters. The "almost" motif is effective, but the story never quite decides what the entity is or wants beyond vague predation, which is fine for horror but the repeated "it needs pretending" explanation starts to feel like it's doing too much explanatory work. The forum post that conveniently explains the rules is a weak moment — it's the story telling us what it's been showing us.

The climax in Chapter 8 is the weakest section. The ceramic mug as a weapon, the "I love you, let go" moment, the hand catching the wrist — these feel like horror movie beats rather than the grounded domestic horror the story had been building. The final image is strong, but getting there involves some melodrama that undercuts the story's earlier restraint.

"Unearned transformations" is a concern: Ethan's possession escalates quite rapidly in the final chapters without sufficient psychological groundwork for why he's more susceptible than Mara, who has apparently resisted this before.

The story is occasionally guilty of telling us emotional states rather than showing them: "The hurt went in and sat down" is a tell dressed up as a show. "Rage ripped through me" is similarly direct.

Overall, this is a genuinely accomplished piece of horror writing that demonstrates real skill with character, dialogue, and sustained atmosphere. Its weaknesses are real but don't undermine the core achievement. It's well above average but not quite masterpiece territory, held back by a weakening final act and occasional prose overreach.

chapter_raw_judges_text.5:
[Analysis]

This chapter represents a strong execution of the plan's central pivot: Ethan consciously chooses to pretend sleep despite Mara's explicit warning, and the consequences arrive immediately and viscerally. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The chapter's greatest achievement is its handling of Ethan's self-deception. The line "It wasn't a decision so much as a slide" is genuinely good — it captures how rationalization works, how the controlling mind reframes a moral failure as methodology. "The part of me that loves tests, loves knowing, stepped forward" is consistent with the character profile and doesn't feel forced. The narration earns its unreliability.

The "There you are" whisper is effective horror. The voice described as "someone had laid tracing paper over it and gone over the lines with a too-fine pen" is one of the chapter's better images — specific, strange, and not overwrought. The cold pressure on the eyelids and the sensation of the outline blurring are handled with appropriate restraint; the author doesn't over-explain the supernatural mechanics.

The morning scene is well-constructed. The bruise, the scratches, the avoidance — these are shown rather than told. Mara's line "Because every time I tell someone a fucked up thing happened at night, I end up in a room with fluorescent lights" is the chapter's best dialogue: it's character-specific, emotionally true, and advances the theme without being a speech. The dinner scene achieves genuine warmth before the horror reasserts itself, which is exactly what the plan called for.

The ending sequence — Ethan waking at the foot of the bed, the tilted head, the word "Almost" — is chilling and well-paced. The echo of the opening chapter's dynamic (now inverted) is handled without announcement, which is the right choice.

The research interlude works as a character beat (Ethan doing what Ethan does) and plants the thematic key ("It's about who pretends") without making it feel like an info-dump. The detail about the user's last post mentioning Mara's former city is a nice, restrained touch.

WEAKNESSES:

Some metaphors don't fully land. "The house exhaled" after the shower cuts off is a bit stock. "Between us, the air felt thick with something listening" as a closing line is functional but not distinguished — it's the kind of line that appears in competent horror without being memorable. The motion light described as "a seed of red like an animal eye" is slightly overwrought given the otherwise grounded prose style.

The dialogue in the dinner scene, while mostly strong, occasionally tips toward the expository. Mara's speech about fluorescent lights is excellent, but "Don't go full Paranormal Activity with your phone. Just be here. With me. On purpose." feels slightly too articulate, too neatly thematic for the moment — it's the author's thesis delivered through a character's mouth. Similarly, "Everybody says that until they're tired enough" is a bit aphoristic.

The scratches on Ethan's arm are introduced and then somewhat dropped — Mara doesn't react to them, and Ethan doesn't press the point, which feels like a slight missed opportunity for the morning confrontation.

The chapter follows the plan closely and faithfully. The key beats — Ethan ignoring the warning, the "There you are" whisper, the scratches and bruise, the webcam being unplugged (actually this detail from the plan is absent — the chapter doesn't include the unplugged webcam discovery, which was a planned beat), the online research, the dinner conversation, and the final sleepwalking episode — are mostly present. The missing webcam detail is a notable omission from the plan.

Overall, this is accomplished horror writing: character-driven, restrained where it needs to be, with genuine dread emerging from the relationship dynamics. It's not a masterpiece — some lines are merely competent, and the dialogue occasionally over-delivers thematic content — but it's well above average.

[Analysis]

This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft and a clear understanding of what the story needs to accomplish. Let me work through the key elements.

VOICE AND NARRATION: The first-person past tense narration is well-calibrated. Ethan's voice is consistent with the profile: methodical, slightly self-congratulatory ("You have to pick the right moments with her"), and prone to reframing. The line "I'd read about it. Parasomnias, micro-awakenings. Her history in neat bullet points on a patient portal" is excellent—it captures his emotional arrogance and his tendency to medicalize her without being heavy-handed about it. The narration doesn't over-announce its own unreliability, which is exactly right.

PACING AND ATMOSPHERE: The opening scene is handled with restraint. The counting of breaths, the "thin curtain" of lashes, the radioactive red of the clock—these are specific and grounded without tipping into purple prose. The underwater dream fragment is used economically. The chapter earns its unease through accumulation rather than declaration.

DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. "This house is a maze of secrets" lands flat in a way that feels intentional—Mara is muted. The exchange about the staring ("Were my eyes open?" / "Yeah." / "Creepy. Sorry.") is naturalistic and carries subtext without forcing it. The "my brain isn't a pet" line is sharp and character-consistent. The dialogue avoids the common horror-fiction trap of having characters speak their themes aloud.

CHARACTER WORK: Both characters are rendered with specificity. Mara's chipped possum mug, her spider rescue, her "old lady hour"—these are the right kind of concrete details. Ethan's self-serving framing ("I'd framed it as 'peaceful' when I found the listing") is subtle but present. The chapter plants the seeds of his controlling tendencies without overplaying them.

METAPHOR AND POETRY: Most of the figurative language lands. "The fridge hummed in the other room" and "thoughts had gone syrup-slow" are clean and functional. "Like cutting my palms and sticking them in aquarium water" is a slightly odd simile—it's vivid but the logic is a bit strained (the image of aquarium water doesn't quite complete the thought about why googling sleep disorders at night is dangerous). It's the one moment where the prose reaches and doesn't fully grasp. "Radioactive red" is a touch overwrought for what is otherwise a restrained register.

ENDING: The final image—both of them potentially faking sleep, watching each other—is the chapter's best move. It echoes the prompt, establishes the central dynamic, and does so without announcing itself. The inversion of who is watching whom is planted here with real economy.

FOLLOWING THE PLAN: The chapter follows the plan closely and well. The opening scene, the morning conversation, the mundane day, the subtle tension about her past, and the closing beat of Ethan waiting to catch her again—all present and executed. The "not like last time" unsaid line is handled exactly as planned.

WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes—the middle section (the afternoon, the streaming show, the porch light) could be trimmed without loss. The "missing poster" line, while good, is the kind of line that announces its own cleverness a little too loudly. The chapter is very competent but rarely transcendent; it's doing its job excellently rather than surprising us with unexpected angles.

OVERALL: This is accomplished, controlled horror writing that prioritizes character and atmosphere over cheap effect. It's not a masterpiece, but it's genuinely good work—the kind of opening chapter that makes a reader trust the author.

Chapter 6 is a strong piece of character-driven horror that largely succeeds at its core ambitions. Let me examine it carefully across multiple dimensions.

STRENGTHS:

The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. The exchange between Ethan and Mara feels genuinely earned and human. Lines like "I know all the words, Ethan. I have a punch card" and "That's not the opinion of the State of Oregon" are sharp, specific, and character-revealing without feeling written. The back-and-forth about the forum post is particularly well-handled: Mara's admission that she didn't show it to him because she didn't want to be like Nate is a quietly devastating character moment that deepens both her psychology and the story's thematic concern with patterns repeating.

Ethan's narration maintains its unreliable quality effectively. His self-serving reframings are present but subtle: "I was going to say we're scaring ourselves with patterns" is exactly the kind of thing he'd tell himself he was going to say. The moment where he admits "No" about whether it felt like nothing is a small but significant crack in his rationalist armor.

The chapter follows its plan faithfully. The relationship fraying, Mara opening up about Nate (during conflict rather than as a neat monologue), the practical separation proposal, the night fragment with the figure in the doorway, and the morning revelation that Ethan came to stand over Mara—all are executed. The final line, "Or you already did," is a genuinely chilling intrusion that earns its place.

The sketchbook detail (bedroom door repeated, eyes peering through the crack) is excellent showing rather than telling. The doorframe divots Mara notices are similarly effective—small, concrete, unsettling.

WEAKNESSES:

Some of the horror beats are slightly telegraphed. The figure in the doorway scene, while competently executed, follows a fairly conventional horror grammar: the shape, the pause, the vanishing. It doesn't quite achieve the uncanny wrongness the best moments in this chapter reach. The "blood roared in my ears" is a minor cliché.

The chapter occasionally tells us emotional states rather than showing them: "Relief flooded her face so fast it hurt to see" is a bit on-the-nose, though not egregiously so. "Her eyes were huge" is similarly thin.

The forum post device is functional but slightly clunky—it's a necessary piece of exposition but the in-text reading of it slows momentum. The post's content is also a bit too on-the-nose as a summary of the story's mechanics.

The ending's final line ("Or you already did") is effective but perhaps slightly too neat—it announces its own cleverness a touch too clearly. The chapter plan called for Ethan's narration to have "slips" that feel "a touch too cold or collective," and this line does that, but it's a bit more explicit than the subtler intrusions the plan envisioned.

The "camping without trees or friends" joke lands adequately but the "not-joke" parenthetical is an unnecessary authorial intrusion.

CHARACTER CONSISTENCY:

Both characters are well-maintained. Ethan's emotional arrogance, his need to reframe, his reflexive "You're not insane" followed by her sharp comeback—all consistent. Mara's sharpened edges, her self-awareness about her own patterns, her magical thinking about rules—all present and believable. The moment where she reaches for his hand and stops herself is quietly perfect.

OVERALL:

This is accomplished literary horror writing. It's not flawless—some conventional horror beats, occasional telling, the forum post as a slightly clunky device—but the character work is genuinely strong, the dialogue is above average for the genre, and the chapter advances both plot and theme with economy and intelligence.
