  - They share one unguarded moment—foreheads nearly touching, neither quite apologizing.
  - The attraction is there, but so is the knowledge it might ruin them.
- End:
  - Jace sets up a very imperfect recording plan (cheap mic, unstable connection).
  - Cass texts the fixer: “We should talk.”

Chapter 7 – The Meeting
Purpose: Climax with tension and mess; no operatic villain, but real risk; partial win earned, not gifted.

Beats:
- Location:
  - Not some Gothic ruin; a half-demolished parking structure slated for Rowe’s condos.
  - Rebar, puddles, the city humming beyond.
- The meet:
  - Cass with the fixer; Jace listening in from a shitty old sedan nearby.
  - Conversation low-key, transactional, chilling:
    - Fixer offers cash, maybe hints at past favors for Cass.
    - Cass nudges for clarity: “Like Luka?”
    - Fixer’s slip: “Luka got himself killed. We just removed temptations.”
- Things go wrong:
  - The mic crackles; some of the key line drops.
  - A second car boxes Jace in: they did expect him.
- Chaos:
  - Short, panicked confrontation.
  - No melodramatic speech, just terse threats, a gun, Cass shoving someone, a shot mis-aimed.
  - Jace manages to get a fragment of video and audio out to Iris/the journo before his phone is kicked away.
- Intervention:
  - Iris and a couple of regulars show up—they’d insisted on backup.
  - Not action heroes; just angry, scared people who create enough confusion for Jace and Cass to get loose.
- End:
  - The fixer retreats, injured or exposed; one license plate and a half-caught phrase tie him to Rowe.
  - Cass shaking, Jace furious and relieved.
  - Sirens near, unclear if that’s good or bad.

Chapter 8 – After
Purpose: Land on a human, unresolved-but-honest note; case dented the machine; Jace and Cass choose something knowing it’s a risk.

Beats:
- Short time jump (days/weeks).
- Fallout:
  - Local journo runs a piece using Jace’s fragments plus community testimonies:
    - Shows pattern of harassment, forced moves, overdoses near Rowe properties.
    - Names the fixer; raises real questions.
  - Rowe:
    - Denies, distances, pauses his campaign.
    - No perp walk. Just a stain that might or might not end him.
  - The fixer:
    - Under investigation, maybe flees.
  - Police:
    - Make noises about an internal probe; no one believes them fully.
- The Palimpsest:
  - Still open. Legal fund tip jar by the register.
  - Quiet memorial corner for Luka—a photo, a candle melted onto an old coaster.
- Jace:
  - Still broke, still in that office.
  - Slight uptick in clients who “heard he gives a damn.”
  - More aware of his limits; less interested in pretending one story fixes anything.
- Cass:
  - Lurking at the edges, work scarcer, people wary.
  - He didn’t sell Jace out. But he can’t undo what he did to Luka.
- Final scene:
  - Outside the bar near dawn, after chairs are stacked.
  - Cass offers a cigarette. Jace takes it, doesn’t smoke; just holds it.
  - Brief, unsentimental talk:
    - Acknowledging Luka, the mess, the fact that neither of them are clean.
    - Cass: “You sure you want to be seen with all my bad ideas?”
    - Jace: “I’m not sure about anything. That’s the point.”
  - They don’t promise forever.
  - Maybe Jace leans in, kisses him once, tastes smoke and rain and regret.
- Last note:
  - Jace walks with Cass back toward the noise and light, aware this could go wrong.
  - The thought is less aphorism than admission:
    - He keeps moving anyway.
  - We end on that movement—a choice into uncertainty, not a framed quote.
step_outputs.5:
# Jace Morrow

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:
- Age: 29–32.
- Grew up in the same city, lower-middle-class side of town. Catholic family; came out as trans in late teens, things went “complicated,” not catastrophic but strained enough he keeps his distance.
- Ex-investigative journalist:
  - Wrote a big corruption story in his mid-20s that exposed cozy ties between a council member’s office and a construction firm.
  - His main source was outed despite Jace’s attempts to protect them; the source later died in what was ruled an accident. Jace believes his own sloppiness and hunger for the scoop helped kill them.
  - Blacklisted by local papers for being “difficult” and “reckless,” quietly smeared as unreliable.
- Now a PI:
  - Business is mostly cheating spouses, background checks, the occasional missing dog.
  - He tells himself he’s chosen quiet work; he knows it’s exile.
- Personality:
  - Dry, sardonic, self-deprecating.
  - Observant bordering on intrusive: he clocked a man’s whole morning from the scuffs on his shoes once and has never let himself forget how good that felt.
  - Avoidant when it comes to intimacy; deflects with jokes or work talk.
- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Drinks too much coffee. Has a favorite chipped mug he stole from a diner closing down (logo half-scraped off, reads: “EAT HERE AND GET GAS”).
  - Always carries a notebook, but half his notes are metaphor fragments he’ll never use and bus route numbers.
  - Cuts the filters off cigarettes when he’s really stressed but rarely lights them; he likes the ritual more than the smoke.
  - Hates voicemail; lets them pile up unheard.
- Flaws:
  - Guilt-driven to the point of paralysis: would rather do nothing than risk doing harm again—until something pushes him, and then he can overcorrect.
  - Self-righteous streak: he believes he sees through bullshit, which blinds him to his own.
  - Jealous of people who still have uncomplicated ideals; he can be cruel about it.
  - Drawn to chaos (see: Cass) and then resents it.

Physicality:
- Build: lean, wiry, more endurance than bulk.
- Appearance:
  - Short, practical haircut he trims himself.
  - Keeps stubble when he can; it matters to him in ways he’d never explain out loud.
  - Clothes: thrift-store button-downs, dark jeans, one halfway decent jacket reserved for clients and court.
  - Old top surgery scars, a few faint cigarette burns, one more serious scar at his ribs from a night he doesn’t talk about.
- How he carries himself:
  - Slight hunch, like he’s apologizing for taking up hallway space.
  - But when focused, he gets very still: weight balanced, gaze direct, pen hovering.
  - In crowds, always with his back to a wall or facing the door.
- Expressions/interactions:
  - Tight little half-smiles; takes a lot to get a real laugh out of him.
  - Flicks his gaze to people’s hands when they talk.
  - On bad days, chews his thumbnail until it bleeds without noticing.

Motives, allegiances, relationships:
- Motives:
  - To feel like he’s not a net harm.
  - To make things add up; he’s allergic to unanswered questions when someone’s dead.
  - In this story: he owes Luka; he owes his dead source; he wants to know if Cass can be anything but damage.
- Allegiances:
  - Suspicious of institutions.
  - Loyal to Iris and the bar in a sideways way: buys drinks he can’t afford, watches the door when there’s trouble.
  - Strong internal code: don’t out people, don’t give the cops more than you have to, don’t use someone’s pain for your own redemption arc.
- Relationships:
  - Iris: part-time ally, part-time moral check; she doesn’t fully forgive him, but she trusts his intentions more than his judgment.
  - Cass: history of on-again hookups and off-again cooperation; attraction salted with resentment.
  - Family: occasional texts with a younger sister; parents mostly off-page, a sore tooth he won’t poke.

Voice samples:
- (Internal) “He looked like a PSA about lung cancer dressed itself in a leather jacket and learned to smirk.”
- (Dialogue) “No, I’m not a cop. If I were a cop, I’d have better shoes and less of a sense of humor.”
- (Internal) “You’d think after getting one man killed I’d have learned to mind my own business. Turns out I’m a slow learner.”
- (To Cass) “You don’t get to confess like it’s foreplay and expect a gold star.”

---

# Cass Vale

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:
- Age: 31–34.
- Grew up one neighborhood over from Jace; foster system kid for a while.
  - Learned early that rules are for other people and so are safety nets.
- Occupation:
  - “Fixer” in the small-time sense: sets up jobs, connects people who want things done off the books, arranges muscle, transport, hush money.
  - Not a mastermind; a middleman with a good Rolodex and worse boundaries.
- Personality:
  - Charismatic, quick with a line, good at reading what people want to hear.
  - Practices detachment, but catches feelings more than he admits.
  - Self-sabotaging: will turn left into the fire even when the exit is clearly marked.
- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Smokes a specific brand because a long-ago boyfriend did; never changed, never admits why.
  - Keeps matchbooks from every bar he’s ever made a deal in; never uses them, just collects.
  - Likes bad reality TV, claims it’s “research.”
  - Carries loose change and always over-tips bartenders he likes.
- Flaws:
  - Compartmentalizes to the point of cruelty: “It’s just business” has justified a lot.
  - Cowardice in slow motion: not the kind to pull a trigger, but the kind to walk away when someone’s dangling.
  - Lies by reflex: about big things, small things, the weather.
  - Enjoys feeling needed; will create crises where he can ride in with a smoke and a solution.

Physicality:
- Build: rangy, slightly underfed look; looks better in clothes than out of them, but pulls it off.
- Appearance:
  - Dark hair, too long at the front, finger-combed back.
  - Permanent hint of stubble, crow’s feet from squinting through smoke.
  - Tattoos: amateurish black ink; a little saint on his forearm, a date on his ribs he won’t explain.
  - Clothes: leather jacket that’s seen things, black jeans, some surprisingly nice shirts from deals gone right.
- How he carries himself:
  - Loose-hipped, lazy confidence, leans more than he stands.
  - Always occupying doorways, thresholds; rarely the center of a room.
- Expressions/interactions:
  - Talks with his cigarette; uses it to point, to underline sarcasm.
  - Slow grin when he’s lying; quicker, crooked one when he’s actually pleased.
  - Hovers close in conversations—invades personal space on purpose.

Motives, allegiances, relationships:
- Motives:
  - Survival, first.
  - Money, but less than people assume; half of it goes to keeping others afloat.
  - To not think too hard about whether he’s a villain.
  - For Luka: guilt and anger; he pulled him into circles that turned lethal.
  - For Jace: the stubborn, humiliating desire to be seen as more than a walking red flag.
- Allegiances:
  - To certain people, not causes:
    - Iris, in his messy way. Some of his money kept the bar’s lights on once.
    - A couple of younger queers he unofficially looks out for.
  - Used to Rowe’s orbit; no real loyalty, just habit, perks, fear.
- Relationships:
  - Jace: ex-hookup, half-partner sometimes, the one person whose opinion can actually wound him.
  - Iris: she doesn’t trust him, but she knows he’s useful; strained mutual dependence.
  - Luka: starts as “kid I got jobs for,” turns into a ghost that won’t leave him alone.

Voice samples:
- (Dialogue) “Relax, Morrow. If they were going to kill you, they wouldn’t text first. Probably.”
- (Internal, if we ever heard it) “He looks at me like I’m the end of the world and still stands close enough to smell the smoke. Who’s the idiot here?”
- (To Iris) “I’m not saying I’m a good person. I’m saying I’m the asshole you’ve got.”
- (To Jace, bitter) “You write one story and the city burns, you’re a martyr. I introduce one idiot to the wrong suit, I’m a murderer. Funny math.”

---

# Iris Navarro

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:
- Age: mid-50s.
- Mexican-American, butch lesbian, has been running The Palimpsest for close to twenty years.
- History:
  - Watched waves of raids, moral panics, and gentrification try to erase her bar.
  - Has buried friends. Has outlasted three landlords and two city councilmen.
- Personality:
  - Sharp, unsentimental, quietly kind when it counts.
  - Doesn’t forgive easily; holds grudges like others collect keychains.
  - Prides herself on knowing everybody’s business and rarely being wrong.
- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Names her bar equipment (the espresso machine is “Lola,” the jukebox is “Santiago”).
  - Keeps a notebook under the bar; looks like a ledger, is half gossip.
  - Pretends to hate karaoke night; knows all the words.
- Flaws:
  - Overprotective in a way that can shade into controlling.
  - Assumes she knows what’s best for “the kids,” which isn’t always true.
  - Distrusts any “cause” that isn’t directly paying the bar’s water bill.

Physicality:
- Build: solid, ex-softball or ex-bouncer shoulders, strong hands.
- Appearance:
  - Salt-and-pepper hair cut close.
  - Wears crisp button-ups, dark jeans, boots meant for standing.
  - Wears a ring on a chain under her shirt; doesn’t talk about it.
- How she carries herself:
  - Commanding behind the bar; everything within reach, like a cockpit.
  - Moves slower than she used to but with certainty.
- Expressions/interactions:
  - One eyebrow does half her talking.
  - Calls most regulars “kid” regardless of age.
  - When she’s worried, she polishes a clean glass.

Motives, allegiances, relationships:
- Motives:
  - Keep the bar open: it’s home, archive, shelter.
  - Keep her people alive, even from their own stunts.
- Allegiances:
  - To her patrons before politics.
  - Deep suspicion of cops and developers; learned the hard way.
- Relationships:
  - Jace: sees him as well-meaning but dangerous; his last big story got someone from “their side” killed.
  - Cass: a walking liability, but he’s hauled in kegs at 3 a.m. and handed over envelopes when the bar was behind.
  - Luka: one of her kids; his death sits on her like a stone.

Voice samples:
- (To Jace) “You chasing this because you care, or because you miss seeing your name in headlines?”
- (To Cass) “If you’re here to ask me to vouch for you, try another bar. Maybe one you haven’t almost gotten shut down.”
- (Internal-ish) “I’ve been promised protection by better liars than Rowe.”

---

# Luka Rivera

(Though dead, Luka’s presence matters; he can’t just be “the body.”)

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:
- Age: 22.
- Worked part-time at The Palimpsest bussing tables, sometimes did door on busy nights.
- Also loosely involved in tenant organizing; frustrated by how slow everything was.
- Personality:
  - Earnest to a fault, loved big gestures.
  - Posted long Instagram captions about community and joy; also subtweeted exes.
  - Alternated between idealism and exhausted cynicism.
- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Collected enamel pins, filled his jacket with them.
  - Kept changing his hair; last time Jace saw him, it was bleached and fried.
  - Always humming under his breath when he thought no one was listening.
- Flaws:
  - Impulsive; trusted the wrong people if they flattered his sense of purpose.
  - Liked the thrill of “having dirt” on powerful men; underestimated consequences.

Physicality:
- Only seen in memory/photos:
  - Slender, sharp cheekbones, dark eyes too old for his age.
  - Hoodie and pins, chipped black nail polish.
- Presence:
  - In the story, he’s a ghost in the social sense: everyone has a different Luka.

Motives, allegiances, relationships:
- Motives (when alive):
  - Make things better, fast.
  - Be seen, taken seriously.
- Allegiances:
  - To his building’s tenants.
  - To Iris’s bar, which felt like home.
- Relationships:
  - Friendly with Jace; had once asked for advice about “going public” with some info.
  - Looked up to Cass’s apparent power; that went badly.

Voice samples:
- (From an old text Jace rereads) “You know how to do this without getting people killed, right?”
- (From Iris’ memory) “Come on, let me run the playlist, I swear it’ll be good tonight.”

---

# Halden Rowe

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws:
- Age: early 40s.
- Polished developer and council candidate.
- History:
  - Family money, rebranded as “self-made.”
  - Owns or manages several “revitalization” projects.
- Personality:
  - Measured, blandly charming, says “we” a lot.
  - Believes he’s net positive: “some displacement is inevitable.”
- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Collects local art but only hangs pieces that match his color schemes.
  - Uses people’s first names constantly.
- Flaws:
  - Morally anesthetized; doesn’t want to see the blood on the numbers.
  - Surrounds himself with fixers so he can keep his hands technically clean.

Physicality:
- Always well-tailored, neutral tones.
- Fit in a way that says personal trainer.
- Rarely raises his voice; rarely looks anyone “beneath him” fully in the eye.

Motives, allegiances, relationships:
- Motives:
  - Win the election; protect and grow his portfolio.
  - Avoid scandal by any means “legal enough.”
- Allegiances:
  - To donors, not neighborhoods.
- Relationships:
  - Doesn’t know Jace personally at first; becomes aware when noise starts.
  - Cass is a former tool via intermediaries.

Voice samples:
- (Public) “We’re not just building condos; we’re building community.”
- (Private, clipped) “Handle it. I don’t want names. I want results.”

---

If you’d like, next we can do a very tight secondary pass on a couple of side characters (the fixer, one or two regulars) or jump into drafting Chapter 1 with these voices guiding choices.
step_outputs.6:
He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas.

Standing in my doorway like he’d been conjured by a headache, Cass leaned one shoulder against the frame, leather jacket slick with rain, cigarette cupped in his hand against the draft. Secondhand smoke curled into my office, tangling with the detergent fug from the laundromat downstairs.

“Place still smells like Tide and regret,” he said.

“It’s part of the brand,” I said. “You’re letting the heat out.”

He stepped in anyway. The hall light behind him went out, sensor timing out, so for a second it was just the orange coal of his cigarette and the pale blur of his face. Then my shitty desk lamp remembered its job and washed everything in nicotine yellow.

Cass looked the same as the last time I’d told him to fuck off, just more tired around the eyes. Hair too long in front, pushed back with his fingers. Stubble he’d call a beard if he was drunk enough. The jacket, the boots, the practiced slouch. He carried himself like the building should be paying him rent for the privilege.

“Hey, Jace,” he said.

“Cass.” I made his name flat as possible. “No offense, but it’s after midnight and I didn’t wish hard enough on a cursed object to see you tonight.”

He smiled. The left corner of his mouth never quite caught up with the right; I’d been stupid about that smile, once.

“You working?” he asked, glancing at the mess on my desk. Three manila folders, two empty coffee cups, a half-filled notebook open to a page of phone numbers and a doodle of a dead plant. Nothing worth the rent.

“Trying not to,” I said. “What do you want?”

He didn’t answer right away. He came closer, close enough for me to smell the wet leather and cheap tobacco under the detergent. He tapped ash into an empty mug without asking. That mug had once belonged to a diner that didn’t exist anymore.

“A kid’s dead,” he said.

I waited. You hang a statement like that in front of me, you know what it does.

“Lot of those,” I said. “It’s a city.”

“Luka,” he said. “Luka Rivera.”

The name flashed. Skinny kid, bleach-fried hair, too many enamel pins on his jacket. Bussing tables at The Palimpsest, trying on different versions of himself under the Christmas lights. Cornering me once by the bathroom to ask about “how you make people listen.”

I hadn’t emailed him back.

“When?” I asked.

“Couple nights ago,” Cass said. “Cops are calling it an overdose. Or a jump. Depends which asshole you ask.”

He said it like the words tasted wrong.

I swiveled my chair so I didn’t have to look straight at him. The blinds rattled in the draft; outside, rain smeared the red laundromat sign into bleeding shapes.

“Condolences,” I said. “That your PSA?”

“This isn’t a joke.” His voice sharpened. “They’ve already closed it. Paper said ‘troubled youth, history of substance use.’ You know the template.”

“And you’re here because…?”

“Because it’s bullshit.”

I let the silence stretch. He took another drag, watched me through it.

“I know bullshit,” he said. “I shovel it for a living. This isn’t that.”

There were ten good reasons not to get within a block of anything Cass considered interesting. I knew most of them firsthand. So I went for the obvious one.

“Cops don’t reopen cases because a guy who smells like an ashtray gets a bad feeling.”

“I’m not asking the cops,” he said.

Of course he wasn’t.

I rubbed my jaw. The stubble bit my fingers. “This your guilt talking? Or did the kid actually say something?”

Cass’s gaze slipped off mine. He stubbed out the cigarette in the mug, filter crushed against faded lettering.

“He was scared,” he said. “Last time I saw him. Kept going on about having something. ‘Proof,’ he said. ‘Receipts.’ He was flashing his damn phone like a target. Said he was going to come talk to you, actually.”

“He didn’t,” I said.

“He texted you,” Cass said. “You don’t check your messages?”

My stomach did a slow, cold turn. I thumbed my screen awake, scrolled through the graveyard of unread notifications. There it was, buried under spam and debt: Luka Rivera, two weeks ago.

hey, you still do that dig up shit on people thing? got something sketchy. need advice.

No follow-up. My thumb hovered over the text, too late in the wrong century.

“Must’ve missed it,” I said.

Cass watched my face, and something in his relaxed. Not relief. Resentment bleeding into vindication, maybe. “Yeah. Well. He’s dead now.”

“That’s not on me,” I said, too fast.

“Didn’t say it was.”

But it was in the air, with the detergent and the smoke and whatever mold lived in the ceiling tiles. I closed my eyes for a second, saw another message on another screen, another dead man’s name. Different story, same gut punch.

“What do you actually have?” I asked.

Cass reached inside his jacket. For a moment my muscles tightened, old instinct that never made sense in this city where guns were rare and paperwork was lethal. He pulled out a dog-eared folder instead and dropped it on top of my own sad pile.

It wasn’t much. A printout of the article about Luka’s death—stock photo of police tape, lines about “suspected narcotics.” A flyer for HALDEN ROWE FOR COUNCIL in glossy blue, corner circled in red. A screenshot of a text thread where Luka bragged to someone about “real shit on Rowe’s guys.” A rent receipt—three months paid in cash the week before he died.

“And?” I asked.

“And that’s not a kid on tips and part-time shifts,” Cass said, tapping the receipt. “That’s someone with sudden friends. Or sudden leverage.”

“You his accountant now?”

“You know how people like Rowe clean up,” Cass said. His voice had gone flat. “Bad optics disappear. Nuisances OD.”

I flipped the flyer over. Someone had scrawled an address on the back, then crossed it out. Luka’s handwriting, maybe. I didn’t know him well enough to be sure. That stung.

“What’s your angle?” I said. “You suddenly doing pro bono justice work?”

He smirked, but it didn’t reach his eyes. “Maybe I liked the kid. Maybe I pointed him at the wrong people. Maybe I don’t want that on my conscience.”

“You have a conscience now?”

“Upgrades all the time.” He leaned forward, palms on my desk. His knuckles were scarred, one fresh bruise blooming. “Look, Jace. You dig. You ask questions. People talk to you. I’m asking you to look at this and tell me if it smells wrong.”

“It all smells wrong,” I said. “That’s your cologne.”

He huffed a laugh. “Take the damn case.”

I should’ve said no. Too messy. Too familiar. Too late. I should’ve pointed him at some activist lawyer with better funding and less baggage.

Instead I looked at the rent receipt again. Luka, who’d been forever bumming smokes, suddenly three months ahead. Luka texting me for help I hadn’t given. The neat period at the end of the sentence: found dead. Case closed.

“How much are you paying?” I asked.

Cass’s smile flickered. “You’ll take money from me?”

“I’ll wash it first.”

He slid an envelope across. It wasn’t thick, but it was more than my last three adultery jobs combined. I didn’t ask where it came from. From him, it was all the same.

“This everything?” I asked.

“For now.” He straightened up, rolled his shoulders like the conversation had weighed more than the cash. “I’ll find you if I remember anything else.”

“That’s not a comfort.”

He was already at the door. Paused, half-turned.

“You check your messages from now on, yeah?” he said. “Kids like Luka, they think people like you are bulletproof.”

“People like me are underinsured,” I said.

He gave me that almost-smile. “Don’t make me regret thinking you might do the right thing.”

“You always regret thinking,” I said, but he was gone.

The hallway sensor clicked, light buzzing on, then off again. Rain chewed at the window. I stared at the envelope, at Luka’s name on the newsprint, at the unread text glowing on my phone.

I didn’t open the envelope. I opened a new page in my notebook, wrote the date, wrote LUKA RIVERA in block letters. Underlined it twice.

I’d already let one man die for a story.

I picked up the phone, thumb hovering over Iris Navarro’s number, and told myself this was different.
step_outputs.7:
The Palimpsest sat in the seam between two versions of the city.

On one side: Halden Rowe’s glossy billboard rising over a block of scaffolds and scraped brick, blue smile promising CLEAN STREETS / SAFE FAMILIES. On the other: a tattoo shop, a pawn shop, a laundromat that always smelled faintly like burnt sugar, and The Palimpsest’s cracked neon, half the letters dead. If you squinted, it just said THE MIST.

It was late afternoon instead of midnight, and the sign hummed in sickly pink as I pushed open the door. The familiar wall of sound hit: low music, ice clinking, laughter too loud for the number of bodies. It always felt like stepping from grayscale into saturation.

Iris was behind the bar, of course. She clocked me in the mirror and didn’t smile.

“Thought you were daytime allergic,” she said, pulling a pint for the only tourist-looking couple in the place.

“Doctor says I should get more queer bar in my diet,” I said.

“Get a new doctor.” She slid the pint down; didn’t even look to see if the guy caught it. He did. She always knew.

The bar looked the way it always had: scuffed wood that refused to shine, Christmas lights drooping year-round, flyers for drag shows layered three deep on the pillar by the bathrooms. The air was a mix of cheap gin, better whiskey, spilled beer, perfume, sweat. Two regulars played pool on the sticky table, arguing about rules in a way that had nothing to do with balls and everything to do with flirting.

I rested my elbows on the bar. “I need to ask you about Luka.”

That got me a full look. Iris was mid-50s, dark hair silvered at the temples, chest and shoulders like a retired linebacker. Her eyes did a slow, unamused slide from my binder-flattened chest to my face.

“Do you,” she said.

“Yeah.”

She hung the towel on her shoulder. “This about the cops? You with them now?”

“God, no.” I made a face. “Private. Cass came to me.”

One of her eyebrows ticked. “He would.”

She grabbed a chipped mug, poured black coffee from a carafe that had seen war. Pushed it at me like a peace offering or a test. I took a sip. It was terrible. Same as always.

“What do you want to know?” she said.

“When did you last see him?”

“Two nights before they fished him out,” she said. No softening. “He did his shift, stayed late. Sang a song at karaoke he had no business trying. Left with a friend. That’s it.”

“A friend?” I asked.

“Human shape. Hoodie. Hood up.” She shrugged. “Kid had friends, Morrow. That was kind of the point.”

“Was he using?” I asked it gently, though there’s no gentle way.

Her mouth flattened. “You read the article.”

“I read the template,” I said. “I want specifics.”

“He liked to party. Sometimes too much. Sometimes I took his keys. But.” She leaned in. “He wasn’t trying to die. Not that week. He’d just gotten square on his rent. Came in waving receipts like they were lottery tickets.”

“Three months ahead?” I said.

“Yeah. I told him not to show off. You know how people get.” She jerked her chin at a cluster of kids in the corner, all studded belts and black eyeliner. “Envy’s a mean bitch.”

“Did he say where the money came from?”

“Said he picked up some side gigs.” Iris wiped at a clean spot on the bar. “Said he was helping with ‘campaign stuff.’”

“Rowe?”

She snorted. “He didn’t use the name, but we can all read. Had those goons in here every other night, buying shots for the prettiest thing in reach, talking about safer streets. Luka liked arguing with them. Thought he was clever.”

“He argue with any one in particular?”

“There was one slick asshole in a suit. Not Rowe, one of his shadows. Luka called him ‘Ken doll.’ They were out back once, voices up. Luka came in red-faced, said he could ruin them. I told him to shut his pretty mouth unless he knew what he was doing.”

“And did he?” I asked.

“Not enough.” She lifted a shoulder.

The pool argument escalated; someone slammed a cue. Over by the jukebox, a baby butch with a buzzcut was crying into her beer while a drag queen patted her shoulder, wings of eyeliner unmoved.

Iris followed my glance. “Why are you stirring this?” she asked, quieter. “Paper did their hatchet job. Cops ticked their box. We light a candle, we drink, we try not to lose another one.”

“He texted me,” I said.

She blinked, once. “You answer?”

“No.”

She let that sit. “So this is guilt.”

“It’s also pattern,” I said. “Kid gets new money from campaign-adjacent work, mouths off about having ‘proof,’ ends up dead. Cops call it an overdose before the body’s cold. You’ve been here long enough. You really think that’s nothing?”

“I think I’ve seen what happens when people like you poke at people like them.” She touched the ring on the chain under her shirt, reflex quick. “You get your story, maybe. We get smashed windows and inspections and ‘anonymous tips’ about drugs behind the bar.”

“I’m not writing a story.” I surprised myself hearing how much I meant it. “I’m just—”

“Playing detective.” She smiled without humor. “Sure.”

The door opened. Draft swept through. Cass sauntered in like he owned the place, flicking ash off a cigarette he’d have to stub out in two steps.

“Iris,” he sang. “Pour me something that’ll kill whatever I picked up on Thirteenth.”

“Already told you we don’t stock penicillin,” she said. “Put that out.”

He caught sight of me. The grin crooked. “Look at that. Two of my least trusting faces in one room. Is it my birthday?”

“Sit down, Cass,” I said.

He slid onto the stool next to me, close enough his knee brushed mine. It was probably an accident. I didn’t move.

“You talk to her?” he asked, like Iris wasn’t two feet away.

“She’s right there,” I said.

“I’m aware,” he said, and finally put the cigarette out in a shell-shaped ashtray, even though it was meant as decoration.

Iris folded her arms. “He says you put Luka in touch with some of Rowe’s people,” she told me, nodding at Cass. “That true?”

Cass’s jaw clenched. Then he performed a lazy shrug. “I introduced him at a party. Grown man, made his own choices.”

“Boy,” Iris said. “He was a boy.”

“Older than I was when I started making bad decisions,” Cass said. “Anyway. He wanted in. Said he had dirt. They like kids with friends and phones at their rallies. Makes good optics.”

“And then he died,” I said.

“Yeah, funny thing,” Cass said, but his eyes weren’t mocking. “You get anything useful?”

“Luka had new cash. Talked about ‘proof,’ ‘receipts,’ Rowe’s shadows. Fought with one out back.” I looked at Iris. “You remember which night?”

She sighed. “Week before he died. Friday. Place was packed. They were in the alley by the trash. I stuck my head out, told them to take it somewhere that wouldn’t stink up my cans.”

“What’d you hear?” I asked.

She shook her head. “Just Luka saying ‘I’m not scared of you’ in that stupid brave voice he used when he was absolutely terrified.”

Cass flinched, small.

“Any cameras back there?” I asked.

She jerked her thumb at a dusty dome above the door. “Dummy. Real ones cost money. Rowe doesn’t pay me enough in campaign coasters.”

“What about inside?” I nodded at the real camera covering the bar.

“That one’s real,” she said. “And already ‘malfunctioned’ that night. Funny, huh? Came in Monday, footage gone from the hard drive. Whole weekend. Just static.”

“Rowe’s boys?” Cass asked.

“I didn’t invite IT over,” she said.

I swallowed. “You tell the cops?”

“I told them my camera crapped out.” She snorted. “Cop said, ‘That’s what you get for buying cheap.’”

Of course he did.

Cass drummed his fingers on the bar. “So. We’ve got money he shouldn’t have, half-bragged receipts, missing footage, and a kid who thought saying ‘I’m not scared’ makes it true.”

“And a developer whose business model is turning places like this into yoga studios,” I said.

Iris shot me a look. “Don’t you dare make this about your think pieces.”

“It’s about Luka,” I said. “And whoever thinks they can make kids disappear as a line item.”

She watched me a moment longer, then reached under the bar. For a second, I thought she was going for a bat. Instead she put down a coaster with Luka’s favorite enamel pin taped to it—a little ghost holding a protest sign.

“Take it,” she said. “If you’re going to do this, remember he was annoying and loud and real. Not just a puzzle to solve.”

I picked it up. The ghost’s grin was chipped.

“What do you want from me?” she asked.

“If anyone else who saw something decides they’re ready to talk,” I said, “send them my way. Or call me. Anonymously, if they want.”

“I’m not putting my kids in your crosshairs,” she said.

“They’re already in someone else’s,” I said. “At least I’m bad at getting away with things.”

Cass snorted. “True.”

Iris looked between us, like she was adding up two bad options and not liking either.

“Fine,” she said. “You get one shot at this, Morrow. You bring heat down on my bar, I’ll personally throw you through that neon.”

“Understood,” I said.

Cass lifted his empty glass. “Love it when a plan starts with threats.”

“We don’t have a plan,” I said.

“You will,” Iris said. “You always do. That’s the problem.”

On my way out, the billboard caught my eye again. Rowe’s smile glowed down, impervious to weather. Someone had spray-painted across his teeth: EAT THE RICH. It was already half-washed away.

Cass fell into step beside me under the awning. Rain spattered his hair, beaded on his jacket.

“So?” he said.

“So it stinks,” I said.

“And you’re in,” he said, like it was settled.

I looked at him, at the smoke-stained fingers, the bruise on his jaw he hadn’t explained.

“I’m in,” I said.
step_outputs.8:
Morning came gray and greasy, like the sky had a hangover.

I’d slept maybe two hours, on the office couch with my binder half-off and my boots still on. Dreamed Luka was in the doorway instead of Cass, asking why I’d left him on read.

The laundromat roared to life downstairs: spin cycles, gossip, a kid crying over a dropped ice cream. I made coffee in the machine I kept promising Iris I’d replace. It tasted like penance, which felt appropriate.

Cass showed up at nine sharp, which annoyed me. Men like him aren’t supposed to know what “sharp” means.

“You look like two-day-old soup,” he said, letting himself in.

“You smell like the lid,” I said. “Come in.”

He took that as permission to sit on my desk, knocking a stack of mail onto the floor. He picked up an envelope from collections, read the red stamp, and raised an eyebrow.

“You planning to pay any of these?” he asked.

“I’m planning to solve the murder of your conscience,” I said. “Priorities.”

He grinned. “Flattered you think it’s murder and not natural causes.”

I pushed the coffee toward him. He sniffed it, made a face, drank anyway.

“Here’s what we know,” I said, flipping open my notebook. “Luka suddenly flush enough for three months’ rent. Says he has ‘receipts’ on Rowe’s people. Fights with a suit in the alley. Security footage disappears. He ends up dead two nights later. You introduced him into that orbit.”

He flinched, barely. “Allegedly.”

“Did I leave anything out?”

“He liked terrible karaoke,” Cass said. “And cherry sours. And ratty hoodies. And he thought he was invincible.”

“Invincible kids die all the time,” I said. “The question’s who helped.”

I’d jotted down the address from the back of the flyer. “Rowe campaign satellite office,” I said. “We start there.”

Cass rolled his shoulders. “Field trip.”

“You’re not coming because you think this is fun,” I said.

He smirked. “I’m coming because you need someone charming.”

“Know any?”

“Asshole,” he said fondly.

We took his car because it had heat. The upholstery smelled like smoke and fast food and an underlying note of something medicinal. His dashboard was a graveyard of empty matchbooks and parking tickets.

“Buckle up, Morrow,” he said, pulling into traffic.

“You drive like a liability,” I said, but I clicked the belt.

The “satellite office” was in a strip mall between a nail salon and a shuttered video place. Inside: folding tables, stacks of glossy pamphlets, a whiteboard with VOTE EARLY scrawled in blue. A handful of volunteers in campaign shirts sat at laptops, making calls. The kind of place democracy and data entry go to share stale donuts.

A guy in a branded polo approached, flashing that practiced volunteer smile.

“Hey there! You folks registered at your current address?”

Cass stepped up before I could. “We’re looking for someone, actually,” he said, dialing up the charm. “Friend of ours was volunteering here. Luka Rivera?”

The guy frowned, thinking. Young, soft around the edges, eyes already tired in the specific way of people who believe phone banking is going to save the world.

“Luka,” he said slowly. “Yeah, I remember him. Blonde kid? Pins on his jacket?”

“That’s him,” I said.

“He helped out a couple nights,” Polo Shirt said. “Real enthusiastic.” He hesitated, smile slipping. “He, uh… I heard what happened.”

“Tragic,” Cass said, all sympathetic murmur. “We’re just trying to, you know, put some pieces together. Was he here a lot?”

“Not really,” Polo said. “We don’t— I mean, we don’t keep tight schedules on volunteers. He came in, did some canvassing shifts, helped at an event downtown.”

“Was there anyone he worked with specifically?” I asked. “Any staff he was close to?”

Polo shifted his weight. “I don’t feel comfortable—”

Cass leaned in a little, conspiratorial. “It’s not about trouble. He mentioned a staffer he was learning a lot from. Thought maybe they’d want to know he… you know.”

That softened him. “He talked to Mariah some? She’s our comms director. And Ben. He’s— uh—” He flicked his eyes toward a corner office with frosted glass. “Ben Pierce. Field coordinator. They’re in a meeting.”

“Of course they are,” I said.

“Was there any… problem?” Cass asked. Casual. “Last time he was here? He seemed stressed about something.”

Polo hesitated. “He was, um, intense. Talked a lot about corruption. Which, like, same, right? But he started saying weird stuff about ‘evidence’ and ‘blackmail’ and that we should pay him more if we wanted his help.”

“That’s weird stuff?” Cass said lightly.

“It made some people uncomfortable,” Polo said. “We don’t— We’re very clear about ethics here.”

“Sure,” I said. “Ethics and donuts.”

Polo didn’t laugh. “Look, if this is some kind of— maybe you should talk to Mariah. She can answer questions.”

“Yeah,” Cass said. “We’ll do that.”

We didn’t. Not yet.

Back in the car, I stared at the glass door, the reflection of the Rowe poster in it.

“He came in hot,” I said. “Trying to sell what he had.”

“Amateur move,” Cass said. “You never open with ‘I have blackmail.’”

“He was twenty-two, not a fixer,” I said.

“Yeah,” Cass said quietly.

I checked my notes. “Ben Pierce,” I said. “Field coordinator. Suit in the alley?”

Cass chewed the inside of his cheek. “Could be. Could be someone above his pay grade. Luka never gave me names.”

“You ever meet Rowe himself?” I asked.

“Couple times,” Cass said. “Smile like a billboard. Hands like he moisturizes with donor tears.” He drummed his fingers on the wheel. “You think he’d get his own hands dirty?”

“No,” I said. “But he’s got people.”

The drive back took us past Luka’s building—a tired brick block with iron balconies and a sagging awning, two blocks from the water. Eviction notices flapped on three doors. A “Future Site of Rowe Development” sign staked in the patchy front lawn.

“Subtle,” I said.

Cass pulled over. “You want to go up?”

“Yeah.”

Inside, the air smelled like boiled cabbage and old paint. The stairwell walls were tattooed with names and phone numbers and I <3 SOMEBODY scratched into the plaster. Third floor, end of the hall: Luka’s door. 3F. New padlock.

I knocked anyway. No answer. The neighbor across the hall cracked her door, chain on.

“You cops?” she asked.

“God, no,” I said. “Private investigator. Jace. I was a friend of Luka’s.”

“Everybody’s a friend now,” she said, but didn’t close the door.

“We heard he’d got square on rent,” I said. “You know where from?”

She shrugged one bony shoulder. “He said he got a gig doing ‘political consulting.’” Her mouth twisted. “Boy liked to talk big.”

“You see anyone around the night he…?” I let it trail.

“Saw fancy car,” she said. “Black SUV. Windows blacker. Parked out front late. Next morning, cops. Landlord smiling like Christmas.” Her gaze moved past me to Cass. “You one of those fancy?”

Cass put his hands up. “Ma’am, I’m barely one of anything.”

She snorted. “They say he jumped. Luka hated heights. Wouldn’t even go on my balcony to smoke. Said wind felt like fingers.” The chain rattled as she pulled the door tighter. “You be careful poking at this. People like him?” She jerked her chin at the Rowe sign visible through the hallway window. “They don’t like questions.”

“I’ve noticed,” I said.

She shut the door.

Back on the sidewalk, rain had started again, soft, needling.

“So,” Cass said. “He waves his ‘evidence’ at campaign kids, sees some extra cash, ends up very dead. Black SUV, happy landlord. Pretty on the nose.”

“If I were writing it, I’d make it less obvious,” I said.

“You are writing it,” Cass said. “Different kind of page, same ink.”

We climbed into his car. I dug my phone out, pulled up the police report I’d begged from an old contact.

“According to this,” I said, scrolling, “Luka’s death was ruled suicide. Witness: landlord heard a thump. No signs of struggle. Toxicology positive for opioids and alcohol. Case closed.”

Cass watched my face. “And?”

“And there’s no mention of a black SUV,” I said. “No interview with neighbors. No notation on missing rent discrepancy. That’s not lazy. That’s curated.”

“Cops don’t like homework,” Cass said. “News at nine.”

“This feels like more than laziness,” I said. “Feels like someone pointed them where not to look.”

Cass tapped the steering wheel. “Which means someone knows what he had. Or thinks they do.”

“Or has it,” I said.

We sat with that a moment. Wipers squeaked across the glass, losing to the downpour.

“You think he actually had anything?” Cass asked. “Or was he just running his mouth?”

“Does it matter?” I said. “If Rowe’s fixer thought he did, that’s enough.”

He lit a cigarette. The flame flared, reflected in the windshield like a tiny hazard signal.

“I’m going to need names,” I said. “The people you introduced him to.”

Cass exhaled smoke. “That’s a lot of bridges, Morrow.”

“They’re leading to a dead kid,” I said. “Maybe let them burn.”

He stared ahead, jaw working, then nodded once.

“There’s a guy,” he said. “Goes by Kellan. Real name probably something WASPier. He handles ‘security and community outreach’ for Rowe. That means muscle and favors.”

“Where do I find him?”

“Thursday nights at The Palimpsest,” Cass said. “Corner table, pretending he likes queer bars more than donor dinners. Or the gym on Fourth. Or any place cameras mysteriously glitch.”

“Friendly?”

“With me, sure,” Cass said. “With you, we’ll see.”

I scribbled KELLAN in the notebook. The name looked like trouble.

We pulled back into traffic. A black SUV slid past us in the opposite lane, windows dark enough to be mirrors. Cass’s eyes tracked it in the rearview a beat too long.

“Relax,” I said.

“I am relaxed,” he said.

“You check your messages these days?” he shot back.

I didn’t answer.

At a red light, our eyes met in the reflection on the windshield. Smoke, rain, the faint outline of Halden Rowe’s grin on a distant billboard.

“This is going to get messy,” Cass said.

“It already is,” I said.

“And you’re still in.”

I thought of Luka’s pinned ghost, riding in my pocket. Of Iris’s threat. Of a dead source and a closed file I saw every time I closed my eyes.

“Yeah,” I said. “I’m still in.”
step_outputs.9:
The Palimpsest was louder that night, the way places get loud when everyone’s trying not to talk about the same thing.

Drag night: stage still sticky with last weekend’s glitter, queen in a gold dress lip-syncing murder ballads, crowd screaming along. Cheap shots, dollar bills, big hair, boots. I slid through it like a ghost with a notebook.

Cass trailed me in, already stripped of his jacket, this time in a dark shirt rolled at the sleeves to show ink. He brushed past two baby gays play-fighting with straws and they both turned their heads. Of course they did.

Iris clocked us and her mouth went thin.

“You’re multiplying,” she said as I leaned on the bar.

“Happy to keep your numbers up,” Cass said. “We’ll tip.”

“You’ll pay double if you’re here on business,” she said. “You want a drink, Morrow?”

“No,” I said. “Coffee, if you have it.”

“We have ‘brown,’” she said. “It’s heated.”

“I’ll risk it.” I turned my mug when she set it down. “I need to ask you something else.”

“Of course you do.” She wiped her hands on her apron. Rings glinted—silver bands, colored stone, the chain at her throat.

“You ever hear the name Kellan in connection with Rowe?” I asked. “Security. Community outreach. That kind of lie.”

Her gaze flicked to Cass and back. “He’s one of them. Comes in, tips too big, laughs too loud, asks too many conversational questions about our security system and liquor license.”

“So he’s subtle,” I said.

“He’s a vulture,” she said. “Has that gym-rat cop swagger without the badge. Smiles at my kids like they’re appetizers.”

“He here tonight?” Cass asked, scanning.

“In the corner,” Iris said. “Where he always is. Not that I told you.”

I followed her glance. Back corner table, near the bathrooms: tailored shirt, close-cut hair, expensive watch. Phone facedown. Beer untouched. He sat with the posture of someone who expects trouble and assumes he’ll win.

“That him?” I asked Cass.

“That’s him,” Cass said.

Iris leaned in. “If you’re going to poke that bear, do it quiet. I like my windows.”

“Noted,” I said.

“Also,” she said, lower, “word got around you’re sniffing. Some of the kids are saying you’re gonna get us raided again.”

“That wasn’t me,” I said.

“It was your story,” she said. “Different man, same idea. Just remember where the shrapnel lands.”

She walked away before I could answer.

Cass drained my coffee. I didn’t call him on it.

“What’s the play?” he asked.

“Friendly catch-up,” I said. “You first.”

He squared his shoulders, putting on a version of himself like a jacket, and sauntered over to Kellan’s table. I trailed at a safer distance, as if I were headed for the bathrooms, pausing where the music swelled.

“Hey, stranger,” Cass said, brushing Kellan’s shoulder with his knuckles.

Kellan looked up and smiled slow. “Cass. Long time.”

Voice smooth, midwest in its bones. He stood to clasp Cass’s hand, pulled him in a fraction too close.

“You dodging me?” Kellan asked.

“Busy,” Cass said. “City doesn’t corrupt itself.”

They laughed. I hated the easy cadence of it.

I ducked between shadows, stage light bouncing off sequins painting everything in flickering gold. From here, I could hear if I wanted to, but I’d look like I was staring, and people remember that. I watched in the mirror behind the bottles instead.

Kellan’s body language said confidence, proprietary. Cass leaned back, easy. Two guys catching up about business. If you didn’t know better, you’d assume they sold cars.

Iris slid up beside me, her presence a wall.

“You eavesdropping in my bar?” she murmured.

“Observing,” I said.

“You’re not subtle either,” she said.

Onstage, the queen hit a note high enough to rattle my fillings. Glasses chimed as applause snapped.

In the mirror, Kellan’s smile thinned a notch. Cass had said something that didn’t fit the script.

Kellan’s hand closed around Cass’s forearm. Not friendly. Cass didn’t flinch, but the muscle in his jaw jumped.

I couldn’t hear the words over the beat, but I could read lips enough to catch “Luka” and “careful who you talk to.”

I stepped away from the bar.

Iris caught my sleeve. “Don’t.”

“I just want to—”

“You want to get thrown out, do it on your own tab,” she said.

At the table, Kellan leaned in close to Cass’s ear, said something that made Cass look at him with those flat, bored eyes he saved for real threats.

Then Kellan glanced up. Right at me. Even in the bad light I felt the recognition click: you are being catalogued.

He said something to Cass, clapped his shoulder like this was all bro talk, and left through the back door. Cass watched him go, then came back to the bar, shoulders tighter than before.

“Well?” I said.

“He sends his regards,” Cass said.

“And?”

“And you’re on his radar.” Cass ordered a whiskey, downed it. “He says Luka was ‘upset,’ made a scene, left. Says he’s sad. Says he hopes everyone’s healing.”

“Those exact words?” I asked.

“He’s not that poetic,” Cass said. “But that’s the gist.”

“He threaten you?”

“Depends on your definition,” Cass said. He mimicked Kellan’s tone: “‘Not everybody appreciates you bringing the wrong kind of attention, Cass. Might be time to remember who’s been generous.’”

Iris snorted, polishing a glass that didn’t need it. “Generous,” she muttered.

“He also mentioned you,” Cass said to me. “Said, ‘I hear your friend likes to dig. Tell him there’s nothing to find.’”

“Charming,” I said. My heart had picked up, but I kept my voice steady.

“He knows what Luka was waving around,” Cass said, lower. “Or he thinks he does.”

“You didn’t give him anything?” I asked.

Cass gave me a look. “You think I’m that dumb?”

I didn’t answer. The fact we both had to consider it sat between us like a third drink.

The rest of the night, eyes followed. Not just Kellan’s people—there were a couple of guys whose haircuts were regulation in everything but uniform—but regulars too. Everyone had a version of the last raid in their heads. The story they told themselves about what I’d cost them.

I slipped out the side door to breathe. The alley smelled like old beer, rain, cigarettes. The neon buzzed overhead. Someone had written LUKA in Sharpie on the bricks and underlined it three times.

Footsteps behind me.

“If you’re going to smoke, at least share,” I said.

“Can’t do anything alone, can you,” Cass said, flipping his lighter open. The flame haloed his face; the alley carved shadows under his cheekbones. He offered me the cigarette like an olive branch.

I took it, held it, didn’t inhale. Habit, not principle.

“You made him nervous,” I said.

“That was the goal,” he said.

“Or he made you nervous,” I said.

He let that go.

“He said Luka came on too strong,” Cass said. “Talking like he had leverage. Kellan told him to fuck off, more or less. Two days later, he’s gone. Kellan doesn’t say the word coincidence, but he wears it.”

“Did he say who wiped the cameras?” I asked.

Cass laughed, humorless. “He said we should be grateful we don’t have footage of our ‘friend’ embarrassing himself.”

I ground the cigarette out against the wall. “They’re not hiding this very well.”

“Why would they?” Cass said. “No one cares about a dead queer kid unless you make them.”

Silence, heavy as wet wool.

“Why did you really get him close to them?” I asked. “No bullshit. If you shrug, I walk.”

He stared down the alley. The rain had started again, misting.

“He wanted money,” Cass said finally. “And he wanted to matter. He thought exposing some slick bastard would make him a hero. Rowe’s people like desperate kids. They’re cheap labor and prettier than lobbyists. I introduced him because I thought that was all it would be. Speeches and parking passes.”

“And?” I pushed.

“And they asked for names of tenants who were ‘open to relocation’,” he said. His mouth twisted. “Luka gave them some. I gave them some. I told myself they’d get gift cards and new leases and everyone would clap at the ribbon-cutting.”

“But they didn’t,” I said.

He looked at me, eyes raw around the edges. “I saw a guy’s belongings on the street the week after I sent them a list. Luka saw, too. He asked me if I thought we’d done that. I lied to him.”

“You believed your own lie?” I asked.

“For a minute,” he said. “Then I tried to pull him out. Said it felt wrong. He told me I was being sentimental. Said he had it handled. ‘I’ve got proof,’ he said. ‘They won’t touch me.’”

“Bad idea,” I said.

His laugh was a cough. “Runs in the neighborhood.”

Inside, cheers went up as someone nailed a high note.

“You should’ve told me that sooner,” I said.

“Would you have listened?” he asked.

“Maybe,” I said. “Or I’d have told you to turn yourself in and you’d have laughed.”

“There you go,” he said. “We know each other so well.”

A bottle clinked in the dumpster. A cat shot past, ghost-quick.

He reached for my arm. Not rough, just there. My body remembered wanting him, hating that.

“Hey,” he said. “Don’t make me the only villain in your head. That’s lonely.”

“You’re not that special,” I said, pulling away.

When I got back to the office, my door was locked, same as I’d left it. The deadbolt slid smooth. But inside, the air felt wrong, displaced. Papers that had been fanned were now stacked; my mug had migrated two inches left.

Luka’s file sat in the center of the desk, opened to the rent receipt.

My back went cold.

I checked the window. Latched. No broken glass. Checked the lock. No scratches.

Inside the folder, an addition: a black-and-white photo, grainy. Cass at a table with Kellan and a third man in a suit I didn’t recognize. Luka blurred in the background, carrying a tray.

No note. No signature. Just the proof someone had been here and wanted me to see exactly that.

I held the photo by the edges. Cass, casual, head tipped back mid-laugh. Kellan watching him, not the camera. The anonymous suit’s hand on Cass’s shoulder.

“Great,” I said to the empty room.

Someone was curating my investigation.

The overhead light buzzed, flickered, steadied. I dropped into the chair, photo in one hand, pen in the other, and wrote three names:

Luka.

Kellan.

Cass.

The question mark could wait.
step_outputs.10:
I took the photo to The Palimpsest the next afternoon because I wanted a witness and a bad cup of coffee when Cass lied to my face.

Iris let me in before opening, keys jangling, sunlight striping the scuffed floor. The bar looked naked without bodies: stools up, stage empty, glitter like ghost spores.

“You look like shit,” she said. It was almost kind.

“Everyone’s a critic,” I said. “I need to borrow a stool and your bullshit detector.”

“Detector’s coin-operated.” She flipped a chair down. “Who we testing?”

“Cass.”

She snorted. “That machine’s already overloaded.”

He showed ten minutes later, which meant Iris had texted him “get here” and he’d actually listened. He walked in rubbing sleep out of his eyes, hair damp like he’d made an effort, leather jacket slung over one finger. When he saw both of us, his mouth did that small tuck that meant oh.

“This feels like an intervention,” he said. “If it is, I’m already high.”

“Sit,” Iris said.

He sat.

I slid the photo across the bar. Face down.

“Found that in my office,” I said. “My door was locked.”

He flipped it. Looked. Didn’t flinch, but his pupils ticked wider.

“Old party,” he said.

“That’s you, Kellan, and some suit I don’t know,” I said. “Luka in the background. Someone broke in just to leave this.”

He set it down carefully. “Yeah.”

“Yeah what?” Iris said.

“Yeah that’s me,” he said. “And yeah, someone’s playing games. That’s what they do.”

I leaned in. “I need the rest, Cass. No more clever edits.”

He ran a hand over his face, palm rasping stubble. “I introduced him. You know that.”

“Why you, why them, why Luka,” I said. “In sentences.”

He stared at the bottles lined up behind Iris like they might rearrange themselves into a better answer.

“I’d been doing errands for Rowe’s people,” he said finally. “Security at events, moving envelopes, smoothing ruffled feathers when somebody’s coke fell out at the wrong time. Kellan liked me. I brought in bodies when they needed bodies. I got paid.”

“How much?” Iris asked.

“Enough I didn’t have to count laundry quarters,” he said.

“You sold access,” I said.

“I sold logistics,” he snapped. “You want to moralize, go stand in a pulpit.”

Iris cleared her throat. He dropped his gaze.

“One of the gigs,” he went on, quieter, “they wanted kids who could work doors, hand out flyers, smile at donors. Look ‘local.’ Not too threatening. Luka liked politics, liked arguing, liked being seen. I figured, easy cash. He asked. I introduced him.”

“And then?” I asked.

“And then they asked for intel.” He tapped the photo. “Kellan. He wanted names of tenants who were, quote, ‘causing friction’ in buildings they had their claws in. People organizing, refusing to sign buyouts, calling inspectors.”

“And you gave them names,” Iris said.

He didn’t bother denying it. “Yeah. Luka did too. They made it sound like helping them ‘target outreach.’ Free moving help, stipends, whatever. I told myself it was smoothing the gears of progress.”

“And what actually happened?” I knew, but I needed him to say it.

“Next week, you got videos of furniture on the sidewalk,” he said. “Locks changed. Cops watching while guys in matching polos threw someone’s mattress into a truck.”

“And Lukasaw that,” Iris said.

“He did,” Cass said. “He came at me in this bar, right about where you’re standing. Said, ‘Did we do that?’ I told him no. Said it was coincidence. ‘They’re moving anyway.’ I lied because it was easier than admitting I’d traded people’s lives for a better liquor budget.”

The only sound for a moment was the fridge humming.

“He kept working for them,” I said.

Cass nodded. “For a bit. Then he started saying different things. That he had ‘proof’ they were paying off inspectors to tag only certain buildings. That they were comping cops’ bar tabs to look the other way when things got rough. He thought he had leverage. He told me he was gonna come to you.”

“He texted,” I said. “I didn’t answer.”

Cass’s look was sharp but brief. “I told him to shut up,” he said. “Said these people don’t play. He said, ‘Relax, Cass. They won’t touch me. I’m useful.’”

“Bad read,” Iris murmured.

“He said he had copies of emails, recordings. I never saw them.” Cass’s fingers worried the edge of the photo. “Last night I saw him, he was wired. Twitchy. Said Kellan’s boss offered him a real job if he’d ‘help keep things smooth.’ Said if they stiffed him, he’d drop everything on the internet and watch them burn.”

“And then he’s dead,” I said. “And your good friends have my office key.”

“I didn’t give them shit,” Cass said, jaw tight.

Iris crossed her arms. “Did they ask?”

He hesitated.

“Cass,” I said.

The defiance went out of him like the air from a punctured tire. “Kellan’s guy—different suit than the photo—cornered me two days ago,” he said. “Asked real nice if I’d heard from Luka. Asked if I happened to know anyone looking into it. I played dumb.”

“He buy it?” Iris asked.

“He smiled like he did,” Cass said. “Then he said, ‘If your PI friend starts chasing fairy tales, remind him this town has a short memory for troublemakers.’”

“You didn’t think to mention that?” I said.

“I figured you’d take it as a love note,” he said, cheap.

Iris leaned in. “You put one of my kids in front of wolves, Cass.”

“I know,” he said, and his voice cracked just enough. “You think I don’t know?”

“You liked the money,” I said.

“Yeah,” he said. “I liked paying my own rent. I liked handing Iris cash when the ice machine died. I liked buying Luka shots instead of watching him count change. I liked not thinking past next week. That make me a villain enough for you?”

“Yes,” I said. “And no.”

“That’s helpful,” he said bitterly.

Iris took the photo, looked once, flared her nostrils, put it back down like it smelled.

“They’re letting you live,” she said to Cass. “They think you’re manageable. That scares me more than if they’d put you in the river too.”

He swallowed.

“Why are they scared of me?” I asked. “Kellan knows my name, knows I dig. They break into my office, leave me this. Why not just… make me go away?”

“Because you’re useful,” Cass said. “If they can steer you, they control the narrative. If you dig and find nothing because they’ve scrubbed it, you help them close the book.”

“Or,” Iris said, “because they think you’ve got no teeth anymore.”

That one landed.

“There’s more,” Cass said. “Last night, after our chat with Kellan, I got a text from an unknown number.”

He slid his phone over. It was a screenshot: a message bubble from “No Caller ID.”

We should talk. Lot of people end up hurt when stories get told wrong. Bring your friend. – B.P.

“Ben Pierce,” I said. Field coordinator. Kellan’s boss, probably. The ghost suit in the photo.

“He wants a meeting,” Cass said. “I didn’t answer yet.”

“Good,” Iris said. “Don’t.”

“We might need to,” I said.

Iris rounded on me. “Absolutely not. You’re not walking him into a trap like Luka.”

“I’m not Luka,” I said.

“No,” she said. “You’re older and stupider.”

“She’s right,” Cass said.

“Thank you for your support,” I said.

He shrugged. “Ben doesn’t text unless he thinks he has leverage. Or unless he thinks he is leverage.”

“He’s rattled,” I said. “The photo, the break-in, the text—it’s pressure. They’re trying to see how we jump.”

“‘We,’” Iris repeated. “You and him are ‘we’ now?”

I didn’t dignify that.

“I’m not saying run to the cops,” I said. “I’m saying get proof. If we can catch them admitting they leaned on Luka, that they scrubbed cameras, anything, we’ve got more than ghosts.”

“And you think they’ll sit down in a wired room and hand you that in complete sentences?” Iris asked.

“No,” I said. “But people who like power like to hear themselves talk. You set the right hook, they brag.”

She looked at Cass. “And you?”

He stared at his hands. There were faint scars across his knuckles, older than this mess.

“I’m not clean,” he said. “If I go in there, I’m leverage too. They know what I’ve done for them. If this goes sideways, they hang me next to Luka and call it balance.”

“So don’t go,” I said.

He glanced up, surprised.

“You thought I’d throw you to them?” I asked.

“Wouldn’t blame you,” he said.

“I would,” Iris said.

“I’m not torching you for sport,” I said. “Tempting as it is some days.”

“Then what?” Iris asked.

“Wait,” I said.

They both stared.

“Someone broke into my office without tripping the lock,” I said. “Someone wanted me to see Cass’s history. That’s not just a scare. That’s recruitment. They want to pull the strings without getting their hands wet. We don’t dance yet. We make them come closer.”

“That’s optimistic,” Cass said.

“You got a better idea?” I asked.

He opened his mouth, closed it. “Drink until it’s someone else’s problem?”

“Already trying that,” Iris said.

I tucked the photo back into my folder, feeling greasy. Luka’s kid-ghost pin rattled in my pocket when I moved.

As I left, Iris caught my sleeve.

“One thing,” she said. “You chase this, you put my bar on a map I’ve spent twenty years erasing from rich men’s heads. You understand?”

“I do,” I said.

“Then don’t fuck it up,” she said. “This isn’t your confessional.”

On the walk back, drizzle turned to real rain, needling my neck. I cut down an alley to save a block. A black SUV idled at the far end, lights off. My heart kicked once, hard.

I walked on. Didn’t speed up. Didn’t slow down. The SUV didn’t move.

In the office, my voicemail light blinked. I hate voicemail. I punched it anyway.

First message: a wrong number. Second: a client about a cheating husband. Third: a voice I recognized before the words.

“Morrow, it’s Alvarez. Call me back. You didn’t get this from me, but that kid’s tox screen came down funny.”

Alvarez used to leak me things back when I was press. We hadn’t spoken in a year.

I called.

“Jesus, Jace, you forget how phones work?” he said. “You poking at the Luka thing?”

“Occupational hazard,” I said. “What’s funny?”

“Levels are off,” he said. “They chalked it as an OD, but the mix is weird. Like somebody approximated a user and got it wrong. No track marks consistent with that dose.”

“Someone staged it,” I said.

“I didn’t say that,” he said. “I’m not saying anything. I’m hanging up.”

“Wait—”

Click.

So. Cass’s guilt. Iris’s warning. A photo like a knife. A fixer’s text. A tox screen that smelled like theater. Luka’s ghost pin, cool against my fingers.

The walls seemed closer.

I stared at the phone until my eyes blurred. I thought about calling Cass, about saying something that wasn’t accusation.

Instead, I poured stale coffee into a mug, watched it slosh, and told myself the ugly truth:

This wasn’t about cleaning Cass up. It wasn’t about saving The Palimpsest. It wasn’t even just about Luka.

It was about the part of me that only felt alive when I was stepping into the same fire that had burned me before.

Bad ideas, I thought.

Runs in the neighborhood.
step_outputs.11:
The knock came just after midnight, when the city was quiet enough that I could hear my upstairs neighbor’s TV murmuring and the soft chug of the laundromat below on some timer glitch. I was at my desk, flipping the tox report details in my head like they’d reshuffle into sense if I stared long enough.

Three slow knocks. Then a fourth, lighter. Not the cops. Not a client.

I opened the door with the chain on.

Cass leaned there, hood up against the drizzle, eyes ringed in purple shadows. He looked wrong in a sweatshirt instead of the jacket, smaller.

“Hi,” he said.

“Office hours are—”

“Don’t be cute,” he said. “Let me in, Morrow.”

He didn’t sound like he wanted a fight. That scared me more than if he had.

I slid the chain. He stepped in past me, left dirty water on the floor. He smelled like rain and stale smoke and something metallic.

“What happened?” I asked.

“Ben Pierce has soft hands,” he said. “His guys don’t.”

He pushed his hood back. There was a split at his lip, a bruise swelling under one eye, a scrape along his cheekbone. Finger-shaped shadows on his throat. My stomach clenched.

I reached for him without thinking; he flinched before I touched him.

“Sit,” I said.

“I’m fine,” he said.

“You’re bleeding on my floor. Sit.”

He sat on the couch. I dug out the dented first-aid kit from the bottom drawer, the one I bought after another bad night years ago. Kneeling in front of him, I tilted his chin, dabbed at the cut with an alcohol pad. He hissed.

“Thought you didn’t do house calls,” he said.

“Shut up,” I said.

For a minute, the only sound was his breathing and the crinkle of packaging.

“Tell me,” I said.

He stared at a spot over my shoulder. “Ben wanted that meeting,” he said. “Sent another text. Iris said I’d be a moron to go. You didn’t say anything either way.”

“I was thinking,” I said.

“Dangerous hobby.” He winced as I cleaned a scrape. “I went.”

“Of course you did,” I said, sharper than I meant.

He gave me a tired half-smile. “He said to come alone. Public place. Bright lights. How bad could it be?”

“Cass—”

“I know,” he said. “Bad ideas. It’s on brand.”

“Where?”

“Parking garage by the mall,” he said. “They like cameras they own.”

“And?”

“Ben wanted to reassure me.” He said the word like it tasted bad. “‘We’re all on the same side, Cass. We all want safer streets. We appreciate your help.’ Like he was giving me a gold star. Then he said there’s ‘unnecessary noise’ around Luka, and ‘your PI friend likes to make noise.’” Cass’s voice flattened into mimicry. “He said, ‘You can calm him down, or you can be a problem like the kid.’”

I pressed a gauze pad too hard. He didn’t complain.

“Then?” I asked.

“Then I told him I don’t handle you,” Cass said. “He said that was disappointing. Couple of his gym toys explained their feelings.”

“They jumped you,” I said.

“Short version,” he said. “Kellan watched. Didn’t touch. That’s always Kellan’s trick.”

I taped the gauze in place. My hands were steadier than I felt.

“Why come here?” I asked. “You could’ve gone to Iris, or home.”

He huffed. “Iris would say I told you so. Home has thin walls. I don’t like bleeding for an audience.”

“And me?”

He met my eyes, something raw in his. “You’re already in it,” he said. “Might as well get the full show.”

“That’s considerate,” I said.

“I also thought you should know they’re done being subtle,” he said. “In case the cheekbones weren’t clear.”

I sat back on my heels. “They told you to deliver me?”

“More or less. ‘Invite him along next time. We’ll clear things up.’” His fingers picked at a loose thread on his sweats. “I said no. Hence the … conversation.”

He’d had options. Say yes. Feed me into their jaws. Walk away.

“You could’ve said I was backing off,” I said.

“I tried,” he said. “They don’t believe in half-measures.”

“So now they know you’re not cooperative,” I said.

“They already knew I was trouble. This just confirms it.”

He was pale under the bruises. I’d seen him take hits before, fists and words, but something about this—about Ben calling him an asset and then reminding him he wasn’t—had clipped something deeper.

“You sure you didn’t give them anything?” I asked.

His gaze flicked up, wounded. “You really have to ask?”

“Yes,” I said.

“No,” he said. “I didn’t.”

“Okay,” I said.

We sat in that small, heavy okay.

“I talked to Alvarez,” I said. “Tox screen’s wrong. Cocktail too neat for a habitual user. Whoever dosed him wanted a death that looked like a file they knew how to close. They just didn’t care enough to make it convincing.”

Cass closed his eyes. “Fuck.”

“Yeah.”

He rubbed his hands over his face, winced. “So Luka didn’t just fall. Or nod off. They tuned him.”

“Looks like it,” I said.

“And I walked him into their circle,” he said softly.

I didn’t rush to contradict him. It would’ve been a lie.

“You tried to pull him out,” I said instead.

“Not hard enough,” he said.

We let that hang.

“Okay,” I said, finally. “Here’s where we are. They’ve staged a kid’s death. They’ve scrubbed cameras. They’re leaning on you and flicking me in the forehead. They think we’re either going to scare easy or come running to ‘talk it out’ under their lights.”

“And what are we actually going to do?” he asked.

I should’ve said: walk. Tell Iris we’re sorry and walk. I should’ve said: send what we have to some national outlet and leave town. I should’ve said anything except the truth.

“We get proof,” I said. “Real proof. Not enough to topple Rowe, maybe, but enough they can’t bury Luka behind a headline.”

He laughed, short and broken. “You hear yourself? After last time?”

I did. That was the worst part.

“You’re not my redemption arc,” I said. “This isn’t about fixing what I did to my source. It’s about Luka. And Iris. And every kid who thinks guys like Ben are a ladder and not a pit.”

“And me?” he asked, quiet.

“You?” I said. “You’re the idiot who walked into the pit ahead of them. Maybe you help me map it.”

He snorted. “Romantic.”

“You came here,” I said. “So either you wanted to bleed on my furniture, or you wanted in.”

He thought about that, worrying the thread until it snapped.

“Ben thinks I’m still pliable,” he said. “Kellan thinks I’m sentimental. They both think you’re predictable.”

“They’re half right,” I said.

“We could use that,” he said slowly. “If I play along. Say I convinced you to meet. Get them talking.”

“That almost got you killed ten minutes ago,” I said.

He shrugged, winced. “They won’t waste me yet. Too much I know. They’ll scare me a few more times first.”

“And if they decide you’re more trouble than you’re worth?”

He looked at me like I was being intentionally dense. “Then you put my name on your list, right after Luka’s.”

“No,” I said, sharper than I intended.

He blinked.

“No,” I repeated. “I’m not feeding you to them to get a sound bite.”

“You’re really bad at leverage,” he said.

“You’re really bad at staying alive,” I shot back.

He smiled, faintly. “We all have our gifts.”

I sat beside him on the couch. Close enough to smell his shampoo beneath the blood. Close enough that the warmth of him cut through the office chill.

“Listen,” I said. “If we use you as bait, it’s on your terms. And we don’t do it until we have somewhere to send whatever we catch. Iris’s network, that journalist Alvarez mentioned. Someone who’ll publish fast enough it can’t be smothered quietly.”

“Or we get shot in a parking garage,” he said.

“Occupational hazard,” I said.

He tilted his head, studying me. “You really are going to do it.”

“I’m not stopping now,” I said. Luka’s ghost pin pressed against my thigh in my pocket, hard little reminder. “Are you?”

He exhaled, long. “I’m tired, Jace.”

“I know.”

“Tired of being their dog on a leash. Tired of being your cautionary tale.”

“You’re not—”

“Don’t lie,” he said, but there was no heat. “Maybe I want to be something else. Just to see if I can.”

We sat like that, the radiator clicking, someone arguing in Spanish through the floor. The city humming like it didn’t care what we decided.

“Stay here tonight,” I said, before I could talk myself out of it.

His eyebrows climbed. “That a proposition?”

“It’s a concussion precaution,” I said. “And I’ve seen your place. Your lock’s a rumor.”

He hesitated like pride wanted to say no. Then his shoulders sagged.

“You got a spare blanket?” he asked.

“Somewhere.” I got one out, the thin one I kept for winter invoices, and tossed it at him.

He caught it. “If I drool on your couch, it’s on purpose.”

“If you smoke on it, I throw you out the window,” I said.

He lay down carefully, boots off, blanket up to his chest. His eyes were already half-closed.

“Jace,” he murmured.

“Yeah?”

“If this all goes to hell…”

“It will,” I said.

He huffed a laugh. “If this all goes to hell,” he repeated, “don’t waste it making me a hero. I’m not.”

“I’m not in the eulogy business,” I said.

“Good,” he said. “Make sure you fuck them properly instead.”

“Sleep,” I said.

He did.

I sat back at the desk, watching his chest rise and fall. The bruises looked worse in lamplight. I opened my notebook.

Plan, I wrote, then scratched it out. No heists. No twelve-step scheme. Just a list:

- Alvarez: push for copies.
- Iris: gather stories quietly.
- Find a reporter who isn’t owned.
- Cass: only if he chooses it.

Outside, sirens wailed somewhere distant, then closer, then away. I thumbed Luka’s pin, cool metal biting my skin, and looked at Cass asleep on my couch.

He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas.

So was I.

“Okay,” I said, to the ghosts and the ceiling. “We do this on our terms.”

For once, nobody argued.
step_outputs.12:
The old parking structure on Harbor was half-demolished, concrete ribs exposed to a low, bruised sky. Someone had hung a HALDEN ROWE FOR COUNCIL banner across the entrance, the wind snapping its clean edges like laughter.

Perfect place to make something disappear.

Cass smoked at the hood of my car, shoulders hunched in a denim jacket I hadn’t seen before. Bruises yellowed along his cheek, ghost-fingerprints on his throat. Under the sodium lamps, he looked more spectral than slick.

“You sure about this?” I asked.

“No,” he said. “But I’m bored.”

I flicked my glance at his phone. The last message from Ben Pierce glowed on the cracked screen: Let’s clear this up. Midnight. Harbor Garage. Just you.

“Ben’s going to see I’m here,” I said.

“I told him you wouldn’t come,” Cass said. “Prove me wrong.”

We’d spent the day making calls, the quiet kind. Iris put us in touch with a reporter from a scrappy local outlet who still worked like the job mattered; I’d sent her Alvarez’s hints, Luka’s texts, photos, names. She was waiting in her shitty car three blocks away with a recorder on standby.

Iris herself had given me exactly three words: “Don’t die here.”

“Last chance,” I said. “We can walk. Let them eat each other.”

Cass blew smoke, watched it shred. “If we walk, they write the ending. Luka stays a junkie suicide. Everyone else learns to shut up faster.”

He wasn’t talking to me. He was talking to himself.

“Okay,” I said. “Here’s what happens. You go in wired. You keep it conversational. You don’t say the word ‘blackmail.’ You don’t say ‘recording.’ You let them talk.”

He tapped the little mic we’d clipped under his collar, cheap and temperamental but already tested. “Feels like a bug bite.”

“Stay near open sightlines,” I said. “Anything feels wrong, you walk back out. You don’t look for me. You just leave.”

“Like you’d stay put,” he said.

“Promise me anyway,” I said.

He studied my face. “Fine. If they go for teeth, I run.”

“If they go for teeth, I’m already running at them,” I said.

He almost smiled. “Bad idea.”

“Runs in the neighborhood,” I said.

He tossed the cigarette, ground it out with his boot. “Let’s go be neighbors.”

He went in alone, hands in his pockets, shoulders loose. I circled to the far stairwell, sliding into the damp stink of piss and wet concrete. From there I could see the open fourth level where the banner flapped, where Cass’s silhouette moved across headlights slashing the dark.

Static hissed in my earbud, then his voice: “Always did like your choice of venues, Ben.”

Another voice answered, smooth and amused. Ben Pierce, I assumed. “Cass. You look like you’ve had a rough week.”

“Occupational hazards,” Cass said.

I eased up to the ledge where I could see without being seen. A black SUV idled by a support pillar, another sedan parked sideways. Two guys in dark jackets. Kellan leaning against the hood, arms folded. And Ben: the blurry suit from the photo, now in high-resolution. Slim, mid-30s, neat beard, an expensive coat that didn’t enjoy the drizzle.

“Appreciate you coming,” Ben said. “Thought we were losing your trust.”

“Well,” Cass said. “Getting jumped in a parking garage sends mixed signals.”

Ben laughed like it was all a misunderstanding. “Sometimes people under me get overzealous. You know how it is.”

“I’m trying not to,” Cass said.

Static crackled. I thumped the receiver once; it cleared.

“We’re friends here,” Ben said. “We’ve always looked after you. We’d like to keep doing that. But this… curiosity of yours. And your PI friend’s… narrative impulses. That’s not helpful.”

“You could’ve said that without rearranging my face,” Cass said.

“People talk,” Ben said. “They say things that make us nervous. Things about secret evidence, corrupt cops, dead boys. We both know Luka was troubled.”

“Do we,” Cass said.

“Come on,” Ben said, voice softening into patronizing. “He liked attention. He liked to party. Tragic, but not a story.”

“He had money he shouldn’t have,” Cass said. “He told people he had receipts.”
