  - Practical decision: unshackles her wrists, leaves ankles/neck bound.
  - Tells himself it’s so she doesn’t die on him.
- The scene:
  - Close space; dripping clothes; shared blanket or cloak out of necessity.
  - She presses him:
    - Why keep going to Irongate now that he knows it’s crooked?
  - He snaps:
    - Admits he took this contract because of the money, yes—but also to prove to himself he can “do it right” this time by following through.
- She reacts badly:
  - Laughs at him, then sees she hit something real.
  - Offers a rough kind of empathy: “You’re chasing a clean line through mud.”
- Physical tension:
  - An almost-touch as they share heat.
  - He is clearly attracted; he hates that it’s toward someone chained.
  - She notices; instead of purring triumphantly, she needles him: “Does that shame help?”
- End:
  - No kiss. No tidy vow.
  - Just both of them very awake, very aware, the storm howling outside.
  - A sense that something fundamental has shifted—and it’s not comfortable.

Chapter 6 – Teeth of the Gate

Purpose:
- Bring Irongate’s shadow into the story via people, not monologue; force Corin into a public, constrained choice.

Beats:
- On the second-to-last day, an official Irongate patrol finds them.
- Led by:
  - Someone from Corin’s past (old captain/chaplain) who remembers him as the man who “lost his stomach” for necessary work.
- The exchange:
  - Polite on the surface; ugly beneath.
  - The officer:
    - Congratulates Corin.
    - Casually mentions alternative orders: if she “caused trouble,” kill her and bring proof.
  - Lysira plays silent, but her eyes are locked on Corin, measuring.
- Corin’s move:
  - He does not defy them openly.
  - He leans on legalistic contract language to keep her in his custody until official handover at the gate.
  - It’s a weaselly, human compromise: small rebellion, not open heroism.
- After they part:
  - She calls him on it:
    - “You had a chance to choose. You chose to delay.”
  - He fires back:
    - “I chose to keep you alive a little longer. Don’t pretend to understand the cost.”
- She shares more:
  - Bits of what Irongate is doing, enough to be plausible, never enough to erase her own sins.
- End:
  - Corin quietly shifts a shackle, rigs her chains so that if needed, they can come off fast.
  - He hasn’t admitted anything, but his hands say it.

Chapter 7 – Irongate

Purpose:
- Tight, sharp confrontation at the threshold: no grand speechifying, just a bad situation snapping.

Beats:
- First sight of Irongate:
  - Stark walls, disciplined lines, a sense of weight.
- At the gatehouse or appointed chamber:
  - Corin presents Lysira.
  - The official reception is efficient, impersonal; they move to take her.
- Tells:
  - Offhand remarks about “disposal.”
  - A clerk starting to mark her “processed” before any hearing is mentioned.
  - The old captain suggesting Corin sign a confidentiality writ.
- Lysira:
  - No theatrics now. She looks at Corin once, something like: well?
- Corin’s breaking point:
  - Not a pure, noble stand. A messy refusal:
    - He balks at signing the writ.
    - Asks one too many questions in front of the wrong people.
  - Tension spikes:
    - Hands go to weapons.
    - Someone orders Lysira gagged and taken below.
- Snap:
  - Corin moves:
    - Uses the sabotaged shackles to free her just enough.
    - Turns on his own former comrades in the confined space.
  - Short, vicious fight:
    - She uses quick, dirty magic—no big fireball, just enough to tip the balance.
- End:
  - They’re not riding triumphantly out of the main gate.
  - They’re forcing a side exit, alarms starting; wounded, guilty, and very much not in control of what comes next.

Chapter 8 – Long Shadows

Purpose:
- Deliver consequences, not catharsis; end with a human-sized, uneasy resolution that honors the prompt.

Beats:
- Aftermath:
  - They’ve made it out of immediate reach—into scrubland, forest, or ruins just beyond Irongate’s walls.
  - Corin is injured; Lysira is scorched from overusing restrained magic.
- Costs:
  - A guard who wasn’t a monster is dead by their hand.
  - Irongate will brand Corin a traitor; his old life is ash.
  - Whatever evidence Lysira has is incomplete; the city won’t fall overnight.
- Conversation:
  - No grand speech about justice.
  - He’s angry—at her, at himself, at Irongate.
  - She’s honest enough to admit:
    - She pulled him toward a choice she wanted.
    - She will probably do things he won’t approve of with her freedom.
  - He doesn’t absolve her; he doesn’t fully trust her.
- Intimacy (handled lightly):
  - She rewraps his bandage; his hands shake when he locks her manacles open for good.
  - A brief, voluntary touch: her forehead to his, or his hand on her jaw.
  - It’s not a promise of happily ever after; it’s “we’re in this now, for better or worse.”
- Ambiguity:
  - One unresolved question about her remains (e.g., whether she engineered the earlier ambush).
  - He suspects; she doesn’t confirm.
- Final note (tying to the prompt):
  - Corin reflecting, wry and tired:
    - The ransom’s gone, Irongate’s after him, and the tiefling is no longer strung up but walking beside him.
    - She’s still talking.
  - Close on a line that implies:
    - She may yet talk him into his grave—but this time, it’ll be a grave he chose walking away from Irongate, not kneeling to it.

This plan should give us a tight, character-driven, morally knotty 8-chapter story that feels less like a template and more like something a specific human with a taste for sharp edges and bad decisions would write. When you’re ready, we can move into drafting chapter 1 with this as our blueprint.
step_outputs.5:
# Corin Hale

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws

- Background:
  - Born in a farming village two days’ ride from Irongate; third of five children. Left because he hated the feeling of being pinned to one patch of dirt.
  - Signed on young with Irongate’s city watch, then the outer legion. Found he was good at orderly violence: following procedures, holding walls.
  - Served in a “pacification” campaign against a tiefling quarter and mage enclaves on Irongate’s orders. The moment that broke him:
    - A house marked “suspected collaborators.” He knew—knew—it was mostly kids and an old woman. He didn’t stop his squad. He held the door.
    - That night he vomited behind the barracks, washed his face, went back in. Stayed another year. That delay is his private shame.
  - Eventually left “honorably” after a lesser incident gave him cover. He didn’t defect in protest; he quit when he couldn’t stand himself. Huge difference, in his mind.
  - Became a bounty runner/escort: contracts, clear terms, good coin, no banner. Tells himself it’s neutral. Knows it isn’t.

- Personality:
  - Dry, restrained, practical. Not clever-quip charming—more the man who says one line that ends the argument.
  - Internally harsher than he appears. Judges himself relentlessly, others sparingly.
  - Hates drama and disorder; gravitates to routines: checking harnesses, counting bolts, sharpening blades.
  - Finds talk exhausting, especially probing or emotional talk. That makes Lysira’s persistence torture.
  - Underneath: wants to believe he’s capable of one clean, correct choice. Suspects he isn’t.

- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Has a habit of:
    - Running a thumb along the edge of his coin when thinking, as if testing its worth.
    - Counting: steps, arrows, knots. Comforts him.
    - Checking the wagon wheels compulsively. Taps each spoke before they leave camp like a ritual.
  - Keeps his gear in strict order:
    - Bedroll folded the same way.
    - Knives arranged by size and use.
    - When something is out of place (because of Lysira), it bothers him more than he admits.
  - Sleeps light, back to a wall/tree, one hand near steel.
  - Does not like to be touched unexpectedly; will flinch or go very still.

- Flaws:
  - Cowardice in slow motion:
    - Not in a physical fight—he’ll stand in blood—but in moral situations. He delays, looks away, lets things slide.
  - Stubbornness:
    - Once he decides “this is the rule,” hates changing course, even when facts shift.
  - Resentment:
    - Resents people who force him to look at himself (Lysira) and sometimes punishes them for it.
  - Repression:
    - Bottles desire and guilt until it leaks as cruelty or sarcasm.
  - Selfishness:
    - His final choices are colored by his own need to not be “that man” again, even if it’s not objectively the smartest call.

Physicality

- Appearance:
  - Mid-30s. Weathered. Dark hair cropped short; streaks of early grey at the temples.
  - Stubble that never fully goes away; jaw is all sharp lines and habitual tension.
  - Eyes: grey or washed-out blue; tired, watchful, not naturally warm.
  - A fighter’s build:
    - Compact muscle, no decorative bulk. Scar at left shoulder from a spear, a ragged pale line at his ribs.
- How he carries himself:
  - Economical movement; few wasted gestures.
  - Often appears calm, but that calm is braced—like a shield wall.
  - Looks at exits and hands before he looks at faces.
  - With Lysira:
    - Keeps a deliberate, professional distance.
    - When he has to touch her to adjust chains, he does so matter-of-factly, then retreats a step too fast.
- How he interacts with the world:
  - Talks little to strangers.
  - Treats animals—especially his horse—with understated kindness.
  - Gives way to civilians on the road without thinking; old habits.

Motives, allegiances, relationships

- Motives:
  - On the surface:
    - Earn the ransom.
    - Prove to himself he can finish a job by the book.
  - Beneath:
    - Trying to buy a piece of land far from Irongate and erase himself into something harmless.
    - Looking (unconsciously) for a situation where doing the “professional” thing and doing the “right” thing coincide, so he can feel clean again.
  - Over the story:
    - Realizes they don’t align.
    - Choice becomes: live as the man who once again looked away, or accept ruin to break the pattern.

- Allegiances:
  - No formal banners anymore.
  - Lingering, complicated loyalty to individual soldiers he served with.
  - Distrusts systems: Irongate, cults, rebels. “Everyone’s feeding someone into a fire.”

- Relationships (existing, pre-story):
  - Family:
    - Mostly distant; sends money when he can, doesn’t visit.
    - One younger sister wrote him a letter once after hearing rumors of the purge. He never answered.
  - Past comrades:
    - Some would spit at his feet for “abandoning.”
    - One or two might still drink with him, but he avoids them.
  - No stable romantic ties:
    - Brothel visits, one-night comforts. Keeps emotions off-limits. It’s cheaper that way.

Flavor quotes

- Dialogue:
  - “You keep talking like we’re in the same story. We’re not. I’m the man on the wagon. You’re the one in chains.”
  - “Irongate doesn’t buy truth. They buy quiet.”
- Internal:
  - “If she’d shut up for an hour I might forget her neck’s worth more gold than I’ve seen in three years.”
  - “Three days. I can hold myself together for three days.”

---

# Lysira Vex

Background, personality, idiosyncrasies, flaws

- Background:
  - Born in Irongate’s lower quarter to a tiefling mother who cleaned brass in a temple, father unknown.
  - Grew up knowing exactly what humans thought of her kind. Learned early to turn their fear and desire into leverage.
  - Ran messages for smugglers as a child; graduated to thief, informant, then infiltrator for those opposing Irongate’s “purity” laws.
  - Briefly served an infernal patronage:
    - Not full demonic thrall, but accepted power/knowledge under contract terms she then broke or twisted.
    - Some of the accusations against her are true; others are inflated propaganda.
  - Stole something vital from Irongate (names, contracts, plans for something ugly) not for fun but as part of a larger, ruthless attempt to expose or destabilize them.

- Personality:
  - Verbal, quick, needling. Loves to pry at contradictions.
  - Keeps talking as armor: if she controls the frame, she’s safer.
  - Uses sexuality theatrically:
    - Flirtation, innuendo, provocative posture—but chooses carefully when it’s weapon, when it’s mask, when it’s genuine.
  - Alternates between mocking bravado and moments of disarming sincerity.
  - Deeply proud. Hates pity. Hates cages more.
  - Dangerous kind of self-awareness: knows she’s compelling and resents people for making her use it.

- Idiosyncrasies:
  - Hums old temple hymns under her breath, warping them with infernal cadences.
  - Plays with her chains when bored, checking their weaknesses, knotting them idly.
  - Catalogs little details:
    - Knows how many times Corin checks the rear wheel.
    - Notices a rider’s uneven boot-heel, a clerk’s ink-stained thumb.
  - Eats quickly when allowed, like someone who has missed too many meals in custody, but conceals it behind jokes.
  - Hates the smell of incense; it reminds her of both worship and raids.

- Flaws:
  - Manipulative by default:
    - Even when she’s sincere, it comes wrapped in hooks. People don’t trust her—and they’re not wrong.
  - Vengeful:
    - She keeps lists. She’d risk collateral damage to hurt certain targets.
  - Overconfident:
    - Assumes she can talk or scheme her way through anything.
  - Callous:
    - Capable of discounting lives (including humans in Irongate) as acceptable losses.
  - Prickly vulnerability:
    - When someone actually sees her as more than monster/temptress/weapon, it rattles her. She may lash out.

Physicality

- Appearance:
  - Early 20s. Red hair like fresh-spilled wine, usually tangled in captivity but still striking.
  - Horns: swept back and slightly curved, dark as polished onyx, with faint etched sigils (some decorative, some once ritual).
  - Eyes: golden-amber, slit pupils; expressive, mocking, predatory and then abruptly soft.
  - Skin: deep red or wine-dark; faint scars at wrists and ankles from older shackles.
  - Tail: expressive, betrays mood (lashes when irritated, curls when amused).
- How she carries herself:
  - Even chained, she holds her head high.
  - Uses what movement she has with precision: leaning, arching, twisting to intrude into personal space without touching.
  - Rarely slumps, even when exhausted; refuses to give captors that satisfaction.
- How she interacts with the world:
  - Intense eye contact when she wants control.
  - Notices everything; comments on what people don’t expect her to have seen.
  - Touch:
    - Calculated. When she chooses to touch, it’s deliberate and usually significant.

Motives, allegiances, relationships

- Motives:
  - Survive. She very much wants to live; no wasted-martyr impulses.
  - Hurt Irongate’s inner circle:
    - For what they did to her people.
    - For something personal (family, lover, friend taken or “cleansed”).
  - Keep hold of what she stole:
    - It’s leverage, bomb, and lifeline.
  - Maintain autonomy:
    - Hates being used as symbol or pawn—even by those ostensibly on “her side.”
- Allegiances:
  - Loosely aligned with a network of dissidents, smugglers, and downworld operators.
  - No blind loyalty:
    - She’s burned contacts before when their goals diverged.
  - Pragmatic relationship with infernal forces:
    - Takes power where she can, always looking for the loophole.
- Relationships (existing):
  - Mother: gone—either dead in a purge or disappeared into the system. That loss is a quiet, live coal.
  - A former co-conspirator/lover in Irongate:
    - Either betrayed her or was captured. Complicated grief and fury.
  - A patron figure (maybe a demon, maybe a mortal spymaster):
    - Wants her either retrieved or silenced. She owes and resents them.

Flavor quotes

- Dialogue:
  - “You tie good knots, soldier. Practiced. How many of mine did you gift to the noose?”
  - “They don’t pay a king’s ransom to ask polite questions. You know that. Somewhere under all that iron, you know.”
  - “Careful, Corin. Keep looking at me like that and I’ll start thinking you’re human.”
- Internal (if we ever dipped in, or implied through speech):
  - “He counts everything but the cost of being the man at the door.”
  - “If they hang me, I hope it’s in the good square. I’d like them to see what they’re wasting.”

---

If you’d like, next we can sketch one or two key side characters (e.g., Corin’s former captain, Lysira’s off-page patron) so their scenes feel grounded, then move into drafting Chapter 1 in Corin’s voice.
step_outputs.6:
Just three days out from Irongate, the road turned mean.

Ruts deep as graves, slick with last night’s rain. The mare took them with the patience of an old campaigner, ears flicking, leather harness creaking. Corin Hale sat the bench steady as stone, one hand on the reins, the other resting near the crossbow propped beside him, every bump counting itself up his spine.

In back, the tiefling swung with the motion of the wagon, chains ringing a dry little song.

She was a smear of red in the corner of his eye—hair, skin, the curve of horns, all of it catching what little light made it through the clouds. He’d tied her wrists to the overhead rail so her hands hung useless above her head. Ankles shackled. Iron collar chained to the rear post so she couldn’t throw herself out and make him explain the mess in Irongate.

He wasn’t worried about her jumping.

He was worried about her talking.

“Three days,” he said, mostly to the road.

Nobody answered. The mare sneezed. Behind him, a chain clinked as she shifted.

He’d gagged her that morning. Practical choice. Night before, her voice had threaded through his sleep, all purr and teeth, tugging on names she shouldn’t know. He’d woken with her laugh in his ears and his hand on his knife, and that was enough of that.

Now he could feel her staring holes in the back of his skull.

Another mile. The sky stayed low and grey, hawks circling far off. Scrubland gave way to sparse trees. The world felt empty.

“Water,” she tried around the leather strap. It came out muffled, more breath than word.

He ignored it.

The wagon hit a rut hard enough to jolt his teeth. Behind him, she swung, struck wood with a heavy thud.

“Mmff.” Sharper this time, pain under it.

Corin exhaled through his nose. Professional. Keep cargo alive. Keep it presentable. They didn’t pay a king’s ransom for a corpse.

He pulled the mare up under a twisted oak. The wagon creaked to a halt.

When he dropped down and came around the back, she went very still, like a cat seeing which way the hand would move.

Up close, she was smaller than the legend. Shackles did that to people. Her bare feet were muddy, one horn chipped at the tip. Red hair plastered to her neck with sweat. The collar had rubbed a raw line at her throat.

Her eyes, though. They met his like she wasn’t the one hanging in chains.

He checked the manacles first. Habit. Wrists abraded but not bleeding through. Ankles swollen. Nothing that would kill her before the gate. He unslung the waterskin.

“Don’t scream,” he said.

She arched a brow over the gag. The look said, Really?

He pulled the strap loose. Her mouth was dry, lips cracked, but when she smiled it was sharp.

“Missed your voice?” she asked, rough.

“Open,” Corin said.

She did, and he tipped water slow. She drank greedily, some spilling down her chin, dark over red skin. The chain above them creaked when she stretched for the last drops.

“Again,” she said.

He obliged, then corked the skin and stepped back out of her reach.

“You could untie me,” she said conversationally. “I promise not to kill you before Irongate.”

“Sure you do.”

“You gagged me,” she went on. “I was wounded.”

“You talked.”

“I speak. It’s a condition.” She flexed her shoulders, winced faintly. “Also you got my name wrong.”

“I didn’t use your name.”

She clicked her tongue. “Not out loud. In your head. Like a prayer you don’t want anyone hearing.”

His fingers tightened on the waterskin. “You don’t know my prayers.”

“Don’t really peg you for the praying sort,” she said. “But you mutter. Corin, right?”

He didn’t ask how she knew. Men in taverns talked. Contracts passed through hands. Her bounty poster had his name on it; Irongate loved its paperwork.

“Lysira Vex,” he said instead. “Wanted for theft, sedition, murder, consorting with infernals. And poor manners.”

“You read the poster. I’m flattered.”

He checked the bindings again, too rough this time. Her lips thinned, but she said nothing.

“Anything broken?” he asked.

“You’d like to know.”

“Be a shame if you didn’t make it to the scaffold in one piece.”

She gave a soft huff of laughter. “They’re not building a scaffold for me, Corin.”

He met her gaze. “No?”

“Takes too long,” she said. “Paperwork. Witnesses. All that shining Irongate law. No, they’ll take me below. Quick knife. Pit. Fire. Whatever suits the sermon that week.”

He’d heard different. Formal charges, public cleansing, big show in the high square. Then again, the coin on her head was high enough to drown doubt.

He hoisted himself back up on the bench without answering.

“You don’t have to gag me again,” she called, as he took the reins. “I’ll behave.”

“You won’t,” he said. “Mouth like yours gets people killed.”

She leaned forward as far as the collar allowed, chains taut, voice light. “Only the right ones.”

He pulled the strap back up, buckled it behind her head. She didn’t fight it, only rolled her eyes, mumbled something obscene around leather.

Back on the road, the rhythm took over. Hooves. Wheels. The faint, constant clink of chain.

By midafternoon, the silence grew teeth.

He kept seeing the chipped horn in his peripheral vision. The raw skin at her throat. Heard her voice under the gag, repeating his name like a fact.

Corin tapped the crossbow stock with two fingers, counting bolts. One, two, three—

Something moved in the treeline ahead.

Not the twitching shadow of a deer. Not wind. A flash of dark cloth, gone quick.

He eased the mare down without making it look like easing, hand sliding to the crossbow.

Another flicker to the right. Low, crouched. Two. Maybe three.

“Of course,” he muttered.

He slid the bow up, nocked and drawn in a practiced motion. The road ahead curved between a stand of pines. If he were setting an ambush, he’d pick—

The first man stepped out onto the road, bow in hand, hood up. Two more ghosts in the brush.

“Easy there, friend,” the man called. Local accent, teeth broken. Not city. “Road’s got a toll.”

Corin felt his jaw set. Bandits. Sloppy ones.

“Already paid,” he said.

“Afraid rates went up.” The man’s gaze flicked past Corin, to the wagon bed. He grinned, slow. “You got color back there. Rare kind. That’ll do.”

Behind him, Lysira’s chains rattled once.

Corin didn’t look back. “Not on the table.”

“Oh, I think she is,” the bandit said. His bow came up.

Corin shot him in the throat.

The man dropped without a sound, arrow loosed wild into the dirt. The other two swore, one charging, one fumbling his shot. Corin was already moving, dropping from the bench, drawing the short sword from his hip.

The charging one met him there. Mud slipped under boots, steel rang once. Corin stepped inside the swing, buried his blade under ribs, twisted out as the man went down screaming.

An arrow hissed past his shoulder, close enough to stir his hair. He pivoted, found the last man in the brush fumbling another shot. Crossbow was empty; no time. Corin threw his knife. It caught the man high in the chest. He staggered, disappeared behind scrub.

Silence crashed down, loud as the fight.

Corin stood, breathing hard, rain damp on his face. Three shapes on the ground. Blue fletching. No sigils. Just scavengers.

He wiped his blade on a corpse’s cloak.

Chains clinked behind him. A muffled syllable.

He went to the wagon. Lysira watched him with bright, intent eyes over the gag. She lifted her bound hands a fraction, chain pulling taut.

He tugged the gag down.

“You’re sloppy,” she said. “Left one alive.”

“He’s got my knife,” Corin said. “He won’t run far.”

She tilted her head. “You sure these are thieves? Not the early welcome party?”

“Irongate doesn’t hire men who can’t string a bow.”

“You’d be surprised.” She nodded toward a dead man’s sleeve. “Check his wrist.”

He did. Pushed up frayed cloth. Found, against the skin, the faint scarred outline of a sigil he recognized from Irongate’s outer companies.

His mouth went dry.

“Three days,” Lysira said softly. “They’re in a hurry.”

He pulled the gag back into place more roughly than he meant to. She took it with a flare of nostrils, a narrowing of eyes.

Back on the bench, Corin flicked blood from his fingers, picked up the reins.

Three days to Irongate.

He could hold himself together for three days.
step_outputs.7:
They made camp where the road bent around a low, scrubby hill, just far enough from the track that passing eyes would have to work to see them. Corin unhitched the mare, rubbed her down, checked each wheel spoke in turn. Tap, tap, tap. Three small comforts: the horse, the wheels, the weight of his blades.

The light went pink, then grey. Wind tugged at his cloak, brought him the smell of damp earth and old ash.

Behind him, chains rasped.

He’d left Lysira tied to the wagon rail while he worked. She said nothing, only watched, the way she did: avid, amused, like she was learning him one repetition at a time.

When he finally turned toward her, she lifted her brows as if to say: Took you long enough.

“Food,” he said.

He hooked a hand around her collar, steadying her as he cut the rope to lower her arms. She hissed once as blood came back into her fingers. He left the iron on her wrists, the chain to the bedpost, the shackles at her ankles. Enough metal to anchor a small ship.

He sat her on a crate. She balanced easily despite the steel.

“Open.”

He pulled the gag loose. She worked her jaw, tongue tasting freedom like it was wine.

“You know,” she said, “there are gentler ways to treat a lady.”

“Don’t see one,” Corin said. He dug a heel into the small fire until it caught. “You bite.”

“Only when invited.” She flexed her hands, winced, then hid it. “Who taught you those knots? Proper, that.”

He skewered strips of dried meat, held them over the flame. “Regulation issue.”

“Irongate issue,” she corrected. “Your little dead friends from the road had the mark. You going to tell me that doesn’t itch, soldier?”

He didn’t answer. The sigil on the bandit’s wrist had sat in his mind all afternoon like a stone under the tongue. Outer company brand, yes. Could’ve deserted. Could’ve gone rogue.

Could’ve.

He flipped the meat, watched fat spit into the fire.

“They were deserters,” he said finally. “Not my concern.”

“Paid to take me off your hands early, more like,” she said. “Or just to make sure your prize arrives with a few pieces missing.”

“I’m under contract,” Corin said. “Signed seal from the magistrate’s clerk. Direct delivery.”

“Mm. You trust paper that much?” She leaned forward, chains clinking. Firelight threw her horns in sharp black arcs behind her. “That’s adorable.”

He met her gaze. “I trust coin.”

“And you think coin will protect you if someone higher decides I shouldn’t reach those nice iron gates with a tongue in my head?”

The meat charred. He swore softly, pulled it back, set it to cool. He sliced bread, cheese, set his plate on an overturned bucket. Gave her a share, because cargo or not, starving her was stupid.

She took the food with manacled fingers, ate fast, efficient. No pretense now.

He watched the way she didn’t quite look at the bread before biting. Hungry, then.

“You enjoy that?” she asked him through a mouthful, nodding toward the corpses still somewhere back on the road. “Killing men who used to be your own?”

“They weren’t my own,” he said.

“No?” She licked grease from her thumb with a pointed flick of tongue. “Funny mark to share, then.”

He ignored the way his shoulders tightened. “I left.”

“So did they.” She waggled her shackled fingers. “Though I imagine your severance package didn’t come with irons.”

“Difference between walking out and being dragged,” he said.

She snorted. “Is there?”

He chewed in silence, watching the fire.

“Why’d you leave, Corin?” she asked, too casually. “Get bored of polishing their boots? Or did something finally stick in your throat?”

“You read that on a poster too?” he asked.

She smiled. “Posters don’t bother with why. That’s the bit that makes people uncomfortable.”

He stabbed his knife into the dirt. “Eat.”

“I am. Multitasking.” She tilted her head, studying his face. “You’re not the pious type. Not cruel enough to enjoy the cleanings. Not soft enough to cry about them. So. What happened? Wrong door kicked in? Wrong child?”

He went very still.

She saw it. Of course she saw it. Her eyes brightened, then, to her credit, dimmed a fraction.

“Ah,” she murmured. “There it is.”

“Careful,” he said. The word came out low. “You’re talking yourself into a beating.”

“Oh, please.” She shifted her chain so it didn’t bite her ankle. “If you wanted to hurt me for sport, it would have been easier before you saw that scar, not after.”

He frowned. “What scar?”

She tapped her own throat, then his with her gaze. “The way you looked at the bodies. You weren’t angry I might be right about who sent them. You were angry they made you carry their mark again.”

He picked up his plate. “You’re noisy.”

“I’ve been told.” She tore off another piece of bread. “If it helps, I don’t think you’re special. Most of your kind are cowards. It’s just some of you know it.”

He stared at her, some mix of anger and grim amusement prickling under his skin.

“You done?” he asked.

“Not remotely,” she said. “But I can pause.”

Silence settled, thicker than the smoke. Crickets started their song in the scrub. The mare shifted, snorted.

He ate. She ate. The fire popped.

“You know what they’ll do to me,” she said finally, quieter. No theatrical roll to her voice now. “Truly. Not the stories they tell in taverns.”

He didn’t want to answer, so he sharpened his knife instead.

She went on anyway. “They can’t put me in the square. Too many would cheer. Too many would jeer. Too messy. No, they take me under. I talk, or I don’t, and it ends the same. Steel or circle.”

“Circle?” he asked before he could stop himself.

She smiled without humor. “You ever seen a soul-binding done properly? They like to practice under the chapel. Sounds like choir practice, if you’re not listening close.”

“You’re lying,” he said, because he needed someone to be.

She shrugged, the iron at her wrists glinting. “Maybe. But you were there when they turned my quarter into kindling. You tell me how far the good city of Irongate will go to keep its hands clean.”

He grit his teeth. He remembered smoke. Screaming. The captain’s voice: by order of the Council, no witnesses.

He remembered holding the door.

“That’s not why I’m taking you in,” he said. “I’m not their priest. I’m a courier.”

“Courier with a conscience,” she said lightly. “Rare breed.”

“I have a contract.”

“You have a choice.” She leaned in, chains scraping. “You make it now, or you pretend you didn’t have one when they open me up.”

He slammed the knife a little too hard into the wood between them. She flinched, just once.

“I make my choices when I’m paid to make them,” he said.

She looked at him for a long moment, then laughed softly and shook her head.

“There it is,” she said. “The slow kind of cowardice.”

His hand tightened on the hilt. “You don’t know a godsdamn thing about me.”

“I know enough,” she said. “You’re not stupid. You’re not kind. You’re tired. You want to believe you can do one thing right if you follow the rules exactly this time.” Her gaze flicked to the dark line of the road. “Only problem is, the rules were written by the people who put a price on my head and a brand on yours.”

Her words landed like thrown stones. Some bounced. One or two found cracks.

He stood abruptly, took her plate, walked it over to the stream trickling in the ditch and rinsed it like it needed purging.

When he came back, she’d gone quiet. She was watching the fire now, humming under her breath—an old Irongate hymn twisted into a minor key. The melody crawled along his spine.

“Don’t do that,” he snapped.

She stopped mid-phrase, smiled to herself. “Touchy.”

He reached for the gag.

“Ah. So we’re sulking,” she said. “By all means, stuff my wicked mouth. Might save someone a nasty thought.”

He hesitated, leather strap in his hands. It would be easier. Quieter. Safer.

“Sleep,” he said instead. “You speak again, I gag you.”

“Promise?” she murmured, but softer now, a thread of weariness under it.

He left her un-gagged. Tied the chain from her collar shorter. Checked every knot twice, then rolled out his bedroll on the far side of the fire, back to a wagon wheel, crossbow within reach.

She lay down as much as the restraints allowed, tail curling close to her body. For a time he listened to the tiny movements of her settling, the clink of metal as she tried to find a position that didn’t hurt.

“Corin,” she said into the dark.

He closed his eyes. “Last word.”

“They don’t pay that much to ask questions,” she said. “Only to make sure I stop answering.”

He didn’t respond. Eventually, the hymn started again under her breath, softer this time, like something she wasn’t aware she was doing.

He stared up at the strip of starless sky and counted the beats between the clinks of her chains until he lost the numbers and, finally, sleep took him.
step_outputs.8:
By midmorning the sky had cleared to a hard, thin blue. The road unspooled ahead in baked ruts and pale dust, bordered by scrub and low stone walls half-swallowed by weeds. Corin sat the bench, reins loose in his hand, the mare choosing her own careful pace.

Behind him, Lysira’s chains knocked lightly with each bump. She was very quiet.

Too quiet.

He let it go for a mile. Two. The silence pressed between his shoulder blades.

“You dead back there?” he called.

A pause. Then, dry: “Disappointed?”

Relief flickered; he stamped it out. “If you die now, I have to explain it.”

“Don’t worry,” she said. “Strangled by your charming knots seems an ignoble end. I’d rather haunt your dreams first.”

Already doing a good job of that, he didn’t say.

He was about to tell her to save her breath when the mare’s ears pricked. The horse’s head came up, nostrils flaring.

“Easy,” Corin murmured, fingers tightening on the reins.

Dust rose ahead, not mare-made. The road bent inside a shallow hollow, flanked by stunted pines. A bad place, too tight and low. He hadn’t liked it on the map. Liked it less now.

He guided the mare to a slower walk, eyes sweeping the flanks of the hollow.

“Corin,” Lysira said. Not flippant this time. Warning.

“I see it,” he said.

Nothing obvious. No man standing in the middle of the road asking for toll this time. Smarter, then.

He let the wagon roll into the cut.

The first arrow came from the right, hissing past his arm to bury itself in the wagon rail by Lysira’s neck.

The mare screamed and danced. Corin ducked, grabbing for the crossbow. More arrows thudded into wood. One carved a hot line across his upper arm.

“Down!” he barked, to the horse, the world, the woman in chains.

She had nowhere to go. Iron clanged as she tried to fold herself smaller.

Men rose from the ditches and shadows of the pines, half a dozen, maybe more. Leather jerkins, mismatched helms. And there, at their center, a man in a proper breastplate with the faint ghost of Irongate’s crest scraped mostly off.

“Corin Hale!” Breastplate bellowed. “Stand down. That cargo is under additional jurisdiction.”

The words had the ring of half-remembered drill. Corin felt a cold, sour taste flood his mouth.

“More deserters?” Lysira called, voice high and mocking. “How popular you are.”

“Shut up,” Corin hissed.

He stood slowly, crossbow leveled just off the man’s chest. No point pretending he hadn’t seen.

“Orders,” Corin said. “Show them.”

Breastplate smirked, reached into his cloak, pulled out a folded, wax-sealed parchment. Held it up between two fingers.

“By directive of the Inner Council,” he shouted, “in the event of irregularities, subject Lysira Vex is to be terminated prior to entry, and all materials secured. Contractor Hale to be compensated upon verification of death.”

Corin’s fingers went numb on the stock. He’d heard the words. His mind stuck on terminate prior to entry.

Behind him, Lysira laughed. It was not a nice sound.

“Oh, they do love you,” she said. “Very trusting.”

“Shut. Up,” he said, without looking back.

Breastplate gestured. Two of his men started forward.

Crossbow bolts. Unarmored throats. He could do it. Then three more would loose at once, and the mare would go down, and so might he, and—

“And if I don’t?” Corin called.

“Then we presume compromise and carry out the order ourselves,” Breastplate said. “Stand aside.”

He didn’t lower the bow. “My contract is with Magistrate Relvar’s office. Delivery in Irongate. Alive.”

“That’s been amended.” Breastplate’s smile didn’t reach his eyes. “You can contest it in writing, if you’re very attached. After.”

An arrow kissed Corin’s boot, close enough to sting splinters.

“They’re not bluffing,” Lysira murmured. “I would know.”

He could hand her over. Let them put an arrow through her eye, watch her drop like any other outlaw. Collect his money from whoever survived the paperwork. Walk away.

If they left him breathing.

He pictured the parchment he’d signed. The wax. The clerk’s cramped handwriting. None of it would matter if she ended up a corpse in a ditch three days out.

“This is an irregularity,” he said.

“It’s an order,” Breastplate snapped. “Move.”

The mare tossed her head, anxious. Corin spoke without thinking: “Whoa, girl.” She calmed a notch. She trusted his voice.

“Corin,” Lysira said, low now. “If they kill me here, they don’t need you. You know that, yes?”

He did. Of course he did. Men like Breastplate didn’t like loose ends.

One of the grunts reached for the wagon’s tailgate.

“Stop,” Corin said.

The man didn’t.

Corin shot him in the face.

The bolt punched through bone with a wet crack. The man went down in the dust.

For a heartbeat, everyone just stared. Corin as much as anyone. He’d expected—he didn’t know. A warning shot. A hand in the way. Something.

“Fuck,” Lysira whispered, almost admiring.

Then the hollow erupted.

Arrows. Shouts. Corin dropped behind the bench as another arrow grazed his neck. Pain burned hot and wet. He grabbed the second crossbow from under the bench, rolled, fired at the archer on the right bank. The man flailed backward into the brush.

“Cut me loose!” Lysira snapped. No more jokes now.

“Not happening,” he grunted, ducking a thrown spear that whanged off the wagon.

“Then at least turn me!” she snarled. “I can’t see a godsdamn thing!”

He swore, scrambled to the back, grabbed her shoulder to pivot her so she faced the rising shapes. An arrow thunked into the wood where his head had been.

“Down!” he shouted, and this time it was for her.

She folded as best she could. He saw her fingers writhe in their chains, shaping something. Felt a prickle along his forearms.

“Infernal whore!” one of the men shouted, loosing wild.

She hissed words that made the air taste like smoke. The ground at the feet of two charging men shivered; a jag of black flame snapped up, not high enough to engulf, but hot enough to send them stumbling, swatting at smoldering leather.

Corin used their panic. Came off the wagon with steel drawn, drove into the closest man’s guard. Steel on steel, pivot, slash. Blood sprayed his sleeve.

“Left!” Lysira shouted.

He turned into a swing that would have taken his neck. Sparks burst as blades met. He shoved the man away with a shoulder, stabbed low.

The air stank of sweat, fear, something darker from Lysira’s muttered syllables.

“Behind you!” she cried.

He dropped as an arrow hissed through where his spine had been. Rolled, flung a knife without sight. A strangled yelp. One less.

There were too many. Five, now three, now—he couldn’t count. Each movement an answer to muscle memory. His world narrowed to edges, angles, the rasp of his own breath.

A hand grabbed for the wagon rail beside Lysira. She bared her teeth and spat a jag of red-tinged sparks directly into the attacker’s face. He screamed, clutching at his eyes, stumbled backward into Corin’s waiting blade.

“Show-off,” Corin muttered, panting.

“Saved your grateful hide,” she shot back.

Something heavy slammed into his ribs. He staggered, felt at once the ugly crunch of metal on bone. Breastplate. The man came in hard with a short sword, fury bright in his eyes.

“You’re under arrest,” Breastplate snarled.

Corin laughed, a ragged, disbelieving sound. “Get in line.”

They crashed together. The other man was bigger, armored; Corin was faster. He let a blow slide off his bracer, stepped inside, hammered an elbow into the weak point under the arm. Breastplate grunted. Corin slammed his forehead into the man’s nose. Cartilage gave with a satisfying crack.

Breastplate reeled. Corin knocked his sword away, drove his own up under the rim of the cuirass, straight into soft gut.

“I had a contract,” Corin said through his teeth.

Breastplate coughed blood on him and sagged down.

Silence, gradually, except for the harsh rasp of breathing and the whimper of one wounded man in the brush. The mare tossed her head, trembling.

Corin stood in the wreckage, blood dripping from his sleeve, chest heaving. One of the men was trying to crawl away, leaving a dark smear.

“Knife,” he muttered.

He walked over, yanked the blade out of the crawling man’s back where it had lodged earlier. The man made a wet sound and went still.

“You left one,” Lysira said from the wagon. “For a minute.”

“Fixed it,” Corin said. His voice sounded wrong in his own ears. Too flat.

He wiped the knife on a cloak, scanned the hollow. Six bodies. No one moving but him and the prisoner.

He went to Breastplate’s corpse, pried stiff fingers open, plucked up the parchment. Broke the seal, read.

The script was tight, familiar.

…terminate asset if contractor compromised… no liability… payment upon presentation of sigil and tongue…

His own name there, in smaller hand: Corin Hale, responsible for secure transfer until such revision. Subject to review.

Lysira watched his face as he read.

“Well?” she asked softly.

“Inner Council,” he said. His throat was dry. “This is real.”

“Congratulations,” she said. “You just killed your first loyal dog for me.”

“Shut up,” he said, but there was no heat.

“You going to hand me a knife now?” she asked. “Or keep pretending you don’t need what I can do?”

He walked to the wagon, grabbed the chain above her wrists. Blood streaked his fingers.

“I cut you loose, you run,” he said. “I don’t get paid either way.”

“If I wanted to be gone,” she said, “I’d have been gone the first time you fell asleep with your bootlace loose.”

He frowned. His laces were never loose.

She smiled. “Think, Corin. They were willing to kill you and leave me in a ditch. You think that stops at the gate?”

He stared up the road, where Irongate’s walls would be, three days away. The wind off the hollow smelled of blood and pine.

“I finish what I start,” he said. It sounded weak.

“Then decide what you’re starting,” Lysira said. For once, no laughter under it. Just iron.

He almost reached for her shackles. Instead he turned away, stumbled back to the mare, hands moving on their own: checking hooves, calming her breath.

Behind him, chains shifted as she resettled, as if he’d done something.

He hadn’t. Not yet.

When they rolled out of the hollow, the bodies were already drawing flies. Irongate waited ahead, patient as a gallows.
step_outputs.9:
They rode until the sun was a white coin hammered high, until the blood on Corin’s sleeve went from tacky to stiff. No sign of pursuit behind them but the memory of it crawled up his back.

By noon, the mare’s flanks were heaving. He pulled them off the road into a shallow dip ringed with scrub and rock, more out of habit than conscious choice. Shade was thin. The air tasted of dust and old iron.

He dismounted stiffly. His ribs protested where Breastplate’s blow had landed, a dull, grinding ache.

“Stop,” Lysira said when he went straight for the wagon rail. “You’re dripping on my good chains.”

He looked down. Blood had traced a line from his shoulder to his wrist, darker against the leather.

“Not yours,” he said.

“It is now.” Her gaze flicked over him, assessing. “You’re hurt.”

“Not enough,” he said. He loosed the knot holding her arms above her head, lowering them again. She hissed as circulation returned.

He should bind his own cuts. Instead he checked hers: wrists raw, shoulders tremoring with strain, a thin burn mark along her forearm where the black flame she’d called had kissed her on its way past.

“You scorch yourself?” he asked.

“Collateral damage,” she said. “I had limited angles. You’re welcome.”

He grunted. Fished in his kit, came up with a clean strip of cloth. Held it out.

She lifted her brows. “How tender.”

“For me,” he said, and wound it around his arm, teeth gritted.

She watched, a little too intent.

“You know,” she said, “it’s rude not to say thank you when someone helps you not die.”

“You lit some men on fire,” he said. “I happen to approve, this once.”

She smiled, small and sharp. “Progress.”

He moved to hitch her chains so she could sit against the wagon wheel. She let him, only flinching when the iron scraped sore skin.

“You’re heavier than you look,” he muttered, more to fill space.

“Dense with sin,” she said. “What did the nice letter say?”

He exhaled. He’d folded the parchment into his coat, like evidence or a curse.

“Terminate asset if contractor compromised,” he said flatly. “Payment on presentation of proof.”

“Tongue,” she said.

He shot her a look.

“They like tongues,” Lysira said. “Hang them on hooks. Reminds everyone what silence looks like.”

He swallowed. His throat felt raw.

“Could be forged,” he said. “Could be some bastard with a stamp.”

She tilted her head. “How much do you need it to be a lie?”

He didn’t answer.

He poured water, handed her the skin. She drank, slower this time, eyes never leaving his.

“You could dump me in a ravine,” she said between swallows. “Tell them it was bandits. Keep your skin.”

“No payoff,” he said.

“I’m flattered that’s what tips the scales.”

“It’s arithmetic,” he said. “I spent bolts, blood, time. I want something back.”

“And yet you shot their man,” she said. “Against orders.”

“They changed the terms mid-road.” He capped the waterskin harder than necessary. “I don’t like cheats.”

She laughed. “Spoken like a gambler who thinks the house should be fair.”

He turned away, busying himself with the small things: checking the horse’s hooves, resetting the wheel blocks, prodding the sky for signs of weather. Anything but sit still.

When he finally sank down on a flat rock near the wagon, the ache in his ribs flared. He pressed a hand to his side, felt the warm tackiness there. Not deep. Annoying.

She watched him with that same too-bright focus.

“They branded you,” she said abruptly.

He blinked. “What?”

“Your neck,” she said. “Cute little ghost of a mark. Same place they like to put it on the soldiers. You had it scraped?”

His hand went, traitor, to the base of his throat. There was a faint roughness there, scar tissue where the Irongate crest had once been burned in. Years ago, he’d gone to a hedge-witch with a bottle of rotgut and said: make it less. She had, mostly.

“None of your business,” he said.

“Oh, it’s very my business,” Lysira said. “You’re the one driving.”

He stared at her. “You going to give me a sermon about complicity, Vex?”

“Mm,” she said, tilting her head back against the wood. “No. You already preach at yourself, I can hear it.”

“Keep your ears out of my skull.”

“But it’s so loud in there.” Her chains chimed as she shifted. “Tell me, Corin. Was it my quarter? Or some other lucky street?”

He found a bit of twig, snapped it between his fingers. “Eat, drink, sleep. Talk less.”

She smiled without much humor. “It matters, you know. Where their pretty boys practiced.”

He didn’t want to say it. But it was there, rising, and she was going to keep digging until she hit it or a bone.

“A tenement on Ash Lane,” he said finally. “We had a list. Doors to kick. Names.”

Her face changed. Only a fraction. The faintest tightening around the eyes.

“Ash Lane,” she repeated. “Close to the temple.”

“Yes.”

“My mother scrubbed floors there,” she said softly. “Temple brass. Ash Lane.”

He stared at her. She stared back.

“You remember?” she asked.

“Smoke,” he said. The word came out thin. “Children in the stairwell. We were told it was a cell. We didn’t—” He stopped. The air felt thick.

“You didn’t check,” she said, as gentle as one of her earlier barbs had been cruel. “You held the door.”

He flinched.

“You’re not special,” she went on. “Most of you did it. Some of you liked it. Some of you only looked away.”

“Why do you care which I was?” he snapped.

“Because I like to know who’s delivering me,” she said. “If I’m being walked to the knife by a man who believes in it, or a man who knows better and does it anyway.”

Silence stretched. A fly buzzed near the horse’s flank. Somewhere a crow called.

“Why’d you stay?” she asked. “After Ash Lane. You left later, yes? But not then.”

He laughed, short and ugly. “You want the true version?”

“I collect those,” she said.

“I stayed,” he said, “because I was two years from a pension.”

She nodded once. No shock. No outrage.

“There it is,” she said.

“You going to tell me how virtuous you were, while we’re confessing?” he asked. “Never took a coin from someone who bled for it? Never sold a name that got the wrong man killed?”

Her jaw flexed. “I’ve done worse things than you,” she said. “Difference is, I don’t pretend they were accidents.”

“So we’re both trash,” he said. “Comforting.”

“No,” she said. “We’re both useful. That’s the uncomfortable part.”

He leaned back against the rock, feeling very tired.

“What did you steal,” he asked, staring at the washed-out sky, “that’s worth all this?”

She shifted, the chains complaining.

“What do they say I stole?” she countered.

“Names. Ledgers. Relics. Secrets. Half a demon, depending who you drink with.” He glanced at her. “Which is closest?”

“Secrets,” she said. “Mostly.”

“Mostly,” he repeated.

She sighed, a breath that seemed to drag chains with it. “They’ve been binding, Corin. You know that much, or you’ve guessed. Whole circles of us, tied under the chapel. Contracts written in blood and righteous ink. They say it keeps the city safe. Maybe some of it does.”

“And you object?”

“I object,” she said, eyes hardening, “to cattle pens. To my people fed into pretty fire so merchants can sleep soft. I object to holy men smiling while they count the coins.”

“And your answer is… what? Burn it all down?”

“If necessary,” she said, without blinking. “But first? Show them. Names. Accounts. Rites. Make it too filthy to ignore.”

He believed her anger. He wasn’t sure he believed her solution.

“And in the process?” he said. “How many die who didn’t sign up?”

“How many already did?” she shot back.

He didn’t have an answer that wouldn’t sound like an excuse.

“Anyway,” she said, some lightness creeping back in, “we’re getting ahead of ourselves. You still haven’t decided whether to sell me or not. Talking about revolutions feels premature.”

“I don’t do revolutions,” he said. “I do deliveries.”

She looked at his blood-stiff sleeve, the fading brand at his throat.

“Keep saying that,” she murmured. “Maybe it’ll be true again.”

Heat climbed up his neck. He stood, sudden.

“I’m tightening your chains,” he said. “You’re getting too comfortable.”

“Ooh,” she said weakly. “Punish me.”

But when he stepped close, she didn’t lean into him or away. Just watched as he checked shackles, adjusted the collar so it didn’t grind raw at the same spot.

“Why bother?” she asked as his fingers brushed the bruise. “If you’re delivering me to a knife, why care if my neck stings?”

He pulled his hand back like she’d burned him. “Cargo is mine until I sign it over. You don’t break what you’re responsible for.”

She looked at him like that was both pathetic and unexpectedly decent.

“Maybe,” she said, “you’re not entirely the wrong man on the wrong road.”

“Don’t start thinking that,” he said.

“Too late,” she said, and smiled, tired and poisonous. “I’m very impressionable.”

The ache in his ribs sharpened. He stepped away, needing air.

The rest of the afternoon sagged. No ambush sprung from the next bend. No letters fell from the sky. Just the drag of wheels and the creak of wood and the dull, mutual awareness that something between captor and captive had shifted sideways.

As dusk came on, Corin made camp with mechanical efficiency. Fire, water, horse. He left Lysira’s gag off. She didn’t push, for once. Only hummed under her breath—a tune he almost recognized, wrong notes in the right places.

Lying on his bedroll later, staring into the scrim of smoke, he listened to her chains settle. His mind kept circling the folded parchment in his coat, the memory of Ash Lane, the way her face had barely changed when he admitted what kept him in uniform that extra year.

He’d expected condemnation. She’d given him something worse: understanding.

Slow cowardice, she’d called it.

Three days to Irongate had become two and some ragged edges. He told himself he would deliver the cargo, take the coin, vanish. That the rest was noise.

Sleep didn’t believe him.
step_outputs.10:
The storm rolled in with the dusk.

It came hard and fast from the east, a slate wall devouring the horizon. Wind hit first, hot and wrong, shoving at the wagon, stirring Lysira’s hair into a whipping red veil. The mare snorted, ears pinning.

“Perfect,” Corin muttered.

He’d hoped to cover more ground before nightfall. Instead he watched the first ragged curtains of rain sweep across the scrub and did the quick sums: open road, iron-laden prisoner, one tired horse. No good.

“There,” Lysira said, nodding ahead.

He saw it a beat later: the silhouette of stone off the road—half-fallen walls, a sagging arch. Old watchpost or shrine, eaten by time. Enough of a shell.

He turned the mare toward it as the rain broke.

Cold sheets slammed down, flattening his cloak, turning dust to slick mud in a heartbeat. He hunched his shoulders, blinking against the water. Behind him, Lysira coughed once as it hit her full in the face.

He got them under the ruined arch with the sky cracking open.

It wasn’t much. Three walls, roof mostly collapsed, one corner intact enough to keep direct rain off. Old fire-black on stone suggested it had sheltered other desperate folk before them.

Corin halted the wagon in the driest patch he could find, set the brake. The mare stood shaking, steam rising from her flanks.

He jumped down into ankle-deep water, teeth gritted as pain bit his bruised ribs.

“Stay,” he told the horse, because it made him feel better. She flicked an ear.

Lysira sat shackled in the open bed, drenched. Water ran in dark rivulets down her throat, over the iron collar. Her hair hung in heavy ropes. The chains pulled at her wrists.

“Well,” she said around the rain, “this is miserable.”

“Hold,” Corin said, and climbed up.

He unhooked the rope that kept her arms high, lowering them again. Her shoulders sagged with relief, then trembled.

“Cold?” he asked.

She gave him a look. “No, I’m enjoying the bath.”

Smart mouth intact. That was something.

He hesitated, then unlocked the chain from the rail, leaving the collar on, the manacles and ankle shackles in place. He guided her—one hand around the iron at her throat, firm but not throttling—down off the wagon. She was lighter than she looked. Or he was more tired.

Inside the crumbled room, he sat her on a low stone ledge. The chain from her collar he looped around a protruding block, snapped the lock, gave it a testing tug. Secure. She could move her arms now, at least within a span.

She watched him all the while, water dripping from her horns.

“You’re spoiling me,” she said softly. Less bite, more bone-deep fatigue.

He shrugged out of his sodden cloak, wrung it once, hung it on a broken beam. The storm roared just beyond their partial roof. Lightning threw their shadows huge and monstrous on the walls.

He got a small fire going with difficulty, hands clumsy with cold. Old, dry stones under the fallen roof gave up enough scrap tinder if you knew where to look. When the first tongue of flame caught, a feeble orange against the grey, relief loosened something in his chest.

“Come closer,” he told her.

She rattled the chain. “Limited options, remember?”

He slid the lock down a notch, giving her another foot. She dragged herself nearer the heat, shackles clinking.

“You planning to roast me?” she asked.

“Thought I’d start with drying you off,” he said.

He took his spare blanket from the wagon, shook it out, and draped it around her shoulders. She stiffened at first, as if expecting a blow. Then the smell of wool and smoke wrapped around her and she let out a breath she maybe hadn’t meant to.

“Careful,” she said. “If you keep this up, I’ll think you’re conflicted.”

“I like my cargo alive,” he said. “Less paperwork.”

Thunder cracked hard overhead. For a second the fire trembled, then held.

Her wet hair clung to her cheeks. She brushed it back with clumsy, manacled hands.

“That last lot,” she said. “Your ex-friends. You dream about them tonight, or me?”

“Neither, if I’m lucky,” he said.

“You’re not,” she said, almost gently.

He checked the chain between her ankles; made sure she couldn’t bolt, even if the collar chain somehow failed. He was close enough to smell her now—a mix of wet stone, acrid ash-smoke from earlier casting, and something copper-sweet underlying it.

When he straightened, her gaze was level with his.

“You’re shaking,” she said.

“It’s cold,” he said.

“Mm.” Her eyes dipped to his ribs. “And you’re bruised. Take it off.”

He snorted. “That your default solution?”

“Yes, but in this case I mean the leather. You’re not going to impress me into submission by dying of stubbornness.”

He hesitated. The wet vest was sucking heat from him. Pride argued. Sense overruled.

He peeled it off, winced as the stiff fabric dragged over the bruise. The mark beneath was an ugly bloom of purple and yellow.

Lysira whistled low. “He got you good.”

“I’ve had worse,” he said.

“Not recently,” she said. “Come here.”

He blinked. “No.”

“You’re an idiot,” she said. “Fire’s small. Either we share it or we both freeze slower. I am, regrettably, invested in your continued breathing.”

He hesitated a second longer, then sat down opposite her, close enough that their knees almost touched, fire between. Rain drummed a steady assault on the shattered stone.

“Tell me something honest,” she said.

“No,” he said.

She smiled faintly. “That’s a start.”

Silence stretched. The kind punctured only by storm and the pop of damp wood. It wasn’t comfortable, but it wasn’t the brittle sparring of before.

“How long were you in their pit?” he asked abruptly. He hadn’t meant to. The question slipped out.

She picked at a link of her chain. “Long enough the dark felt more familiar than daylight for a bit.”

“That’s not an answer,” he said.

“Three months under,” she said, eyes on the fire. “Before that, in and out. Cells. Interrogations. You know the type.”

He didn’t move. “They hurt you for names?”

“They hurt me because it’s what they do,” she said. “Names were garnish.”

“And yet here you are, still talking,” he said.

She glanced up. “What else is there?”

He watched the light move over her face. The easy mask had slipped; beneath it was something thinner, stretched, not unbreakable.

“Why didn’t they finish it?” he asked.

She shrugged, blanket shifting. “Because I had something they wanted more alive than dead, for a while. Because someone misjudged the rope. Because I’m very charming.”

He huffed. “You weaponize pity.”

“Oh, yes,” she said. “Almost as effectively as you weaponize silence.”

Lightning flashed, freezing them in stark black and white. For a heartbeat, he saw the iron circle at her throat like a halo turned wrong.

“Corin,” she said, softer. “They meant to kill you today. On paper. In ink. Is that really the house you’re breaking your back to serve clean?”

“I serve the contract,” he said automatically. “Not the house.”

“The contract is theirs,” she said. “You know this. You keep pretending it’s separate because then you don’t have to admit you’re walking me back to the same pit you crawled out of.”

He felt that like another blow to his ribs. “I’m not crawling.”

“No,” she agreed. “You’re marching.”

The fire snapped. Rain beat time.

“Why do you care what I do?” he asked.

“Because it decides if I live,” she said simply. “Why do you think I’m talking so damn much?”

He almost laughed. It came out a rasp. “Honest at last.”

“I can do that too,” she said. “Now and then.”

He shifted. Too close. Too raw. The bruise howled, tiredness clawed. He rubbed a hand over his face.

She watched the motion, head tilted.

“You also don’t want to be that man again,” she said. “The one at the door. The one who stayed for a pension.”

He stared into the fire. Embers reflected in his eyes.

“Shut up,” he said, but there was no edge.

“You want me to stop,” she said, “put the gag back on. You’re the one who left it off.”

He met her gaze and saw, for once, no triumph. Just a mirror.

“I left it off so I’d hear if you tried a spell,” he said.

“Lie,” she said softly.

The storm’s roar blurred the edges of the world. In the close, damp little ruin, their breath plumed faint in the cold.

She drew the blanket tighter, chains clinking. One manacled wrist slipped free enough that she could extend her hand a few inches toward the fire. The movement brought her closer to him.

He was aware, suddenly and acutely, of her nearness. The line of her throat above the iron. The pulse there. The way her tail flicked, restless, betraying tension she wasn’t voicing.

“This is a bad idea,” he muttered.

“Which part?” she asked. “The storm shelter, the near-executions, or talking to your prisoner like a person?”

He shifted again, closer to the wall, as if space alone could help.

Her eyes tracked him. Amusement ghosted there, but underneath it something more uncertain.

“If I said,” she began, then stopped. Tried again. “If I said I don’t know which part of you I’m trying to wake up—the mercenary or the man who walked away—would that make this easier or worse?”

“Worse,” he said.

“Thought so,” she said, and laughed softly at herself.

The sound drew the thinnest of smiles out of him. It felt foreign on his face.

Thunder rolled, more distant now. The worst of the rain shifted from pounding to steady.

“You going to ask?” she said.

“What.”

“If I’m using a charm on you,” she said. “If I’m bending your will with wicked tiefling arts.”

He met her gaze. “Are you?”

“No,” she said. “You’re doing this to yourself.”

He sat with that. He couldn’t say she was wrong.

Her hand, resting near the fire, shivered. Cold. The blanket only did so much.

Without thinking it all the way through, he reached out and caught the edge of the wool, tugging it more securely around her shoulders. His fingers brushed damp skin at the base of her neck where the collar cut.

She sucked in a breath. Not dramatic—just sharp, involuntary.

“Easy,” he said. He wasn’t sure which of them he meant.

For a heartbeat, they were close enough that he could see the fine, darker freckling along her collarbone. Close enough to feel her warmth under the chill.

Then he pulled his hand back like he’d touched coals.

“Don’t mistake survival for sentiment,” he said.

“Too late,” she murmured. “I’m very bad at that sort of math.”

He almost reached for the gag then, craving the simplicity of silence. But something in her face—a flicker of bone-deep tired, the echo of temple hymns and Ash Lane—stopped him.

Instead he tossed another stick on the fire and leaned back, putting a sliver more distance between them.

“Sleep,” he said. “We move at first light.”

She watched him a moment longer, eyes reflecting flame.

“If you change your mind before then,” she said quietly, “I’d prefer you do it while I’m awake.”

“About what,” he asked, though he knew.

“About where you’re delivering me,” she said.

The storm muttered away into the distance. In the dripping hush that followed, he lay down with his back to her, crossbow within reach. He listened to her chains settle, to the soft rustle as she tried to find a position that didn’t grind metal into bone.

At some point, his bruised ribs and frayed mind dragged him under.

Behind him, the tiefling who talked too much lay watching the faint glow of embers, feeling the ghost of a calloused hand at her throat, wondering which was more dangerous: his duty, or the crack in it.
step_outputs.11:
By dawn the storm had wrung itself out, leaving the world scrubbed and sullen. Water dripped from the ruined stone in slow, patient beats. Mist clung low over the scrub like something that hadn’t decided yet whether to rise or sink.

Corin’s bruises had stiffened overnight. When he pushed himself up from the bedroll, his ribs screamed, his shoulder throbbed, and his throat felt raw from too little sleep and too much silent argument.

The embers still glowed faintly. Lysira was awake, watching them, blanket around her shoulders, chains coiled in her lap like a nest of tired snakes.

“Morning,” she said. Her voice was husked by cold and disuse, none of the earlier gleam.

He grunted. “You try anything?”

“Dreamed loudly,” she said. “Not sure if that counts.”

He checked her restraints. Nothing tampered with beyond the small adjustments he himself had made. Either she was better at hiding her tricks than he was at finding them, or—for now—she’d left them alone.

“What’s the verdict?” she asked as he unlocked her collar chain from the stone. “Back to marching me toward the pit?”

“Back to Irongate,” he said. “We stick to the road. We watch for more irregularities. We don’t die in a ditch. That’s the plan.”

“Bold,” she said dryly.

He helped her back into the wagon—hands brief, impersonal as he could make them. She gave him no theatrics, just a hiss when iron bit an old bruise.

By the time the sun had burned the mist down to a pale haze, they were moving again.

The land rose gently now, hedgerows and low stone fences more common. A distant smear of darker line on the horizon could have been woods, or the first hint of outer watchtowers. Irongate’s invisible gravity tugged at them with every mile.

They’d gone an hour in cursory silence when the mare’s ears pricked and a faint jingle answered from ahead: harness, tack, disciplined.

“Riders,” Corin said.

Lysira shifted, chains chiming. “Uniform?”

“Sounds like it.”

He felt her gaze on the back of his neck. “You going to gag me?”

“If I haven’t yet,” he said, “you must be doing something wrong.”

She snorted. “Flatterer.”

The riders crested the low slope ahead in a clean wedge. Five of them in Irongate grey, mail bright, tabards crisp. At their point rode a man in better steel—the lines of his armor familiar even before Corin made out his face.

“Shit,” Corin muttered.

“Well, well,” Lysira said softly. “Old friends.”

Captain Jorin Kest reined his horse to a halt across the road, raising one hand. Same square jaw. Same tidy beard. A few more threads of white in his dark hair, that was all.

“Hale,” Kest called. His voice carried easy. “Heard you took a fat contract. And lived this long with it. Surprising.”

Corin checked the crossbow at his side, then forced himself to relax his grip. “Captain.”

He slowed the wagon to a stop a polite distance away.

Kest’s gaze slid past him to Lysira in the back. A small, satisfied curve at the corner of his mouth. Not leering. Assessment. Asset confirmed.

“Well done,” Kest said. “Council will be pleased.”

His eyes flicked to the bruising at Corin’s throat, the tear in his sleeve. “We had word of interference. You encountered trouble?”

Corin shrugged as if his ribs didn’t hurt. “Road trash. Took care of it.”

Kest nodded once. “Your report will mention those details.”

One of the soldiers to his left—a woman with a new-issue spear and old scars on her knuckles—was looking at Lysira like she’d seen posters, heard stories. Curiosity, edged with contempt. The usual.

“Orders changed on the road,” Corin said. “Met a group waving something with red wax and big words.”

Kest’s mouth thinned. “Show me.”

Corin hesitated a fraction, then dug into his coat, drew out the folded parchment, and passed it over.

Kest scanned it without dismounting, his face a careful blank.

“Authentic,” he said. “And out of date. Superseded this morning. You’re in luck.”

“Am I,” Corin said.

“The directive to terminate on the road was rescinded,” Kest said. “New instructions.” He folded the parchment, tucked it into his own satchel. “You’ll deliver the prisoner to the inner holding, as contracted. With supervision.”

Lysira laughed once, sharp. “They couldn’t decide whether to kill me in a ditch or in a cellar, so they split the difference.”

Kest’s gaze snapped to her. “Silence that thing,” he said, almost lazily.

Corin didn’t move.

Kest looked back at him. “Problem, Hale?”

“She’s secure,” Corin said. “She talks. It’s noise.”

“She’s filth,” one of the others muttered.

Lysira smiled sweetly at him. “You shave with that mouth?”

Kest’s jaw ticked. “Gag her.”

It was an order. It came with the weight of years of drilled obedience behind it, the memory of standing straighter when that voice snapped on the training yard.

Corin climbed down. He felt the soldiers’ gazes on him like pricks. He went to the back, picked up the gag.

Lysira watched him. No plea. No mockery. Just waiting to see which man he was today.

He hesitated a heartbeat too long.

“Hale,” Kest said. Not loud. Very clear.

Corin slid the gag between her teeth, buckled it. Her eyes flickered with something he pretended he hadn’t seen.

“Good,” Kest said. “You always were reliable when given clear direction.”

The compliment tasted like rust.

“How far out?” Corin asked.

“A day, at this pace,” Kest said. “Less for us.” He gestured, and two riders nudged their horses closer, flanking the wagon. “We’ll escort you. Irongate wants this done clean, and there are…concerns about external interference.”

“External,” Corin repeated.

“You’ve drawn attention,” Kest said. “Her allies. Other parties. You’ve done your job. Don’t ruin it feeling important.”

Corin’s fingers twitched near the reins.

“And Hale,” Kest added, voice lowering just enough for it to be meant, if not entirely private, “when this is over, come see me. There’s work for solid men. You don’t have to spend the rest of your life in ditches with rats and refuse.”

Refuse. His gaze had flicked, minutely, to Lysira as he said it.

Corin gave a noncommittal noise. “We’ll see.”

They set out again, the little escort column folding him into its structure. Two ahead, two behind, Kest alongside for a time, horse’s hooves matched to the wagon’s slow roll.

It felt like a noose tightening.

He kept his eyes on the road, on the mare’s ears, on anything but the precise, familiar cut of Kest’s armor.

“So,” Kest said, conversational. “Any trouble with the asset?”

“Talks,” Corin said. “A lot.”

“She’ll say anything,” Kest said. “She’s a trained manipulator. Tells each man what he needs to hear.”

“Sure,” Corin said.

“She get under your skin?” Kest’s tone was still light, but the question had an edge.

Corin shrugged. “Harder to sleep with someone trying to sell you blasphemy every hour. Nothing I can’t handle.”

Kest chuckled. “Good. Wasn’t sure, after you…lost heart, last time.”

Ash Lane flickered at the edge of Corin’s vision. Smoke. Screams. Kest’s hand on his shoulder, saying, We do what we must.

“I left,” Corin said.

“You ran,” Kest said, still smiling. “Now you’re walking back. Circle closed. Shame about the pay grade.”

One of the rear riders laughed obligingly.

Corin focused on breathing. In. Out. The bruises helped. Pain anchored.

Behind them, Lysira’s chains shifted. He knew she was listening. Even gagged, she’d be cataloguing every word.

They rode like that for miles: Irongate grey at his flanks, Kest’s presence a solid weight.

As the sun leaned west, the walls appeared.

At first only a darker line on the horizon, then teeth. High black stone, towers biting the sky, banners snapping. Irongate.

Corin felt something in his chest knot tight. The brand at his throat seemed to itch phantom-wise.

Kest watched him see it. “Home,” he said.

For who, Corin didn’t ask.

A little further on, they passed a gallows at a crossroads. Old, weathered. Rope frayed. A warning more than a tool these days. A few bones lay scattered beneath, bleached.

One of the younger soldiers spat. “String her up here, save time.”

Kest gave him a look. “We do not waste a prize. Nor do we improvise justice. That is the difference between us and them.”

Them. The word tasted the same as it had on Ash Lane.

Corin’s knuckles whitened briefly on the reins.

When they stopped at a waystation before the final stretch, it was still light. A fortified house for Irongate couriers: stone yard, well, small chapel, stables. Safe, in the official sense.

“Camp here,” Kest said. “We ride at dawn. Straight through the outer gate, no delays.”

The soldiers moved with practiced ease, unsaddling, watering horses, loosening mail. One was sent inside with a packet of papers.

Corin took the wagon to the far side of the yard, near the shadow of the wall. He set the brake. For a moment, no one was looking directly at him.

He went to the back, unbuckled Lysira’s gag.

She spat once, jaw working. “Your friends are charming,” she whispered. “He is going to kill you.”

“Keep your voice down,” Corin said.

Her eyes glittered. “He wasn’t joking about work. Men like him don’t offer, they recruit. One way or another.”

“I can handle Kest,” Corin said. It felt like a lie.

“Mm.” She tilted her head, studying his face. “You stiffened up nicely when he called me refuse.”

“He’s an ass,” Corin said. “Always was.”

“And you were his good hound,” she said. “Walked well on his leash. You planning to put it back on?”

“Shut up,” he said, too fast.

She smiled, faint and vicious. “There. Nerve.”

Footsteps approached. Corin snapped the gag half into place, leaving it hanging loose enough she could bite it up if anyone looked too closely.

Kest came around the wagon, hands clasped behind his back.

“How’s our guest?” he asked.

“Contained,” Corin said.

“Good.” Kest studied Lysira’s face. “You know what waits for you, creature?”

She met his gaze and, very deliberately, did not answer.

“Still proud,” Kest said. “No matter. The circle is ready. Councilor will have what he paid for.”

He turned back to Corin. “Paperwork’s in order. You’ll receive your coin tomorrow, after transfer of custody. You will come inside with us. There are statements to sign.”

Inside. Below. Sign.

Corin nodded once. “Understood.”

Kest clapped his shoulder, friendly. The weight made his ribs ache. “Good man.”

When he was gone, Lysira let out a slow, careful breath.

“Circle,” she mouthed. “Not scaffold.”

“I heard,” Corin said.

“You still pretending you didn’t know?” she asked.

He looked at the small chapel across the yard, its iron-banded door. A memory crawled up: chanting from below. Heat.

“I suspected,” he said. “Didn’t want to…confirm.”

“There’s your problem,” she said. “You think not looking makes it less real.”

He stared at her. The chain at her throat. The bruises he’d helped put on her.

“What do you want me to do?” he asked, low. “Right now. Be specific.”

She blinked once, surprised by the question. Then: “Don’t let them take me below.”

“You want me to fight half a garrison in their own yard?” His laugh came out ragged. “That your grand plan?”

“I want you,” she said, and now there was no mockery at all, “not to help them.”

He didn’t answer.

Night settled. Lamps lit along the inner wall. The soldiers parceled out watches. Corin spread his bedroll near the wagon wheel again, crossbow at hand. The familiar ritual felt suddenly thin.

Later, as the yard quieted, Kest sat at a small table by the waystation door, going over papers. One of the younger guards dozed on his feet.

Lysira’s chains rustled softly.

“Corin,” she whispered.

“What.”

“If you do nothing, you’re choosing,” she said. “Just so we’re clear.”

He closed his eyes. “Sleep.”

“You, too,” she said. “You’ll need it to walk me through those gates.”

He lay there, staring up at a square of less-dark sky. Irongate’s walls loomed in the near distance, solid as judgment.

He’d told himself all along he was a courier, nothing more. Now the path narrowed to a single point: tomorrow, he would walk into the city that had made him, with a woman they meant to carve open, under the eyes of a man who’d once told him which children counted.

His fingers found the edge of the folded parchment in his coat. He thought of burning it. He thought of handing it over. He thought of cutting a chain.

He did none of those things.

Not yet.

Sleep, when it came, was thin and jagged. He dreamed of doors. On one side, Ash Lane. On the other, the chapel’s iron stairs. His hand on the handle either way.
step_outputs.12:
They rode at dawn.

Mist pooled in the hollows, thin and grey, clinging low to the ground like smoke that had forgotten its fire. The wagon creaked, the mare’s breath plumed. Irongate’s walls rose ahead, no longer a line on the horizon but a fact: black stone, iron-crowned towers, banners with the city’s crest snapping sharp in the morning wind.

Corin had seen those walls a hundred times. They’d never looked that tall.

Captain Kest rode at the head of their little column, posture straight, every piece of his kit in regulation order. Two riders flanked the wagon; two more brought up the rear. Lysira sat chained in the back, blanket gone, hair dried to a rough red snarl. Gag loose around her neck. Collar chain short.

“Eyes forward, Hale,” Kest called lightly without turning. “You start to look like a penitent, they’ll charge extra in confession.”

The outer gate yawned ahead. A double arch of stone and steel; portcullises raised, for now. Merchants, drovers, peasants queued in the right-hand lane; their little group took the left, the one reserved for official business. Guards in grey checked seals, lifted hands in salute as Kest held his signet aloft.

Corin’s palms felt damp on the reins.

As they passed under the arch, Lysira’s chains shivered. Corin glanced back.

She was watching the stone above them. The carvings of angels and swords, of bound demons underfoot. Her jaw was clenched so tight a muscle jumped in her cheek.

“Homecoming,” Kest said.

“For some,” Corin muttered.

On the far side, Irongate unfolded: tight, clean streets; slate roofs; smokestacks. The smell of forge and tanner and too many bodies. Bells rang somewhere deeper in the city, neat as clockwork.

They kept to the main artery, people parting around them. Some stared at Lysira. Word had passed—about the bounty, the tiefling witch. Corin heard a murmur: that’s her. Devil’s whore. Murderess. A child pointed. A woman dragged the child’s hand down, eyes bright with something like hunger.

Lysira sat very straight, chin up, letting them look. No flinch.

Corin felt something sour crawl up his throat.

“They’re excited,” Kest said, catching his glance. “Don’t worry. We won’t give them a circus.”
